  • every day
  • on weekdays

docs.title: 🎧 Listening / Reading
docs.text:
Text:

I wake up at 6:45. I take a shower and have breakfast. Then I go to work by bus. I have lunch at 1. In the evening, I go home and cook dinner. After that, I watch TV. I go to bed at 11.

docs.title: 🗣️ Speaking
docs.text:
Ripeti:

  • I wake up at 7.
  • I have breakfast at 7:30.
  • Then I go to work.
  • I have lunch at 1.
  • In the evening, I relax.
  • I go to bed at 11.

Ora completa con informazioni vere:

  • I wake up at ______
  • I have breakfast at ______
  • Then I go to ______
  • I have lunch at ______
  • In the evening, I ______
  • I go to bed at ______

docs.title: ✍️ Writing
docs.text:
Scrivi 6–8 frasi sulla tua giornata tipica.

Guida:

  1. wake up
  2. breakfast
  3. work / school
  4. lunch
  5. evening
  6. bedtime

Esempio:

I wake up at 7. I have breakfast at 7:30. Then I go to work. I have lunch at 1. In the evening, I study English. I go to bed at 11.

docs.title: 🔊 Pronunciation
docs.text:
Attenzione a queste espressioni:

  • wake up → /weɪk ʌp/
  • get up → /ɡet ʌp/
  • go to work → spesso suona come go t'work nel parlato veloce
  • have breakfast → la h va pronunciata

Ripeti a voce alta:

  • I wake up at seven.
  • I go to work at eight.
  • I go to bed at eleven.

docs.title: ⚠️ Errori comuni
docs.text:
❌ I make a shower. ✅ I take a shower.

❌ I go at home at 6. ✅ I go home at 6.

❌ I breakfast at 7. ✅ I have breakfast at 7.

❌ In the night I watch TV. ✅ At night I watch TV.

docs.title: Completa
docs.text:
1. I ___ up at 7.
  2. I ___ breakfast at 7:30.
  3. Then I ___ to work.
  4. I ___ lunch at 1.
  5. I ___ TV in the evening.
  6. I go to ___ at 11.

docs.title: Metti in ordine
docs.text:
• I go to work
  • I wake up
  • I have lunch
  • I go to bed

docs.title: 🚀 Mini task
docs.text:
Parla per 1 minuto della tua giornata tipica usando almeno:

  • 4 verbi di routine
  • 3 espressioni di tempo
  • 2 parole di sequenza

docs.title: Professional
docs.text: Modalità Professional attiva. Questa versione amplia la lezione su Daily Routine con routine reali, collocazioni frequenti, differenza tra have breakfast / get dressed / go home, uso naturale delle sequenze temporali, errori tipici degli italiani, speaking drills articolati, writing guidato e self-check finale.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • descrivere una giornata tipica in modo ordinato e naturale
  • parlare di abitudini quotidiane usando il Present Simple
  • usare correttamente espressioni di tempo e sequenza
  • costruire mini-racconti semplici ma fluidi
  • evitare errori frequenti nelle collocazioni della routine quotidiana

docs.title: 🧭 Focus della lezione
docs.text:
Molti studenti A1 sanno già dire frasi isolate come I work, I study, I eat lunch, ma quando devono parlare davvero della loro giornata producono una lista disordinata di parole.

L'obiettivo di questa unità non è solo imparare nuovi verbi, ma imparare a collegare le azioni in una sequenza chiara:

  1. inizio della giornata
  2. preparazione del mattino
  3. lavoro / studio
  4. pranzo e pomeriggio
  5. sera
  6. fine della giornata

In altre parole, qui lavoriamo sulla fluency di base: non solo parole corrette, ma frasi che scorrono.

docs.title: Present Simple for routines
docs.text:
Usiamo il Present Simple per parlare di ciò che facciamo abitualmente.

  • I wake up at 6:30.
  • I leave home at 8.
  • I have lunch at work.
  • I usually go to bed at 11.

Il punto importante è che non stiamo descrivendo quello che succede adesso, ma quello che succede di solito.

docs.title: Time and sequence
docs.text: Per parlare bene della routine non bastano i verbi. Servono anche marcatori temporali e parole di collegamento.

docs.title: Time expressions
docs.text:
• at 6:30
  • in the morning
  • in the afternoon
  • in the evening
  • at night
  • every day
  • on weekdays
  • after work
  • before dinner
  • around 8
  • usually
  • normally
  • sometimes

docs.title: Sequence words
docs.text:
• first
  • then
  • after that
  • later
  • finally

Esempio:

First, I wake up at 6:30. Then I take a shower and get dressed. After that, I leave home and go to work. In the evening, I cook dinner. Finally, I go to bed.

Questa organizzazione rende il tuo inglese molto più chiaro e credibile.

docs.text:
• wake up
  • get up
  • take a shower
  • get dressed

docs.text:
• have breakfast
  • have lunch
  • have dinner
  • take a shower
  • get dressed
  • go home
  • start work

  • go to bed early

docs.text:
• always

  • often

Esempi:

  • I usually have breakfast at home.
  • I often go to work by bus.
  • I sometimes read before bed.

docs.title: 🧩 Funzioni comunicative
docs.text: Con questa unità impari a:

docs.title: 1. Descrivere la tua giornata tipica
docs.text:
• I wake up at 7 and I have breakfast at home.
  • Then I go to work by train.
  • In the evening, I study English.

docs.title: 2. Raccontare la routine di un'altra persona
docs.text:
• My sister starts work at 9.
  • My father usually gets home at 7.
  • My son does homework after dinner.

docs.title: 3. Organizzare il discorso in modo lineare
docs.text:
• First...
  • Then...
  • After that...
  • Later...
  • Finally...

docs.title: 4. Specificare orari e momenti della giornata
docs.text:
• at 7:15
  • around 8
  • before lunch
  • after work
  • at night

docs.title: Text 1 – Office routine
docs.text: I usually wake up at 6:45 on weekdays. First, I take a shower and get dressed. Then I have breakfast and leave home at about 7:45. I go to work by bus and I start work at 8:30. I have lunch with my colleagues at 1. In the evening, I go home, cook dinner and relax. I usually go to bed around 11.

docs.title: Text 2 – Student routine
docs.text: My daughter wakes up at 7. She has breakfast and goes to school at 8. She finishes school in the afternoon. After that, she does her homework. In the evening, she watches a video or reads a book. She goes to bed at 10.

docs.title: Notice
docs.text: Osserva che i testi non sono una lista casuale di verbi: ogni frase ha una posizione logica nel racconto.

docs.title: Drill 1 – Build your routine step by step
docs.text:
Completa oralmente:

  • I usually wake up at ______.
  • First, I ______.
  • Then I ______.
  • I go to work / school by ______.
  • I have lunch at ______.
  • In the evening, I ______.
  • Finally, I go to bed at ______.

docs.title: Drill 2 – Add more detail
docs.text:
Espandi le frasi:

  • I wake up at 7. → I usually wake up at 7 on weekdays.
  • I go to work. → Then I go to work by bus.
  • I eat dinner. → In the evening, I have dinner with my family.

docs.title: Drill 3 – Talk about another person
docs.text:
Parla di una persona reale:

  • My partner usually wakes up at ______.
  • He / She starts work at ______.
  • He / She has lunch at ______.
  • In the evening, he / she ______.
  • He / She goes to bed at ______.

docs.title: Drill 4 – One-minute fluency task
docs.text:
Parla per 1 minuto senza leggere e prova a usare:

  • first
  • then
  • after that
  • in the evening
  • finally

docs.title: Task 1 – My weekday routine
docs.text:
Scrivi 8–10 frasi sulla tua giornata tipo nei giorni feriali.

Checklist:

  • wake-up time
  • morning actions
  • transport / work / school
  • lunch
  • afternoon
  • evening
  • bedtime
  • at least 3 sequence words

docs.title: Task 2 – Compare two routines
docs.text:
Scrivi 6 frasi confrontando la tua routine con quella di un'altra persona.

Esempi di struttura:

  • I wake up at 6:30, but my brother wakes up at 8.
  • I go to work by car, but he goes by train.
  • I go to bed late, but she goes to bed early.

docs.title: Routine chunks to pronounce smoothly
docs.text:
L'obiettivo non è pronunciare parola per parola, ma allenare piccoli gruppi di parole.

Ripeti questi chunk:

  • wake up at seven
  • get up early
  • have breakfast at home
  • go to work by bus
  • go home after work
  • go to bed at eleven

docs.title: Points to notice
docs.text:
• get up e wake up hanno una parte finale breve e rapida
  • go to work nel parlato veloce tende a unirsi
  • have breakfast non significa fare colazione parola per parola: è una collocazione fissa

docs.title: 1. Usare il verbo sbagliato con "shower"
docs.text: ❌ I make a shower. ✅ I take a shower.

docs.title: 2. Tradurre letteralmente "vado a casa"
docs.text: ❌ I go to home at 6. ✅ I go home at 6.

docs.title: 3. Usare il sostantivo al posto del verbo corretto
docs.text: ❌ I breakfast at 7. ✅ I have breakfast at 7.

docs.title: 4. Dire in the night quando parli di routine
docs.text: ❌ In the night I study. ✅ At night I study.

docs.title: 5. Dimenticare la sequenza logica
docs.text:
❌ I dinner, I work, I wake up, I TV. ✅ I wake up, then I go to work, and in the evening I watch TV.

In inglese corretto non basta conoscere le parole: bisogna anche costruire una mini-sequenza chiara.

docs.title: Complete the routine
docs.text:
1. I ___ up at 6:30.
  2. I take a ___ and get dressed.
  3. Then I have ___.
  4. I go to ___ by bus.
  5. I have ___ at 1.
  6. In the evening, I go ___ and relax.
  7. Finally, I go to ___ at 11.

docs.title: Put the actions in a logical order
docs.text:
• go home
  • wake up
  • have lunch
  • go to work
  • go to bed

docs.title: Choose the correct option
docs.text:
1. I take / make a shower.
  2. I go to home / home at 6.
  3. I have / do breakfast at 7.
  4. I study at night / in the night.
  5. Then I go / goes to work.

docs.title: Rewrite in a more natural way
docs.text:
1. I wake up. I go to work. I eat. I sleep.
  2. I wake up at 7. I breakfast. I go to home.

Prova a trasformarle in piccole routine fluenti.

docs.title: 🧠 Self-check
docs.text:
Controlla se sai fare queste cose senza guardare la teoria:

  • descrivere una giornata tipica in almeno 8 frasi
  • usare almeno 5 verbi di routine comuni
  • aggiungere 3 o più espressioni di tempo
  • collegare le frasi con first / then / after that / finally
  • evitare errori come I make a shower o I go to home

Se riesci a farlo, stai passando da frasi isolate a un vero mini-racconto.

docs.title: 🚀 Mini task
docs.text:
Tema:

“My routine from morning to night”

Parla o scrivi per circa 1 minuto. Prova a includere:

  • wake up
  • breakfast
  • work / school
  • lunch
  • evening activity
  • bedtime
  • 3 sequence words

Esempio breve:

I usually wake up at 6:45. First, I take a shower and have breakfast. Then I go to work by bus. I have lunch at 1. In the evening, I go home and study English for thirty minutes. Finally, I go to bed at about 11.

docs.title: Base
docs.text: Modalità Base attiva. Questa versione ti aiuta a usare there is / there are per descrivere una stanza, un ufficio o un altro luogo in modo semplice. Per vedere la versione estesa usa il toggle BASE / PRO nella barra in alto.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • dire cosa c’è o cosa non c’è in un luogo
  • usare there is con il singolare
  • usare there are con il plurale
  • descrivere una stanza con frasi semplici e corrette

docs.title: There is
docs.text:
Usiamo there is per dire che c’è una sola cosa.

  • There is a table.
  • There is a bed in the room.
  • There is a lamp on the desk.

docs.title: There are
docs.text:
Usiamo there are per dire che ci sono più cose.

  • There are two chairs.
  • There are three books on the table.
  • There are two windows in the room.

docs.title: Schema rapido
docs.text: Singular Plural There is There are

docs.title: Forme negative
docs.text:
• There isn’t a TV.
  • There aren’t any plants.

docs.title: Domande utili
docs.text:
• Is there a bathroom?
  • Is there a desk?
  • Are there two chairs?
  • Are there any books?

Risposte brevi:

  • Yes, there is.
  • No, there isn’t.
  • Yes, there are.
  • No, there aren’t.

docs.title: In a room
docs.text:
• room
  • table
  • chair
  • bed
  • desk
  • lamp
  • sofa
  • shelf
  • door
  • window
  • wall
  • picture

docs.title: In an office or study area
docs.text:
• computer
  • laptop
  • screen
  • keyboard
  • mouse
  • printer
  • book
  • notebook
  • pen
  • bag

docs.title: Position words
docs.text:
• in
  • on
  • under
  • next to
  • near

docs.title: 🎧 Listening / Reading
docs.text:
Text:

There is a desk in my room. There is a lamp on the desk. There are two books next to the lamp. There is a chair under the window. There are three pictures on the wall. There isn’t a TV in the room.

docs.title: 🗣️ Speaking
docs.text:
Ripeti:

  • There is a table in the room.
  • There is a lamp on the desk.
  • There are two chairs.
  • There are three books on the shelf.
  • There isn’t a sofa.
  • Are there any windows?

Ora completa con informazioni vere:

  • There is a ______ in my room.
  • There are ______ on my desk.
  • There isn’t a ______ in the room.
  • There are ______ windows.

docs.title: ✍️ Writing
docs.text:
Scrivi 6 frasi per descrivere una stanza.

Guida:

  1. a bed / a desk / a table
  2. one lamp
  3. two or three books
  4. one window
  5. something that is not in the room
  6. one object with on / under / next to

Esempio:

There is a bed in the room. There is a desk near the window. There is a lamp on the desk. There are two books on the desk. There is a chair next to the desk. There isn’t a TV.

docs.title: Forma contratta
docs.text:
Nel parlato naturale:

  • There is → There’s

Esempi:

  • There’s a chair.
  • There’s a lamp on the table.

Con il plurale di solito manteniamo chiaramente:

  • There are two books.
  • There are three windows.

docs.title: ⚠️ Errori comuni
docs.text:
❌ There are a chair. ✅ There is a chair.

❌ There is two books. ✅ There are two books.

❌ Is a desk in the room. ✅ There is a desk in the room.

❌ There have two windows. ✅ There are two windows.

👉 Controlla sempre se il nome è singolare o plurale.

docs.title: Completa
docs.text:
1. There ___ a bed in the room.
  2. There ___ two chairs.
  3. There ___ a lamp on the desk.
  4. There ___ three books on the shelf.
  5. There ___ not a TV.
  6. ___ there any windows?

docs.title: Trasforma
docs.text:
1. There is a chair. → (plurale) ______
  2. There are two books. → (singolare) ______
  3. There is a lamp. → (negativa) ______

docs.title: 🚀 Mini task
docs.text:
Descrivi la tua stanza, il tuo ufficio o la tua cucina usando:

  • almeno 5 oggetti
  • almeno 2 frasi con there is
  • almeno 2 frasi con there are
  • almeno 1 frase negativa

docs.title: Professional
docs.text: Modalità Professional attiva. Questa versione amplia la lezione su there is / there are con descrizioni più naturali di ambienti reali, uso di a / an / some / any, posizione degli oggetti, forme interrogative e negative, errori tipici degli italofoni, speaking drills, writing guidato e self-check finale.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • descrivere stanze, uffici e spazi quotidiani in modo chiaro e credibile
  • usare correttamente there is / there are al singolare e al plurale
  • costruire frasi negative e domande naturali
  • combinare there is / there are con a, an, some, any e con semplici preposizioni di luogo
  • evitare gli errori più comuni degli italofoni quando descrivono ciò che vedono intorno a sé

docs.title: 🧭 Focus della lezione
docs.text:
Questa unità non serve solo a dire c’è / ci sono. Serve a fare qualcosa di molto più utile: descrivere uno spazio in modo organizzato.

In inglese iniziale, saper descrivere una stanza, una casa, una scrivania o un ufficio ti permette di:

  1. spiegare dove ti trovi
  2. parlare di oggetti presenti o assenti
  3. rispondere a domande pratiche
  4. fare una descrizione visiva semplice ma efficace
  5. prepararti alle unità successive su preposizioni, casa, città e descrizione di luoghi

L’obiettivo non è creare frasi isolate, ma produrre una mini-descrizione coerente.

docs.title: There is / There are
docs.text: Usiamo there is / there are per introdurre l’esistenza di qualcosa in un luogo.

docs.title: Singular: there is
docs.text:
Per una cosa sola:

  • There is a desk in the room.
  • There is an old sofa near the wall.
  • There is a lamp on the table.

docs.title: Plural: there are
docs.text:
Per più cose:

  • There are two chairs in the office.
  • There are some books on the shelf.
  • There are three windows in the living room.

Il punto essenziale è questo:

  • there is → una cosa
  • there are → più cose

docs.title: A / An / Some / Any
docs.text: Quando descrivi un luogo, molto spesso queste parole lavorano insieme a there is / there are.

docs.title: a / an
docs.text:
Per un nome singolare numerabile:

  • There is a chair.
  • There is a table.
  • There is an armchair.
  • There is an office next door.

docs.title: some
docs.text:
Molto comune nelle frasi affermative plurali:

  • There are some books on the desk.
  • There are some files in the drawer.

docs.title: any
docs.text:
Molto comune nelle domande e nelle frasi negative:

  • Are there any chairs in the room?
  • There aren’t any pictures on the wall.

👉 A questo livello va benissimo ricordare questa regola pratica:

  • some → spesso affermativa
  • any → spesso domanda / negativa

docs.title: Singular
docs.text:
• There isn’t a TV in the room.
  • There isn’t a window in this office.

docs.title: Plural
docs.text:
• There aren’t any chairs here.
  • There aren’t any books on the shelf.

Quando parli di ciò che manca, queste forme sono essenziali.

docs.title: Singular
docs.text:
• Is there a bathroom?
  • Is there a printer in the office?
  • Is there a lamp on the desk?

docs.title: Plural
docs.text:
• Are there any windows?
  • Are there two meeting rooms?
  • Are there any people in the office?

docs.title: Short answers
docs.text:
• Yes, there is.
  • No, there isn’t.
  • Yes, there are.
  • No, there aren’t.

docs.title: There is / It is
docs.text: Questa distinzione è molto importante.

docs.title: There is / There are
docs.text:
Si usa per introdurre qualcosa:

  • There is a laptop on the desk.
  • There are two windows in the room.

docs.title: It is / They are
docs.text:
Si usa quando l’oggetto è già noto e vuoi aggiungere informazioni:

  • There is a laptop on the desk. It is new.
  • There are two windows in the room. They are very large.

👉 Quindi:

  • there is / there are = esistenza
  • it is / they are = descrizione dell’oggetto già introdotto

docs.title: Prepositions of place you need now
docs.text:
Per rendere la descrizione più utile, aggiungi semplici preposizioni di luogo:

  • in the room
  • on the desk
  • under the table
  • next to the bed
  • near the window
  • behind the chair
  • in front of the sofa

Esempi:

  • There is a lamp on the desk.
  • There are two boxes under the table.
  • There is a chair next to the window.

docs.title: Furniture and room items
docs.text:
• table
  • chair
  • bed
  • desk
  • sofa
  • armchair
  • shelf
  • lamp
  • carpet
  • wardrobe
  • drawer
  • mirror
  • picture
  • plant

docs.title: Office and study objects
docs.text:
• laptop
  • computer
  • screen
  • keyboard
  • mouse
  • printer
  • file
  • notebook
  • charger
  • document
  • pen
  • folder

docs.title: Structural elements
docs.text:
• room
  • wall
  • floor
  • ceiling
  • door
  • window
  • corner

docs.title: 🧩 Funzioni comunicative
docs.text: Con questa unità impari a:

docs.title: 1. Descrivere una stanza
docs.text:
• There is a bed next to the wall.
  • There are two shelves above the desk.

docs.title: 2. Parlare di ciò che manca
docs.text:
• There isn’t a sofa in the room.
  • There aren’t any pictures on the wall.

docs.title: 3. Fare domande pratiche
docs.text:
• Is there a bathroom?
  • Are there any plugs near the desk?

docs.title: 4. Guidare visivamente qualcuno
docs.text:
• There is a printer on the left.
  • There are some files under the desk.

docs.title: 5. Fare una mini-presentazione di casa o ufficio
docs.text: • In my office, there is a large desk and there are two screens.

docs.title: Text 1 – Describing a bedroom
docs.text: There is a bed in the middle of the room. There is a small desk near the window. There is a lamp on the desk and there are some books under it. There are two pictures on the wall. There isn’t a TV, but there is a laptop on the chair.

docs.title: Text 2 – Describing an office
docs.text: In our office, there are six desks and twelve chairs. There is a printer near the door. There are some documents on the main table. There isn’t a meeting room on this floor, but there are two phone booths. There are also a few plants near the windows.

docs.title: Notice
docs.text:
Osserva che le descrizioni più naturali non sono una lista casuale. Spesso seguono un ordine:

  1. visione generale del luogo
  2. oggetti principali
  3. oggetti secondari
  4. elementi mancanti
  5. dettagli finali

docs.title: Drill 1 – Singular or plural?
docs.text:
Completa oralmente:

  • There is a ______ in the room.
  • There are two ______ near the wall.
  • There isn’t a ______ on the table.
  • There aren’t any ______ under the desk.

docs.title: Drill 2 – Add position
docs.text:
Espandi le frasi:

  • There is a lamp. → There is a lamp on the desk.
  • There are two chairs. → There are two chairs near the window.
  • There is a bag. → There is a bag under the table.

docs.title: Drill 3 – Ask and answer
docs.text:
Pratica in coppia o da solo:

  • Is there a printer in the office?
  • Are there any books on the shelf?
  • Is there a sofa in the living room?
  • Are there two doors in the room?

Rispondi con short answers e poi con una frase completa.

docs.title: Drill 4 – One-minute room description
docs.text:
Parla per 1 minuto e prova a usare:

  • 3 frasi con there is
  • 3 frasi con there are
  • 1 frase negativa
  • 2 preposizioni di luogo

docs.title: Task 1 – Guided description
docs.text:
Scrivi 8–10 frasi su una stanza reale o immaginaria.

Checklist:

  • una frase introduttiva generale
  • almeno 3 oggetti singolari
  • almeno 3 oggetti plurali
  • almeno 2 dettagli con preposizioni di luogo
  • almeno 1 frase negativa

Modello:

In my room, there is a desk near the window. There is a lamp on the desk. There are some books next to the lamp. There is a chair under the window. There are two pictures on the wall. There isn’t a TV. There is a small plant on the shelf.

docs.title: Task 2 – Compare two spaces
docs.text:
Scrivi 4 frasi su una stanza a casa e 4 frasi su un ufficio o aula.

Obiettivo: imparare a riusare la stessa struttura in contesti diversi.

docs.title: There is → There’s
docs.text:
Nel parlato naturale la forma contratta è molto comune:

  • There’s a chair by the door.
  • There’s a lamp on the desk.

docs.title: There are
docs.text:
Non sempre si contrae nel parlato standard lento. All’inizio è meglio pronunciarlo bene:

  • There are two books.
  • There are three people in the office.

docs.title: Rhythm tip
docs.text:
Le parole forti di solito sono i nomi e le posizioni:

  • There’s a lamp on the desk.
  • There are two chairs near the window.

docs.title: 1. Usare there have
docs.text: ❌ There have two chairs. ✅ There are two chairs.

docs.title: 2. Confondere singolare e plurale
docs.text:
❌ There is two windows. ✅ There are two windows.

❌ There are a desk. ✅ There is a desk.

docs.title: 3. Dimenticare there
docs.text: ❌ Is a lamp on the table. ✅ There is a lamp on the table.

docs.title: 4. Usare male any
docs.text: ❌ There aren’t some books. ✅ There aren’t any books.

docs.title: 5. Usare is con liste plurali
docs.text:
❌ There is a desk and two chairs. ✅ There are two chairs and a desk. ✅ There is a desk and there are two chairs.

👉 In inglese reale, quando la frase diventa lunga, spesso è più chiaro dividerla in due.

docs.title: Completa
docs.text:
1. There ___ a chair next to the desk.
  2. There ___ three books on the table.
  3. There ___ not a printer in this room.
  4. Are there ___ windows in the office?
  5. There are ___ files in the drawer.
  6. Is there ___ lamp near the bed?

docs.title: Correggi gli errori
docs.text:
1. There is two computers.
  2. There are a chair near the window.
  3. There isn’t some books on the shelf.
  4. There have a printer in the office.
  5. Is there any chairs?

docs.title: Trasforma
docs.text:
1. There is a lamp on the desk. → negativa
  2. There are some chairs in the room. → domanda
  3. There is a window near the bed. → plurale
  4. There aren’t any pictures on the wall. → affermativa

docs.title: 🧠 Think & Speak
docs.text:
Rispondi oralmente:

  • Is there a desk in your room?
  • Are there any books near your bed?
  • Is there a TV in your kitchen?
  • Are there plants in your office or study area?
  • What is on your desk right now?

Poi prova a unire le risposte in una mini descrizione continua.

docs.title: 🚀 Mini task
docs.text:
Immagina di mostrare la tua stanza o il tuo ufficio a una persona straniera in videochiamata.

Parla per 60–90 secondi e includi:

  • descrizione generale del luogo
  • oggetti principali
  • posizione di almeno 3 oggetti
  • una cosa che manca
  • una frase finale con la tua opinione

Esempio finale:

My room is small but comfortable. There is a desk near the window and there is a lamp on it. There are some books under the desk and there are two pictures on the wall. There isn’t a TV, but there is a laptop on the chair. I like this room because it is simple and quiet.

docs.title: Base
docs.text: Modalità Base attiva. Questa versione ti aiuta a parlare dei luoghi della città e a fare domande semplici con Is there...? / Are there...?. Per vedere la versione estesa usa il toggle BASE / PRO nella barra in alto.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • nominare luoghi comuni in una città
  • dire se un posto esiste oppure no
  • fare domande semplici con Is there...? / Are there...?
  • descrivere in modo essenziale la tua città o il tuo quartiere

docs.title: Domande con there is / there are
docs.text:
Usiamo Is there...? per chiedere se c’è un solo luogo.

  • Is there a bank?
  • Is there a supermarket near here?
  • Is there a park in your town?

Usiamo Are there...? per chiedere se ci sono più luoghi.

  • Are there any shops?
  • Are there restaurants in the centre?
  • Are there any hotels near the station?

docs.title: Risposte brevi
docs.text:
• Yes, there is.
  • No, there isn’t.
  • Yes, there are.
  • No, there aren’t.

Esempi:

  • Is there a hospital? → Yes, there is.
  • Are there any cafés? → No, there aren’t.

docs.title: Frasi affermative
docs.text:
• There is a bank near my house.
  • There is a park in my town.
  • There are two cafés in my street.
  • There are many shops in the centre.

docs.title: Places in town
docs.text:
• restaurant
  • café
  • bar
  • supermarket
  • bank
  • school
  • hospital
  • pharmacy
  • park
  • hotel
  • station
  • shop
  • cinema
  • museum

docs.title: Useful words
docs.text:
• near
  • far
  • here
  • there
  • in the centre
  • in my town
  • on my street

docs.title: 🎧 Listening / Reading
docs.text:
Dialogue:

A: Excuse me, is there a bank near here? B: Yes, there is. It’s next to the supermarket. A: Are there any cafés in this area? B: Yes, there are two cafés on this street. A: Is there a pharmacy? B: No, there isn’t.

docs.title: 🗣️ Speaking
docs.text:
Ripeti:

  • Is there a restaurant near here?
  • Is there a bank in your town?
  • Are there any shops on this street?
  • There is a park near my house.
  • There are two cafés in the centre.

Ora completa con informazioni vere:

  • There is a ______ in my town.
  • There are ______ near my house.
  • There isn’t a ______ in my area.
  • Are there any ______ in your town?

docs.title: ✍️ Writing
docs.text:
Scrivi 6–8 frasi sulla tua città o sul tuo quartiere.

Guida:

  1. un luogo vicino a casa tua
  2. due luoghi nel centro
  3. una cosa che non c’è
  4. una domanda che puoi fare a un turista

Esempio:

There is a supermarket near my house. There is a park in my town. There are two cafés in the centre. There are many shops on the main street. There isn’t a museum in my area. Is there a hotel near the station?

docs.title: 🔊 Pronunciation
docs.text:
Attenzione a queste parole:

  • restaurant
  • pharmacy
  • station
  • museum

docs.title: Tip
docs.text: In inglese la pronuncia non segue sempre le regole italiane. Ascolta e ripeti più volte intere frasi, non solo parole isolate.

docs.title: ⚠️ Errori comuni
docs.text:
❌ Is there restaurants? ✅ Are there restaurants?

❌ There is many shops. ✅ There are many shops.

❌ Is there a cafés? ✅ Are there cafés?

❌ In my town have a park. ✅ In my town, there is a park.

docs.title: Completa
docs.text:
1. ___ there a hotel near here?
  2. ___ there any parks in your town?
  3. There ___ a station in the centre.
  4. There ___ two cafés on this street.
  5. There ___ not a hospital in my area.

docs.title: Trasforma in domanda
docs.text:
1. There is a bank near here. → ______?
  2. There are shops in the centre. → ______?

docs.title: 🚀 Mini task
docs.text:
Immagina di parlare con un turista.

Fai 4 domande:

  • Is there a hotel near here?
  • Is there a restaurant in the centre?
  • Are there any cafés nearby?
  • Is there a pharmacy on this street?

Poi rispondi come abitante della città.

docs.title: Professional
docs.text: Modalità Professional attiva. Questa versione amplia la lezione su places in town con lessico più ricco, descrizione di quartieri e servizi, uso naturale di there is / there are, domande utili per orientarsi, collocazioni frequenti, mini-dialoghi realistici, errori tipici degli italofoni, speaking drills, writing guidato e self-check finale.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • parlare della tua città o del tuo quartiere in modo più organizzato e realistico
  • usare there is / there are per descrivere servizi e luoghi pubblici
  • fare domande naturali per chiedere se un luogo esiste e dove si trova
  • usare un lessico più preciso per orientamento, servizi, centro città e zona residenziale
  • evitare errori frequenti quando descrivi una città in inglese

docs.title: 🧭 Focus della lezione
docs.text:
Questa unità non serve solo a memorizzare parole come bank o park. Serve a fare tre cose molto utili nella comunicazione reale:

  1. descrivere un luogo dove vivi o che conosci
  2. aiutare qualcuno a orientarsi
  3. fare domande pratiche da turista o da residente

In inglese A1, saper parlare della propria città è una competenza molto importante perché unisce:

  • grammatica di base
  • lessico quotidiano
  • micro-interazione reale
  • descrizione guidata di uno spazio pubblico

L’obiettivo finale non è fare frasi isolate, ma costruire una mini-presentazione credibile della propria zona.

docs.title: Talking about places with there is / there are
docs.text: Quando descrivi una città o un quartiere, there is / there are ti serve per dire quali servizi o luoghi sono presenti.

docs.title: Singular: there is
docs.text:
Per un solo luogo o servizio:

  • There is a pharmacy near my house.
  • There is a small park in my neighbourhood.
  • There is a railway station in the city centre.
  • There is a museum near the river.

docs.title: Plural: there are
docs.text:
Per più luoghi:

  • There are two supermarkets in my area.
  • There are many cafés in the centre.
  • There are several shops on this street.
  • There are three bus stops near the station.

👉 Regola chiave:

  • there is = una cosa
  • there are = più cose

docs.title: Questions: Is there...? / Are there...?
docs.text: Queste forme servono per chiedere se un luogo esiste.

docs.title: Singular
docs.text:
• Is there a bank near here?
  • Is there a post office in your town?
  • Is there a bus stop near the hotel?
  • Is there a pharmacy open now?

docs.title: Plural
docs.text:
• Are there any restaurants in this area?
  • Are there cafés near the station?
  • Are there any parks in your neighbourhood?
  • Are there many hotels in the city centre?

docs.title: Short answers
docs.text:
• Yes, there is.
  • No, there isn’t.
  • Yes, there are.
  • No, there aren’t.

docs.title: Full answers sound more natural
docs.text:
• Yes, there is a bank next to the supermarket.
  • No, there isn’t a pharmacy on this street.
  • Yes, there are two cafés near the square.

👉 In una conversazione vera, la short answer è corretta, ma spesso è meglio aggiungere un’informazione in più.

docs.title: Some / Any / A lot of
docs.text: Quando parli della città, queste parole sono molto frequenti.

docs.title: some
docs.text:
Nelle affermazioni plurali:

  • There are some shops near my house.
  • There are some good restaurants in the centre.

docs.title: any
docs.text:
Nelle domande e nelle negative:

  • Are there any cafés near here?
  • There aren’t any buses after midnight.

docs.title: a lot of / many
docs.text:
Per quantità maggiori:

  • There are a lot of tourists in summer.
  • There are many restaurants in the old town.

👉 A livello iniziale, a lot of suona spesso più naturale di many nelle frasi spontanee.

docs.title: Useful prepositions and place phrases
docs.text: Per descrivere una città non basta dire che un posto esiste. Devi anche dire dove si trova.

docs.title: Basic location phrases
docs.text:
• near my house
  • near the station
  • in the centre
  • in the city centre
  • on this street
  • next to the bank
  • opposite the hotel
  • between the supermarket and the café
  • far from my home

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• There is a pharmacy near the station.
  • There are two cafés on this street.
  • There is a bank next to the supermarket.
  • There is a hotel opposite the station.
  • There are some restaurants in the city centre.

docs.title: Public places and services
docs.text:
• bank
  • pharmacy
  • hospital
  • school
  • library
  • museum
  • cinema
  • hotel
  • station
  • bus stop
  • post office
  • police station

docs.title: Food and social places
docs.text:
• restaurant
  • café
  • bar
  • bakery
  • market
  • supermarket

docs.title: Outdoor places
docs.text:
• park
  • square
  • street
  • bridge
  • river
  • playground

docs.title: Area words
docs.text:
• town
  • city
  • neighbourhood
  • area
  • centre
  • suburb
  • old town
  • main street

docs.title: 🧩 Funzioni comunicative
docs.text: Con questa unità impari a:

docs.title: 1. Dire cosa c’è nella tua zona
docs.text:
• There is a supermarket near my house.
  • There are two parks in my neighbourhood.

docs.title: 2. Chiedere se un servizio esiste
docs.text:
• Is there a pharmacy near here?
  • Are there any taxis near the station?

docs.title: 3. Dare informazioni utili a un turista
docs.text:
• There is a good restaurant near the square.
  • There are several cafés in the centre.

docs.title: 4. Descrivere differenze tra centro e periferia
docs.text:
• In the city centre there are many shops.
  • In my area there aren’t many restaurants.

docs.title: 5. Esprimere una valutazione semplice
docs.text:
• My town is small but convenient.
  • My neighbourhood is quiet and there is a nice park nearby.

docs.title: Dialogue 1 – Tourist information
docs.text: A: Excuse me, is there a pharmacy near here? B: Yes, there is. It’s next to the bank. A: And are there any cafés in this area? B: Yes, there are two cafés on the main street. A: Is there a bus stop nearby? B: Yes, there is one opposite the hotel.

docs.title: Dialogue 2 – Talking about your town
docs.text: A: What is your town like? B: It’s quite small, but there are a lot of useful places. A: Really? What is there? B: There is a library, there is a sports centre, and there are several shops in the centre. There isn’t a big museum, but there is a nice park by the river.

docs.title: Notice
docs.text:
Le descrizioni più naturali spesso seguono questo ordine:

  1. impressione generale della città
  2. luoghi principali
  3. servizi utili
  4. cosa manca
  5. valutazione finale

docs.title: Drill 1 – Complete the sentence
docs.text:
Completa oralmente:

  • There is a ______ near my house.
  • There are two ______ in my area.
  • There isn’t a ______ in my neighbourhood.
  • Are there any ______ in the centre?
  • Is there a ______ near the station?

docs.title: Drill 2 – Add location
docs.text:
Espandi le frasi:

  • There is a bank. → There is a bank near the station.
  • There are cafés. → There are cafés on the main street.
  • There is a park. → There is a park next to the school.

docs.title: Drill 3 – Ask and answer like a tourist
docs.text:
Pratica queste coppie:

  • Is there a hotel near here?
  • Is there a pharmacy open now?
  • Are there any restaurants in this area?
  • Are there buses to the city centre?

Rispondi prima con una short answer, poi con una frase completa.

docs.title: Drill 4 – Describe your area in one minute
docs.text:
Parla per 1 minuto e usa:

  • 3 frasi con there is
  • 3 frasi con there are
  • 1 frase negativa
  • 2 espressioni di luogo
  • 1 frase finale con la tua opinione

docs.title: Task 1 – Guided paragraph
docs.text:
Scrivi 8–10 frasi sulla tua città, sul tuo paese o sul tuo quartiere.

Checklist:

  • una frase introduttiva generale
  • almeno 3 luoghi singolari
  • almeno 3 luoghi plurali
  • almeno 2 dettagli con near / next to / opposite / in the centre
  • almeno 1 frase negativa
  • una frase finale con opinione personale

Modello:

My town is small but pleasant. There is a supermarket near my house. There is a pharmacy on the main street. There are two cafés in the centre. There are some small shops near the square. There is a park next to the school. There isn’t a train station, but there is a bus stop near the church. I like my town because it is quiet and practical.

docs.title: Task 2 – Compare two places
docs.text:
Scrivi 4 frasi sul centro città e 4 frasi sulla tua zona.

Obiettivo: imparare a confrontare due contesti usando le stesse strutture.

docs.title: Focus words
docs.text:
• restaurant
  • pharmacy
  • museum
  • station
  • neighbourhood

docs.title: Rhythm tip
docs.text:
Nelle domande, le parole più importanti sono spesso il luogo e la posizione:

  • Is there a bank near here?
  • Are there any cafés in the centre?

docs.title: Fluency tip
docs.text:
Non leggere parola per parola. Prova a pronunciare interi blocchi:

  • near my house
  • in the city centre
  • next to the station
  • opposite the hotel

docs.title: 1. Confondere singolare e plurale
docs.text:
❌ Is there restaurants near here? ✅ Are there restaurants near here?

❌ There is many cafés in the centre. ✅ There are many cafés in the centre.

docs.title: 2. Dimenticare there
docs.text: ❌ In my town is a park. ✅ In my town, there is a park.

docs.title: 3. Tradurre dall’italiano con have
docs.text:
❌ My town has a lot of shops near here. ✅ There are a lot of shops in my town.

Nota: My town has... non è sempre sbagliato, ma a questo livello, quando descrivi cosa esiste in un luogo, there is / there are è la scelta più sicura e centrale.

docs.title: 4. Usare male any
docs.text: ❌ There aren’t some parks in my area. ✅ There aren’t any parks in my area.

docs.title: 5. Omettere l’articolo con i luoghi singolari
docs.text: ❌ Is there bank near here? ✅ Is there a bank near here?

docs.title: Completa
docs.text:
1. ___ there a pharmacy near the station?
  2. ___ there any cafés in the centre?
  3. There ___ a hotel opposite the square.
  4. There ___ two supermarkets in my area.
  5. There ___ not any museums in this town.
  6. There are ___ lot of restaurants near the river.

docs.title: Correggi gli errori
docs.text:
1. Is there restaurants in the centre?
  2. There is many shops on this street.
  3. In my town is a library.
  4. There aren’t some cafés near the station.
  5. Are there a hotel near here?

docs.title: Trasforma
docs.text:
1. There is a bank near the museum. → domanda
  2. There are some cafés on this street. → negativa
  3. There isn’t a hospital in my area. → affermativa
  4. Are there any parks near your house? → risposta completa positiva

docs.title: 🧠 Think & Speak
docs.text:
Rispondi oralmente:

  • Is there a supermarket near your house?
  • Are there any restaurants in your neighbourhood?
  • Is there a park in your town?
  • Are there many shops in the centre?
  • What places do tourists usually visit in your city?

Poi unisci le risposte in una mini descrizione continua di 6–8 frasi.

docs.title: 🚀 Mini task
docs.text:
Immagina di aiutare un turista appena arrivato nella tua città.

Parla o scrivi una risposta di 60–90 secondi includendo:

  • una breve descrizione generale della città
  • almeno 4 luoghi utili
  • 2 indicazioni di posizione
  • 1 luogo che manca
  • 1 consiglio finale

Esempio finale:

My town is quite small, but it is comfortable and easy to visit. There is a pharmacy near the station and there is a hotel in the city centre. There are several cafés on the main street and there are some good restaurants near the square. There is a park next to the museum, but there isn’t a train station. I think it is a good place for a short visit because everything is close.

docs.title: Base
docs.text: Modalità Base attiva. Questa versione ti aiuta a usare can / can't per parlare di abilità, dire cosa sai o non sai fare e fare domande semplici sulle capacità personali. Per vedere la versione estesa usa il toggle BASE / PRO nella barra in alto.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • parlare di abilità personali con can / can't
  • dire cosa sai fare e cosa non sai fare
  • fare domande semplici con Can you...?
  • rispondere in modo breve e naturale

docs.title: Forma affermativa
docs.text:
Usiamo can + verbo base per dire che abbiamo un’abilità o una capacità.

  • I can swim.
  • You can cook.
  • She can drive.
  • We can speak English.

👉 Dopo can il verbo resta sempre nella forma base.

docs.title: Forma negativa
docs.text:
Usiamo can't o cannot per dire che non sappiamo o non possiamo fare qualcosa.

  • I can't drive.
  • He can't cook.
  • They cannot speak Spanish.

👉 can't è la forma più comune nel parlato.

docs.title: Forma interrogativa
docs.text:
Per fare domande:

Can + soggetto + verbo base?

  • Can you swim?
  • Can she drive?
  • Can they play tennis?

docs.title: Risposte brevi
docs.text:
• Yes, I can.
  • No, I can't.
  • Yes, she can.
  • No, she can't.

Esempi:

  • Can you cook? → Yes, I can.
  • Can he drive? → No, he can't.

docs.title: Skills and abilities
docs.text:
• swim
  • drive
  • speak English
  • read
  • write
  • cook
  • dance
  • sing
  • play football
  • play the piano
  • use a computer

docs.title: Useful expressions
docs.text:
• very well
  • quite well
  • a little
  • not very well

docs.title: 🎧 Listening / Reading
docs.text:
Dialogue:

A: Can you swim? B: Yes, I can. A: Can you drive? B: No, I can't. A: Can you cook? B: Yes, a little. A: Can you speak English? B: Yes, quite well.

docs.title: 🗣️ Speaking
docs.text:
Ripeti:

  • I can swim.
  • I can cook.
  • I can't drive.
  • Can you speak English?
  • Yes, I can.
  • No, I can't.

Ora parla di te:

  • I can ______.
  • I can’t ______.
  • I can ______ quite well.
  • I can ______ a little.

Fai 4 domande a voce:

  • Can you swim?
  • Can you drive?
  • Can you cook?
  • Can you use a computer?

docs.title: ✍️ Writing
docs.text:
Scrivi 6 frasi:

  • 3 cose che sai fare
  • 3 cose che non sai fare

Esempio:

I can swim. I can cook. I can speak English a little. I can't drive. I can't play the piano. I can't dance very well.

docs.title: 🔊 Pronunciation
docs.text:
Attenzione alla differenza tra:

  • can
  • can't

Nel parlato, can spesso è debole nella frase.

  • I can swim.
  • I can't swim.

👉 Ascolta e ripeti la frase completa, non solo la parola isolata.

docs.title: ⚠️ Errori comuni
docs.text:
❌ I can to swim. ✅ I can swim.

❌ She can to cook. ✅ She can cook.

❌ Can you can drive? ✅ Can you drive?

❌ I no can swim. ✅ I can't swim.

docs.title: Completa
docs.text:
1. I ___ swim.
  2. She ___ drive.
  3. He can't ___ the piano.
  4. ___ you cook?
  5. They ___ speak English very well.

docs.title: Trasforma
docs.text:
1. I can swim. → negativo: ______
  2. She can drive. → domanda: ______
  3. They can't cook. → affermativa: ______

docs.title: 🚀 Mini task
docs.text:
Fai una mini intervista orale.

Domande:

  • Can you swim?
  • Can you cook?
  • Can you drive?
  • Can you speak English?
  • Can you use a computer?

Poi rispondi come se stessi parlando con una nuova persona.

docs.title: Professional
docs.text: Modalità Professional attiva. Questa versione amplia la lezione su can / can't con uso reale delle abilità, differenza tra livello di competenza e semplice possibilità, collocazioni frequenti, mini-dialoghi realistici, errori tipici degli italofoni, speaking drills progressivi, writing guidato e self-check finale.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • parlare delle tue abilità in modo più naturale e preciso
  • usare can / can't per descrivere capacità presenti e limiti reali
  • fare domande autentiche sulle competenze di un’altra persona
  • aggiungere dettagli come well, quite well, a little, very well
  • evitare errori tipici nella struttura con i modali

docs.title: 🧭 Focus della lezione
docs.text:
Questa unità non serve solo a dire I can swim. Serve a fare qualcosa di più utile:

  1. parlare delle tue competenze in situazioni reali
  2. chiedere a qualcuno cosa sa fare
  3. descrivere il tuo livello in modo semplice ma credibile
  4. distinguere tra abilità, conoscenza di una lingua e attività pratiche

A livello A1, can è uno dei modali più importanti perché permette subito di fare conversazione personale:

  • presentarti
  • parlare di hobby
  • descrivere competenze quotidiane
  • fare mini-interviste

L’obiettivo non è produrre frasi isolate, ma costruire una breve presentazione di te che suoni naturale.

docs.title: Using can to talk about ability
docs.text:
Usiamo can + verbo base per dire che una persona ha la capacità di fare qualcosa.

  • I can swim.
  • She can drive.
  • We can cook.
  • They can speak English.

docs.title: Regola fondamentale
docs.text:
Dopo can:

  • non usiamo to
  • non cambiamo la forma del verbo
  • non aggiungiamo -s alla terza persona

Corretto:

  • She can cook.
  • He can speak Italian.

Non corretto:

  • She can cooks.
  • He can to speak Italian.

docs.title: Negative form: can't / cannot
docs.text:
Per dire che una persona non sa fare qualcosa:

  • I can't drive.
  • He can't swim.
  • We cannot play the piano.

docs.title: Nota d’uso
docs.text:
• can't = forma più comune nel parlato
  • cannot = più formale o enfatica nello scritto

Per l’A1, can't è la scelta più naturale nella comunicazione quotidiana.

docs.title: Questions with Can...?
docs.text:
Per fare domande:

  • Can you swim?
  • Can she drive?
  • Can your brother cook?
  • Can they speak English?

docs.title: Short answers
docs.text:
• Yes, I can.
  • No, I can't.
  • Yes, she can.
  • No, she can't.

docs.title: Full answers sound better in conversation
docs.text:
• Yes, I can swim quite well.
  • No, I can't drive yet.
  • Yes, she can cook very well.
  • No, they can't speak English.

👉 Le risposte complete sono più utili perché fanno avanzare la conversazione.

docs.title: Ability vs level of ability
docs.text: Con can non dici solo sì o no. Puoi anche indicare quanto bene sai fare qualcosa.

docs.title: Common adverbs and expressions
docs.text:
• very well
  • quite well
  • well
  • a little
  • not very well

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• I can speak English a little.
  • She can cook very well.
  • He can drive quite well.
  • I can swim, but not very well.

👉 Questo rende il tuo inglese più realistico, perché spesso nella vita vera l’abilità non è totale: è parziale o dipende dalla situazione.

docs.title: Common collocations with can
docs.text: Alcuni verbi compaiono molto spesso con can.

docs.title: Personal abilities
docs.text:
• can swim
  • can drive
  • can cook
  • can dance
  • can sing
  • can run fast

docs.title: Language and school-related skills
docs.text:
• can speak English
  • can read English
  • can write emails
  • can understand simple texts
  • can use a computer

docs.title: Hobbies and activities
docs.text:
• can play football
  • can play the guitar
  • can take good photos
  • can draw
  • can ride a bike

👉 Imparare blocchi come can play the piano è più utile che memorizzare singole parole isolate.

docs.title: Skills and daily abilities
docs.text:
• swim
  • drive
  • cook
  • dance
  • sing
  • draw
  • use a computer
  • ride a bike
  • take photos
  • play chess

docs.title: Language-related abilities
docs.text:
• speak English
  • read in English
  • write simple messages
  • understand basic questions
  • spell words

docs.title: Useful expressions
docs.text:
• very well
  • quite well
  • a little
  • not very well
  • at all
  • yet

docs.title: 🧩 Funzioni comunicative
docs.text: Con questa unità impari a:

docs.title: 1. Dire quali abilità hai
docs.text:
• I can cook.
  • I can drive.
  • I can speak English a little.

docs.title: 2. Dire quali abilità non hai
docs.text:
• I can't swim.
  • I can't play the guitar.
  • I can't understand fast English.

docs.title: 3. Chiedere a qualcuno cosa sa fare
docs.text:
• Can you cook?
  • Can you drive?
  • Can you play the piano?
  • Can you speak another language?

docs.title: 4. Specificare il tuo livello
docs.text:
• I can speak English a little.
  • I can cook quite well.
  • I can dance, but not very well.

docs.title: 5. Fare una mini-presentazione personale
docs.text: • I can cook and I can use a computer very well, but I can't drive yet.

docs.title: Dialogue 1 – First conversation in class
docs.text: A: Can you speak English well? B: A little. I can understand simple questions, but I can't speak very fast. A: That's fine. Can you write in English? B: Yes, I can write short messages.

docs.title: Dialogue 2 – Talking about skills
docs.text: A: Can your sister drive? B: Yes, she can. She can drive very well. A: Can she cook too? B: Yes, a little, but she can't cook Italian food very well.

docs.title: Dialogue 3 – Interview with a new friend
docs.text: A: Can you play any instruments? B: Yes, I can play the guitar a little. A: Can you sing too? B: No, I can't sing at all.

docs.title: Notice
docs.text:
Le conversazioni più naturali non si fermano a Yes, I can / No, I can't. Spesso aggiungono:

  • un dettaglio di livello
  • un contrasto con but
  • un esempio concreto

Esempio:

  • I can cook, but only simple dishes.

docs.title: Drill 1 – True sentences about you
docs.text:
Completa oralmente:

  • I can ______ very well.
  • I can ______ a little.
  • I can't ______.
  • I can't ______ yet.
  • I can ______, but not very well.

docs.title: Drill 2 – Question practice
docs.text:
Fai queste domande ad alta voce:

  • Can you swim?
  • Can you cook well?
  • Can you drive?
  • Can you speak English at work?
  • Can you use a computer well?
  • Can you play a musical instrument?

docs.title: Drill 3 – Short answer + full answer
docs.text:
Per ogni domanda, rispondi in due modi:

  1. risposta breve
  2. risposta completa

Esempio:

  • Can you cook?
  • Yes, I can.
  • Yes, I can cook quite well.

docs.title: Drill 4 – Speak for 1 minute
docs.text:
Parla di te e usa:

  • 3 frasi con can
  • 2 frasi con can't
  • 2 espressioni di livello
  • 1 frase con but
  • 1 frase finale sul tuo obiettivo

Esempio di chiusura:

  • I can't drive yet, but I want to learn.

docs.title: Task 1 – Guided profile
docs.text:
Scrivi 8–10 frasi su di te.

Checklist:

  • 3 abilità che hai
  • 2 cose che non sai fare
  • 2 frasi con well / quite well / a little / not very well
  • 1 frase con but
  • 1 frase finale su cosa vuoi migliorare

Modello:

I can use a computer very well. I can cook quite well. I can speak English a little. I can't drive. I can't sing at all. I can swim, but not very well. I want to improve my English.

docs.title: Task 2 – Interview a classmate or invent one
docs.text:
Scrivi 5 domande con Can you...? e poi scrivi risposte complete.

Esempi:

  • Can you cook?

  • Yes, I can cook simple meals.

  • Can you play the piano?

  • No, I can't play the piano, but I can play football.

docs.title: can vs can't
docs.text:
Nel parlato questa differenza è importante.

  • can è spesso debole nella frase
  • can't è più forte e marcato

Confronta:

  • I can swim.
  • I can't swim.

docs.title: Tip pratico
docs.text:
Non allenare solo la parola singola. Ripeti sempre la frase intera, perché il ritmo della frase aiuta a sentire la differenza.

Ripeti:

  • I can drive.
  • I can't drive.
  • She can cook.
  • She can't cook.
  • We can speak English.
  • We can't speak English very well.

docs.title: ⚠️ Errori comuni degli italiani
docs.text:
❌ I can to swim. ✅ I can swim.

❌ She can cooks. ✅ She can cook.

❌ Can you can drive? ✅ Can you drive?

❌ I no can speak English. ✅ I can't speak English.

❌ He can speaks English very well. ✅ He can speak English very well.

👉 Ricorda: can è già un verbo modale. Non ha bisogno di do, to o della -s.

docs.title: 🧠 Pattern fondamentali
docs.text:
Memorizza questi blocchi:

  • I can + verbo
  • I can't + verbo
  • Can you + verbo?
  • Yes, I can.
  • No, I can't.
  • I can + verbo + well / a little / not very well

Esempi:

  • I can cook.
  • I can't drive.
  • Can you swim?
  • I can speak English a little.

docs.title: Completa
docs.text:
1. She ___ drive very well.
  2. I can't ___ the piano.
  3. ___ you swim?
  4. He can speak English, but not very ___.
  5. We ___ use a computer.
  6. They can't ___ very fast.

docs.title: Correggi l’errore
docs.text:
1. I can to cook. → ______
  2. Can you can swim? → ______
  3. She can drives. → ______
  4. I no can sing. → ______

docs.title: Trasforma
docs.text:
1. I can swim. → negativo: ______
  2. She can't drive. → affermativa: ______
  3. They can cook. → domanda: ______
  4. He can speak English a little. → negativo: ______

docs.title: Skill interview
docs.text:
Fai o immagina una mini intervista di 6 domande.

Usa almeno queste:

  • Can you swim?
  • Can you cook?
  • Can you drive?
  • Can you speak English?
  • Can you use a computer well?
  • Can you play a sport?

Poi scrivi o pronuncia risposte complete, non solo yes o no.

docs.title: 🧪 Self-check finale
docs.text:
Riesci a fare queste cose senza guardare la teoria?

  • dire 5 cose che sai fare
  • dire 3 cose che non sai fare
  • fare 5 domande con Can you...?
  • aggiungere dettagli come a little, quite well, not very well
  • evitare l’errore can + to + verbo

Se sì, la lezione è davvero acquisita a livello operativo.

docs.title: Base
docs.text: Modalità Base attiva. Questa unità è una review finale del livello A1. Qui ripassi le strutture principali del livello e verifichi se sai usarle insieme. Per la versione estesa usa il toggle BASE / PRO nella barra in alto.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • ripassare le strutture principali del livello A1
  • parlare di te in modo semplice ma continuo
  • fare e capire domande personali molto comuni
  • descrivere una routine, un luogo e alcune abilità
  • verificare se sei pronto a passare al livello A2

docs.title: 🧠 Grammar Review
docs.text:
In A1 hai studiato:

  • to be → I am, you are, she is
  • Present Simple → I work, he works
  • WH questions → What, Where, How old, When
  • this / that / these / those
  • there is / there are
  • can / can't

Ora il punto non è ricordare una regola alla volta, ma usarle insieme in una situazione reale.

docs.title: 📚 Vocabulary Review
docs.text:
Sai parlare di:

  • dati personali
  • lavoro e studio
  • oggetti quotidiani
  • routine giornaliera
  • casa e stanza
  • luoghi della città
  • abilità personali

docs.title: 🔁 Warm-up Review
docs.text:
Completa oralmente queste frasi:

  1. My name is ...
  2. I am from ...
  3. I am ... years old.
  4. I work / study ...
  5. I wake up at ...
  6. In my room there is ...
  7. In my city there are ...
  8. I can ...
  9. I can't ...
  10. My favourite place in town is ...

docs.title: 📝 TEST 1 – Grammar
docs.text:
Completa con la forma corretta.

  1. I ___ from Italy.
  2. She ___ a student.
  3. They ___ in my class.
  4. He ___ in an office. (work)
  5. I ___ up at 7. (wake)
  6. There ___ a table in the room.
  7. There ___ two chairs near the window.
  8. ___ there a bank near here?
  9. ___ you swim?
  10. What time ___ she start work?

docs.title: 📝 TEST 2 – Trasformazioni
docs.text:
Trasforma le frasi come richiesto.

  1. I am a teacher. → negativa
  2. She works in a shop. → domanda
  3. There is a park near my house. → plurale
  4. You can drive. → negativa
  5. This is my book. → plurale
  6. They are from Spain. → domanda

docs.title: 🗂️ Mixed vocabulary check
docs.text:
Completa con una parola adatta.

  1. I have breakfast in the ______.
  2. There is a sofa in the ______ room.
  3. A hospital is a place in ______.
  4. I can ride a ______.
  5. I go to work in the ______.
  6. A lamp is on the ______.

docs.title: 🎧 Reading
docs.text:
Leggi il testo.

Hello, my name is Sofia and I am 24 years old. I am from Italy and I live in a small town. I work in a café. Every day I wake up at 6:30, have breakfast and go to work by bus. In my room there is a bed, a desk and a small lamp. In my town there is a bank, a supermarket and a park. I can cook quite well, but I can't drive.

docs.title: Comprensione
docs.text:
Rispondi:

  1. How old is Sofia?
  2. Where is she from?
  3. Where does she work?
  4. What is in her room?
  5. Can she drive?

docs.title: 🗣️ Speaking Test
docs.text:
Parla per 1 minuto senza fermarti troppo.

Devi includere:

  • name
  • age
  • country / city
  • job or studies
  • daily routine
  • one description of a room or town
  • two things you can do
  • one thing you can't do

docs.title: Modello utile
docs.text: Hi, my name is ... I am ... years old and I am from ... I work / study ... Every day I wake up at ... In my room there is ... / In my town there are ... I can ..., but I can't ...

docs.title: ✍️ Writing Test
docs.text:
Scrivi un testo di 8–10 frasi su di te.

Devi includere:

  • informazioni personali
  • routine
  • luogo dove vivi o una stanza
  • abilità
  • una preferenza personale

docs.title: 🔊 Pronunciation Check
docs.text:
Ripeti lentamente e poi più fluentemente:

  • I'm from Italy.
  • I work every day.
  • There are two chairs in the room.
  • Can you swim?
  • She works in a bank.

docs.title: ⚠️ Errori comuni finali
docs.text:
❌ I have 25 years. ✅ I am 25 years old.

❌ He work in an office. ✅ He works in an office.

❌ There is two chairs. ✅ There are two chairs.

❌ I can to swim. ✅ I can swim.

❌ Where you live? ✅ Where do you live?

docs.title: ✅ Self-check
docs.text:
Metti una spunta se sai fare queste cose:

  • [ ] presentarti
  • [ ] fare domande personali semplici
  • [ ] parlare della tua routine
  • [ ] descrivere un luogo
  • [ ] parlare delle tue abilità
  • [ ] scrivere un testo breve su di te

Se hai spuntato quasi tutto, sei pronto per il livello successivo.

docs.title: 🚀 Next Step
docs.text:
Se questa unità ti risulta gestibile, puoi passare ad A2, dove inizierai a usare:

  • Past Simple
  • espressioni di tempo più ricche
  • conversazioni più naturali
  • testi più lunghi e connettori più chiari

Small sentences become real communication when you use them together.

docs.title: Professional
docs.text: Modalità Professional attiva. Questa unità trasforma la review A1 in una verifica integrata di competenza. Non ripassi solo le regole: controlli se sai davvero combinare grammatica, lessico, speaking, writing e autocorrezione in situazioni realistiche.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • usare insieme le principali strutture A1 senza trattarle come blocchi isolati
  • sostenere una breve interazione personale con presentazione, domande e risposte
  • descrivere in modo semplice ma coerente la tua routine, il tuo ambiente e le tue abilità
  • individuare i tuoi errori più frequenti e correggerli
  • capire se sei pronto a passare ad A2 oppure se devi consolidare ancora alcuni punti

docs.title: 🧭 Focus della review finale
docs.text:
In una review efficace non basta sapere la regola. Devi verificare se sai:

  1. recuperare la struttura giusta velocemente
  2. scegliere il lessico adatto al contesto
  3. collegare le frasi in modo logico
  4. mantenere la correttezza nelle forme più frequenti
  5. parlare e scrivere con un minimo di continuità

A livello A1, il vero salto di qualità è questo: passare da frasi singole come I am Marco o There is a table a una micro-produzione continua come una presentazione personale, una descrizione della propria giornata o una breve interazione con una persona nuova.

docs.title: 🧠 A1 Grammar Map
docs.text: Queste sono le strutture chiave che devi sapere integrare.

docs.title: 1. Identity and personal information
docs.text:
• I am ...
  • I am from ...
  • I am ... years old.
  • I am a student / a teacher / an engineer.

docs.title: 2. Daily habits and routines
docs.text:
• I wake up at ...
  • I go to work by ...
  • She works in a bank.
  • He studies English every day.

docs.title: 3. Questions
docs.text:
• What is your name?
  • Where are you from?
  • What do you do?
  • Where do you work?
  • Can you swim?
  • Is there a supermarket near your house?

docs.title: 4. Objects and reference
docs.text:
• this / that
  • these / those
  • singular / plural

docs.title: 5. Description of places
docs.text:
• There is a ...
  • There are ...
  • There isn't ...
  • Are there any ...?

docs.title: 6. Ability
docs.text:
• I can ...
  • I can't ...
  • She can speak English quite well.

👉 Se sai usare questi sei blocchi nello stesso compito, il tuo A1 è davvero operativo.

docs.title: Personal profile
docs.text:
• name
  • surname
  • age
  • country
  • city
  • job
  • student
  • colleague
  • family

docs.title: Daily routine
docs.text:
• wake up
  • get dressed
  • have breakfast
  • go to work
  • start work
  • finish work
  • go home
  • have dinner
  • go to bed

docs.title: Home and room
docs.text:
• room
  • desk
  • chair
  • bed
  • lamp
  • shelf
  • window
  • wall
  • kitchen
  • bathroom

docs.title: Town
docs.text:
• bank
  • supermarket
  • pharmacy
  • station
  • school
  • park
  • café
  • hospital
  • bus stop
  • street

docs.title: Skills
docs.text:
• cook
  • drive
  • swim
  • use a computer
  • speak English
  • write emails
  • take photos
  • play football

docs.title: 🧪 Diagnostic Check – Form Recognition
docs.text:
Scegli la forma corretta.

  1. She is / are from Spain.
  2. He work / works in a hotel.
  3. Do / Does she live in Rome?
  4. There is / are two books on the desk.
  5. Can / Does you swim?
  6. These is / are my keys.
  7. I can't / don't can drive.
  8. What time do / does he start work?
  9. There isn't some / any milk.
  10. Is / Are there a pharmacy near here?

docs.title: Chiave
docs.text:
1. is
  2. works
  3. Does
  4. are
  5. Can
  6. are
  7. can't
  8. does
  9. any
  10. Is

docs.title: 🧩 Integrated Grammar Practice
docs.text:
Completa il testo con la forma corretta.

Hello, my name ___ Elena. I ___ 29 years old and I ___ from Italy. I ___ in a small office in the city centre. Every day I ___ up at 7:00 and ___ breakfast at home. Then I ___ to work by bus. In my office there ___ a big desk and there ___ two computers. Near my office there ___ a café and a bank. I ___ cook quite well, but I ___ drive.

docs.title: Chiave
docs.text:
is / am / am / work / wake / have / go / is / are / is / can / can't

👉 Qui vedi se riesci a cambiare struttura in pochi secondi: to be, Present Simple, there is / there are, can / can't.

docs.title: 🔄 Transformation Drill
docs.text:
Trasforma mantenendo il significato.

  1. I am a student. → negative
  2. She works in a shop. → question
  3. There is a park near my house. → negative
  4. There are two chairs in the room. → singular
  5. He can swim. → negative
  6. They are from France. → question
  7. This is my bag. → plural
  8. I go to work by bus. → third person singular

docs.title: Possibili soluzioni
docs.text:
1. I am not a student.
  2. Does she work in a shop?
  3. There isn't a park near my house.
  4. There is a chair in the room.
  5. He can't swim.
  6. Are they from France?
  7. These are my bags.
  8. He goes to work by bus.

docs.title: 🎧 Reading + Language Notice
docs.text:
Leggi il testo.

Hi, I'm Daniel. I'm 31 and I'm from Dublin, but now I live in Milan. I work in a hotel near the station. My day starts early: I wake up at 6:15, have coffee and leave home at 7:00. I usually go to work by tram. At work there is a reception desk, a small office and a staff room. There are also two computers and a printer. In my free time I can cook quite well and I can take good photos, but I can't play any musical instrument.

docs.title: Comprensione
docs.text:
1. Where is Daniel from?
  2. Where does he live now?
  3. What time does he leave home?
  4. What is in his workplace?
  5. What can he do?
  6. What can't he do?

docs.title: Language notice
docs.text:
Nel testo compaiono insieme:

  • I'm / I'm from → identità
  • I work / I wake up / I go → routine
  • there is / there are → descrizione di luogo
  • I can / I can't → abilità

Questa è esattamente la competenza che devi consolidare a fine A1.

docs.title: Task 1 – 30 seconds
docs.text:
Presentati usando almeno 4 informazioni:

  • name
  • age
  • country/city
  • job/studies

docs.title: Task 2 – 45 seconds
docs.text:
Descrivi una giornata tipica:

  • what time you wake up
  • how you go to work or school
  • what you do in the evening

docs.title: Task 3 – 45 seconds
docs.text:
Descrivi una stanza o il tuo quartiere:

  • there is / there are
  • at least 4 objects or places
  • one opinion sentence

docs.title: Task 4 – 30 seconds
docs.text:
Parla delle tue abilità:

  • two things you can do
  • one thing you can't do
  • one thing you can do only a little

docs.title: Combined final task – 2 minutes
docs.text:
Simula di incontrare una persona nuova. Devi:

  • presentarti
  • fare 2 domande
  • parlare della tua routine
  • descrivere dove vivi
  • parlare di una tua abilità

docs.title: Useful speaking frame
docs.text: Hi, my name is ... I'm ... years old and I'm from ... I live in ... and I work / study ... Every day I wake up at ... and I go to ... by ... In my room / in my area there is ... and there are ... I can ..., but I can't ... very well. What about you? Where are you from? What do you do?

docs.title: ✍️ Writing Task – Guided Production
docs.text: Scrivi un testo di 120–150 parole. Il tema è:

docs.title: About me: my life, my routine and my town
docs.text:
Il testo deve includere:

  • who you are
  • where you are from / where you live
  • what you do
  • your typical day
  • a short description of your room or town
  • what you can and can't do
  • one personal preference or opinion

docs.title: Suggested structure
docs.text:
Paragraph 1 – Personal profile Name, age, country, city, job/studies

Paragraph 2 – Daily routine Morning, transport, work/study, evening

Paragraph 3 – Description + ability Room/town, places or objects, abilities

docs.title: Useful connectors
docs.text:
• and
  • but
  • then
  • after that
  • usually
  • also

docs.title: 🛠️ Error Clinic – Typical Italian Learner Problems
1. Age
docs.text: ❌ I have 28 years. ✅ I am 28 years old.

docs.title: 2. Third person singular
docs.text: ❌ She work in a bank. ✅ She works in a bank.

docs.title: 3. Questions without auxiliary
docs.text:
❌ Where you live? ✅ Where do you live?

❌ What he does? ✅ What does he do?

docs.title: 4. Incorrect modal structure
docs.text:
❌ I can to swim. ✅ I can swim.

❌ She doesn't can drive. ✅ She can't drive.

docs.title: 5. Singular/plural confusion with there is / there are
docs.text: ❌ There is two parks near my house. ✅ There are two parks near my house.

docs.title: 6. Overuse of "is" before main verbs
docs.text:
❌ He is work in an office. ✅ He works in an office.

👉 Se questi errori compaiono ancora spesso, il passaggio ad A2 è possibile solo dopo un breve consolidamento.

docs.title: 🔊 Pronunciation and Fluency Review
docs.text:
Ripeti in gruppi di senso, non parola per parola:

  • I'm from Italy and I live in Turin.
  • I work in a café near the station.
  • There are two chairs next to the desk.
  • Can you speak English quite well?
  • I can cook, but I can't drive yet.

docs.title: Mini fluency drill
docs.text:
Leggi tre volte questa micro-presentazione, ogni volta un po’ più fluentemente:

Hi, I'm Sara. I'm 26 years old and I'm from Italy. I work in a bookshop. Every day I wake up at 7, have breakfast and go to work by bus. In my room there is a desk and there are a lot of books. I can speak English a little and I can cook quite well, but I can't drive.

docs.title: 📋 Final Self-Assessment
docs.text:
Segna Yes / Almost / Not yet.

Can do statement Yes Almost Not yet I can introduce myself clearly. I can ask basic personal questions. I can describe my daily routine. I can describe a room or area with there is / there are. I can talk about abilities with can / can't. I can write a short text about my life. I can avoid the most common A1 grammar mistakes.

docs.title: How to interpret your result
docs.text:
• Mostly Yes → you are ready to move into A2 work
  • Many Almost → you can continue, but review weak points in speaking and writing
  • Many Not yet → repeat selected A1 units before moving on

docs.title: 🚀 Bridge to A2
docs.text:
Nel livello A2 inizierai a fare qualcosa di nuovo: non solo descrivere il presente, ma anche parlare del passato, organizzare meglio il discorso e gestire scambi più naturali.

Per arrivarci bene, alla fine di A1 devi avere tre basi forti:

  1. frasi corrette
  2. domande corrette
  3. mini-produzioni continue di 1–2 minuti

Se queste basi ci sono, A2 diventa molto più fluido e molto meno frustrante.

A1 ends when basic language becomes usable, not when rules are simply memorised.

docs.title: Base
docs.text: Modalità Base attiva. Questa unità ti aiuta a usare il Present Continuous per parlare di azioni in corso, cioè di ciò che sta succedendo adesso o in questo momento. Per la versione estesa usa il toggle BASE / PRO nella barra in alto.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • usare il Present Continuous in forma affermativa, negativa e interrogativa
  • descrivere azioni in corso nel momento in cui parli
  • fare domande come What are you doing?
  • distinguere tra una routine abituale e un’azione temporanea

docs.title: Forma affermativa
docs.text:
Usiamo il Present Continuous per parlare di qualcosa che sta succedendo adesso.

Subject + to be + verb-ing

  • I am working.
  • You are studying.
  • She is reading.
  • They are watching TV.

👉 Il verbo principale prende la forma -ing.

docs.title: Forma negativa
docs.text:
Subject + to be + not + verb-ing

  • I am not working.
  • He is not sleeping.
  • We are not studying.

Forme contratte più comuni:

  • I'm not
  • isn't
  • aren't

Esempi:

  • She isn't listening.
  • They aren't eating.

docs.title: Forma interrogativa
docs.text:
To be + subject + verb-ing?

  • Are you working?
  • Is she studying?
  • Are they coming?

Risposte brevi:

  • Yes, I am.
  • No, I'm not.
  • Yes, she is.
  • No, they aren't.

docs.title: 🔁 Present Simple vs Present Continuous
docs.text:
Confronta:

  • I work every day. → routine
  • I am working now. → adesso

Altri esempi:

  • She studies in the evening. → abitudine
  • She is studying now. → in questo momento

👉 now / right now / at the moment aiutano spesso a capire che serve il Present Continuous.

docs.title: Verbi comuni
docs.text:
• work
  • study
  • eat
  • read
  • write
  • sleep
  • watch
  • talk
  • listen
  • cook
  • drive
  • wait

docs.title: Time expressions
docs.text:
• now
  • right now
  • at the moment
  • today
  • this week

docs.title: ✍️ Spelling con -ing
docs.text:
Osserva questi cambiamenti:

  • work → working
  • read → reading
  • make → making
  • write → writing
  • sit → sitting
  • run → running

Regole base:

  • di solito aggiungi -ing: work → working
  • se il verbo finisce in -e, togli la -e: make → making
  • in alcuni verbi brevi raddoppi la consonante finale: sit → sitting

docs.title: 🎧 Listening / Reading
docs.text:
Dialogue:

A: Hi Anna, what are you doing? B: I'm doing my English homework. A: Are you studying alone? B: No, I'm studying with my brother. A: Is he doing the same exercise? B: No, he isn't. He's writing an email.

docs.title: Comprensione
docs.text:
1. What is Anna doing?
  2. Is she studying alone?
  3. What is her brother doing?

docs.title: 🗣️ Speaking
docs.text:
Ripeti:

  • I am studying now.
  • She is writing an email.
  • We are waiting for the bus.
  • They aren't sleeping.
  • Are you listening?

Ora completa oralmente:

  • I am ___ right now.
  • My friend is ___ at the moment.
  • We are ___ today.

Fai domande:

  • What are you doing?
  • Are you working now?
  • Is your teacher speaking English?

docs.title: ✍️ Writing
docs.text:
Scrivi 6–8 frasi su cosa sta succedendo intorno a te adesso.

Puoi includere:

  • cosa stai facendo tu
  • cosa fanno altre persone
  • cosa non sta succedendo
  • dove ti trovi

docs.title: Modello utile
docs.text: I am sitting at my desk. I am studying English. My phone is not ringing. People are walking outside. My family is having dinner.

docs.title: 🔊 Pronunciation
docs.text:
Attenzione alla finale -ing:

  • working → /ˈwɜːrkɪŋ/
  • reading → /ˈriːdɪŋ/
  • sitting → /ˈsɪtɪŋ/

👉 Il suono finale è /ɪŋ/, non “ingh” italiano.

docs.title: ⚠️ Errori comuni (italiani)
docs.text:
❌ I am work now. ✅ I am working now.

❌ She working. ✅ She is working.

❌ Are studying? ✅ Are you studying?

❌ I work now. (se parli di questo momento) ✅ I am working now.

docs.title: ✅ Review & Practice
docs.text:
Completa con la forma corretta del verbo.

  1. I am ___ (read).
  2. She is ___ (study).
  3. They are ___ (watch) TV.
  4. We are ___ (sit) in class.
  5. He is ___ (make) dinner.
  6. Are you ___ (listen)?

docs.title: 🔄 Trasforma
docs.text:
Trasforma usando il Present Continuous.

  1. I work. → (now) ______
  2. She reads a book. → (at the moment) ______
  3. They eat lunch. → (right now) ______
  4. He writes an email. → negativa ______
  5. You study English. → domanda ______

docs.title: 🚀 Mini task
docs.text:
Guarda una stanza, una foto o una scena intorno a te e descrivi 3–5 azioni in corso.

Esempio:

  • A woman is talking on the phone.
  • Two children are playing.
  • A man is drinking coffee.

docs.title: Professional
docs.text: Modalità Professional attiva. In questa unità non impari solo una nuova forma verbale: impari a scegliere tra routine e azione in corso, a gestire la forma -ing con precisione e a descrivere situazioni reali in modo più naturale e controllato.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • costruire il Present Continuous con maggiore sicurezza in frasi affermative, negative e interrogative
  • distinguere con chiarezza tra Present Simple e Present Continuous
  • descrivere scene, attività temporanee e azioni in corso con lessico più ricco
  • evitare gli errori più frequenti su to be, verb-ing e ordine delle parole
  • sostenere brevi micro-interazioni realistiche usando domande e risposte spontanee

docs.title: 🧭 Quando usiamo il Present Continuous
docs.text: Il Present Continuous si usa soprattutto per tre funzioni centrali a questo livello.

docs.title: 1. Azioni in corso nel momento in cui parli
docs.text:
• I'm talking to you now.
  • She is studying at the moment.
  • They are waiting outside.

docs.title: 2. Situazioni temporanee, non permanenti
docs.text:
• I'm staying with a friend this week.
  • He is working from home today.
  • We are taking an online course this month.

docs.title: 3. Scene che osservi e descrivi
docs.text:
• A man is running in the park.
  • Two children are playing near the door.
  • Someone is speaking on the phone.

👉 A questo stadio, questa terza funzione è molto importante perché allena il passaggio da frasi isolate a descrizione continua.

docs.title: Forma affermativa
docs.text:
Subject + am / is / are + verb-ing

  • I am reading.
  • You are listening.
  • He is cooking.
  • We are waiting.

docs.title: Forma negativa
docs.text:
Subject + am / is / are + not + verb-ing

  • I'm not reading.
  • She isn't listening.
  • They aren't waiting.

docs.title: Forma interrogativa
docs.text:
Am / Is / Are + subject + verb-ing?

  • Are you reading?
  • Is he cooking?
  • Are they waiting?

docs.title: Risposte brevi
docs.text:
• Yes, I am.
  • No, I'm not.
  • Yes, he is.
  • No, they aren't.

👉 L'errore più comune non è il verbo principale, ma dimenticare il verbo to be.

docs.title: ✍️ Formazione della forma -ing
docs.text: Questa parte merita attenzione, perché qui nascono moltissimi errori ortografici.

docs.title: Regola 1: aggiungi semplicemente -ing
docs.text:
• work → working
  • read → reading
  • play → playing
  • listen → listening

docs.title: Regola 2: togli la -e finale
docs.text:
• make → making
  • write → writing
  • come → coming
  • take → taking

docs.title: Regola 3: raddoppia la consonante finale in alcuni verbi brevi
docs.text:
• sit → sitting
  • run → running
  • swim → swimming
  • stop → stopping

docs.title: Nota utile
docs.text:
Non tutti i verbi finali in consonante raddoppiano. Per esempio:

  • open → opening
  • visit → visiting
  • happen → happening

👉 All'inizio conviene memorizzare i casi più frequenti, non tutte le eccezioni teoriche.

docs.title: 🔁 Present Simple vs Present Continuous
docs.text: Questa è la vera competenza-obiettivo della unità.

docs.title: Present Simple = abitudine / routine / fatto generale
docs.text:
• I work in a bank.
  • She studies English every Tuesday.
  • They live in Rome.

docs.title: Present Continuous = adesso / periodo temporaneo / scena in corso
docs.text:
• I am working at home today.
  • She is studying right now.
  • They are living with friends this month.

docs.title: Coppie a confronto
docs.text:
• He works in a restaurant. → lavoro abituale

  • He is working in the kitchen now. → azione in corso

  • I read before bed. → abitudine

  • I am reading an email now. → adesso

  • We go to the office every day. → routine

  • We are going to a meeting right now. → azione in corso

👉 Molti studenti italiani usano troppo il Present Simple perché in italiano il presente copre spesso entrambe le funzioni. In inglese la distinzione va resa in modo più esplicito.

docs.title: Action verbs for everyday situations
docs.text:
• work
  • study
  • read
  • write
  • cook
  • drive
  • wait
  • look for
  • listen to
  • talk to
  • clean
  • watch
  • use
  • carry
  • wear

docs.title: Time markers
docs.text:
• now
  • right now
  • at the moment
  • today
  • this week
  • these days

docs.title: Useful classroom / work phrases
docs.text:
• I'm taking notes.
  • She's checking her email.
  • We're working on a project.
  • They're waiting for the lesson to start.
  • I'm not using my phone.

docs.title: 🎧 Reading – Scene Description
docs.text:
Leggi il testo.

It is 8:30 in the morning and the office is already busy. Julia is talking to a client on the phone. Her colleague Mark is answering emails and drinking coffee at his desk. Two interns are carrying some boxes into the meeting room. The manager isn't sitting in his office; he is speaking to a visitor near the reception desk.
