Daniel: Sure. I work for a software company, and I’m part of the customer success team.

Laura: Interesting. What does your role involve?

Daniel: I mainly deal with onboarding new clients, monitoring account performance and helping customers use the platform effectively.

Laura: How long have you been doing that?

Daniel: I’ve been in this position for about eighteen months, but I’ve worked in customer-facing roles for much longer.

Laura: And what are you focusing on at the moment?

Daniel: Right now I’m trying to improve my data analysis skills because I’d like to move into a more strategic role in the future.

docs.title: Why this works
docs.text:
Questo dialogo funziona perché mostra:

  • introduzione professionale naturale
  • domande e risposte realistiche
  • dettagli concreti, non vaghi
  • esperienza + obiettivo futuro
  • lessico professionale riutilizzabile

docs.title: Common questions
docs.text:
• What do you do?
  • Can you tell me about your current role?
  • What are your main responsibilities?
  • What do you enjoy most about your work?
  • What skills have you developed?
  • Why are you interested in this position?

docs.title: Useful answer starters
docs.text:
• At the moment, I work as...
  • My main responsibilities include...
  • One of the things I enjoy most is...
  • Over the last few years, I’ve developed...
  • I’m interested in this role because...

docs.title: Stronger answer pattern
docs.text:
Instead of:

  • I work in an office and I do many tasks.

Try:

  • I work in the administrative department, and my main tasks include preparing documents, coordinating appointments and supporting internal communication.

docs.title: 9. Work and study collocations you should sound comfortable with
Work collocations
docs.text:
• meet deadlines
  • take responsibility for
  • work under pressure
  • gain experience
  • apply for a position
  • attend a meeting
  • deal with clients
  • solve problems
  • take part in a project

docs.title: Study collocations
docs.text:
• attend classes
  • submit an assignment
  • revise for an exam
  • meet a deadline
  • take notes
  • do research
  • improve academic skills
  • complete a course

docs.title: Drill 1 – Present yourself
docs.text:
Completa a voce:

  • I currently work as ____.
  • I work in the ____ sector.
  • My main responsibilities include ____.
  • I’ve been in this role for ____.
  • At the moment, I’m trying to improve ____.
  • In the future, I’d like to ____.

docs.title: Drill 2 – Sound more professional
docs.text:
Trasforma le frasi basiche in frasi migliori:

  1. I work in an office.
  2. I do many things.
  3. I speak with customers.
  4. I study economics.
  5. I want a better job.

Prova a espandere così:

  • I work in the administrative department of...
  • My role involves...
  • I regularly communicate with...
  • I’m currently studying...
  • In the future, I’d like to...

docs.title: Drill 3 – Interview practice
docs.text:
Rispondi oralmente in 4–5 frasi:

  • Can you tell me about your current work or studies?
  • What are your responsibilities?
  • What have you learned so far?
  • What would you like to improve next?

docs.title: ✍️ Writing Task
docs.text:
Scrivi un testo di 140–180 parole su uno di questi temi:

  1. My professional profile
  2. My studies and career goals
  3. My current role and what I want to improve

Il testo deve includere:

  • presentazione iniziale
  • contesto lavorativo o accademico
  • responsabilità o attività principali
  • esperienza già acquisita
  • competenze sviluppate
  • un obiettivo concreto per il futuro

docs.title: Model opening
docs.text: I currently work as a customer support specialist for an international company. My role involves answering client requests, solving problems and coordinating with other departments when necessary. I’ve been in this position for nearly two years, and during this time I’ve developed stronger communication and time-management skills. At the moment, I’m also improving my English because I’d like to take on more international responsibilities in the future.

docs.title: 🔊 Pronunciation & rhythm
docs.text:
Parole da curare:

  • work → /wɜːrk/
  • world → /wɜːrld/
  • career → /kəˈrɪr/
  • responsible → /rɪˈspɒnsəbəl/
  • colleague → /ˈkɒliːɡ/
  • experience → /ɪkˈspɪəriəns/

docs.title: Rhythm tip
docs.text:
In inglese professionale, molte frasi diventano più naturali se pronunciate in gruppi di significato:

  • I work in sales / and I’m responsible for key accounts.
  • I’ve been in this role / for just over two years.
  • At the moment / I’m focusing on process improvement.

docs.title: 1. Work / job confusion
docs.text: ❌ I have a good work ✅ I have a good job

docs.title: 2. Experience as countable noun
docs.text: ❌ I made many experiences ✅ I had many experiences / I gained a lot of experience

docs.title: 3. Responsible + wrong preposition
docs.text: ❌ I’m responsible to manage the team ✅ I’m responsible for managing the team

docs.title: 4. Study prepositions
docs.text: ❌ I study to university ✅ I study at university

docs.title: 5. Literal Italian transfer
docs.text: ❌ My work is very formative ✅ My job has taught me a lot / It has been a valuable experience

docs.title: 6. Duration mistakes
docs.text: ❌ I work here since three years ✅ I’ve worked here for three years

docs.title: ✅ Final Review
docs.text:
Completa in modo naturale:

  1. I work ___ a logistics company.
  2. I’m responsible ___ coordinating deliveries.
  3. I’ve worked in this field ___ 2021.
  4. My job involves ___ with clients.
  5. I’d like to move ___ a more strategic role.

docs.title: Rewrite more professionally
docs.text:
1. I do many things in my job.
  2. I speak with customers every day.
  3. I study marketing.
  4. I want a better work.
  5. I work here from two years.

docs.title: 🚀 Final Performance Task
docs.text:
Prepara una breve presentazione orale di 60–90 secondi su di te.

Deve includere:

  • chi sei
  • cosa fai o studi
  • responsabilità o attività principali
  • esperienza o competenze già acquisite
  • cosa stai migliorando ora
  • obiettivo futuro

Se riesci a fare questa presentazione in modo chiaro, con collocations corrette e tempi verbali ben scelti, stai già lavorando su una competenza molto concreta e spendibile del livello B1 professional.

docs.title: Unità 27 – Modals: must, should, have to
🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità saprai:

  • dare consigli in modo naturale con should / shouldn’t
  • esprimere obblighi, necessità e regole con must, mustn’t e have to
  • distinguere tra un obbligo personale e una regola esterna
  • parlare di ciò che è obbligatorio, consigliato, vietato o non necessario
  • evitare gli errori più comuni con i verbi modali

docs.title: 🧠 Grammar in context
docs.text:
I verbi modali aiutano a esprimere atteggiamento, obbligo, consiglio, divieto o necessità. In questa unità ci concentriamo su tre strutture molto utili nel parlato reale:

  • should → consiglio
  • must → obbligo forte / regola percepita come importante
  • have to → necessità pratica o regola esterna

docs.title: Forma base
docs.text:
• You should rest.
  • You must wear a seatbelt.
  • I have to leave early tomorrow.

Dopo should e must usiamo sempre il verbo base, senza to.

docs.title: 1. Should = advice
docs.text: Usiamo should per dire che qualcosa è una buona idea, un suggerimento o un consiglio.

docs.title: Esempi
docs.text:
• You should drink more water.
  • You should talk to your manager.
  • You should go to bed earlier.
  • We should leave now.

docs.title: Negativa
docs.text:
• You shouldn’t stay up so late.
  • He shouldn’t worry too much.

docs.title: Domande
docs.text:
• Should I call her?
  • What should we do?

docs.title: 2. Must = strong obligation or rule
docs.text: Usiamo must quando l’obbligo è sentito come forte, importante o categorico.

docs.title: Esempi
docs.text:
• You must wear protective equipment.
  • I must finish this report today.
  • You must stop when the light is red.

docs.title: Negativa: mustn’t
docs.text:
Mustn’t significa proibizione.

  • You mustn’t smoke here.
  • Students mustn’t use their phones during the exam.

docs.title: 3. Have to = external obligation / practical necessity
docs.text: Usiamo have to quando c’è una regola esterna, una necessità concreta o un obbligo pratico.

docs.title: Esempi
docs.text:
• I have to work on Saturday.
  • We have to wear ID badges at the office.
  • She has to take two buses to get to work.

docs.title: Negativa
docs.text:
• I don’t have to work tomorrow.
  • You don’t have to pay now.

Attenzione: don’t have to non significa proibizione. Significa non è necessario.

docs.title: 4. Key difference
docs.text: Struttura Significato principale Esempio should consiglio You should rest. must obbligo forte / regola importante You must wear a helmet. have to necessità pratica / regola esterna I have to be at work at 8. mustn’t proibizione You mustn’t park here. don’t have to non necessario You don’t have to come early.

docs.title: Advice
docs.text:
• advice
  • suggestion
  • healthy
  • rest
  • sleep
  • exercise
  • improve
  • relax

docs.title: Rules and obligations
docs.text:
• rule
  • obligation
  • permission
  • badge
  • uniform
  • deadline
  • appointment
  • seatbelt
  • helmet
  • safety

docs.title: Useful verbs
docs.text:
• wear
  • follow
  • bring
  • remember
  • pay
  • leave
  • study
  • finish
  • avoid
  • check

docs.title: 🎧 Reading / Dialogue
docs.text:
Dialogue 1 – Advice

A: I’m really tired these days. B: You should take a short break. A: I know. I also sleep very little. B: Then you should go to bed earlier.

Dialogue 2 – Rules at work

A: Do we need our badges every day? B: Yes, we have to wear them at all times. A: Can we eat in the meeting room? B: No, we mustn’t eat there.

Dialogue 3 – Practical necessity

A: Why are you leaving so early? B: I have to catch a train. A: Do you have to go now? B: Yes, otherwise I’ll miss it.

docs.title: 🗣️ Speaking
docs.text:
Ripeti:

  • You should take a break.
  • You shouldn’t worry so much.
  • You must wear a helmet.
  • You mustn’t use your phone here.
  • I have to get up early.
  • We don’t have to finish today.

Ora completa a voce:

  • You should ____.
  • At work, I have to ____.
  • In my country, drivers must ____.
  • Students shouldn’t ____.
  • Today I have to ____ before 6 p.m.

docs.title: ✍️ Writing
docs.text:
Scrivi un testo di 90–110 parole su uno di questi temi:

  1. Advice for a healthy routine
  2. Rules in my workplace / school
  3. Things I have to do during the week

Cerca di includere:

  • almeno 2 frasi con should
  • almeno 2 frasi con have to
  • almeno 1 regola con must o mustn’t

docs.title: Example
docs.text: During the week I have to wake up early and go to work by bus. I also have to prepare reports for my team. I think I should sleep more and do more exercise because I often feel tired. At work, we must wear our ID badges, and we mustn’t leave confidential documents on our desks. I don’t have to work on Sundays, so I usually relax and spend time with my family.

docs.title: 🔊 Pronunciation
docs.text:
Attenzione a queste forme:

  • should → /ʃʊd/
  • must → /mʌst/
  • have to → spesso nel parlato suona vicino a /hæftə/
  • shouldn’t → la l non si sente

Ripeti:

  • You should call her.
  • We have to leave now.
  • You mustn’t park here.

docs.title: ⚠️ Errori comuni (italiani)
docs.text:
❌ You should to study more. ✅ You should study more.

❌ I must to go now. ✅ I must go now.

❌ I no have to work tomorrow. ✅ I don’t have to work tomorrow.

❌ You don’t must do that. ✅ You mustn’t do that. / You don’t have to do that. (dipende dal significato)

Ricorda:

  • dopo should e must → verbo base
  • mustn’t = vietato
  • don’t have to = non necessario

docs.title: 🧠 Pattern fondamentali
docs.text:
• Subject + should + base verb
  • Subject + must + base verb
  • Subject + have to / has to + base verb
  • Subject + don’t / doesn’t have to + base verb

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• You should speak to him.
  • She must be here by nine.
  • I have to finish this task.
  • He doesn’t have to come tomorrow.

docs.title: ✅ Review & Practice
docs.text:
Completa con should, must, mustn’t, have to, don’t have to.

  1. You ____ drink more water.
  2. We ____ wear a seatbelt in the car.
  3. Employees ____ smoke in this area.
  4. I ____ go to the office tomorrow; it’s optional.
  5. She ____ finish the report before noon.
  6. What ____ I do if I feel stressed?

docs.title: 🔄 Trasforma
docs.text:
Riscrivi usando la parola tra parentesi.

  1. It is a good idea to rest more. (should)
  2. It is not necessary to come early. (don’t have to)
  3. It is forbidden to park here. (mustn’t)
  4. It is necessary for me to wear a uniform. (have to)

docs.title: 🧠 Think & Speak
docs.text:
Parla per 1–2 minuti su uno di questi temi:

  • three things you should do to improve your English
  • rules at your workplace or school
  • things you have to do every week
  • one thing people must do to stay healthy

docs.title: 🚀 Mini task
docs.text:
Immagina di dare consigli a un nuovo collega o a uno studente straniero.

Scrivi o registra 8 frasi:

  • 3 consigli con should
  • 3 obblighi con have to / must
  • 2 proibizioni con mustn’t

docs.title: 💬 Extra practice
docs.text:
Rispondi:

  • What should you do to improve your English quickly?
  • What do you have to do before work or class?
  • What must people do in public places?
  • What mustn’t people do in your office, school or city?

docs.title: 🎯 Challenge
docs.text:
Scrivi un piccolo guide sheet intitolato:

Rules and advice for new students / new employees

Usa almeno:

  • 3 frasi con should
  • 3 con have to
  • 2 con mustn’t

English becomes more useful when you can give advice, explain rules and talk about real obligations.

docs.title: Unità 27 – Modals: must, should, have to
🎯 Lesson Goals
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • dare consigli in modo più maturo, preciso e pragmatico
  • distinguere chiaramente tra advice, internal obligation, external obligation, prohibition e lack of necessity
  • usare should, must, have to, mustn’t e don’t have to senza tradurre meccanicamente dall’italiano
  • formulare regole, raccomandazioni, linee guida e priorità in contesti reali: lavoro, salute, studio, sicurezza, servizio al cliente
  • evitare gli errori tipici degli italofoni, soprattutto con mustn’t vs don’t have to, l’uso scorretto di to dopo i modali, e la tendenza a usare must dove l’inglese reale preferisce have to

Questa unità è importante perché porta l’uso dei modali dal livello “regola scolastica” a un livello operativo e comunicativo: non si tratta solo di costruire la frase corretta, ma di scegliere la forma che esprime il grado giusto di forza.

docs.title: 🧠 Core idea: modals express stance, not just grammar
docs.text:
Molti studenti imparano:

  • should = consiglio
  • must = obbligo
  • have to = dovere

Ma nel parlato reale il punto non è solo il significato base. Il punto è chi impone l’obbligo, quanto è forte, e come suona la frase.

Confronta:

  • You should check the figures again. (consiglio professionale)
  • You must send this today. (urgenza / obbligo forte, spesso deciso da chi parla)
  • We have to submit the report by 5 p.m. (scadenza esterna / requisito pratico)

La differenza è sottile ma decisiva.

docs.title: 1. Should: recommendation, expectation, strategic advice
docs.text:
Should non significa solo “dovresti”. A livello B1 serve per:

  • dare consigli
  • fare raccomandazioni professionali
  • suggerire una strategia
  • indicare ciò che è ragionevole o opportuno
  • parlare di aspettative moderate

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• You should speak to your manager before making a decision.
  • We should review the contract more carefully.
  • You shouldn’t reply when you’re angry.
  • The meeting should end by 4 p.m.

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: Should è molto utile perché è abbastanza forte da guidare una persona, ma non aggressivo come must. In molti contesti professionali è la scelta più naturale.

docs.title: Useful professional patterns
docs.text:
• You should probably...
  • We should consider...
  • I think you should...
  • You shouldn’t ignore...
  • The best thing to do is...

docs.title: Example in context
docs.text: • If the client is unhappy, you should acknowledge the problem first and then offer a solution.

docs.title: 2. Must: strong necessity, rule, urgency, speaker authority
docs.text: Must esprime un obbligo forte. Spesso comunica che la regola è percepita come importante, urgente o non negoziabile.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• You must wear protective equipment in this area.
  • We must act quickly.
  • I must remember to call her.
  • You must read this before the meeting.

docs.title: Important nuance
docs.text:
Nel parlato quotidiano, soprattutto quando l’obbligo viene da circostanze esterne, l’inglese usa spesso have to più di must. Must resta molto comune per:

  • istruzioni forti
  • safety rules
  • formal notices
  • decisioni personali forti
  • enfasi

docs.title: Internal obligation
docs.text:
• I must remember to back up my files.
  • I must stop wasting time.

Qui l’obbligo parte dal parlante, non da una regola esterna.

docs.title: 3. Have to: external obligation, requirement, practical necessity
docs.text: A livello B1, have to è spesso la forma più naturale per parlare di doveri quotidiani, scadenze, regole organizzative e necessità pratiche.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• I have to attend a training session tomorrow.
  • We have to wear badges in the office.
  • She has to take medication twice a day.
  • Do you have to work this weekend?

docs.title: Why learners overuse must
docs.text:
Molti italofoni traducono “devo” sempre con must, ma in inglese reale have to è spesso più naturale quando l’obbligo è legato a:

  • orari
  • procedure
  • regolamenti
  • scadenze
  • condizioni esterne

Confronta:

  • I must finish this. (forte, personale, urgente)
  • I have to finish this by noon. (scadenza concreta)

docs.title: 4. The crucial contrast: mustn’t vs don’t have to
docs.text: Questa è una delle distinzioni più importanti di tutta l’unità.

docs.title: Mustn’t = prohibition
docs.text:
Significa: non devi farlo perché è vietato / proibito / non consentito.

  • You mustn’t enter this area.
  • Staff mustn’t share passwords.
  • You mustn’t use your phone during the exam.

docs.title: Don’t have to = no necessity
docs.text:
Significa: non è necessario.

  • You don’t have to come if you’re busy.
  • We don’t have to print the document.
  • She doesn’t have to wear formal clothes on Fridays.

docs.title: Compare
docs.text:
• You mustn’t be late. = È proibito / inaccettabile fare tardi.
  • You don’t have to be early. = Non serve arrivare in anticipo.

Questa distinzione va interiorizzata, non solo memorizzata.

docs.title: 5. Choosing the right modal by communicative force
A. Gentle advice
docs.text:
• You should take a break.
  • You should update the client.

docs.title: B. Strong recommendation bordering on obligation
docs.text: • You really should check the figures again.

docs.title: C. Strong obligation / urgency
docs.text: • You must send this now.

docs.title: D. External requirement
docs.text: • We have to follow company policy.

docs.title: E. Prohibition
docs.text: • You mustn’t disclose confidential information.

docs.title: F. Lack of necessity
docs.text:
• You don’t have to answer immediately.

Un buon parlante B1 sceglie il modal in base alla forza pragmatica, non solo alla traduzione italiana.

docs.title: 6. Workplace English: advice, rules and compliance
docs.text: Questa unità è molto utile in ambito lavorativo.

docs.title: Advice to a colleague
docs.text:
• You should summarise the key points more clearly.
  • You should confirm the deadline in writing.
  • You shouldn’t promise something you can’t deliver.

docs.title: Company rules / procedures
docs.text:
• Employees have to complete the form before access is granted.
  • Visitors must wear a pass at all times.
  • Staff mustn’t leave confidential material unattended.

docs.title: Practical necessity
docs.text:
• I have to finish this before the weekly meeting.
  • We have to update the database by Friday.

docs.title: Health advice
docs.text:
• You should cut down on sugar.
  • You should get more sleep.
  • You shouldn’t ignore these symptoms.

docs.title: Study advice
docs.text:
• You should revise a little every day.
  • You shouldn’t leave everything until the night before the exam.
  • You have to practise regularly if you want to improve.

docs.title: Everyday obligations
docs.text:
• I have to renew my passport.
  • We have to leave early to avoid traffic.
  • You mustn’t park in front of the gate.

docs.title: 8. Form and structure: keep it clean
Positive
docs.text:
• should + base verb
  • must + base verb
  • have to + base verb

docs.title: Negative
docs.text:
• shouldn’t + base verb
  • mustn’t + base verb
  • don’t / doesn’t have to + base verb

docs.title: Questions
docs.text:
• Should I send it now?
  • Must we sign this today?
  • Do you have to attend the meeting?

docs.title: Past / future note
docs.text:
Must non ha tutte le forme verbali in uso quotidiano come have to. Per passato e futuro si usa spesso had to / will have to.

  • I had to cancel the appointment.
  • We’ll have to leave earlier next time.

Questo è utile per rendere l’inglese più realistico e meno scolastico.

docs.title: 🎧 Reading / Dialogue
docs.text:
Dialogue 1 – Team leader giving advice

A: I’m not sure how to handle the client’s complaint. B: You should reply quickly, but you shouldn’t sound defensive. A: Should I call them or send an email? B: You should probably email first and then offer a call.

Dialogue 2 – Office policy

A: Do we have to wear badges even on Fridays? B: Yes, we have to wear them every day. A: And can we share login details if someone is absent? B: No, you mustn’t share passwords under any circumstances.

Dialogue 3 – Personal obligation vs external rule

A: Why are you still at the office? B: I have to finish the monthly report. A: You look exhausted. B: I know. I should manage my time better.

docs.title: 🗣️ Speaking Drills
docs.text:
Ripeti con attenzione al tono:

  • You should double-check the figures.
  • We have to follow the procedure.
  • You mustn’t interrupt the patient.
  • I don’t have to be there in person.
  • We’ll have to discuss this again tomorrow.

docs.title: Controlled practice
docs.text:
Completa a voce:

  • If a colleague is stressed, you should ____.
  • In my job, we have to ____.
  • Visitors mustn’t ____.
  • Students don’t have to _, but they should _.
  • To improve your English, you should never ____.

docs.title: Contrast drill
docs.text:
Scegli la forma giusta e spiega perché:

  1. You ____ wear protective glasses in the lab.
  2. You ____ come tomorrow if you’re busy.
  3. I ____ remember to send that invoice.
  4. You ____ leave confidential files on your desk.

docs.title: ✍️ Writing
docs.text:
Scrivi un testo di 140–180 parole su uno di questi temi:

  1. Advice for new employees in my company
  2. Rules and expectations in a professional environment
  3. What people should do to work or study more effectively

Cerca di includere:

  • almeno 3 frasi con should / shouldn’t
  • almeno 3 con have to / don’t have to
  • almeno 2 con must / mustn’t
  • almeno una distinzione chiara tra prohibition e lack of necessity

docs.title: Model opening
docs.text: New employees should ask questions when they are unsure, and they shouldn’t try to solve every problem alone. In our company, staff have to follow security procedures and update their tasks regularly. They mustn’t share confidential information, but they don’t have to work late every day. In my opinion, people should organise their priorities better and communicate clearly when deadlines are unrealistic.

docs.title: Focus points
docs.text:
• should → vocale breve /ʃʊd/
  • mustn’t → spesso parlato rapidamente, attenzione alla t
  • have to → in connected speech spesso suona come /hæftə/
  • should have / must have / have to non si leggono parola per parola come in italiano

docs.title: Practice lines
docs.text:
• You should tell them as soon as possible.
  • We have to finish this by five.
  • You mustn’t discuss this outside the team.

L’obiettivo non è la perfezione fonetica, ma una delivery chiara, sicura e fluida.

docs.title: 1. Adding “to” after modals
docs.text:
❌ You should to call him. ✅ You should call him.

❌ I must to leave now. ✅ I must leave now.

docs.title: 2. Confusing prohibition with no necessity
docs.text:
❌ You don’t have to smoke here. ✅ You mustn’t smoke here.

❌ You mustn’t come tomorrow if you’re busy. ✅ You don’t have to come tomorrow if you’re busy.

docs.title: 3. Overusing must for everything
docs.text: ❌ I must go to work at 8 every day. (possible, but often too strong) ✅ I have to go to work at 8 every day.

docs.title: 4. Direct translation from Italian “devo”
docs.text:
In inglese non tutti i “devo” sono uguali. Chiediti sempre:

  • è un consiglio?
  • è una regola?
  • è un obbligo esterno?
  • è vietato?
  • non è necessario?

Questo ti aiuta a scegliere la forma giusta.

docs.title: ✅ Review & Practice
docs.text:
Scegli la forma migliore: should, must, have to, mustn’t, don’t have to.

  1. If you want to improve, you ____ practise consistently.
  2. Visitors ____ enter the building without a pass.
  3. We ____ wear formal clothes on Fridays; it’s optional.
  4. I ____ finish this today because the deadline is tomorrow.
  5. You really ____ speak to HR before making a complaint.
  6. Staff ____ store passwords in shared documents.
  7. Do we ____ attend the training session, or is it optional?
  8. You ____ ignore repeated mistakes in a safety procedure.

docs.title: 🔄 Transformation Drill
docs.text:
Riscrivi con il modal richiesto.

  1. It is a good idea to take notes during meetings. (should)
  2. It is forbidden to use personal USB devices. (mustn’t)
  3. It is not necessary to reply tonight. (don’t have to)
  4. It is necessary for all staff to sign the form. (have to)
  5. I feel a strong personal need to change my routine. (must)

docs.title: 🧠 Final Speaking Task
docs.text:
Parla per 2–3 minuti su uno di questi temi:

  • advice for someone starting a new job
  • rules people have to follow in your workplace or country
  • what students should do to learn more effectively
  • the difference between something that is forbidden and something that is simply optional

Per fare una buona performance, cerca di:

  • usare almeno 6 modals diversi in totale
  • includere esempi reali
  • spiegare il motivo delle tue scelte
  • non limitarti a frasi isolate: costruisci un discorso coerente

docs.title: 🚀 Performance Task
docs.text:
Scrivi una mini guida intitolata:

Advice and Rules for New Team Members

Lunghezza consigliata: 150–180 parole.

La guida deve includere:

  • consigli pratici
  • regole di comportamento
  • obblighi organizzativi
  • almeno 2 proibizioni
  • almeno 1 esempio di cosa non è necessario fare

A strong B1 speaker does not just know modal verbs. They know how to use them to guide, warn, advise and manage real situations.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità saprai:

  • scrivere email e messaggi chiari in inglese
  • distinguere il tono informale, neutro e più formale
  • organizzare bene apertura, corpo e chiusura di un testo scritto
  • fare richieste, dare informazioni e rispondere in modo naturale
  • evitare errori tipici degli italiani nelle email e nei messaggi

docs.title: 🧠 Grammar in context
docs.text:
In questa unità la grammatica è importante, ma il focus principale è la comunicazione scritta.

Quando scriviamo email o messaggi in inglese, non basta usare il tempo verbale corretto: dobbiamo anche scegliere:

  • il tono giusto
  • il livello di formalità giusto
  • la struttura giusta
  • formule naturali per iniziare, sviluppare e chiudere il messaggio

Qui useremo spesso:

  • Present Simple per informazioni generali
  • Present Continuous per azioni in corso o piani
  • Past Simple / Present Perfect per aggiornamenti
  • will / going to per passi successivi e proposte
  • forme utili come I’m writing to..., Could you...?, Please find..., Let me know...

docs.title: A. Informal message
docs.text:
Si usa con:

  • amici
  • colleghi con cui hai confidenza
  • chat veloci
  • inviti o aggiornamenti semplici

docs.title: Esempi
docs.text:
• Hi Luca,
  • How are you?
  • Are you free tomorrow?
  • Let me know.
  • See you soon.

docs.title: B. Neutral / professional email
docs.text:
Si usa con:

  • colleghi
  • clienti
  • insegnanti
  • aziende
  • persone che non conosci bene

docs.title: Esempi
docs.text:
• Hello Sarah,
  • I’m writing to ask about...
  • Could you confirm...?
  • Please let me know.
  • Best regards,

docs.title: C. More formal email
docs.text: Si usa quando serve un tono più professionale o più distante.

docs.title: Esempi
docs.text:
• Dear Mr Smith,
  • I am writing to enquire about...
  • I would be grateful if you could...
  • Thank you for your time.
  • Kind regards,

docs.title: 2. Basic structure of an email
docs.text: Una buona email ha quasi sempre tre parti.

docs.title: 1. Opening
docs.text:
Serve per salutare e introdurre il motivo del messaggio.

  • Hi Anna,
  • Hello David,
  • Dear Ms Brown,
  • I hope you’re well.
  • I’m writing to ask about the meeting.

docs.title: 2. Main message
docs.text:
Qui scrivi l’informazione principale.

  • I’d like to confirm the time.
  • We need to change the date.
  • I’ve attached the document.
  • Could you send me the details?
  • I’m available on Tuesday afternoon.

docs.title: 3. Closing
docs.text:
Qui chiudi in modo chiaro e cortese.

  • Let me know.
  • Thanks in advance.
  • See you soon.
  • Best regards,
  • Kind regards,

docs.title: Asking for information
docs.text:
• Could you send me the schedule?
  • Can you confirm the address?
  • I’d like to know if the course is still available.
  • Do you have any updates?

docs.title: Giving information
docs.text:
• The meeting starts at 10 a.m.
  • The course will begin next Monday.
  • I’ve attached the file.
  • We’re currently reviewing your request.

docs.title: Making requests
docs.text:
• Could you reply by Friday?
  • Please send the final version when ready.
  • Can we move the meeting to Wednesday?

docs.title: Replying
docs.text:
• Thanks for your email.
  • Thanks for getting back to me.
  • I’m happy to confirm that...
  • I’m sorry for the delay.

docs.title: Closing politely
docs.text:
• Let me know if you need anything else.
  • I look forward to your reply.
  • Best regards,
  • All the best,

docs.title: 4. Formal vs informal tone
docs.text:
Informal More professional Hi Tom Hello Tom / Dear Mr Taylor Can you send me...? Could you send me...? I want to know... I’d like to know... Send me the file Please send me the file See you Best regards

Il contenuto può essere simile, ma il tono cambia molto.

Confronta:

  • Send me the report today. (troppo diretto in molti contesti)
  • Could you send me the report today, please? (molto meglio)

docs.title: Messages
docs.text:
I messaggi sono spesso:

  • più brevi
  • più diretti
  • più colloquiali

Esempi:

  • Hi, I’m running late.
  • Are we still meeting at 6?
  • I’ll call you later.
  • Thanks!

docs.title: Emails
docs.text:
Le email sono di solito:

  • più complete
  • più organizzate
  • più formali o semi-formali

Esempi:

  • Hello Maria,
  • I’m writing to confirm our meeting tomorrow at 6 p.m.
  • Please let me know if this still works for you.
  • Best regards,

docs.title: Email words
docs.text:
• subject
  • attachment
  • reply
  • recipient
  • message
  • update
  • request
  • confirmation
  • schedule
  • deadline

docs.title: Useful verbs
docs.text:
• send
  • attach
  • confirm
  • ask
  • reply
  • forward
  • receive
  • check
  • arrange
  • postpone

docs.title: Tone and organisation
docs.text:
• formal
  • informal
  • polite
  • clear
  • brief
  • detailed
  • available
  • urgent
  • professional

docs.title: Informal message
docs.text: Hi Emma, Are you free on Friday evening? We’re planning to have dinner with a few friends. Let me know if you can come. See you soon!

docs.title: Professional email
docs.text: Hello Mr Carter, I’m writing to confirm our meeting on Thursday at 11 a.m. I’ve attached the latest version of the report. Please let me know if you would like any changes before the meeting. Best regards, Marco Rossi

docs.title: Reply email
docs.text: Hi Marco, Thanks for your email. Thursday at 11 a.m. works well for me. I’ve received the report and I’ll review it this afternoon. Best regards, Daniel Carter

docs.title: 🗣️ Speaking
docs.text:
Anche se questa unità è sulla scrittura, leggi ad alta voce le formule più utili:

  • I’m writing to ask about...
  • Could you confirm...?
  • I’ve attached the file.
  • Please let me know.
  • Thanks for your email.
  • I’m sorry for the delay.

Ora completa a voce:

  • I’m writing to ____.
  • Could you ____ by Friday?
  • I’ve attached ____.
  • Thanks for ____.
  • Please let me know if ____.

docs.title: ✍️ Writing
docs.text: Scrivi due testi.

docs.title: Task 1 – Informal message
docs.text:
Scrivi un messaggio di 50–70 parole a un amico per:

  • proporre un incontro
  • cambiare un orario
  • chiedere conferma

docs.title: Task 2 – Professional email
docs.text:
Scrivi una email di 90–120 parole per:

  • chiedere informazioni su un corso
  • confermare un meeting
  • inviare un file o un aggiornamento

Cerca di includere:

  • un’apertura corretta
  • almeno 2 formule di cortesia
  • una richiesta chiara
  • una chiusura naturale

docs.title: Model opening
docs.text: Hello, I’m writing to ask for some information about your English course. I’d like to know the start date, the timetable and the course fee.

docs.title: 🔊 Pronunciation
docs.text:
Attenzione a queste parole:

  • reply → /rɪˈplaɪ/
  • regards → /rɪˈɡɑːrdz/
  • attached → /əˈtætʃt/
  • schedule → BrE /ˈʃedjuːl/ – AmE /ˈskedʒuːl/
  • message → /ˈmesɪdʒ/

Ripeti:

  • I’m writing to confirm the meeting.
  • I’ve attached the document.
  • Could you reply by tomorrow?

docs.title: 1. Too direct
docs.text: ❌ Send me the information. ✅ Could you send me the information, please?

docs.title: 2. Wrong translation of “scrivo per”
docs.text: ❌ I write you for ask... ✅ I’m writing to ask...

docs.title: 3. Wrong preposition
docs.text: ❌ I wait your reply. ✅ I look forward to your reply. / I’m waiting for your reply.

docs.title: 4. Unnatural greeting
docs.text: ❌ Dear friend ✅ Hi Anna / Hello Anna

docs.title: 5. Missing subject or opening
docs.text: Una email in inglese professionale senza apertura o senza formula iniziale può sembrare brusca.

docs.title: ✅ Review & Practice
docs.text:
Completa:

  1. I’m writing to __ about the meeting.
  2. Could you __ the time, please?
  3. I’ve __ the file to this email.
  4. Thanks for your __.
  5. Best __,
  6. Let me know if you need anything __.

docs.title: 🔄 Transformation Drill
docs.text:
Trasforma in forma più professionale.

  1. Send me the file.
  2. I want to know the price.
  3. Hi! Give me an answer soon.
  4. I write you for ask information.

docs.title: 🧠 Final Speaking Task
docs.text:
Parla per 2 minuti su uno di questi temi:

  • la differenza tra un messaggio e una email
  • quando usare un tono formale o informale
  • come scrivere una email professionale chiara

docs.title: 🚀 Mini Task
docs.text:
Scrivi una email breve per:

  • chiedere informazioni
  • confermare un appuntamento
  • rispondere a un ritardo o a un cambiamento di programma

A good B1 writer is clear, polite and organised.

docs.title: 🎯 Lesson Goals
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • scrivere email e messaggi con un tono adeguato al contesto, al rapporto con il destinatario e all’obiettivo comunicativo
  • distinguere con sicurezza tra informal, neutral professional e more formal English
  • organizzare una email in modo credibile: subject, opening, context, request / update, closing action, sign-off
  • formulare richieste, solleciti, conferme, aggiornamenti, scuse e follow-up in modo naturale
  • evitare gli errori tipici degli italofoni, soprattutto su register, eccessiva direttività, calchi dall’italiano e formule poco idiomatiche

Questa unità è fondamentale perché a livello B1 non basta più “scrivere corretto”: bisogna scrivere in modo chiaro, efficiente e socialmente appropriato.

docs.title: 🧠 Core idea: good written English is about register and purpose
docs.text:
Molti studenti si concentrano solo sulla grammatica della frase singola. In realtà, una buona email o un buon messaggio dipendono da tre domande:

  1. Who am I writing to?
  2. Why am I writing?
  3. How direct should I be?

Confronta:

  • Send me the updated file today.
  • Could you send me the updated file today, please?
  • Could you please send me the updated file by the end of the day?

Tutte e tre le frasi sono grammaticalmente corrette, ma non comunicano la stessa cosa. La differenza è di tono, relazione e professionalità.

A livello B1 l’obiettivo è imparare a scegliere la formula che suona naturale nel contesto giusto.

docs.title: A. Informal
docs.text: Si usa con amici, persone con cui hai confidenza, chat veloci, messaggi personali.

docs.title: Typical openings
docs.text:
• Hi,
  • Hi Tom,
  • Hey,
  • Just a quick message...
  • Sorry, I’ve only just seen your message.

docs.title: Typical closings
docs.text:
• See you soon,
  • Talk soon,
  • Let me know,
  • Thanks,
  • Bye for now,

docs.title: B. Neutral professional
docs.text: È il registro più utile per la maggior parte delle email di lavoro o studio. Suona educato ma non rigido.

docs.title: Typical openings
docs.text:
• Hello Anna,
  • Hello Mr Walker,
  • Thank you for your email.
  • I’m writing to confirm...
  • I’m writing to ask about...

docs.title: Typical closings
docs.text:
• Best regards,
  • Kind regards,
  • Thank you,
  • Please let me know if you need anything else.

docs.title: C. More formal
docs.text: Si usa con aziende, istituzioni, persone senior, contesti amministrativi o situazioni delicate.

docs.title: Typical openings
docs.text:
• Dear Ms Green,
  • Dear Sir or Madam,
  • I am writing to enquire about...
  • I would like to request...
  • I would be grateful if you could...

docs.title: Typical closings
docs.text:
• Kind regards,
  • Yours sincerely, (se sai il nome)
  • Yours faithfully, (se usi Dear Sir or Madam in British English)

A livello B1 non serve essere eccessivamente cerimoniosi, ma è utile riconoscere il grado di formalità.

docs.title: 2. Email architecture: write in blocks, not in one long paragraph
docs.text: Una email professionale chiara segue spesso questa struttura:

docs.title: 1. Subject line
docs.text: L’oggetto deve essere chiaro e specifico.

docs.title: Good examples
docs.text:
• Meeting confirmation – Thursday 14 May
  • Request for information about the course
  • Updated report attached
  • Follow-up on yesterday’s call

docs.title: Weak examples
docs.text:
• Information
  • Hello
  • Important
  • Question

docs.title: 2. Opening
docs.text:
L’apertura serve per stabilire il tono e introdurre il motivo del messaggio.

  • Hello Sara,
  • Dear Mr Hall,
  • Thanks for your message.
  • I’m writing to confirm the details of...
  • I’m writing to ask whether...

docs.title: 3. Context
docs.text:
Se necessario, dai un minimo di contesto.

  • Following our meeting yesterday...
  • As discussed this morning...
  • Further to your email...
  • I’m contacting you regarding...

docs.title: 4. Main purpose
docs.text:
Qui vai al punto:

  • Could you confirm the final schedule?
  • I’d like to request an extension.
  • I’m sending you the updated version.
  • We need to postpone the meeting.

docs.title: 5. Action / next step
docs.text:
Una buona email dice chiaramente cosa succede adesso.

  • Please let me know by Friday.
  • Could you reply when you have a moment?
  • I look forward to hearing from you.
  • Let me know if Tuesday works for you.

docs.title: 6. Sign-off
docs.text:
• Best regards,
  • Kind regards,
  • Many thanks,
  • See you soon, (solo informale / semi-informale)

docs.title: 3. Common email functions and natural language
Neutral professional
docs.text:
• I’m writing to ask for some information about...
  • Could you tell me whether...?
  • I’d like to know if...
  • Could you confirm...?

docs.title: More formal
docs.text:
• I would like to enquire about...
  • I would be grateful if you could provide...

docs.title: B. Making a request
docs.text:
• Could you send me the latest version?
  • Could you please review the attached document?
  • Would it be possible to reschedule the meeting?
  • Please let me know if this is possible.

docs.title: C. Giving an update
docs.text:
• I’m writing to let you know that...
  • I’ve completed the first draft.
  • We’re still waiting for confirmation.
  • The project is currently on schedule.

docs.title: D. Confirming
docs.text:
• I’m happy to confirm that...
  • This is to confirm our meeting at 3 p.m.
  • Thursday works well for me.
  • I can confirm that the payment has been received.

docs.title: E. Apologising / explaining delay
docs.text:
• I’m sorry for the delay in replying.
  • Apologies for the late response.
  • I’m afraid I won’t be able to attend.
  • Sorry, but I need to move the meeting to next week.

docs.title: F. Following up
docs.text:
• I’m just following up on my previous email.
  • I wanted to check whether you had a chance to review the document.
  • Just a quick reminder that...

Queste funzioni sono più utili della pura grammatica, perché riflettono situazioni reali.

docs.title: 4. Directness: polite does not mean weak
docs.text:
Molti italofoni oscillano tra due estremi:

  • troppo diretti: Send me the contract today.
  • troppo lunghi o vaghi: I wanted to write maybe to ask if perhaps...

L’inglese professionale efficace è spesso:

  • chiaro
  • breve
  • cortese
  • orientato all’azione

docs.title: Too direct
docs.text: • Send me the slides today.

docs.title: Better
docs.text: • Could you send me the slides today, please?

docs.title: More efficient and professional
docs.text:
• Could you please send me the slides by 4 p.m. today?

L’ultima frase è la migliore perché unisce cortesia e chiarezza operativa.

docs.title: 5. Emails vs messages: different channels, different expectations
Messages
docs.text:
I messaggi, specialmente su chat o app di lavoro, tendono a essere:

  • più brevi
  • meno strutturati
  • meno formali
  • più immediati

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• Hi, I’m running 10 minutes late.
  • Are we still on for 4?
  • I’ve sent the file.
  • Can I call you later?

docs.title: Emails
docs.text:
Le email sono preferibili quando:

  • serve traccia scritta più stabile
  • devi spiegare qualcosa con ordine
  • stai scrivendo a un cliente, docente, azienda o ufficio
  • devi allegare documenti
  • devi formulare una richiesta più ufficiale

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• I’m writing to confirm the revised timeline.
  • Please find the updated budget attached.
  • Could you let me know if you approve the proposal?

Un buon parlante B1 sa scegliere non solo le parole giuste, ma anche il canale giusto.

docs.title: 6. Tone markers that make your writing sound natural
Softer requests
docs.text:
• Could you...?
  • Could you please...?
  • Would it be possible to...?
  • When you have a moment, could you...?

docs.title: Clear but polite action points
docs.text:
• Please let me know by Thursday.
  • I’d appreciate your feedback.
  • Let me know if you’d prefer a different time.

docs.title: Friendly but professional warmth
docs.text:
• I hope you’re well.
  • Thanks for your help.
  • Thanks again for your time.
  • It was great speaking with you yesterday.

docs.title: Closing signals
docs.text:
• I look forward to your reply.
  • I look forward to hearing from you.
  • Please don’t hesitate to contact me if you have any questions.

Queste formule aiutano molto a costruire un tono credibile senza risultare rigido.

docs.title: Asking
docs.text:
• I’m writing to ask whether...
  • Could you confirm if...?
  • I’d like to know when...

docs.title: Sending / attaching
docs.text:
• I’ve attached the file for your review.
  • Please find the document attached.
  • I’m sending you the latest version.

docs.title: Scheduling
docs.text:
• Are you available on Tuesday afternoon?
  • Could we move the meeting to Friday?
  • Thursday works for me.
  • I’m afraid I need to reschedule.

docs.title: Clarifying
docs.text:
• Just to clarify,
  • For reference,
  • As mentioned above,
  • To avoid confusion,

docs.title: Closing with next step
docs.text:
• Please let me know what you think.
  • I look forward to your feedback.
  • Thanks in advance for your help.

docs.title: A. Informal message
docs.text: Hi Laura, Just a quick message to check if you’re still free this evening. I’m finishing work a bit later than expected, so I may arrive around 7.30. Let me know if that still works for you. See you later!

docs.title: Why it works
docs.text:
• apertura naturale
  • tono personale
  • informazione chiara
  • richiesta semplice
  • chiusura coerente con il rapporto

docs.title: B. Neutral professional email
docs.text: Hello Mr Reed, I’m writing to confirm our meeting tomorrow at 10 a.m. I’ve attached the updated agenda and a short summary of the main points we’ll discuss. Please let me know if you would like me to add anything before the meeting. Best regards, Martina Bianchi

docs.title: Why it works
docs.text:
• motivo del messaggio immediato
  • informazione operativa chiara
  • allegato segnalato bene
  • invito all’azione finale
  • tono professionale, non rigido

docs.title: C. Follow-up email
docs.text: Hello Daniel, I’m just following up on my previous email about the training schedule. Could you let me know whether the final dates have been confirmed? This will help us organise the internal calendar for next month. Many thanks, Elisa

docs.title: Why it works
docs.text:
• follow-up gentile
  • richiesta precisa
  • spiega perché la risposta serve
  • tono professionale e collaborativo

docs.title: 🗣️ Speaking Drills
docs.text:
Leggi ad alta voce queste formule come blocchi pronti, non parola per parola:

  • I’m writing to ask about...
  • I’m writing to confirm that...
  • Could you please send me...?
  • Please let me know if this works for you.
  • I’m sorry for the delay in replying.
  • I’m just following up on my previous email.
  • I’ve attached the updated document for your review.

docs.title: Controlled practice
docs.text:
Completa a voce:

  • I’m writing to confirm ____.
  • Could you please send me ____ by Friday?
  • I’m sorry for the delay in ____.
  • I’d like to know whether ____.
  • Please let me know if ____ works for you.

docs.title: Register drill
docs.text:
Trasforma a voce da troppo diretto a professionale:

  1. Send me the file.
  2. Change the meeting.
  3. Answer me today.
  4. I want information about the course.

docs.title: ✍️ Writing
docs.text: Scrivi due testi.

docs.title: Task 1 – Professional email
docs.text:
Scrivi una email di 120–150 parole per una di queste situazioni:

  1. chiedere informazioni su un corso o servizio
  2. confermare e organizzare un meeting
  3. inviare un file e spiegare cosa contiene
  4. fare follow-up su una richiesta precedente

Il testo deve includere:

  • subject line
  • opening appropriata
  • motivo del messaggio
  • richiesta o aggiornamento chiaro
  • closing action
  • sign-off adeguato

docs.title: Task 2 – Short work message
docs.text:
Scrivi un messaggio di 40–60 parole a un collega per:

  • avvisare di un ritardo
  • cambiare rapidamente un orario
  • confermare l’invio di un file

docs.title: Model opening
docs.text: Subject: Request for information about the evening course Hello, I’m writing to ask for some information about your evening English course. I’d like to know the start date, the weekly timetable and whether the lessons are available online.

docs.title: 🔊 Pronunciation and delivery
docs.text: Anche nella scrittura professionale, leggere ad alta voce aiuta a sentire se una frase è naturale.

docs.title: Focus words
docs.text:
• reply → /rɪˈplaɪ/
  • regards → /rɪˈɡɑːrdz/
  • attached → /əˈtætʃt/
  • forward → /ˈfɔːwəd/ o /ˈfɔrwərd/
  • schedule → BrE /ˈʃedjuːl/ – AmE /ˈskedʒuːl/
  • available → /əˈveɪləbəl/

docs.title: Practice lines
docs.text:
• Please find the updated file attached.
  • I look forward to hearing from you.
  • Could you confirm whether you’re available next Tuesday?

Se la frase è difficile da leggere ad alta voce, spesso è troppo lunga o poco naturale anche da scrivere.

docs.title: 1. Translating directly from Italian openings
docs.text:
❌ I write you for ask information. ✅ I’m writing to ask for information.

❌ I contact you for the course. ✅ I’m contacting you about the course.

docs.title: 2. Using “I want” in professional requests
docs.text:
❌ I want to know the price. ✅ I’d like to know the price.

❌ I want an answer today. ✅ Could you please reply today?

docs.title: 3. Being too direct with imperatives
docs.text:
❌ Send me the document. ✅ Could you send me the document, please?

❌ Confirm the date. ✅ Could you confirm the date?

docs.title: 4. Wrong or unnatural closings
docs.text:
❌ Dear friend ✅ Hi Marco / Hello Marco

❌ Kisses / Hug in a professional email ✅ Best regards / Kind regards

docs.title: 5. Confusing “reply” and “answer”
docs.text:
In molti contesti scritti, reply è più naturale di answer.

  • Thank you for your reply.
  • I look forward to your reply.

docs.title: 6. Overusing long formal phrases in normal emails
docs.text:
A volte gli studenti cercano di suonare professionali usando formule troppo pesanti.

❌ I would be extremely grateful if you could kindly provide me with... (possibile, ma spesso troppo) ✅ Could you please send me...? (più naturale nella maggior parte dei casi)

Il buon inglese professionale è spesso semplice ma preciso.

docs.title: ✅ Review & Practice
docs.text:
Completa con una parola o espressione adatta.

  1. I’m writing to __ whether the meeting has been confirmed.
  2. Please find the updated file __.
  3. I’m sorry for the delay in __.
  4. Could you __ the final price, please?
  5. I look forward to __ from you.
  6. Best __,
  7. Just a quick message to let you __ that I’m running late.
  8. Thanks for getting back __ me.

docs.title: 🔄 Transformation Drill
docs.text:
Rendi più naturale e professionale.

  1. Send me the new version today.
  2. I want information about your course.
  3. Answer me soon.
  4. I write to you for confirm the meeting.
  5. Hi friend, I wait your answer.

Poi riscrivi le frasi in due registri diversi:

  • semi-formal email
  • short informal message

docs.title: 🧠 Final Speaking Task
docs.text:
Parla per 2–3 minuti su uno di questi temi:

  • la differenza tra scrivere a un amico e scrivere a un’azienda
  • come rendere una richiesta chiara ma cortese
  • quando usare un messaggio e quando usare una email
  • gli errori più comuni degli italiani nella scrittura professionale in inglese

Per una buona performance:

  • usa esempi concreti
  • confronta almeno due registri diversi
  • includi almeno 6 formule utili della lezione
  • spiega non solo cosa scrivere, ma anche perché

docs.title: 🚀 Performance Task
docs.text: Scrivi una email professionale completa di 140–180 parole in una di queste situazioni:

docs.title: Option A – Request
docs.text: Vuoi informazioni su un corso, un servizio o una collaborazione.

docs.title: Option B – Follow-up
docs.text: Hai già scritto una email e vuoi fare un sollecito educato.

docs.title: Option C – Confirmation + attachment
docs.text:
Confermi un meeting e invii un documento allegato.

La tua email deve includere:

  • un subject line chiaro
  • apertura appropriata
  • tono coerente
  • un motivo del messaggio espresso con chiarezza
  • almeno una richiesta formulata in modo cortese
  • una closing line con prossimo passo
  • un sign-off credibile

A strong B1 writer does not just know useful phrases. They know how to sound clear, polite and appropriate in real written communication.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità saprai:

  • capire cosa sono i phrasal verbs e perché sono così comuni nell’inglese parlato
  • usare alcuni phrasal verbs frequenti in contesti quotidiani e professionali semplici
  • distinguere tra significato letterale e significato idiomatico
  • riconoscere alcuni phrasal verbs separabili e non separabili
  • evitare traduzioni troppo letterali dall’italiano

docs.title: 🧠 What is a phrasal verb?
docs.text:
Un phrasal verb è formato da:

  • verb + particle

La particella può essere:

  • un avverbio
  • una preposizione
  • a volte una combinazione di entrambi

docs.title: Esempi
docs.text:
• get up → alzarsi
  • turn on → accendere
  • find out → scoprire
  • look after → prendersi cura di
  • give up → smettere / rinunciare

Molto spesso il significato non è letterale.

  • look for non significa “guardare per”
  • find out non significa semplicemente “trovare fuori”

Per questo i phrasal verbs vanno imparati come unità di significato.

docs.title: 1. Why are phrasal verbs important?
docs.text:
A livello B1 iniziano a diventare fondamentali perché:

  • sono molto usati nel parlato
  • rendono l’inglese più naturale
  • compaiono in email, serie TV, podcast, conversazioni e riunioni informali
  • spesso esistono alternative più formali, ma il phrasal verb è la scelta più spontanea

Confronta:

  • continue → più formale

  • go on → più naturale nel parlato

  • discover → più formale

  • find out → più naturale nel parlato

  • remove → più formale

  • take off → più naturale in molti contesti

docs.title: A. Daily routine
docs.text:
• wake up → svegliarsi
  • get up → alzarsi
  • go out → uscire
  • come back → tornare
  • sit down → sedersi
  • put on → indossare
  • take off → togliersi

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• I usually wake up at 6:30.
  • I get up a few minutes later.
  • We don’t go out much during the week.
  • I come back home at about seven.
  • Please sit down.
  • She puts on a jacket when it’s cold.

docs.title: B. Home and objects
docs.text:
• turn on → accendere
  • turn off → spegnere
  • pick up → raccogliere / andare a prendere
  • throw away → buttare via
  • clean up → riordinare / pulire
  • fill in → compilare

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• Can you turn on the light?
  • Please turn off your phone.
  • I need to pick up my bag.
  • Don’t throw away that paper.
  • We have to clean up the kitchen.
  • Please fill in this form.

docs.title: C. Communication and information
docs.text:
• find out → scoprire
  • look for → cercare
  • work out → capire / trovare una soluzione
  • check in → fare il check-in
  • call back → richiamare
  • write down → scrivere / annotare

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• I need to find out what time the meeting starts.
  • She is looking for her keys.
  • We’re trying to work out the problem.
  • We can check in online.
  • I’ll call you back later.
  • Please write down the address.

docs.title: A. Separable phrasal verbs
docs.text:
Con alcuni phrasal verbs l’oggetto può stare:

  • dopo il phrasal verb
  • tra verbo e particella

docs.title: Esempi
docs.text:
• Turn on the TV.

  • Turn the TV on.

  • Pick up the phone.

  • Pick the phone up.

  • Write down the number.

  • Write the number down.

docs.title: Attenzione con i pronomi
docs.text:
Se l’oggetto è un pronome, deve stare in mezzo.

  • Turn it on.
  • Pick it up.
  • Write it down.

❌ Turn on it. ❌ Pick up it.

docs.title: B. Non-separable phrasal verbs
docs.text: Con altri phrasal verbs non puoi separare verbo e particella.

docs.title: Esempi
docs.text:
• look for
  • look after
  • find out
  • run into

Frasi corrette:

  • I’m looking for my glasses.
  • She looks after her younger brother.
  • We need to find out the answer.

Frasi scorrette:

  • ❌ I’m looking my glasses for.
  • ❌ She looks her brother after.

docs.title: 4. Mini dialogue
docs.text:
At home before going out

A: Can you turn off the lights before we leave?

B: Sure. I just need to pick up my keys.

A: I can’t find my phone.

B: Are you looking for it? It’s on the table.

A: Thanks. Let’s go out now.

B: Wait a second. I need to put on my coat.

docs.title: 5. Listening / Reading
docs.text:
Leggi il testo e individua i phrasal verbs.

I usually wake up early and get up at about 6:45. I put on my clothes, turn on the coffee machine and write down my plans for the day. In the evening I often go out with friends, but sometimes I just stay home and clean up the kitchen.

docs.title: Comprehension questions
docs.text:
1. What time does the speaker get up?
  2. What does the speaker turn on in the morning?
  3. What does the speaker write down?
  4. What does the speaker sometimes clean up?

docs.title: 6. Speaking practice
docs.text:
Ripeti ad alta voce:

  • I wake up at 7.
  • I turn off my alarm.
  • I pick up my phone.
  • I look for my keys.
  • I go out in the evening.
  • I come back late on Fridays.

docs.title: Ora completa oralmente
docs.text:
• I usually ___ up at...
  • Before I leave home, I always ___ off...
  • When I can’t find something, I ___ for it.
  • In the evening I often ___ out with...

docs.title: 7. Writing practice
docs.text:
Scrivi 8 frasi su:

  • la tua routine
  • la tua casa
  • il tuo lavoro o studio

Usa almeno 6 phrasal verbs tra questi:

  • wake up
  • get up
  • turn on
  • turn off
  • pick up
  • write down
  • look for
  • find out
  • go out
  • come back

docs.title: 🔊 Pronunciation
docs.text:
Molti phrasal verbs si pronunciano in modo fluido, quasi come un’unica unità.

Ripeti:

  • get up
  • wake up
  • turn on
  • turn off
  • pick up
  • find out

Attenzione al ritmo:

  • TURN it OFF
  • PICK it UP
  • WRITE it DOWN

docs.title: 1. Tradurre parola per parola
docs.text: ❌ I search my keys. ✅ I’m looking for my keys.

docs.title: 2. Separare un verbo non separabile
docs.text: ❌ I looked the information for. ✅ I looked for the information.

docs.title: 3. Mettere male il pronome
docs.text: ❌ Turn off it. ✅ Turn it off.

docs.title: 4. Usare solo verbi “latini” molto formali
docs.text: ✅ We need to find out the problem. ✅ Please write down the address.

docs.title: A. Complete the sentences
docs.text:
1. I usually ___ up at 7.
  2. Can you ___ off the TV?
  3. I need to ___ for my passport.
  4. Please ___ down your phone number.
  5. We’re trying to ___ out what happened.
  6. She always ___ back home late on Fridays.

docs.title: B. Choose the correct option
docs.text:
1. Turn the light on / Turn on the light → both are correct?
  2. Look for the keys / Look the keys for → which is correct?
  3. Pick it up / Pick up it → which is correct?

docs.title: C. Transform
docs.text:
1. continue → go on
  2. discover → find out
  3. remove your shoes → take off your shoes
  4. note the address → write down the address

docs.title: 🧠 Think & Speak
docs.text:
Rispondi oralmente:

  • What time do you usually get up?
  • What do you turn on first in the morning?
  • What do you do when you can’t find something?
  • What do you usually do before you go out?
  • What time do you come back home?

docs.title: 🚀 Mini task
docs.text:
Parla per 2 minuti della tua giornata usando almeno 8 phrasal verbs.

Puoi seguire questo schema:

  • morning
  • work / study
  • evening
  • problems / solutions
  • free time

docs.title: 🎯 Self-check
docs.text:
Se riesci a:

  • riconoscere un phrasal verb comune
  • usare correttamente almeno 10 phrasal verbs frequenti
  • distinguere alcuni casi separabili e non separabili
  • produrre frasi naturali senza tradurre dall’italiano

allora questa unità è stata assimilata bene.

docs.title: 🎯 Lesson Goals
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • capire perché i phrasal verbs sono una parte essenziale dell’inglese naturale, soprattutto nel parlato e nella scrittura informale o semi-professionale
  • usare con maggiore sicurezza phrasal verbs frequenti in contesti quotidiani, accademici e lavorativi
  • distinguere tra phrasal verbs con significato quasi trasparente e phrasal verbs con significato idiomatico
  • gestire con precisione i casi separabili e non separabili, specialmente quando l’oggetto è un pronome
  • scegliere tra verbo semplice più formale e phrasal verb più naturale a seconda del registro
  • evitare gli errori tipici degli italofoni, soprattutto su traduzione letterale, ordine delle parole e collocazioni poco naturali

Questa unità è molto importante perché a livello B1 il problema non è solo “capire la grammatica”: è iniziare a suonare più naturale. I phrasal verbs sono uno degli elementi che più chiaramente distinguono un inglese scolastico da un inglese vivo.

docs.title: 🧠 Core idea: phrasal verbs are meaning units, not word-by-word combinations
docs.text:
Un phrasal verb è normalmente formato da:

  • verb + particle

La particella può essere:

  • un avverbio: sit down, wake up
  • una preposizione: look after
  • talvolta una combinazione più lunga: put up with

Il punto centrale, però, non è la classificazione tecnica. Il punto è questo:

Un phrasal verb va imparato come un blocco di significato.

Confronta:

  • turn off → spegnere
  • give up → rinunciare / smettere
  • find out → scoprire
  • bring up → crescere un figlio / sollevare un argomento

In alcuni casi il significato è intuitivo. In altri casi è molto meno trasparente. Per questo non conviene tradurre parola per parola.

docs.title: 1. Why phrasal verbs matter at B1
docs.text:
A livello B1 inizi a incontrare l’inglese in contesti più autentici:

  • conversazioni spontanee
  • email meno rigide
  • video e podcast
  • serie TV
  • istruzioni di lavoro e studio
  • interazioni quotidiane con colleghi, clienti o compagni di corso

In tutti questi contesti i phrasal verbs compaiono continuamente.

Confronta le coppie seguenti:

  • continue → più neutro / formale

  • go on → più colloquiale e frequente nel parlato

  • discover → più formale

  • find out → più naturale e comune

  • postpone → più formale

  • put off → più colloquiale

  • tolerate → più formale

  • put up with → molto comune nel parlato

Non significa che i verbi formali siano sbagliati. Significa che, senza phrasal verbs, il tuo inglese rischia di risultare corretto ma poco naturale.

docs.title: 2. Types of phrasal verbs you need to recognize
A. Literal or semi-literal phrasal verbs
docs.text:
Il significato è abbastanza trasparente.

  • sit down
  • stand up
  • go out
  • come back
  • take off (togliersi qualcosa)
  • put on (indossare)

Questi sono ottimi da introdurre presto perché aiutano a familiarizzare con il meccanismo.

docs.title: B. Idiomatic phrasal verbs
docs.text:
Il significato non si ricava facilmente dalla somma delle parti.

  • find out → scoprire
  • give up → rinunciare / smettere
  • work out → risolvere / capire / allenarsi, a seconda del contesto
  • put off → rimandare
  • bring up → menzionare / crescere
  • carry on → continuare

Qui il contesto è fondamentale.

docs.title: A. Daily life and habits
docs.text:
• wake up → svegliarsi
  • get up → alzarsi
  • go out → uscire
  • come back → tornare
  • sit down → sedersi
  • stand up → alzarsi in piedi
  • put on → indossare
  • take off → togliersi

docs.title: B. Objects, actions and space
docs.text:
• turn on → accendere
  • turn off → spegnere
  • pick up → raccogliere / prendere / andare a prendere qualcuno
  • throw away → buttare via
  • clean up → mettere in ordine / pulire
  • set up → organizzare / configurare

docs.title: C. Information and problem solving
docs.text:
• find out → scoprire
  • look for → cercare
  • work out → trovare una soluzione / capire
  • check out → controllare / vedere / fare il check-out / esaminare, a seconda del contesto
  • write down → annotare
  • go over → rivedere con attenzione

docs.title: D. Interaction and communication
docs.text:
• call back → richiamare
  • get back to → ricontattare / tornare su un punto
  • bring up → introdurre un argomento
  • point out → far notare
  • talk over → discutere con calma

docs.title: E. Progress, difficulty and attitude
docs.text:
• go on → continuare
  • carry on → continuare
  • give up → rinunciare / smettere
  • put off → rimandare
  • deal with → gestire / affrontare
  • put up with → sopportare

docs.title: 4. Meaning depends on context
docs.text: Uno stesso phrasal verb può avere più usi.

docs.title: Example: pick up
docs.text:
• I picked up my bag from the floor. → ho raccolto la borsa
  • I picked up my sister from the station. → sono andato a prenderla
  • Business is picking up. → sta migliorando / sta riprendendo

docs.title: Example: work out
docs.text:
• We need to work out a solution. → trovare una soluzione
  • I can’t work out this exercise. → capire / risolvere
  • I work out three times a week. → mi alleno

docs.title: Example: take off
docs.text:
• Please take off your shoes. → togliersi
  • The plane took off at 9. → decollare
  • Her career really took off last year. → decollare in senso figurato / avere un grande sviluppo

A questo livello non devi imparare tutti i significati possibili, ma devi abituarti all’idea che il contesto decide il significato.

docs.title: A. Separable phrasal verbs
docs.text:
Con alcuni phrasal verbs l’oggetto può andare:

  • dopo il phrasal verb
  • tra verbo e particella

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• Turn off the light.

  • Turn the light off.

  • Pick up the document.

  • Pick the document up.

  • Write down the password.

  • Write the password down.

docs.title: Very important: pronouns
docs.text:
Quando l’oggetto è un pronome, di solito deve stare in mezzo:

  • Turn it off.
  • Pick it up.
  • Write it down.
  • Put it on.

Non dire:

  • ❌ Turn off it.
  • ❌ Pick up it.
  • ❌ Write down it.

docs.title: B. Non-separable phrasal verbs
docs.text: Con altri phrasal verbs verbo e particella non si separano.

docs.title: Common examples
docs.text:
• look for
  • look after
  • run into
  • deal with
  • get over
  • come across

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• I’m looking for a better solution.
  • She looks after her grandfather.
  • We need to deal with this issue.
  • I ran into an old friend yesterday.

Non dire:

  • ❌ I’m looking a better solution for.
  • ❌ We need to deal this issue with.

docs.title: 6. Phrasal verbs and register
docs.text: Una delle competenze più utili a livello B1 è capire quando il phrasal verb è la scelta naturale e quando invece un verbo più formale può essere preferibile.

docs.title: Common natural spoken choices
docs.text:
• find out instead of discover
  • go on instead of continue
  • put off instead of postpone
  • deal with instead of handle in many everyday contexts
  • set up instead of organise / establish in practical situations

docs.title: Compare
docs.text:
• We need to find out why the system failed. → naturale e comune

  • We need to discover why the system failed. → possibile, ma meno naturale in questo contesto

  • Let’s go over the report again. → naturale in team / study contexts

  • Let’s review the report again. → corretto, un po’ più formale

  • Can we put off the meeting? → naturale e parlato

  • Can we postpone the meeting? → corretto, più formale

Il vero obiettivo non è usare sempre il phrasal verb, ma saperlo riconoscere e usare quando rende il discorso più credibile.

docs.title: 7. Dialogue 1 – Office context
docs.text:
A: Have you looked over the draft yet?

B: Not completely. I need to go over the final section again.

A: Can you get back to me this afternoon?

B: Sure. I want to find out one more thing before I send it.

A: No problem. We can talk it over after lunch.

B: Great. If something urgent comes up, I’ll call you back.

docs.title: Useful points
docs.text:
• look over / go over = rivedere rapidamente / con attenzione
  • get back to someone = ricontattare / rispondere più tardi
  • talk something over = discuterne con calma
  • come up = emergere / presentarsi

docs.title: 8. Dialogue 2 – Everyday problem solving
docs.text:
A: I can’t figure out why my laptop keeps turning off.

B: Did you check out the battery settings?

A: Not yet. I was trying to work out the problem on my own.

B: Maybe you should look up the manual online.

A: Good idea. If I can’t solve it, I’ll take it in tomorrow.

docs.title: Useful points
docs.text:
• figure out / work out = capire, risolvere
  • look up = cercare informazioni
  • take something in = portare un oggetto a riparare / consegnarlo

docs.title: 9. Listening / Reading
docs.text:
Leggi il testo e sottolinea tutti i phrasal verbs. Poi prova a spiegarli con parole tue.

This morning I woke up late because my alarm didn’t go off. I quickly got up, put on my clothes and ran out of the house. On the bus I tried to go over my notes for the meeting. During the meeting, my manager brought up a problem with a client account, so we had to work out a new plan. After lunch, I got back to two people, wrote down the next steps and finally wrapped up the day at about six.

docs.title: Comprehension questions
docs.text:
1. Why did the speaker wake up late?
  2. What did the speaker do on the bus?
  3. What problem came up during the meeting?
  4. What did the speaker write down?
  5. What does wrap up the day probably mean?

docs.title: Match the phrasal verb to the meaning
docs.text:
1. bring up
  2. go over
  3. get back to
  4. work out
  5. wrap up
  6. run out

Meanings:

  • a. ricontattare / rispondere più tardi
  • b. rivedere con attenzione
  • c. finire / concludere
  • d. uscire di corsa
  • e. trovare una soluzione
  • f. introdurre un argomento

docs.title: A. Complete the sentences
docs.text:
1. I need to ___ out what time the train leaves.
  2. Can you ___ this number down for me?
  3. Please ___ the lights off before you leave.
  4. We had to ___ up the meeting because the manager was ill.
  5. I can’t ___ up with that noise any longer.
  6. I’ll ___ back to you tomorrow.
  7. Let’s ___ over the plan one more time.
  8. She had to ___ after her little brother.

docs.title: B. Choose the correct option
docs.text:
1. I’m looking for / looking the keys for my phone charger.
  2. Turn it off / Turn off it before you go.
  3. We need to deal with / deal the problem with it soon.
  4. Can we put off / postpone off the meeting?
  5. I found out / discovered out the answer yesterday.

docs.title: C. Rewrite with a phrasal verb
docs.text:
1. continue → ____
  2. discover → ____
  3. postpone → ____
  4. tolerate → ____
  5. record in writing → ____
  6. collect from the station → ____

docs.title: 12. Speaking drills
docs.text:
Ripeti con ritmo naturale:

  • I need to find out what happened.
  • Can you write it down?
  • We’ll go over the report tomorrow.
  • I’ll get back to you later.
  • Please turn it off.
  • I can’t put up with this delay.

docs.title: Expansion drill
docs.text:
Completa oralmente:

  • I need to find out...
  • Before every meeting, I go over...
  • If I can’t understand something, I try to work out...
  • At the end of the day, I usually write down...
  • One thing I can’t put up with is...

docs.title: Task 1 – Daily life
docs.text:
Scrivi un testo di 120–150 parole su una tua giornata tipica o su una giornata particolarmente intensa.

Usa almeno 8 phrasal verbs tra questi:

  • wake up
  • get up
  • put on
  • turn off
  • go out
  • come back
  • write down
  • find out
  • work out
  • deal with

docs.title: Task 2 – Study / work context
docs.text:
Scrivi una breve email o aggiornamento interno in cui:

  • dici che devi controllare alcune informazioni
  • prometti che risponderai più tardi
  • proponi di rivedere un documento o un piano
  • menzioni un problema da affrontare

Prova a usare:

  • find out
  • get back to
  • go over
  • deal with
  • bring up

docs.title: Error 1: translating literally from Italian
docs.text: ❌ I search for a solution? / I search my keys. ✅ I’m looking for a solution. ✅ I’m looking for my keys.

docs.title: Error 2: separating a non-separable phrasal verb
docs.text: ❌ We need to deal the issue with. ✅ We need to deal with the issue.

docs.title: Error 3: putting the pronoun in the wrong place
docs.text:
❌ Turn off it. ✅ Turn it off.

❌ Write down it. ✅ Write it down.

docs.title: Error 4: overusing formal verbs in very natural contexts
docs.text:
Non è sempre un errore grammaticale, ma spesso è un errore di naturalezza.

  • less natural in everyday speech: I must postpone our chat.

  • more natural: Can we put off our chat?

  • less natural in casual context: I discovered the answer.

  • more natural: I found out the answer.

docs.title: Error 5: not learning the phrasal verb as a chunk
docs.text:
Se impari solo il verbo base, rischi di produrre frasi poco naturali. Meglio memorizzare la frase completa.

Per esempio:

  • turn it off
  • look for my keys
  • get back to you tomorrow
  • go over the report

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text:
Sei in un contesto di studio o lavoro. Devi:

  • spiegare cosa hai fatto durante la giornata
  • dire quale problema è emerso
  • spiegare come lo state affrontando
  • dire cosa farai dopo

Parla per 2–3 minuti usando almeno 10 phrasal verbs.

Puoi organizzarti così:

  1. opening of the day
  2. unexpected problem
  3. discussion with others
  4. solution or next step
  5. wrap-up

docs.title: 16. Self-check
docs.text:
Chiediti se riesci a:

  • riconoscere phrasal verbs frequenti in un testo o in una conversazione
  • scegliere il significato corretto in base al contesto
  • usare correttamente almeno 12–15 phrasal verbs ad alta frequenza
  • distinguere i casi separabili e non separabili più comuni
  • usare alcuni phrasal verbs per suonare più naturale in contesti quotidiani e semi-professionali

Se la risposta è sì, la tua competenza lessicale in inglese sta diventando molto più credibile.

docs.title: 🚀 Homework
docs.text:
1. Scegli 12 phrasal verbs da questa unità.
  2. Per ciascuno scrivi:
  3. una definizione semplice in inglese
  4. una frase personale
  5. una mini-collocation utile
  6. Registra un audio di 90 secondi in cui racconti una giornata reale usando almeno 8 phrasal verbs.
  7. Riascolta e verifica:
  8. naturalezza
  9. ordine delle parole
  10. uso corretto dei pronomi

The more phrasal verbs you notice, the more natural English starts to sound.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità saprai:

  • ripassare le strutture principali studiate nel livello B1
  • parlare di esperienze, opinioni, piani, condizioni e obblighi con maggiore sicurezza
  • gestire una conversazione più lunga e naturale
  • scrivere un testo o un’email chiari e ben organizzati

docs.title: 🧠 Grammar & Communication Review
docs.text:
Nel livello B1 hai studiato e usato:

  • Present Perfect in modo più reale e naturale
  • Present Perfect vs Past Simple
  • future forms: will, going to, Present Continuous
  • First Conditional
  • modals: must, should, have to, mustn’t, don’t have to
  • modi per esprimere opinioni
  • lessico e strutture per work and study
  • scrittura di emails and messages
  • phrasal verbs ad alta frequenza

👉 In questa unità usi tutto insieme.

docs.title: 📚 What you should be able to do now
docs.text:
A questo punto dovresti riuscire a:

  • raccontare esperienze e risultati recenti
  • spiegare la differenza tra un’esperienza generale e un evento concluso nel passato
  • parlare del futuro usando la forma più adatta
  • esprimere opinioni e motivarle
  • dare consigli e parlare di regole o necessità
  • scrivere un messaggio o un’email semplice ma ben fatta
  • capire e usare alcuni phrasal verbs comuni

docs.title: 📝 TEST 1 – Mixed Grammar
docs.text:
Completa le frasi con la forma corretta.

  1. I have never ___ in a multinational company. (work)
  2. She ___ to Berlin last year. (go)
  3. Look at those clouds! It ___ rain. (going to)
  4. If you study regularly, you ___ feel more confident. (will)
  5. You ___ wear a helmet here. It’s the rule. (must / have to)
  6. You ___ bring food to the meeting if you don’t want to. (don’t have to / mustn’t)
  7. I think online courses are ___ useful than people think. (more)
  8. Can we ___ the meeting off until Friday? (phrasal verb)
  9. I’m sorry for the delay. I’ll ___ back to you tomorrow. (phrasal verb)
  10. I haven’t finished the report ___. (yet / already)

docs.title: 📝 TEST 2 – Transformations
docs.text:
Trasforma le frasi come indicato.

  1. I visited London in 2022. → (experience)
  2. We’ve finished the task. → (specific past time: yesterday)
  3. I plan to start a new course in September. → (going to)
  4. Maybe it will be difficult. → (opinion sentence)
  5. If you don’t hurry, you’ll miss the train. → (negative result condition)
  6. You mustn’t park here. → (explain the rule in your own words)
  7. I’ll contact you later. → (phrasal verb)
  8. I don’t agree. → (softer opinion)

docs.title: 🗣️ SPEAKING REVIEW
docs.text:
Parla per 3 minuti e includi:

  • chi sei e cosa fai
  • una tua esperienza importante
  • un’opinione su studio, lavoro o tecnologia
  • una regola o consiglio utile
  • un piano futuro
  • una situazione con if

docs.title: Traccia possibile
docs.text:
1. personal introduction
  2. work / study situation
  3. life experience
  4. opinion and reason
  5. advice or obligation
  6. future plan
  7. one conditional sentence

docs.title: ✍️ WRITING TASK
docs.text:
Scrivi un testo di 140–170 parole su uno di questi temi:

  • My work or study life and my future plans
  • Why learning English is useful today
  • A professional situation I would like to manage well in English

Il testo deve includere:

  • almeno 1 Present Perfect
  • almeno 1 Past Simple
  • almeno 1 future form
  • almeno 1 opinion
  • almeno 1 modal
  • almeno 1 phrasal verb

docs.title: 🧠 THINK & SPEAK
docs.text:
Rispondi oralmente:

  • Have you ever had to use English in a real situation?
  • What did you do last weekend?
  • What are you going to do next month?
  • What should a good student do to improve quickly?
  • What do you think makes communication effective?
  • If you had more time, what would you study more?

docs.title: 🔊 PRONUNCIATION CHECK
docs.text:
Leggi ad alta voce:

  • I’ve already sent the email.
  • I went to a conference last month.
  • We’re meeting the client tomorrow.
  • If you need help, I’ll call you back.
  • You don’t have to stay, but you mustn’t be late next time.

Fai attenzione a:

  • contrazioni come I’ve, I’ll, we’re
  • differenza tra worked /t/ e needed /ɪd/
  • collegamento naturale tra le parole

docs.title: ⚠️ Errori comuni
docs.text:
❌ I have seen him yesterday. ✅ I saw him yesterday.

❌ If it will rain, we will stay home. ✅ If it rains, we will stay home.

❌ You mustn’t come if you don’t want. ✅ You don’t have to come if you don’t want.

❌ I very agree. ✅ I completely agree.

❌ I will contact to you tomorrow. ✅ I will contact you tomorrow.

docs.title: 🚀 FINAL TASK
docs.text:
Simula una conversazione con una persona conosciuta a un evento professionale o in un corso.

Devi:

  • presentarti
  • parlare di lavoro o studio
  • raccontare un’esperienza
  • esprimere un’opinione
  • dare un consiglio
  • parlare di un piano futuro
  • usare almeno 2 phrasal verbs
  • fare almeno 4 domande

docs.title: 🎯 Autovalutazione
docs.text:
Metti ✔️ se sai fare queste cose:
