  • when you have time
  • if possible
  • thank you in advance

Esempio:

  • Send me the report.
  • Could you send me the report, please?

docs.title: 3. Evita formulazioni troppo brusche
docs.text:
Meglio:

  • I’m writing to ask about...
  • I’d like to know whether...
  • Could you please let me know...?

docs.title: Confronti utili
docs.text: Informal More formal Hi Hello / Dear ... Thanks Thank you I want I would like Can you...? Could you...? Send it today Could you send it today, please? I need help I’d appreciate your help

docs.title: Situation 1: asking for information
docs.text:
Informal:

Hi, I want info about the course.

Better:

Hello, I would like some information about the course.

docs.title: Situation 2: asking for a document
docs.text:
Informal:

Can you send me the file?

Better:

Could you send me the file, please?

docs.title: Situation 3: changing an appointment
docs.text:
Informal:

I can’t come. Let’s change it.

Better:

I’m sorry, but I won’t be able to attend. Could we reschedule the meeting?

docs.title: Useful polite phrases
docs.text:
• I would like to...
  • Could you please...?
  • Would you mind...?
  • I’m writing to ask about...
  • Please let me know if...
  • Thank you for your time.

docs.title: Errore 1: essere troppo diretti
docs.text:
❌ Send me the file.

✅ Could you send me the file, please?

docs.title: Errore 2: tradurre in modo letterale
docs.text:
❌ I ask you if...

✅ I would like to ask whether...

docs.title: Errore 3: usare sempre lo stesso tono
docs.text:
Non usare lo stesso inglese per:

  • un amico;
  • un collega;
  • un ufficio;
  • un’università;
  • un cliente.

docs.title: Speaking practice
docs.text:
Trasforma oralmente le frasi:

  1. I want more details.
  2. Can you answer today?
  3. Send me the new version.
  4. I need your help.

Possibili risposte:

  • I would like more details.
  • Could you answer today, please?
  • Could you send me the new version?
  • I’d appreciate your help.

docs.title: Writing practice
docs.text:
Trasforma da informale a più professionale:

  1. Hi, I want info.
  2. Can you call me today?
  3. Send me the document.
  4. I need an answer now.

docs.title: Mini task
docs.text:
Scrivi:

  1. un messaggio informale a un amico per chiedere aiuto;
  2. una breve email più formale per chiedere informazioni su un corso.

docs.title: Review finale
docs.text:
Completa:

  1. I want → I would ______
  2. Can you → Could you ______
  3. Thanks → Thank ______
  4. Send me the details → Could you ______

docs.title: Self-check
docs.text:
Sai:

  • distinguere tra tono informale e formale?
  • fare una richiesta in modo più cortese?
  • migliorare una frase troppo diretta?
  • scrivere una breve email in tono appropriato?

Se la risposta è sì, sei pronto per continuare.

docs.title: Unità 38 – Nuance & Tone (Formal vs Informal English)
Obiettivo della lezione
docs.text:
In questa unità porti il tuo inglese a un livello più maturo imparando a gestire:

  • register (informal, neutral, formal);
  • distance and politeness;
  • tone softening;
  • differenza tra messaggio naturale e messaggio troppo diretto;
  • riscrittura di email e richieste in modo più appropriato.

L’obiettivo non è solo “parlare correttamente”, ma parlare in modo adatto al contesto.

docs.title: Perché il tono conta davvero
docs.text:
A livello B2, molte frasi non suonano sbagliate grammaticalmente, ma possono risultare:

  • troppo brusche;
  • troppo informali;
  • eccessivamente rigide;
  • poco naturali nel contesto professionale o accademico.

Confronta:

Send me the report today.

Questa frase è grammaticalmente corretta, ma in molti contesti suona dura.

Versioni più appropriate:

Could you send me the report today, please?

Would it be possible to send me the report today?

Could you please send me the report by the end of the day?

Il contenuto non cambia molto. Cambia la relazione che costruisci con l’altra persona.

docs.title: Informal
docs.text:
Tipico di:

  • amici;
  • chat rapide;
  • colleghi molto vicini;
  • ambienti con bassa distanza sociale.

Esempi:

  • Hi, can you send it?
  • Thanks a lot!
  • No problem.
  • Let’s do it tomorrow.

docs.title: Neutral professional
docs.text:
È il registro più utile nella vita reale. Va bene per:

  • email di lavoro ordinarie;
  • richieste semplici;
  • coordinamento tra colleghi;
  • messaggi chiari ma cortesi.

Esempi:

  • Hello, could you send it when you have a moment?
  • Thanks for your help.
  • Please let me know if you need anything else.
  • I’m writing to confirm our meeting.

docs.title: Formal
docs.text:
Tipico di:

  • contesti istituzionali;
  • candidature;
  • richieste delicate;
  • situazioni in cui vuoi aumentare deferenza e distanza.

Esempi:

  • Dear Ms Turner,
  • I would like to enquire about...
  • I would be grateful if you could...
  • Please accept my apologies for...

docs.title: 1. Indirectness
docs.text:
Più una richiesta è indiretta, più tende a suonare cortese.

Direct Softer More formal Send me the file. Could you send me the file? I would be grateful if you could send me the file. Tell me the price. Could you tell me the price? I was wondering if you could let me know the price. Change the meeting. Could we change the meeting? Would it be possible to reschedule the meeting?

docs.title: 2. Softening expressions
docs.text:
Espressioni molto utili:

  • please
  • when you have time
  • if possible
  • a bit
  • I think
  • perhaps / maybe
  • I was wondering if...
  • Would it be possible to...?

Esempi:

  • Could you check this?
  • Could you check this when you have time?
  • Would it be possible to check this today?

docs.title: 3. Depersonalising the request
docs.text:
In certi contesti, rendere la frase meno personale la fa suonare più professionale.

  • I need you to send the invoice.

  • The invoice needs to be sent today.

  • You must complete this form.

  • This form needs to be completed by Friday.

docs.title: Tone shift: stessa idea, tono diverso
Situation: asking for information
docs.text:
Informal

Hi, I want info about the training course.

Neutral professional

Hello, I would like some information about the training course.

More formal

Dear Sir or Madam, I am writing to enquire about the training course and would appreciate any further information you could provide.

docs.title: Situation: asking for a meeting change
docs.text:
Informal

I can’t make it. Let’s change the meeting.

Neutral professional

I’m sorry, but I can’t make the meeting. Could we move it to another time?

More formal

I’m afraid I won’t be able to attend the meeting as scheduled. Would it be possible to reschedule it?

docs.title: Situation: following up
docs.text:
Informal

Did you see my message?

Neutral professional

I just wanted to check whether you had seen my previous message.

More formal

I am writing to follow up on my previous email regarding the proposal.

docs.title: Formality is not only vocabulary
docs.text:
Molti studenti pensano che il tono dipenda solo da parole come would like o please. In realtà dipende anche da:

  • struttura della frase;
  • lunghezza e densità informativa;
  • grado di esplicitazione;
  • presenza di mitigazione;
  • opening e closing appropriati.

Confronta:

Send me the updated version today.

Could you send me the updated version today, please?

Could you please send me the updated version by the end of the day, if possible?

I’d appreciate it if you could send me the updated version by the end of the day.

Ogni frase aumenta la cortesia e modifica il rapporto interpersonale.

docs.title: Common tone problems for Italian speakers
1. Overdirect translation
docs.text:
❌ I ask you to send me...

In inglese spesso suona duro o artificiale.

Meglio:

✅ Could you send me...?

✅ I’d like to ask whether you could send me...

docs.title: 2. Using “I want” in professional contexts
docs.text:
❌ I want to know...

Meglio:

✅ I’d like to know...

✅ I was wondering whether...

docs.title: 3. Missing politeness framing
docs.text:
❌ Answer me today.

✅ Could you get back to me today, please?

✅ I’d appreciate a reply today if possible.

docs.title: 4. Being too formal in casual contexts
docs.text:
Anche l’eccesso di formalità può essere innaturale.

A un collega con cui lavori ogni giorno, questa frase può risultare rigida:

I would be grateful if you could provide the document at your earliest convenience.

In molti casi è meglio:

Could you send me the document when you have a moment? Thanks.

docs.title: Register map: common rewrites
docs.text: Too direct / basic Better neutral More formal I want information. I’d like some information. I am writing to enquire about... Can you help me? Could you help me? I would appreciate your assistance with... Send me the file. Could you send me the file? Would it be possible to send me the file? I can’t come. I won’t be able to attend. I’m afraid I won’t be able to attend. Did you read it? Have you had a chance to read it? I am writing to ask whether you have had a chance to review it.

docs.title: Informal chat
docs.text:
A: Hey, did you finish the slides?

B: Not yet. I’ll send them later.

A: OK, thanks.

Per una chat tra colleghi può andare bene.

docs.title: Professional email tone
docs.text:
A: Hello,

I just wanted to check whether the slides are ready.

Could you please send them later today if possible?

Thanks in advance.

B: Of course. I’m still making a few changes, but I’ll send the final version this afternoon.

Qui il contenuto è simile, ma il tono è molto più adatto a un contesto professionale.

docs.title: Speaking drills
docs.text: Riformula oralmente in tre registri.

docs.title: Prompt 1
docs.text:
You need the new schedule.

  • informal:
  • neutral:
  • formal:

docs.title: Prompt 2
docs.text:
You want to move a meeting.

  • informal:
  • neutral:
  • formal:

docs.title: Prompt 3
docs.text:
You need more information about a course.

  • informal:
  • neutral:
  • formal:

docs.title: Prompt 4
docs.text:
You are following up because you have not received an answer.

  • neutral:
  • formal:

docs.title: Transformation lab
docs.text:
Trasforma le frasi in una versione più appropriata per un’email professionale.

  1. Hi, I want the details now.
  2. Can you send me the contract today?
  3. I need your answer fast.
  4. Did you read my last email?
  5. I can’t come. Change the meeting.

docs.title: Possible upgraded versions
docs.text:
1. Hello, I’d like to receive the details as soon as possible.
  2. Could you send me the contract today, please?
  3. I’d appreciate a prompt reply.
  4. I just wanted to check whether you had the chance to read my previous email.
  5. I’m afraid I won’t be able to attend. Would it be possible to reschedule the meeting?

docs.title: Task 1
docs.text: Scrivi un messaggio informale a un collega con cui hai molta confidenza per chiedere un file.

docs.title: Task 2
docs.text: Scrivi una email neutra-professionale a un collega per chiedere di spostare una riunione.

docs.title: Task 3
docs.text:
Scrivi una email più formale a un centro di formazione per chiedere informazioni su:

  • prezzo;
  • date;
  • livello richiesto;
  • modalità del corso.

Lunghezza consigliata:

  • Task 1: 40–60 parole
  • Task 2: 80–100 parole
  • Task 3: 120–160 parole

docs.title: Checklist di revisione
docs.text:
Controlla se il tuo testo:

  • ha un tono coerente con il contesto;
  • evita frasi troppo dirette;
  • usa forme di richiesta adeguate;
  • contiene opening e closing appropriati;
  • non suona né troppo brusco né eccessivamente rigido.

docs.title: Self-check finale
docs.text:
Sai:

  • distinguere informal, neutral e formal English?
  • mitigare una richiesta?
  • rendere più professionale una frase troppo diretta?
  • scegliere il tono giusto per email, chat e richieste delicate?
  • riscrivere lo stesso messaggio in registri diversi?

Se sì, stai usando l’inglese in modo molto più vicino a un vero livello B2.

docs.title: Obiettivo della lezione
docs.text:
In questa unità impari a:

  • raccontare un’esperienza in modo chiaro e ordinato;
  • usare collegamenti logici per non parlare “a pezzi”;
  • aggiungere dettagli utili e non solo frasi molto brevi;
  • esprimere un’opinione e sostenerla con una ragione;
  • parlare più a lungo con maggiore fluidità.

docs.title: Perché è importante
docs.text:
A livello B2 non basta dire singole frasi corrette. Devi anche saper:

  • costruire una storia;
  • mantenere il filo del discorso;
  • far capire perché qualcosa è importante;
  • collegare fatti, opinioni e conclusioni.

Confronta:

  • I went to a conference. It was good.
  • Last week I went to a conference in Milan, and it turned out to be much more useful than I expected because I met people from my field and got some practical ideas.

La seconda risposta è più ricca, più naturale e più vicina a un vero livello B2.

docs.title: Struttura base di uno storytelling
docs.text:
Una storia semplice ma efficace può seguire questo schema:

  1. setting – dove / quando / con chi;
  2. main event – cosa è successo;
  3. details – perché è stato interessante, difficile o sorprendente;
  4. result – come è finita;
  5. reflection – cosa hai pensato o imparato.

Schema utile:

Last weekend... / At the beginning... / Then... / In the end... / Looking back...

docs.title: Linking words essenziali
docs.text: Per evitare frasi isolate, usa parole di collegamento.

docs.title: Sequenza
docs.text:
• first
  • then
  • after that
  • later
  • in the end / finally

docs.title: Contrasto
docs.text:
• but
  • however
  • although

docs.title: Causa e risultato
docs.text:
• because
  • so
  • as a result

docs.title: Opinione e commento
docs.text:
• I think...
  • In my view...
  • What I found interesting was...

docs.title: Esempio guidato: raccontare un’esperienza
docs.text:
Last Saturday I met some friends from university. At first, we planned to have a quick coffee, but we ended up spending the whole afternoon together. First, we went to a small café near the station. Then we started talking about work and travel plans. After that, we decided to walk around the city centre. In the end, it was a very simple day, but I really enjoyed it because it helped me relax.

Perché funziona:

  • c’è un inizio chiaro;
  • gli eventi sono in ordine;
  • ci sono dettagli;
  • c’è una conclusione.

docs.title: Come aggiungere dettagli utili
docs.text:
Per parlare meglio, non dire solo what happened. Aggiungi:

  • where;
  • when;
  • who;
  • why;
  • how you felt.

Confronta:

  • I went to a restaurant.

  • I went to a new restaurant near my office with two colleagues after work.

  • It was good.

  • It was surprisingly good, especially because the atmosphere was relaxed and the service was excellent.

docs.title: Dare opinioni con una struttura chiara
docs.text:
Una struttura semplice ma efficace è:

I think... because... for example...

Esempio:

I think travelling is one of the best ways to learn because it puts you in real situations. For example, when I visited Spain, I had to speak English every day and that made me more confident.

Altre formule utili:

  • In my opinion...
  • I believe...
  • One reason is that...
  • For instance...
  • That’s why...

docs.title: Mini-modelli utili per parlare più a lungo
Model 1: event + detail + result
docs.text: I joined the course in January, and at first I wasn’t sure it would help. However, after a few weeks I realised it was very practical, so I kept attending regularly.

docs.title: Model 2: opinion + reason + example
docs.text: I think remote work can be very effective because it gives people more flexibility. For example, many employees can organise their time better and avoid wasting hours commuting.

docs.title: Model 3: expectation + reality
docs.text: I expected the meeting to be quite boring, but it was actually useful because the discussion was clear and well organised.

docs.title: Errore 1: frasi troppo corte e separate
docs.text:
❌ I went out. It was nice. I ate pizza. It was good.

✅ I went out with some friends and we had pizza in a small restaurant, which was nice because the atmosphere was very relaxed.

docs.title: Errore 2: usare sempre gli stessi linkers
docs.text:
Se usi solo and e because, il discorso suona piatto.

Prova anche:

  • however
  • so
  • in the end
  • actually
  • what happened next was...

docs.title: Errore 3: opinione senza supporto
docs.text:
❌ I think it is good.

✅ I think it is useful because it helps people communicate more efficiently.

docs.title: Speaking practice
docs.text:
Completa oralmente i modelli:

  1. Last weekend, I...
  2. At first, I thought..., but...
  3. What happened next was...
  4. In the end, I felt... because...
  5. In my opinion, ... because...

Poi prova a parlare per 60–90 secondi su uno di questi temi:

  • a memorable day;
  • a difficult situation;
  • a useful course or event;
  • a trip you enjoyed.

docs.title: Task 1
docs.text: Scrivi un breve racconto di 8–10 frasi su una giornata interessante.

docs.title: Task 2
docs.text:
Scrivi un breve paragrafo di opinione su questo tema:

Is travelling important for personal growth?

Usa:

  • una tesi chiara;
  • almeno una ragione;
  • un esempio.

docs.title: Mini task finale
docs.text:
Parla per 2–3 minuti su uno di questi argomenti:

  • tell a story about a good experience;
  • describe a problem and explain how it ended;
  • give your opinion on learning through experience.

Cerca di usare:

  • first / then / after that / finally
  • because / so / however
  • almeno un’opinione e un esempio

docs.title: Self-check
docs.text:
Sai:

  • raccontare una storia con un inizio, uno sviluppo e una conclusione?
  • usare linking words in modo più naturale?
  • aggiungere dettagli rilevanti?
  • esprimere un’opinione e sostenerla con una ragione?
  • parlare per più tempo senza perdere il filo?

Se sì, stai già lavorando in modo più vicino a un vero B2 spoken performance.

docs.title: Obiettivo della lezione
docs.text:
In questa unità porti il tuo speaking a un livello più maturo lavorando su:

  • story structure;
  • narrative pacing;
  • argument building;
  • uso strategico di linkers, comment clauses e stance markers;
  • gestione di esempi, precisazioni e conclusioni;
  • produzione orale più lunga, più densa e più convincente.

L’obiettivo non è solo parlare di più, ma parlare con architettura.

docs.title: Perché storytelling e argomentazione contano davvero
docs.text:
A livello B2, una buona performance orale non dipende solo dal vocabolario o dalla grammatica. Dipende molto anche da:

  • come organizzi le idee;
  • quanto bene segnali il passaggio da una fase all’altra;
  • come introduci dettagli, spiegazioni e conseguenze;
  • quanto riesci a sostenere un’opinione senza sembrare schematico.

Molti studenti riescono a dire frasi corrette, ma fanno fatica a:

  • costruire una narrativa coerente;
  • evitare salti logici;
  • passare dai fatti all’interpretazione;
  • sostenere una posizione con esempi convincenti.

È qui che lo speaking comincia a sembrare davvero B2.

docs.title: La struttura di una buona storia orale
docs.text:
Un racconto efficace di solito contiene cinque elementi:

  1. Context – situazione iniziale;
  2. Trigger / turning point – cosa fa partire la storia;
  3. Development – cosa succede dopo;
  4. Outcome – come si conclude;
  5. Reflection – cosa significa per te o cosa hai imparato.

docs.title: Formula utile
docs.text:
What happened was that...

At first...

Then things changed when...

In the end...

Looking back, I think...

Questa struttura ti permette di evitare liste di eventi senza direzione.

docs.title: Narrative progression: come far avanzare il discorso
docs.text: Una storia forte non è solo una sequenza di fatti. Deve anche dare al listener la sensazione che il racconto stia andando da qualche parte.

docs.title: Sequencing
docs.text:
• at first
  • to begin with
  • then
  • shortly afterwards
  • meanwhile
  • eventually
  • in the end

docs.title: Turning points
docs.text:
• the problem started when...
  • that was the moment when...
  • what changed everything was...
  • from that point on...

docs.title: Reflection
docs.text:
• what I realised was that...
  • looking back, ...
  • in hindsight, ...
  • the main lesson for me was...

docs.title: Densità informativa: dalla frase scolastica alla frase naturale
docs.text:
Confronta:

I went to an event. It was interesting. I met people. It was useful.

Versione più matura:

I went to a professional event last month, and it turned out to be much more useful than I had expected because I met several people working in my field and ended up having two really valuable conversations.

Perché funziona meglio:

  • integra più informazioni in una sola unità;
  • segnala relazione tra i fatti;
  • introduce valutazione personale;
  • suona meno scolastica e più naturale.

docs.title: Argomentare in modo credibile
docs.text:
Una buona argomentazione orale di livello B2 spesso segue questo schema:

  1. claim – la tua tesi;
  2. reason – perché la sostieni;
  3. example – un caso concreto;
  4. comment / implication – perché quell’esempio conta.

docs.title: Modello base
docs.text:
I believe that...

The main reason is that...

For example...

This shows that...

docs.title: Esempio
docs.text: I believe that working abroad can be extremely valuable. The main reason is that it pushes people to adapt quickly and communicate more effectively. For example, when a friend of mine moved to the Netherlands, she improved not only her English but also her problem-solving skills. This shows that international experience often has a wider impact than people expect.

docs.title: Stance markers e hedging
docs.text: Per parlare in modo maturo non devi sempre fare affermazioni assolute. Spesso è meglio modulare ciò che dici.

docs.title: Stance markers
docs.text:
• I think...
  • I believe...
  • Personally, ...
  • From my point of view, ...
  • As far as I’m concerned, ...

docs.title: Hedging
docs.text:
• to some extent
  • in many cases
  • generally speaking
  • it seems to me that...
  • I wouldn’t say that..., but...
  • that depends on the situation

Questo ti aiuta a sembrare più preciso e meno rigido.

docs.title: Costruire discorsi più lunghi senza perdersi
docs.text: Quando devi parlare per 2–4 minuti, usa dei blocchi mentali chiari.

docs.title: Formula 1: story block
docs.text:
• where / when
  • what happened
  • why it mattered
  • how it ended
  • what you learned

docs.title: Formula 2: opinion block
docs.text:
• opinion
  • reason 1
  • example
  • reason 2
  • short conclusion

docs.title: Formula 3: compare and evaluate
docs.text:
• introduce two options
  • say which is better and why
  • mention limits / exceptions
  • conclude

docs.title: Example answer: storytelling
docs.text: A few months ago, I attended a workshop that I originally thought would be quite boring. At first, I only went because a colleague recommended it, and I didn’t really expect much from it. However, once the session started, I realised it was much more practical than I had imagined. The speaker used real examples, and that made the content easier to follow. What made the biggest difference, though, was the discussion part, because participants were encouraged to share their own experiences. In the end, I came away with a few useful ideas that I later applied to my own work. Looking back, I’d say the main lesson was that even events that seem unpromising can turn out to be surprisingly valuable.

docs.title: Example answer: argument
docs.text: Personally, I think travelling is one of the most effective ways to learn. The main reason is that it exposes people to real communication rather than controlled classroom situations. For instance, when you are in another country, you often have to solve small problems, ask questions, and adapt quickly to unfamiliar situations. That kind of experience tends to build confidence very fast. That said, I wouldn’t claim that travelling is the only way to learn, because a lot also depends on motivation and attitude. Still, in many cases, it gives learners a kind of practical pressure that can be extremely useful.

docs.title: Common speaking problems for Italian speakers
1. Listing instead of structuring
docs.text:
❌ idea 1 + idea 2 + idea 3 without hierarchy

Meglio:

✅ signal the main point, then support it.

docs.title: 2. Overusing “and”
docs.text:
❌ I went there and it was nice and I met people and it was useful and I liked it.

Meglio usare varietà:

  • however
  • as a result
  • in the end
  • what I found most interesting was...
  • the reason why...

docs.title: 3. Opinion without evidence
docs.text:
❌ I think it is important.

✅ I think it is important because..., for example..., so...

docs.title: 4. Finishing too early
docs.text:
Molti studenti danno una risposta corretta ma troppo breve.

Per allungarla in modo naturale, aggiungi:

  • a concrete example;
  • a contrast;
  • a consequence;
  • a personal reflection.

docs.title: Fluency extensions: formule molto utili
docs.text:
• What made it interesting was...
  • The thing I remember most clearly is...
  • What happened next was that...
  • The main reason I say that is...
  • That’s one of the reasons why...
  • If I had to sum it up, I’d say...
  • What I learned from that experience was...

docs.title: Drill 1: expand the sentence
docs.text:
Espandi oralmente:

  1. I went to an event.
  2. It was useful.
  3. I think online learning is effective.
  4. The experience changed my mind.

docs.title: Drill 2: story skeleton
docs.text:
Parla seguendo questa traccia:

  • where were you?
  • why were you there?
  • what happened?
  • what changed?
  • how did it end?
  • what did you learn?

docs.title: Drill 3: opinion with support
docs.text:
Rispondi per 90–120 secondi:

  1. Do people learn more from books or from experience?
  2. Is remote work better than office work?
  3. Are short courses really useful?

Obbligo:

  • one clear claim;
  • at least two supporting reasons;
  • one example;
  • one balanced remark.

docs.title: Transformation lab
docs.text:
Rendi queste risposte più mature e strutturate.

  1. I went to a meeting. It was good.
  2. I think travel is important because yes.
  3. It was difficult but good.
  4. I learned a lot.

docs.title: Possibili upgrade
docs.text:
1. I went to a meeting last week, and it turned out to be more useful than I expected because the discussion was focused and practical.
  2. I think travel is important because it forces people to deal with real situations and become more independent.
  3. Although it was quite challenging at first, it ended up being a positive experience overall.
  4. I learned a lot, especially about how important preparation and communication are in unfamiliar situations.

docs.title: Task 1 – narrative paragraph
docs.text:
Scrivi un paragrafo di 140–170 parole su un’esperienza che ti ha insegnato qualcosa.

Deve includere:

  • contesto;
  • evento principale;
  • difficoltà o sorpresa;
  • risultato;
  • riflessione finale.

docs.title: Task 2 – opinion paragraph
docs.text:
Scrivi un testo di 140–180 parole su questo tema:

Do people develop more through challenging experiences than through routine?

Deve includere:

  • una posizione chiara;
  • almeno due ragioni;
  • un esempio;
  • una mini-conclusione.

docs.title: Checklist di revisione
docs.text:
Controlla se la tua produzione orale o scritta:

  • ha una struttura visibile;
  • usa linkers vari e non solo and;
  • distingue fatti, opinioni e conseguenze;
  • contiene almeno un esempio concreto;
  • si chiude con una conclusione o una riflessione;
  • suona naturale e non frammentata.

docs.title: Self-check finale
docs.text:
Sai:

  • raccontare un’esperienza con una struttura chiara?
  • sostenere un’opinione con ragioni ed esempi?
  • usare stance markers e hedging in modo naturale?
  • rendere una risposta più lunga senza riempirla di ripetizioni?
  • passare da speaking “scolastico” a speaking più convincente?

Se sì, stai costruendo una performance orale molto più vicina a un vero B2 high-performance speaking.

docs.title: Obiettivo della lezione
docs.text:
In questa unità finale ripassi e integri i contenuti principali del livello B2. L’obiettivo non è studiare una regola nuova, ma dimostrare che sai usare l’inglese in modo più autonomo, fluido e strutturato.

Alla fine della lezione dovresti riuscire a:

  • sostenere una conversazione più lunga senza fermarti continuamente;
  • esprimere opinioni con ragioni ed esempi;
  • raccontare esperienze e storie in modo ordinato;
  • scrivere un testo chiaro con introduzione, sviluppo e conclusione;
  • adattare tono e registro in base alla situazione.

docs.title: Cosa hai consolidato nel livello B2
docs.text:
Nel blocco B2 hai lavorato su:

  • speaking più fluido e strategico;
  • frasi complesse e connettori;
  • collocations e lessico più naturale;
  • ascolto strategico e paraphrasing;
  • discussion skills;
  • writing più strutturato;
  • relative clauses;
  • nuance e tone;
  • storytelling e argomentazione.

In questa unità li usi insieme, non separatamente.

docs.title: Ripasso rapido: cosa deve saper fare uno studente B2
docs.text:
Uno studente B2 non parla perfettamente, ma sa:

  • spiegare idee con una certa precisione;
  • mantenere una conversazione su temi familiari e semi-astratti;
  • dare opinioni motivate;
  • collegare idee in modo chiaro;
  • capire il senso principale di contenuti autentici non troppo specialistici;
  • produrre testi abbastanza ordinati e leggibili.

docs.title: Review Check 1 – Grammar and Structure
docs.text:
Completa le frasi.

  1. I have never ___ such an interesting talk before.
  2. If I ___ more time, I would definitely read more in English.
  3. This is the colleague ___ helped me with the project.
  4. I was tired; ___, I decided to finish the task.
  5. I’m not sure I agree ___ that point.
  6. We need to come ___ with a better solution.
  7. The meeting was useful because it gave us a clearer ___ of the problem.
  8. I usually write in a neutral tone, ___ I’m talking to close friends.
  9. What I found most useful was the way she ___ the idea.
  10. In the end, the experience turned ___ to be very positive.

docs.title: Review Check 2 – Sentence Upgrade
docs.text:
Rendi le frasi più naturali e più da livello B2.

  1. I went to a course. It was useful.
  2. I think online learning is good.
  3. I met a person. She works in marketing.
  4. The email was direct. It sounded rude.
  5. The meeting was long. It was helpful.

Suggerimento: usa almeno uno tra questi elementi:

  • relative clause
  • linker
  • reason
  • example
  • contrast

docs.title: Speaking Review – Task 1
docs.text:
Parla per 2–3 minuti su questo tema:

Describe a useful experience that changed the way you study, work or communicate.

Nel tuo intervento prova a includere:

  • contesto;
  • evento principale;
  • perché è stato importante;
  • cosa hai imparato;
  • una riflessione finale.

Frasi utili:

  • What made the experience useful was...
  • At first, I thought... but then...
  • Looking back, I’d say...

docs.title: Speaking Review – Task 2
docs.text:
Parla per 2–3 minuti su questo tema:

Do you think technology has improved communication?

Nel tuo intervento cerca di usare:

  • un’opinione chiara;
  • almeno due ragioni;
  • un esempio concreto;
  • una piccola concessione o limitazione.

Struttura utile:

I think... The main reason is that... For example... That said... Overall...

docs.title: Writing Review
docs.text: Scrivi un testo di 140–170 parole su uno di questi argomenti:

docs.title: Opzione A
docs.text: Describe a learning experience that was more useful than you expected.

docs.title: Opzione B
docs.text:
Write an opinion paragraph about whether online communication has made relationships better or worse.

Il testo dovrebbe includere:

  • una buona apertura;
  • sviluppo con dettagli o esempi;
  • almeno due connettori;
  • una conclusione chiara.

docs.title: Listening & Paraphrasing Check
docs.text:
Leggi queste frasi e riformulale con parole diverse mantenendo lo stesso significato.

  1. The presentation was surprisingly clear.
  2. I didn’t fully agree with the proposal.
  3. The course helped me become more confident.
  4. The message sounded too direct.
  5. We eventually found a practical solution.

Esempio:

  • The presentation was surprisingly clear.
  • The talk was much easier to follow than I expected.

docs.title: Pronunciation Check
docs.text:
Ripeti lentamente e poi in modo più fluido:

  • What made it useful was the practical part.
  • I didn’t completely agree with that argument.
  • The person who led the meeting was very clear.
  • In the end, it turned out better than expected.

Concentrati su:

  • ritmo della frase;
  • parole chiave accentate;
  • collegamento naturale tra parole frequenti.

docs.title: 1. Frasi corrette ma troppo semplici
docs.text:
❌ It was good. It was interesting. It was useful.

✅ It was more useful than I expected because it gave me practical ideas I could apply immediately.

docs.title: 2. Opinione senza supporto
docs.text:
❌ I think it is important.

✅ I think it is important because it improves communication and makes collaboration easier.

docs.title: 3. Linkers usati male o troppo poco
docs.text:
❌ frasi separate senza collegamento

✅ usa however, although, as a result, in the end, for example

docs.title: 4. Tono inadatto
docs.text:
❌ email troppo diretta in contesto professionale

✅ scegli un tono più neutro e collaborativo

docs.title: Self-check finale
docs.text:
Chiediti:

  • Riesco a parlare per alcuni minuti senza fermarmi dopo ogni frase?
  • Riesco a dare un’opinione e sostenerla con ragioni ed esempi?
  • Riesco a usare un tono abbastanza appropriato?
  • Riesco a scrivere un testo strutturato e comprensibile?
  • Riesco a riformulare un’idea in modo più naturale?

Se la risposta è spesso sì, il tuo B2 è in consolidamento reale.

docs.title: Task finale
docs.text:
Scegli un tema tra questi:

  • a useful professional experience;
  • the pros and cons of remote work;
  • a course, event or workshop you found valuable;
  • how communication changes across different contexts.

docs.title: Fai due cose:
docs.text:
1. Parla per 3–4 minuti.
  2. Scrivi un testo di 140–170 parole sullo stesso tema.

Questo ti permette di verificare se sai gestire lo stesso contenuto sia nello speaking sia nel writing.

docs.title: Conclusione
docs.text:
Arrivare a B2 significa non solo conoscere l’inglese, ma iniziare a usarlo con maggiore autonomia.

Non vuol dire essere perfetti. Vuol dire riuscire a:

  • spiegarti bene;
  • reagire in conversazione;
  • sostenere un’opinione;
  • gestire testi e messaggi con più consapevolezza.

Questo è il punto in cui l’inglese smette di essere solo “materia di studio” e diventa davvero uno strumento.

docs.title: Obiettivo della lezione
docs.text:
Questa unità finale serve a verificare se il tuo B2 è davvero operativo. Non si tratta di ricordare singole regole, ma di dimostrare che sai integrare:

  • fluency management;
  • complex sentence control;
  • collocational accuracy;
  • paraphrasing ability;
  • discussion management;
  • register awareness;
  • structured speaking and writing.

A questo livello, la domanda non è più solo “Conosco questa struttura?”, ma soprattutto:

So usare l’inglese in modo sufficientemente naturale, chiaro e flessibile in compiti reali?

docs.title: Profilo di uscita B2: che cosa significa davvero
docs.text:
Un utente B2 sa generalmente:

  • partecipare a conversazioni relativamente lunghe con un certo controllo;
  • sostenere un’opinione articolata con ragioni, esempi e concessioni;
  • capire il punto principale e molti dettagli rilevanti di contenuti autentici non eccessivamente specialistici;
  • adattare il tono in contesti informali, neutrali e professionali di base;
  • produrre testi coerenti con una struttura riconoscibile;
  • riformulare quando non trova subito la parola perfetta.

Non è un livello di perfezione. È un livello di indipendenza comunicativa solida.

docs.title: Mastery Check 1 – Integrated Grammar & Lexis
docs.text:
Completa le frasi in modo accurato e naturale.

  1. I wish I ___ more time to read in English during the week.
  2. The workshop, ___ was led by an experienced trainer, turned out to be extremely practical.
  3. I don’t completely agree ___ the idea that remote work is always more productive.
  4. The main reason ___ I found the event useful was the networking part.
  5. We need to come ___ with a more realistic plan before Friday.
  6. Although the message was grammatically correct, the tone came across ___ too direct.
  7. I had never dealt ___ that kind of situation before, so I had to improvise.
  8. What made the presentation effective was the way the speaker ___ down a complex topic.
  9. If the company offered more flexibility, employees might feel more ___ to stay.
  10. Looking back, I would say the experience had a bigger impact ___ I expected.
  11. The colleague ___ desk is next to mine works on international projects.
  12. In many cases, people improve faster when they are exposed ___ real communication pressure.

docs.title: Mastery Check 2 – Sentence Transformation and Upgrade
docs.text:
Trasforma le frasi in una versione più densa, naturale e da B2.

  1. I attended a meeting. It was useful.
  2. I disagree. The idea is not practical.
  3. I met a woman. She runs a start-up.
  4. The email was polite. It was still too vague.
  5. The event was interesting. I learned a lot.
  6. We solved the problem. It took longer than expected.

Obiettivo: in ogni risposta prova a inserire almeno due di questi elementi:

  • relative clause
  • concession / contrast
  • reason + consequence
  • collocation naturale
  • stance marker
  • precise qualifier

Esempio:

  • I attended a meeting. It was useful.
  • I attended a meeting that turned out to be far more useful than I had expected, mainly because it gave us a clearer framework for the next stage of the project.

docs.title: Speaking Performance Task 1 – Extended Storytelling
docs.text:
Parla per 3–4 minuti sul tema:

Describe a professional, academic or personal experience that changed the way you think or work.

Il tuo intervento dovrebbe includere:

  • context;
  • turning point;
  • development;
  • outcome;
  • reflection.

docs.title: Linguaggio utile
docs.text:
• At first, I assumed...
  • What changed my perspective was...
  • One thing that stood out was...
  • Looking back, I would say...
  • The main lesson I took from it was...

docs.title: Obiettivo nascosto del task
docs.text:
Non limitarti a raccontare i fatti. Devi anche mostrare:

  • selezione dei dettagli rilevanti;
  • controllo della progressione narrativa;
  • capacità di commentare l’esperienza;
  • uso di valutazioni personali credibili.

docs.title: Speaking Performance Task 2 – Discussion & Argument
docs.text:
Parla per 3–4 minuti sul tema:

Has technology improved communication overall, or has it made it more superficial?

Nel tuo intervento cerca di includere:

  • una posizione chiara;
  • almeno due ragioni;
  • un esempio concreto;
  • una concessione;
  • una conclusione equilibrata.

docs.title: Frame utile
docs.text:
Personally, I would argue that...

The main reason is that...

For instance...

That said, I can also see why...

Overall, I would still maintain that...

docs.title: Cosa stiamo verificando qui
docs.text:
• stance management;
  • hedging;
  • uso di esempi;
  • gestione del contrasto;
  • capacità di concludere senza semplificare troppo.

docs.title: Writing Performance Task
docs.text: Scrivi un testo di 170–220 parole su uno dei seguenti temi:

docs.title: Opzione A – Reflective piece
docs.text: Describe an experience that improved one of your communication skills, and explain why it had such an impact on you.

docs.title: Opzione B – Opinion piece
docs.text: Do you think digital communication has improved professional relationships? Discuss advantages and limitations.

docs.title: Opzione C – Semi-formal response
docs.text: Write a message or short email to a colleague or course organiser after attending a useful event, explaining what you found valuable and suggesting a follow-up action.

docs.title: Criteri di qualità
docs.text:
Il testo dovrebbe mostrare:

  • apertura chiara;
  • sviluppo coerente;
  • paragrafi o sezioni riconoscibili;
  • uso di connettori variati;
  • lessico relativamente naturale;
  • conclusione adeguata al compito.

docs.title: Paraphrasing Lab
docs.text:
Riformula ogni frase in almeno due modi diversi.

  1. The presentation was surprisingly clear.
  2. I wasn’t fully convinced by the proposal.
  3. The course helped me become more confident.
  4. The message sounded too direct.
  5. We eventually found a workable solution.
  6. The discussion became more productive after that point.

docs.title: Strategie da usare
docs.text:
• sinonimo;
  • definizione più semplice;
  • cambio di struttura;
  • spostamento del focus;
  • riformulazione valutativa.

Esempio:

  • I wasn’t fully convinced by the proposal.
  • I had some doubts about the proposal.
  • The proposal didn’t strike me as entirely convincing.

A livello B2, la capacità di riformulare è fondamentale perché ti permette di continuare a comunicare anche quando non trovi subito la formulazione perfetta.

docs.title: Register & Tone Check
docs.text:
Riscrivi le frasi adattandole a un contesto più appropriato.

  1. Send me the file today.
  2. I don’t agree with your idea.
  3. Your explanation was confusing.
  4. We need a better plan.
  5. I can’t join the meeting.

Prova a produrre:

  • una versione informal;
  • una versione neutral professional;
  • una versione more tactful / diplomatic.

Questo esercizio serve a verificare se il tuo B2 è solo grammaticale o anche pragmatico.

docs.title: Pronunciation & Delivery Check
docs.text:
Ripeti le frasi prima lentamente e poi in modo più naturale, cercando di mantenere chunk e ritmo.

  • What made the session valuable was the practical feedback we received.
  • I wouldn’t say I completely disagree, but I do have some reservations.
  • The colleague whose presentation we saw explained the issue very clearly.
  • In the end, the experience turned out to be much more useful than expected.
  • That said, I can understand why some people might see it differently.

Concentrati su:

  • thought groups;
  • parole chiave accentate;
  • riduzione di elementi meno importanti;
  • continuità del flusso.

docs.title: Error Clinic – Problemi tipici da fine B2
1. Accurate but flat English
docs.text:
❌ corretto ma piatto:

It was good and useful.

✅ più naturale:

It turned out to be far more useful than I had expected, especially because the discussion was practical and well structured.

docs.title: 2. Over-direct professional tone
docs.text:
❌ Send me the report by 5.

✅ Could you send me the report by 5, if possible?

docs.title: 3. Opinion without support or nuance
docs.text:
❌ Technology is good.

✅ Overall, I think technology has improved communication in many contexts, although it has also made some interactions more superficial.

docs.title: 4. Good grammar, weak organisation
docs.text:
Molti studenti costruiscono frasi corrette ma non organizzano bene il discorso.

Verifica sempre:

  • ho introdotto l’idea principale?
  • ho dato una ragione?
  • ho aggiunto un esempio?
  • ho fatto una mini-conclusione?

docs.title: 5. Limited lexical range under pressure
docs.text:
Quando lo speaking si allunga, molti studenti tornano a parole molto generiche.

Allenati a sostituire:

  • good → effective / useful / convincing / valuable
  • bad → ineffective / unclear / unhelpful / weak
  • important → significant / relevant / essential
  • interesting → engaging / insightful / thought-provoking

docs.title: Final Integrated Task
docs.text:
Scegli uno di questi temi:

  • a communication challenge you faced;
  • a useful event, workshop or course;
  • whether remote work improves productivity;
  • how tone affects professional relationships.

docs.title: Step 1 – Speaking
docs.text:
Parla per 4–5 minuti.

Devi includere:

  • introduzione chiara;
  • sviluppo strutturato;
  • almeno un esempio;
  • un contrasto o una concessione;
  • una conclusione.

docs.title: Step 2 – Writing
docs.text: Scrivi un testo di 180–220 parole sullo stesso tema.

docs.title: Step 3 – Reflection
docs.text:
Rispondi per iscritto a queste domande:

  • Dove il mio inglese suona naturale?
  • Dove diventa troppo semplice?
  • Riesco a sostenere un’opinione o mi fermo presto?
  • Riesco ad adattare il tono alla situazione?
  • Quando non trovo una parola, riesco a riformulare?

docs.title: Autovalutazione finale B2
docs.text:
Se riesci con una certa regolarità a:

  • parlare per alcuni minuti con struttura chiara;
  • sostenere opinioni con ragioni ed esempi;
  • raccontare esperienze con progressione logica;
  • scrivere testi leggibili, coerenti e relativamente naturali;
  • gestire tono, lessico e riformulazione in modo abbastanza flessibile;

allora il tuo B2 è credibile e utilizzabile in molti contesti reali.

docs.title: Conclusione
docs.text:
Arrivare a B2 significa aver superato la fase in cui l’inglese serve solo per esercizi controllati.

A questo punto l’inglese diventa uno strumento per:

  • lavorare;
  • collaborare;
  • discutere;
  • partecipare;
  • spiegare e convincere.

Non vuol dire sapere tutto. Vuol dire saper funzionare bene.

Ed è esattamente questo che un buon B2 dovrebbe permetterti di fare.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità saprai:

  • usare i tempi verbali con maggiore precisione, non solo “in modo corretto”
  • capire la differenza tra tempo grammaticale e significato comunicativo
  • scegliere tra forme simili in base a durata, risultato, contesto e prospettiva
  • parlare e scrivere con più controllo nelle narrazioni, nelle descrizioni e nelle spiegazioni
  • evitare alcuni errori avanzati molto comuni anche a livelli alti

docs.title: 🧠 Il salto da B2 a C1
docs.text:
A livello B2, spesso la domanda è:

“Questa frase è giusta?”

A livello C1, la domanda diventa:

“Questa è la scelta più precisa, naturale e adatta al contesto?”

Questa differenza è fondamentale.

docs.title: Confronto semplice
docs.text:
• I lived in London for two years.
  • I have lived in London for two years.

Le due frasi non dicono la stessa cosa.

  • I lived in London for two years. → esperienza conclusa
  • I have lived in London for two years. → esperienza collegata al presente, forse ancora in corso

A livello avanzato, scegliere il tempo giusto significa scegliere la prospettiva giusta.

docs.title: ⚖️ Present Perfect vs Past Simple
docs.text: Questa è una delle differenze più importanti dell’inglese avanzato.

docs.title: Past Simple
docs.text:
Usiamo il Past Simple quando l’evento è visto come:

  • concluso
  • collocato in un tempo finito
  • separato dal presente

Esempi:

  • I worked there in 2019.
  • She moved to Madrid last year.
  • We met at university.

docs.title: Present Perfect
docs.text:
Usiamo il Present Perfect quando il fatto è collegato al presente:

  • esperienza di vita
  • risultato presente
  • cambiamento nel tempo
  • periodo non finito

Esempi:

  • I have worked with international clients.
  • She has moved to a bigger apartment.
  • We have already discussed this issue.

docs.title: Confronto utile
docs.text: Frase Significato principale I saw that film last week. momento specifico nel passato I have seen that film. esperienza, rilevanza presente She lived in Rome for ten years. periodo finito She has lived in Rome for ten years. collegamento al presente

docs.title: 🔄 Present Perfect Simple vs Present Perfect Continuous
docs.text: Entrambe le forme possono parlare del passato collegato al presente, ma con focus diverso.

docs.title: Present Perfect Simple
docs.text:
Focus su:

  • risultato
  • completamento
  • quantità ottenuta

Esempi:

  • I have written three reports today.
  • She has finished the presentation.
  • We have completed the first phase.

docs.title: Present Perfect Continuous
docs.text:
Focus su:

  • durata
  • processo
  • attività in corso o appena conclusa

Esempi:

  • I have been writing reports all morning.
  • She has been working on the presentation since 8 a.m.
  • We have been trying to solve the problem for hours.

docs.title: Confronto
docs.text: Forma Focus I have written three emails. risultato / quantità I have been writing emails all morning. processo / durata

docs.title: 🕰️ Past Simple vs Past Continuous
docs.text:
Il Past Continuous non serve solo per indicare un’azione “in corso”.

Serve spesso per:

  • creare lo sfondo di una scena
  • descrivere il contesto
  • mostrare durata o processo in un momento del passato

Esempio:

  • I was walking home when I saw Laura.

Qui:

  • was walking = contesto / background
  • saw = evento principale

docs.title: Altri esempi
docs.text:
• While I was working, my phone rang.
  • They were having dinner when the lights went out.
  • I was thinking about the problem when I found the answer.

docs.title: 🔮 Future forms and nuance
docs.text: A livelli alti, il futuro non si sceglie “a memoria”, ma in base all’intenzione.

docs.title: Will
docs.text:
Uso frequente per:

  • decisione presa nel momento in cui si parla
  • promessa
  • offerta
  • previsione personale

Esempi:

  • I’ll call you later.
  • Don’t worry, I’ll help you.
  • I think they’ll accept the proposal.

docs.title: Be going to
docs.text:
Uso frequente per:

  • intenzione già presente
  • previsione basata su evidenze

Esempi:

  • I’m going to apply for that job.
  • Look at those clouds. It’s going to rain.

docs.title: Present Continuous
docs.text:
Uso frequente per:

  • piano già organizzato
  • appuntamento fissato

Esempi:

  • I’m meeting the client tomorrow at 10.
  • We’re flying to Berlin on Friday.

docs.title: 🧩 Time perspective: la domanda giusta
docs.text:
Quando scegli un tempo verbale, fatti queste domande:

  • il fatto è finito o ha ancora effetto sul presente?
  • voglio enfatizzare il risultato o il processo?
  • sto raccontando un evento principale o descrivendo lo sfondo?
  • il piano è spontaneo, intenzionale o già organizzato?

Queste domande ti aiutano a scegliere il tempo giusto in modo molto più affidabile delle regole imparate a memoria.

docs.title: 📘 Reading
docs.text:
Leggi il testo e osserva i tempi:

I have been working in digital marketing for six years, and I’ve recently started managing international projects. Before that, I worked for a smaller company where I learned most of the basics. At the moment, I’m leading a team, and next month I’m meeting a new client in Amsterdam.

docs.title: Che cosa noti?
docs.text:
• have been working → durata collegata al presente
  • have recently started → cambiamento recente con rilevanza attuale
  • worked → esperienza conclusa
  • am leading → situazione attuale
  • am meeting → futuro organizzato

docs.title: 🗣️ Speaking
docs.text:
Rispondi oralmente usando almeno tre tempi diversi:

  1. How has your work or study life changed in recent years?
  2. What were you doing a few years ago?
  3. What are you working on at the moment?
  4. What are you going to do next?

docs.title: Modello utile
docs.text: I have been studying English seriously for the last two years. Before that, I only used it occasionally at work. Right now, I’m focusing on speaking, and next month I’m going to start a new project in English.

docs.title: ✍️ Writing
docs.text:
Scrivi un testo di 10–12 frasi sul tuo percorso personale o professionale.

Includi:

  • una situazione passata conclusa
  • un’esperienza collegata al presente
  • un’attività attuale
  • un piano futuro

docs.title: ⚠️ Errori comuni
docs.text: Errore Corretto I have worked yesterday. I worked yesterday. I am living here since 2022. I have been living here since 2022. I have been written three emails. I have written three emails. I’m going to call him at 6, it’s already fixed. I’m calling him at 6.

docs.title: ✅ Review & Practice
docs.text:
Completa con la forma corretta del verbo tra parentesi.

  1. I ___ here since 2021. (work)
  2. We ___ the report yesterday. (finish)
  3. She ___ all morning. (study)
  4. I think they ___ the idea. (like)
  5. We ___ the manager tomorrow at 9. (meet)

docs.title: 🔄 Trasforma
docs.text:
Riscrivi le frasi in modo più preciso.

  1. I live here since 2020. → ______
  2. I worked here for three years and I still work here. → ______
  3. I will meet the client tomorrow at 10. (appuntamento già fissato) → ______
  4. I wrote emails all morning. (focus sul processo) → ______

docs.title: 🚀 Mini task
docs.text:
Parla per 3–4 minuti del tuo percorso usando:

  • Past Simple
  • Present Perfect / Present Perfect Continuous
  • Present Continuous
  • una forma di futuro

docs.title: 🎯 Insight finale
docs.text:
A1–B2: impari i tempi verbali.

C1: impari a scegliere il tempo giusto per dire esattamente ciò che intendi.

At advanced level, grammar is not only about correctness. It is about control, precision and perspective.

docs.title: 🎯 Lesson Goals
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • usare i tempi verbali come scelte di significato, non come semplici etichette grammaticali
  • distinguere con maggiore precisione tra completed event, current relevance, ongoing process, background scene, immediate decision, prior intention e scheduled arrangement
  • riconoscere quando due forme sono entrambe grammaticalmente possibili ma non equivalenti in termini di focus, perspective, stance e discourse effect
  • controllare meglio il passaggio tra tempi diversi all’interno dello stesso testo o dello stesso turno di parola
  • evitare errori avanzati tipici degli italofoni, soprattutto quelli legati a trasferimenti dal passato prossimo e dall’imperfetto italiani
  • produrre speaking e writing più credibili a livello C1, con maggiore finezza nella gestione del tempo e dell’aspetto

Questa unità non serve a “ripassare i tempi”. Serve a fare il salto verso una grammatica più matura: grammar as meaning management.

docs.title: 🧠 From correct to precise
docs.text:
A livello intermedio spesso basta sapere che una frase è corretta.

A livello avanzato, invece, devi chiederti:

  • What exactly am I highlighting?
  • Am I focusing on the event, the result, the duration, or the surrounding context?
  • Is the time frame closed or still open?
  • Does the action matter because it happened, because it is still relevant, or because it has been developing over time?

Questo è il vero cuore della competenza C1.

docs.title: Compare
docs.text:
• I lived in London for two years.
  • I have lived in London for two years.
  • I have been living in London for two years.

Le tre frasi possono sembrare molto simili a uno studente meno avanzato, ma in realtà cambiano:

  • la relazione con il presente
  • il focus sullo stato vs sul processo
  • la prospettiva del parlante

docs.title: Quick interpretation
docs.text:
• I lived in London for two years. → esperienza conclusa, cornice chiusa
  • I have lived in London for two years. → durata fino al presente, focus più neutro sul fatto
  • I have been living in London for two years. → durata/processo, maggiore enfasi sull’ongoing situation

docs.title: ⚖️ Present Perfect vs Past Simple: not just a rule, but a perspective
docs.text:
Molti studenti conoscono la regola scolastica:

  • Past Simple = passato finito
  • Present Perfect = collegamento al presente

È vero, ma a livello C1 bisogna andare oltre.

docs.title: 1. Closed frame vs open relevance
docs.text:
• I worked there in 2019.
  • I have worked in similar environments before.

Nel primo caso il riferimento temporale è chiuso. Nel secondo caso il tempo non è importante in sé: importa l’esperienza e la sua rilevanza attuale.

docs.title: 2. Experience vs specific event
docs.text:
• Have you ever worked abroad?
  • When did you work abroad?

La prima domanda apre lo spazio dell’esperienza. La seconda richiede di passare a un evento preciso.

docs.title: 3. Result now vs event then
docs.text:
• She has broken her glasses. → il risultato conta ora
  • She broke her glasses last night. → l’evento è collocato nel passato

docs.title: 4. Change over time
docs.text:
• My English has improved a lot.
  • My English improved a lot during that course.

Nel primo caso l’attenzione è sul risultato attuale. Nel secondo, sul cambiamento dentro una cornice passata e conclusa.

docs.title: Frequent Italian interference
docs.text:
L’italiano spesso permette il passato prossimo dove l’inglese richiede il past simple.

❌ I have finished the report yesterday. ✅ I finished the report yesterday.

Il problema non è grammaticale in astratto: è che yesterday chiude la cornice temporale.

docs.title: 🔄 Present Perfect Simple vs Present Perfect Continuous
docs.text: Questa è una delle aree più ricche per il livello C1, perché non si tratta di “tempo diverso”, ma di aspetto diverso.

docs.title: Present Perfect Simple: completion, result, quantity
docs.text:
• I have written six emails this morning.
  • We have completed the first draft.
  • She has read all the documents.

Qui il focus cade su:

  • what has been achieved
  • how much has been done
  • the completed outcome

docs.title: Present Perfect Continuous: duration, process, activity in progress or recently active
docs.text:
• I have been writing emails all morning.
  • We have been working on the first draft for weeks.
  • She has been reading about the issue recently.

Qui il focus cade su:

  • ongoing process
  • temporal extension
  • repeated or sustained activity

docs.title: Compare carefully
docs.text:
• I have written three pages. → risultato quantificabile

  • I have been writing for three hours. → durata

  • She has taught at this school for ten years. → fatto stabile / experience frame

  • She has been teaching all afternoon. → attività in corso o appena conclusa

docs.title: When both are possible
docs.text:
A volte entrambe le forme sono corrette, ma non dicono esattamente la stessa cosa.

  • I have worked on this project for months.
  • I have been working on this project for months.

La prima può suonare leggermente più orientata al fatto generale. La seconda mette più luce sul processo e sul fatto che l’impegno si è protratto nel tempo.

docs.title: 🕰️ Past Simple vs Past Continuous: event structure
docs.text:
Molti studenti pensano che il Past Continuous serva solo per “un’azione lunga interrotta da una corta”. È una semplificazione utile all’inizio, ma insufficiente per C1.

Il Past Continuous serve anche a:

  • costruire il background di una scena
  • mostrare developing activity
  • creare una prospettiva più cinematografica o narrativa
  • attenuare il focus sull’evento puntuale per dare più spazio al contesto

docs.title: Compare
docs.text:
• I walked through the city when I saw her.
  • I was walking through the city when I saw her.

La seconda è molto più naturale se vuoi creare una scena in corso.

docs.title: Another contrast
docs.text:
• At 8 p.m., I worked on the report.
  • At 8 p.m., I was working on the report.

La seconda è la forma naturale per descrivere ciò che era in corso in un momento specifico del passato.

docs.title: Narrative value
docs.text:
• People were talking quietly, music was playing in the background, and everyone seemed relaxed. Then the lights went out.

Qui il Past Continuous costruisce la scena; il Past Simple introduce l’evento che cambia la situazione.

docs.title: 🔮 Future meaning: will, going to, present continuous
docs.text: Anche il futuro, a livelli alti, è una questione di significato e di speaker intention.

docs.title: Will
docs.text:
Usi principali:

  • decision taken now
  • promise / offer / willingness
  • prediction based on opinion or judgement

Examples:

  • I’ll send it right away.
  • Don’t worry, I’ll take care of it.
  • I think the market will recover.

docs.title: Be going to
docs.text:
Usi principali:

  • prior intention
  • evidence-based prediction

Examples:

  • I’m going to apply for that role next week.
  • Look at the sky. It’s going to storm.

docs.title: Present Continuous
docs.text:
Usi principali:

  • arrangement already made
  • concrete future plan with logistical reality

Examples:

  • I’m meeting the supplier on Thursday.
  • We’re flying out early tomorrow morning.

docs.title: Compare the nuance
docs.text:
• I’ll talk to her. → decision now / promise
  • I’m going to talk to her. → already intended
  • I’m talking to her after lunch. → arranged / scheduled

Questa distinzione rende il discorso più preciso e più naturale.

docs.title: 🧭 Time perspective and discourse control
docs.text: A livello C1 non basta scegliere il tempo giusto nella singola frase. Devi anche saper combinare i tempi in modo coerente dentro un testo.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
I have been working in this field for almost a decade. I started in a small company, where I learned the basics very quickly. Since then, I have taken on more responsibility, and I’m currently managing a team. Next month, I’m presenting our new strategy to an international client.

Perché questo paragrafo funziona?

  • have been working → durata fino al presente
  • started / learned → eventi conclusi del passato
  • have taken on → sviluppo con rilevanza attuale
  • am currently managing → situazione presente in corso
  • am presenting → futuro già organizzato

Questa è la logica del controllo avanzato: non un singolo tempo, ma una temporal architecture coerente.

docs.title: 🔬 Meaning lab: tiny changes, big effect
docs.text: Osserva come cambia il significato:

docs.title: Pair 1
docs.text:
• I taught English for five years.
  • I have taught English for five years.
  • I have been teaching English for five years.

docs.title: Pair 2
docs.text:
• She wrote three articles today.
  • She has written three articles today.
  • She has been writing articles all day.

docs.title: Pair 3
docs.text:
• We discussed the issue yesterday.
  • We have discussed the issue.
  • We have been discussing the issue for weeks.

Domanda chiave: in ogni caso, che cosa viene messo in primo piano?

  • the event?
  • the result?
  • the duration?
  • the current relevance?

docs.title: 📘 Reading analysis
docs.text:
Leggi il testo e analizza i tempi:

I have been working remotely for the last three years, and it has completely changed the way I organise my day. Before that, I worked in a traditional office, where meetings often took up most of the afternoon. Recently, I have started managing a small international team, so I am speaking English more frequently than before. Next month, I’m attending a conference in Lisbon, where I’m going to present a short case study.

docs.title: Analysis
docs.text:
• have been working → durata fino al presente
  • has changed → risultato attuale
  • worked → esperienza conclusa
  • have started → cambiamento recente con effetto attuale
  • am speaking → tendenza/attività attuale
  • I’m attending → arrangement
  • I’m going to present → prior intention inside a planned context

docs.title: Drill 1 – Build a timeline
docs.text:
Parla per 90 secondi su questi punti:

  • what you did before
  • what changed
  • what you have been doing recently
  • what you are doing now
  • what you are doing next week

docs.title: Drill 2 – Result vs process
docs.text:
Rispondi due volte alla stessa domanda, cambiando focus.

Question: What have you done today?

Versione A: focus sul risultato Versione B: focus sul processo

docs.title: Drill 3 – Narrative frame
docs.text:
Racconta una situazione in cui qualcosa è successo mentre eri impegnato in un’altra attività.

Usa:

  • background with Past Continuous
  • main event with Past Simple

docs.title: ✍️ Writing lab
docs.text:
Scrivi un testo di 180–220 parole su uno di questi temi:

  1. How your professional or academic life has changed in recent years
  2. A project you worked on, what happened during it, and what effect it has had on you
  3. A shift in your habits, routines or priorities over time

docs.title: Required language
docs.text:
Nel testo devi includere:

  • almeno un Present Perfect
  • almeno un Present Perfect Continuous
  • almeno un Past Simple
  • almeno un Past Continuous
  • una forma di futuro con will / going to / Present Continuous

docs.title: Self-check questions
docs.text:
• Ho usato ogni tempo per un motivo preciso?
  • Ho distinto bene result e duration?
  • Ho evitato riferimenti chiusi con il Present Perfect?
  • Il passaggio tra i tempi è chiaro per il lettore?

docs.title: 1. Present Perfect with finished past time
docs.text: ❌ I have seen him yesterday. ✅ I saw him yesterday.

docs.title: 2. Present tense with since/for where English prefers perfect aspect
docs.text: ❌ I live here since 2021. ✅ I have lived here since 2021. ✅ I have been living here since 2021.

docs.title: 3. Wrong perfect continuous form
docs.text: ❌ I have been written emails all morning. ✅ I have been writing emails all morning.

docs.title: 4. Will used for arranged future
docs.text: ❌ I will meet the client tomorrow at 10. It’s all scheduled. ✅ I’m meeting the client tomorrow at 10.

docs.title: 5. Past Continuous used where no background meaning exists
docs.text:
❌ Yesterday I was going to the bank and I deposited some money. ✅ Yesterday I went to the bank and deposited some money.

Il Past Continuous non serve a rendere la frase “più avanzata”. Serve quando il significato lo giustifica.

docs.title: ✅ Controlled practice
docs.text:
Completa con la forma più adatta del verbo.

  1. I ___ in this company since 2020. (work)
  2. We ___ the final version yesterday afternoon. (send)
  3. She ___ on the proposal all morning. (work)
  4. At 9 p.m. last night, I ___ to finish the report. (try)
  5. I think the meeting ___ longer than expected. (be)
  6. We ___ the regional director tomorrow at 11. (meet)
  7. They ___ the issue several times already. (discuss)
  8. Before I moved here, I ___ in Milan for four years. (live)

docs.title: 🔄 Transformation drill
docs.text:
Riscrivi le frasi secondo l’indicazione.

  1. I live here since 2019. → correggi la prospettiva temporale

  2. I wrote emails all morning. → riscrivi con focus sul processo

  3. I will see the dentist tomorrow at 4. → riscrivi come appuntamento già fissato

  4. I have worked there in 2018. → correggi il riferimento temporale

  5. I was working on the report. Then my boss called. → unisci in una frase narrativa più naturale

  6. I studied English for six years and I still study it now. → riscrivi in una forma più precisa

docs.title: 🧠 Think like an advanced speaker
docs.text:
Prima di parlare o scrivere, prova a chiederti:

  • What is the time frame?
  • Is it finished or still connected to now?
  • Am I highlighting the action, the result or the duration?
  • Is this a spontaneous decision, a prior intention or a fixed arrangement?
  • Am I telling the event, or am I building the scene around it?

Queste domande trasformano la grammatica in uno strumento di controllo reale.

docs.title: 🚀 Final challenge
docs.text:
Parla per 4–5 minuti oppure scrivi un testo di 220 parole sul seguente tema:

Describe how your professional, academic or personal life has evolved over time, what you were doing before, what has changed recently, what you are focusing on now and what you are planning next.

docs.title: You must use
docs.text:
• Past Simple
  • Past Continuous
  • Present Perfect
  • Present Perfect Continuous
  • Present Continuous or going to / will for the future

docs.title: Success criteria
docs.text:
• tempi scelti per significato, non a caso
  • passaggi coerenti tra le diverse fasi temporali
  • tono fluido e controllato
  • maggiore precisione semantica

docs.title: 🎯 Final insight
docs.text:
A livello iniziale, impari che i tempi verbali hanno delle regole.

A livello avanzato, capisci che i tempi verbali sono strumenti di prospettiva.

Non servono solo a dire quando succede qualcosa. Servono a dire:

  • come il parlante guarda l’evento
  • quanto quel fatto conta adesso
  • se il focus è sul risultato, sul processo o sul contesto
  • quale interpretazione vuole guidare nell’ascoltatore o nel lettore

At C1 level, tense choice is not mechanical. It is a way of shaping meaning.

docs.title: Obiettivo della lezione
docs.text:
In questa unità impari a:

  • ripassare con sicurezza zero, first, second e third conditional
  • capire quando usare un mixed conditional
  • esprimere ipotesi, conseguenze, rimpianti e possibilità
  • riconoscere la differenza tra condizioni reali, improbabili e impossibili

docs.title: 1. Perché questa unità è importante
docs.text:
A livelli più alti l’inglese non richiede solo di conoscere la forma grammaticale. Richiede soprattutto di collegare bene:

  • tempo
  • causa
  • conseguenza
  • grado di realtà

I conditionals servono proprio a questo.

docs.title: Zero Conditional
docs.text:
Usiamo lo zero conditional per fatti generali, regole, verità scientifiche o abitudini ripetibili.

Struttura:

if + present simple, present simple

Esempi:

  • If you heat ice, it melts.
  • If people don’t sleep enough, they feel tired.
  • If I drink coffee late, I don’t sleep well.

docs.title: First Conditional
docs.text:
Usiamo il first conditional per possibilità reali o probabili nel futuro.

Struttura:

if + present simple, will + verbo

Esempi:

  • If I finish early, I’ll call you.
  • If it rains tomorrow, we’ll stay inside.
  • If you study regularly, you’ll improve faster.

docs.title: Second Conditional
docs.text:
Usiamo il second conditional per situazioni immaginarie o improbabili nel presente o nel futuro.

Struttura:

if + past simple, would + verbo

Esempi:

  • If I had more time, I would learn another language.
  • If she were here, she would help us.
  • If we lived nearer the office, we would walk to work.

docs.title: Third Conditional
docs.text:
Usiamo il third conditional per immaginare un passato diverso da quello reale.

Struttura:

if + past perfect, would have + past participle

Esempi:

  • If I had left earlier, I would have caught the train.
  • If they had prepared better, they would have done well.
  • If you had told me, I would have helped.

docs.title: 3. Il salto di livello: mixed conditionals
docs.text:
I mixed conditionals collegano due momenti diversi nel tempo.

Di solito servono per esprimere:

  • una causa nel passato con conseguenza nel presente
  • una situazione presente che ha avuto una conseguenza nel passato

docs.title: A. Past cause → present result
docs.text:
Struttura:

if + past perfect, would + verbo

Significato: nel passato è successa o non è successa una cosa; oggi ne vediamo la conseguenza.

Esempi:

  • If I had studied medicine, I would be a doctor now.
  • If she had moved abroad, she would speak English more confidently now.
  • If we had invested earlier, we would be in a stronger position today.

docs.title: B. Present situation → past result
docs.text:
Struttura:

if + past simple, would have + past participle

Significato: una situazione attuale ha influenzato qualcosa nel passato.

Esempi:

  • If I were more organized, I would have finished the report yesterday.
  • If he spoke better English, he would have answered during the meeting.
  • If they were more flexible, they would have accepted the proposal.

docs.title: 4. Come scegliere il conditional giusto
docs.text: Fatti queste domande:

docs.title: 1) La situazione è reale o immaginaria?
docs.text:
• reale / possibile → first conditional
  • immaginaria / improbabile → second conditional
  • impossibile perché appartiene al passato → third conditional

docs.title: 2) Causa e conseguenza stanno nello stesso tempo?
docs.text:
• sì → conditional classico
  • no → mixed conditional

docs.title: 3) Vuoi parlare di rimpianto, possibilità o logica?
docs.text:
• logica generale → zero
  • possibilità futura → first
  • immaginazione presente/futura → second
  • rimpianto o passato alternativo → third
  • collegamento tra passato e presente → mixed

docs.title: 5. Alternative utili a if
docs.text: A livello alto non usiamo solo if.

docs.title: Unless
docs.text:
Significa if not.

  • I won’t go unless you come.
  • Unless we act now, the problem will get worse.

docs.title: As long as / provided that / providing
docs.text:
Indicano una condizione necessaria.

  • You can borrow the car as long as you’re careful.
  • We can change the schedule provided that everyone agrees.

docs.title: Even if
docs.text:
Indica che il risultato non cambia.

  • Even if it rains, we’ll continue.
  • Even if I had more time, I wouldn’t accept the job.

docs.title: Errore 1: usare will dopo if
docs.text: ❌ If I will see him, I will tell him. ✅ If I see him, I will tell him.

docs.title: Errore 2: confondere second e third conditional
docs.text: ❌ If I would have known, I would have called. ✅ If I had known, I would have called.

docs.title: Errore 3: dimenticare il significato del mixed conditional
docs.text: ❌ If I had studied more, I would have a better job yesterday. ✅ If I had studied more, I would have a better job now.

docs.title: Errore 4: usare was invece di were nei casi formali
docs.text:
In inglese reale si sente spesso was, ma in registro più controllato è preferibile:

  • If I were you, I’d wait.

docs.title: Dialogue 1
docs.text: A: If you had accepted that offer, where would you be now? B: I’d probably be living in Berlin. A: Do you regret saying no? B: Sometimes, yes.

docs.title: Dialogue 2
docs.text: A: If you were more confident, would you have spoken during the presentation? B: Yes, definitely. I knew the answer, but I was too nervous. A: That happens to a lot of people.

docs.title: A. Scegli la forma corretta
docs.text:
1. If I ___ more free time, I would start a new course. a) have b) had c) had had

  2. If they had left earlier, they ___ the flight. a) would catch b) would have caught c) will catch

  3. If I had taken that job, I ___ in another country now. a) live b) would live c) would have lived

  4. Unless you ___ now, you’ll miss the deadline. a) start b) will start c) started

docs.title: B. Trasforma le frasi
docs.text:
1. I didn’t study engineering. Now I don’t work in that field. → If...

  2. She is very shy. She didn’t ask the question yesterday. → If...

  3. We don’t have a backup plan. We feel stressed now. → If...

  4. You don’t listen carefully, so you make mistakes. → If...

docs.title: 9. Speaking task
docs.text:
Rispondi oralmente a queste domande con frasi complete:

  • If you could change one decision from last year, what would it be?
  • If you had chosen a different career path, where would you be now?
  • If you were better at managing time, what would have changed recently?
  • What would you do if you suddenly had six months free?

Cerca di usare almeno:

  • un second conditional
  • un third conditional
  • un mixed conditional

docs.title: 10. Writing task
docs.text:
Scrivi 120–150 parole su questo tema:

A decision that changed your life — or could have changed it

Puoi includere:

  • una scelta reale
  • un rimpianto o una possibilità mancata
  • una conseguenza presente
  • cosa faresti in modo diverso oggi

docs.title: 11. Self-check finale
docs.text:
Controlla se sai:

  • distinguere i quattro conditional principali
  • riconoscere quando serve un mixed conditional
  • usare unless, even if e provided that
  • evitare errori come if I will... e if I would have known...
  • parlare di possibilità, rimpianti e conseguenze con naturalezza

Se riesci a farlo con sicurezza, sei sulla strada giusta per un uso davvero avanzato dei conditionals.

docs.title: Learning outcomes
docs.text:
By the end of this lesson, you should be able to:

  • choose conditional structures according to logic, stance and time reference
  • use mixed conditionals with control and precision
  • move beyond basic if-sentences using unless, provided that, even if, whether or not
  • express regret, speculation, strategic advice, constraints and alternative scenarios in a more sophisticated way
  • produce conditionals naturally in discussion, analysis and reflective writing

docs.title: 1. What makes conditional language advanced?
docs.text:
At lower levels, learners often memorize conditional “types.” At C1, that is not enough.

You need to control:

  • the degree of reality of a situation
  • the speaker’s attitude toward the condition
  • the timeline between cause and consequence
  • the difference between neutral logic, personal regret, criticism, advice, distance and politeness

In other words, conditional language at C1 is not just grammar. It is reasoning architecture.

docs.title: 2. Rapid framework: conditionals as meaning systems
Zero conditional
docs.text:
Use it for:

  • general truths
  • predictable patterns
  • procedures and policies

Examples:

  • If demand rises, prices tend to increase.
  • If employees don’t receive clear instructions, delays usually follow.

docs.title: First conditional
docs.text:
Use it for:

  • realistic future possibility
  • warning, planning, promises and consequences

Examples:

  • If we don’t revise the timeline, we’ll run into problems.
  • If you send me the draft today, I’ll review it tonight.

docs.title: Second conditional
docs.text:
Use it for:

  • unreal, hypothetical or distant situations in the present/future
  • softening advice or criticism

Examples:

  • If I were in your position, I’d wait a little longer.
  • If the company were more flexible, it would attract better candidates.

docs.title: Third conditional
docs.text:
Use it for:

  • counterfactual past
  • regret, criticism, reflection and post-event evaluation

Examples:

  • If we had tested the product earlier, we would have noticed the problem.
  • If she had asked for help, the outcome might have been different.

docs.title: 3. Mixed conditionals: the real C1 zone
docs.text: Mixed conditionals matter because real life is rarely confined to one time frame.

docs.title: Pattern A: Past cause → present consequence
docs.text:
Form: if + past perfect, would + base verb

Use it when a past decision, event or omission has a present-day effect.

Examples:

  • If I had taken that scholarship, I would be working abroad now.
  • If we had invested in training earlier, the team would be far more independent today.
  • If he had listened more carefully at the start, he wouldn’t be struggling now.

Interpretation:

  • the past is closed
  • the present is still affected

docs.title: Pattern B: Present condition → past consequence
docs.text:
Form: if + past simple, would have + past participle

Use it when a current characteristic or ongoing limitation explains a past result.

Examples:

  • If I were more decisive, I would have responded immediately.
  • If she spoke better English, she would have handled the call herself.
  • If they were less risk-averse, they would have accepted the proposal last month.

Interpretation:

  • the current condition is still true
  • it explains an earlier outcome

docs.title: Pattern C: Present condition → present consequence
docs.text:
This is the classic second conditional, but at C1 it often overlaps with evaluation and stance.

  • If the system were more intuitive, users would adopt it faster.

docs.title: Pattern D: Past condition → past consequence
docs.text:
Classic third conditional, but often used in analysis and criticism.

  • If management had communicated earlier, the transition would have been smoother.

docs.title: A. Regret vs criticism
docs.text:
Compare:
