  • If I had left earlier, I would have caught the train. → personal regret
  • If you had checked the figures, we wouldn’t be in this situation. → criticism / blame

docs.title: B. Advice vs distance
docs.text:
• If I were you, I’d speak to her directly. → advice
  • If that were the case, we’d need to reconsider everything. → analytical distance

docs.title: C. Real possibility vs rhetorical framing
docs.text:
• If we reduce costs, we’ll improve margins. → practical first conditional
  • If we were to reduce costs dramatically, what would happen to quality? → more formal, speculative framing

docs.title: Unless
docs.text:
Means if not, but not in all contexts.

  • We can’t move forward unless everyone agrees.
  • Unless the data changes, our conclusion will remain the same.

docs.title: Provided that / providing that / as long as
docs.text:
Used for controlled conditions or formal agreements.

  • We can approve the plan provided that the budget remains stable.
  • You may work remotely as long as deadlines are respected.

docs.title: Even if
docs.text:
Shows that the result does not depend on the condition.

  • Even if they apologise, trust will take time to rebuild.
  • Even if I had known, I’m not sure I would have acted differently.

docs.title: Whether or not
docs.text:
Useful in more formal or analytical language.

  • Whether or not they accept the offer, we need a backup plan.

docs.title: 6. Inversion: formal and high-level conditional style
docs.text: In formal English, we can omit if and invert the structure.

docs.title: Had + subject + past participle
docs.text:
• Had I known, I would have reacted differently.
  • Had the team prepared more thoroughly, the launch would have gone better.

docs.title: Were + subject...
docs.text:
• Were I in your position, I would reconsider the timing.
  • Were the market to decline further, investors would become even more cautious.

docs.title: Should + subject + base verb
docs.text:
• Should you need any clarification, please let me know.
  • Should the situation change, we will update the plan accordingly.

These forms are especially useful in:

  • formal writing
  • presentations
  • reports
  • policy and business communication

docs.title: 1. Overusing mechanical “types”
docs.text: Many learners know the rule but not the communicative effect.

docs.title: 2. Using would in the if-clause
docs.text: ❌ If I would have known, I would have called. ✅ If I had known, I would have called.

docs.title: 3. Using future in the if-clause unnecessarily
docs.text: ❌ If they will arrive late, we’ll start without them. ✅ If they arrive late, we’ll start without them.

docs.title: 4. Confusing unless with every kind of negation
docs.text: Not every if not sentence sounds natural with unless.

docs.title: 5. Ignoring time logic in mixed conditionals
docs.text:
Learners often combine forms mechanically instead of asking:

  • What happened first?
  • What is still true now?
  • Which consequence am I describing?

docs.title: 8. Model text
docs.text: If I had accepted the position three years ago, I would probably be managing an international team by now. At the time, however, I felt unprepared and chose stability over growth. Looking back, I can see that, if I were less cautious by nature, I would have taken the risk. Even so, I do not see the decision as a failure. It taught me that, unless we occasionally step outside our comfort zone, we tend to repeat the same patterns. Were a similar opportunity to arise today, I would almost certainly respond differently.

docs.title: Why this text is strong
docs.text:
It includes:

  • third conditional
  • mixed conditional
  • second conditional
  • unless
  • inverted formal conditional
  • reflective, controlled tone

docs.title: 9. Guided analysis
docs.text: Explain the difference in meaning between these pairs.

docs.title: Pair 1
docs.text:
• If I had prepared more, I would have performed better.
  • If I had prepared more, I would feel more confident now.

docs.title: Pair 2
docs.text:
• If he were more diplomatic, he would be easier to work with.
  • If he were more diplomatic, he would have avoided the conflict.

docs.title: Pair 3
docs.text:
• If we reduce costs, we’ll stay competitive.
  • If we were to reduce costs drastically, we might damage quality.

docs.title: A. Complete the sentence naturally
docs.text:
1. If the company had communicated earlier, ...
  2. If I were less hesitant, ...
  3. Unless we change our approach, ...
  4. Even if the plan succeeds, ...
  5. Had I realised how serious it was, ...
  6. Should you require further information, ...

docs.title: B. Rewrite using the prompt
docs.text:
1. If I had known the deadline was final, I would have replied sooner. → Had...

  2. We can continue only if everyone agrees. → provided that

  3. If I were you, I wouldn’t make a decision yet. → more formal version

  4. It doesn’t matter if they complain; we still need to finish the project. → even if

docs.title: C. Correct the error
docs.text:
1. If I would know the answer, I would tell you.
  2. Unless you won’t hurry, we’ll be late.
  3. If he had spoken better English, he would speak during the meeting yesterday.
  4. If I will see her, I’ll mention it.

docs.title: 11. Speaking lab
docs.text:
Respond in extended form. Aim for 60–90 seconds per answer.

  1. Describe a past decision that still affects your present life.
  2. Talk about a missed opportunity and explain how your life might be different now.
  3. Give advice to someone facing a difficult decision, using conditionals strategically.
  4. Discuss a professional or social problem and explain what would happen under different conditions.

docs.title: Required language
docs.text:
Try to include:

  • one second conditional
  • one third conditional
  • one mixed conditional
  • one alternative to if
  • one inverted formal conditional

docs.title: 12. Writing task
docs.text: Write 180–220 words on one of the following topics:

docs.title: Option A
docs.text: A decision I regret — and what it taught me

docs.title: Option B
docs.text: If one key event in my life had gone differently, where would I be now?

docs.title: Option C
docs.text:
How better decisions are made under uncertainty

Your text should:

  • show clear time logic
  • include at least four different conditional patterns
  • use at least two advanced alternatives to if
  • maintain a controlled, reflective C1 tone

docs.title: 13. Final checklist
docs.text:
You are ready to move on if you can:

  • choose conditional forms according to meaning, not only rule labels
  • use mixed conditionals with correct time relationships
  • control the tone difference between regret, criticism, analysis and advice
  • use unless, provided that, even if, whether or not naturally
  • produce more formal forms such as Had I known..., Were I..., Should you need...
  • speak and write about hypothetical situations with precision and flexibility

docs.title: Homework
docs.text:
Write five original sentences:

  • one zero conditional
  • one first conditional
  • one second conditional
  • one third conditional
  • one mixed conditional

Then rewrite two of them in a more formal C1 style using inversion or an alternative linker.

docs.title: Obiettivo della lezione
docs.text:
In questa unità impari a:

  • fare deduzioni logiche sul presente e sul passato
  • esprimere diversi gradi di certezza usando must, may, might, could, can’t
  • distinguere tra fatto e interpretazione
  • usare correttamente le strutture modal + base verb e modal + have + past participle
  • descrivere situazioni in modo più maturo e naturale

docs.title: 1. Perché questa unità è importante
docs.text:
A livelli più alti non basta più dire solo what happened. Devi anche saper dire:

  • cosa pensi che stia succedendo
  • cosa deduci da certi segnali
  • quanto sei sicuro della tua interpretazione

Per questo i modal verbs di deduzione sono fondamentali: ti permettono di passare dalla semplice descrizione alla lettura della realtà.

docs.title: 2. Fatti vs interpretazioni
docs.text:
Confronta:

  • The office is empty. → fatto osservabile
  • They must be in a meeting. → deduzione

Oppure:

  • He didn’t answer the phone. → fatto
  • He might be busy. → interpretazione

Questa distinzione è il cuore della lezione.

docs.title: Must
docs.text:
Usiamo must quando la deduzione è molto forte.

  • She must be tired.
  • They must be at work.
  • This must be the right address.

docs.title: May / Might / Could
docs.text:
Usiamo may, might, could quando qualcosa è possibile ma non certa.

  • He may be at home.
  • She might be working.
  • They could be on the train.

docs.title: Can’t
docs.text:
Usiamo can’t quando riteniamo qualcosa impossibile.

  • He can’t be at home. His car isn’t there.
  • This can’t be true.
  • They can’t be ready yet.

docs.title: 4. Grado di certezza
docs.text:
Struttura Significato must quasi certo may / might / could possibile can’t impossibile

Non stiamo parlando di regole o permessi. Qui i modali servono a esprimere inference, cioè deduzione.

docs.title: 5. Deduction nel passato
docs.text:
Quando vogliamo fare deduzioni su qualcosa che probabilmente è successo nel passato, usiamo:

modal + have + past participle

docs.title: Must have + past participle
docs.text:
• He must have left early.
  • She must have forgotten the meeting.
  • They must have taken the wrong train.

docs.title: May / Might / Could have + past participle
docs.text:
• He might have missed the bus.
  • She may have gone home already.
  • They could have misunderstood the instructions.

docs.title: Can’t have + past participle
docs.text:
• He can’t have done it alone.
  • She can’t have seen the email.
  • They can’t have arrived yet.

docs.title: 6. Presente o passato?
docs.text:
Confronta:

  • He must be tired. → deduzione sul presente

  • He must have been tired. → deduzione sul passato

  • She might be at home. → forse è a casa adesso

  • She might have gone home. → forse è andata a casa prima

La struttura cambia in base al momento a cui fai riferimento.

docs.title: Dialogue 1
docs.text: A: The lights are off and nobody is answering. B: They must be out. A: Or they might be upstairs.

docs.title: Dialogue 2
docs.text: A: Tom wasn’t at the meeting this morning. B: He might have forgotten about it. A: No, he can’t have forgotten. He sent the agenda yesterday.

docs.title: Dialogue 3
docs.text: A: Maria looks exhausted. B: She must have worked late last night.

docs.title: Errore 1: aggiungere to dopo il modal
docs.text: ❌ He must to be tired. ✅ He must be tired.

docs.title: Errore 2: sbagliare la struttura del passato
docs.text: ❌ He must has gone home. ✅ He must have gone home.

docs.title: Errore 3: usare can’t have come se fosse presente
docs.text: ❌ He can’t have at home. ✅ He can’t be at home. ✅ He can’t have gone home yet.

docs.title: Errore 4: confondere fatto e deduzione
docs.text: ❌ He is late, so he is in traffic. ✅ He is late, so he must be stuck in traffic.

docs.title: A. Complete the sentences
docs.text:
1. He isn’t answering. He ___ be busy.
  2. She isn’t here. She ___ have left already.
  3. They ___ be in the office. The building is closed.
  4. He looks worried. He ___ have received bad news.

docs.title: B. Choose the best option
docs.text:
1. The lights are on. They must / can’t be at home.
  2. She missed the train. She must have / must be left late.
  3. He isn’t online. He might be / might have asleep.
  4. They know the answer. They can’t have / must understood the question.

docs.title: C. Rewrite as a deduction
docs.text:
1. Maybe she is at work.
  2. I’m sure he forgot.
  3. It’s impossible that they are ready.
  4. Maybe she missed the email.

docs.title: 10. Speaking task
docs.text:
Look at a situation and make deductions.

Possible prompts:

  • a person who looks tired
  • an empty classroom
  • a colleague who didn’t reply
  • a friend who arrived late
  • a cancelled event

Try to use:

  • must
  • might / may / could
  • can’t
  • at least one deduction about the past

docs.title: 11. Writing task
docs.text:
Write 10 original sentences.

Include:

  • 3 deductions about the present
  • 3 deductions about the past
  • 2 sentences with can’t
  • 2 sentences with might / may / could

Then write a short paragraph describing a mysterious situation using at least five modal deductions.

docs.title: 12. Final checklist
docs.text:
You are ready to move on if you can:

  • distinguish clearly between fact and interpretation
  • use must, might, may, could, can’t for present deduction
  • use must have, might have, could have, can’t have for past deduction
  • choose structures according to the correct time reference
  • avoid basic structural errors with modal verbs

docs.title: Homework
docs.text:
Write a short text about an unusual event (real or invented). Describe:

  • what you observed
  • what you think happened
  • what probably happened before
  • what definitely did not happen

Use at least eight modal structures from this unit.

docs.title: Learning outcomes
docs.text:
By the end of this lesson, you should be able to:

  • express different degrees of certainty with precision and control
  • distinguish between observation, inference and conclusion
  • use modal verbs to speculate about the present, the past and, when relevant, developing situations
  • combine evidence, tone and logic in order to sound more analytical and credible
  • use modal deduction naturally in discussion, reporting, commentary and reflective speaking

docs.title: 1. Why modal deduction matters at C1
docs.text:
At lower levels, learners often use modal verbs mainly for:

  • obligation
  • permission
  • advice
  • ability

At C1, modal verbs also become tools for epistemic stance: they show how certain, uncertain or doubtful you are about an interpretation.

That means you are no longer just describing events. You are showing:

  • how you read evidence
  • how strong your conclusion is
  • how cautious or confident you want to sound

This is a major shift in advanced English.

docs.title: 2. Observation → inference → stance
docs.text:
Consider the difference:

  • He isn’t answering his phone. → observation
  • He must be in a meeting. → strong inference
  • He might be driving. → tentative inference
  • He can’t be at home; the lights are off. → negative deduction

A strong C1 speaker can move from visible facts to carefully framed interpretation.

docs.title: Must
docs.text:
Use must when the evidence leads you to a strong conclusion.

  • She must be exhausted after that flight.
  • They must know what happened by now.
  • This must be the updated version.

Important: this is logical certainty, not absolute factual certainty.

docs.title: May / Might / Could
docs.text:
Use these when something is plausible but not confirmed.

  • He may be working from home today.
  • She might be avoiding the discussion.
  • They could be waiting for final approval.

At C1, the choice is often less about strict grammar and more about tone:

  • may can sound slightly more formal or neutral
  • might often sounds a little more tentative
  • could sometimes highlights one possible explanation among others

docs.title: Can’t
docs.text:
Use can’t for strong negative deduction.

  • He can’t be serious.
  • They can’t be ready yet.
  • This can’t be the final report; it’s missing three sections.

docs.title: 4. Past deduction: where precision becomes visible
docs.text:
To speculate about the past, we use:

modal + have + past participle

docs.title: Must have + past participle
docs.text:
Use it for a strong conclusion about a past event.

  • She must have left before eight.
  • He must have misunderstood the instructions.
  • They must have changed the schedule at the last minute.

docs.title: May / Might / Could have + past participle
docs.text:
Use these for possible past explanations.

  • She may have missed the message.
  • He might have fallen asleep.
  • They could have taken a different route.

docs.title: Can’t have + past participle
docs.text:
Use it for strong negative deduction about the past.

  • He can’t have written this alone.
  • She can’t have seen the warning.
  • They can’t have finished already.

docs.title: 5. Time reference and meaning control
docs.text:
Compare carefully:

  • He must be tired. → present state
  • He must have been tired. → past state
  • She might be working late. → present activity / ongoing situation
  • She might have worked late. → past possibility
  • They can’t be at home. → impossible now
  • They can’t have stayed at home all evening. → impossible interpretation of the past

At C1, one of the key skills is matching the modal structure to the timeline you want to express.

docs.title: 6. Degrees of certainty and communicative effect
docs.text:
Structure Typical force Communicative effect must strong inference confident, reasoned conclusion may / might / could possibility cautious, open interpretation can’t strong negative inference confident rejection of an explanation

This matters especially when you are:

  • analysing a situation
  • discussing causes
  • evaluating behaviour
  • interpreting incomplete information
  • speaking in professional or academic settings

docs.title: 7. Beyond grammar: evidence language
docs.text: Strong speakers often support modal deductions with evidence markers.

docs.title: Useful patterns
docs.text:
• Judging by ...
  • From the way ...
  • Given that ...
  • Apparently ...
  • It looks as if / It sounds as if ...

Examples:

  • Judging by his tone, he must be under pressure.
  • Given that nobody was informed, they might have made the decision very late.
  • From the way she reacted, she can’t have expected that question.
  • It looks as if they may be reconsidering the proposal.

These patterns make your deductions sound more grounded and credible.

docs.title: 8. Subtle meaning differences learners often miss
A. Must vs probably
docs.text:
• He must be tired.
  • He is probably tired.

Both show strong probability, but must sounds more inferential: it suggests a conclusion drawn from evidence.

docs.title: B. Might vs could
docs.text:
In many contexts they overlap, but the nuance can differ:

  • He might be avoiding us. → tentative interpretation
  • He could be avoiding us. → one possible explanation among several

docs.title: C. Can’t vs must not
docs.text:
These are not the same.

  • He can’t be at work. → impossible / I’m sure he isn’t there
  • He must not be at work. → obligation / prohibition context, not deduction

This difference is essential.

docs.title: 1. Confusing certainty with factual truth
docs.text: Learners sometimes use must as if it meant “I know for a fact.” In reality, it means “I am strongly concluding this from evidence.”

docs.title: 2. Forgetting have in past deduction
docs.text: ❌ He must gone home. ✅ He must have gone home.

docs.title: 3. Using to after a modal
docs.text: ❌ She might to know. ✅ She might know.

docs.title: 4. Confusing deduction with permission or obligation
docs.text: ❌ You can’t be late = maybe intended as prohibition ✅ You mustn’t be late = prohibition ✅ He can’t be late because he arrived an hour ago = deduction

docs.title: 5. Translating directly from Italian intuition
docs.text: Italian often allows learners to rely on context. English requires a clearer grammatical signal for the degree of inference.

docs.title: 10. Model analysis
docs.text:
Read the paragraph:

Nobody replied to the email, and the meeting room was empty when I arrived. They must have changed the plan without telling everyone. Sarah might have known about it, because she looked unusually calm, but James can’t have been informed; he seemed genuinely confused. They may be discussing the issue elsewhere, or they could be waiting for final confirmation before saying anything.

docs.title: Why this is strong
docs.text:
It combines:

  • present deduction
  • past deduction
  • different levels of certainty
  • visible evidence
  • controlled interpretative tone

docs.title: A. Complete naturally
docs.text:
1. He isn’t replying. He must ...
  2. She looks relieved. She may ...
  3. They can’t ... because the office is locked.
  4. He must have ... if he missed the entire briefing.
  5. They might have ... before the official announcement.
  6. She can’t have ...; she was abroad that week.

docs.title: B. Rewrite using modal deduction
docs.text:
1. I’m sure she forgot the appointment.
  2. Maybe they changed their minds.
  3. It’s impossible that he wrote this email himself.
  4. I’m almost certain they are negotiating privately.
  5. Perhaps she misunderstood what we meant.

docs.title: C. Correct the error
docs.text:
1. He must to be tired.
  2. She might has left already.
  3. They can’t have at home.
  4. He must not be at the office; his car isn’t there.
  5. She could to have forgotten the password.

docs.title: 12. Speaking lab
docs.text:
Respond in extended form. Aim for 60–90 seconds per answer.

  1. A colleague suddenly stops answering messages. What might be happening?
  2. A project fails unexpectedly. What must have gone wrong?
  3. Someone reacts very coldly in a meeting. What might explain that behaviour?
  4. You arrive somewhere and find everything empty and silent. What deductions can you make?

docs.title: Required language
docs.text:
Try to include:

  • at least two strong deductions with must
  • at least two tentative explanations with may / might / could
  • at least two negative deductions with can’t
  • at least two past deductions

docs.title: 13. Writing task
docs.text: Write 180–220 words on one of the following topics:

docs.title: Option A
docs.text: A misunderstanding at work or university: what probably happened?

docs.title: Option B
docs.text: An unexplained situation and the most plausible interpretations

docs.title: Option C
docs.text:
Why a plan failed: evidence, conclusions and alternative explanations

Your text should:

  • distinguish clearly between facts and deductions
  • use at least eight modal deduction structures
  • include both present and past speculation
  • use at least three evidence markers such as judging by, given that, from the way
  • maintain a controlled C1 tone

docs.title: 14. Final checklist
docs.text:
You are ready to move on if you can:

  • choose modal deduction forms according to degree of certainty
  • control the difference between present and past inference
  • distinguish deduction from obligation, permission and prohibition
  • support your interpretation with visible evidence
  • speak and write about uncertain situations with nuance and precision

docs.title: Homework
docs.text:
Observe three real or imaginary situations and write:

  • one paragraph about what must be true
  • one paragraph about what might / may / could be true
  • one paragraph about what can’t be true

In each paragraph, include at least one deduction about the past and one about the present.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità saprai:

  • usare il passive voice in modo naturale, non meccanico
  • capire quando il passive è più adatto dell’active
  • formare il passive in tempi diversi con maggiore sicurezza
  • usare il passive in contesti più formali, descrittivi e professionali
  • riconoscere alcune strutture avanzate molto frequenti, come modal passive, impersonal passive e get passive

docs.title: 🧠 Il salto da B2 a C1
docs.text:
A livello B2 spesso il passive è visto come una trasformazione grammaticale:

  • They built the bridge in 1998.
  • The bridge was built in 1998.

A livello C1, invece, il punto non è solo saperlo trasformare.

Il punto è capire:

  • perché il passive viene scelto
  • che effetto produce
  • quanto è formale, neutro o distaccato
  • se conviene mettere al centro chi agisce o ciò che accade

In altre parole, il passive è uno strumento di focus e di register control.

docs.title: 🧩 Cos’è davvero il Passive?
docs.text:
Il passive serve a spostare l’attenzione:

  • dall’agente → all’azione o al risultato
  • da chi fa qualcosa → a ciò che viene fatto

docs.title: Confronto
docs.text:
• Someone wrote the report yesterday.
  • The report was written yesterday.

La seconda frase non è “più corretta”. È semplicemente diversa nel focus.

docs.title: Quando il passive è utile
docs.text:
1. quando l’agente non è importante
  2. quando l’agente è sconosciuto
  3. quando l’agente è ovvio
  4. quando vogliamo un tono più formale o impersonale
  5. quando il risultato conta più dell’azione in sé

docs.title: 🏗️ Struttura base
docs.text:
La struttura del passive è:

be + past participle

docs.title: Esempi nei tempi principali
docs.text:
Tempo Active Passive Present Simple They make cars here. Cars are made here. Past Simple They launched the product last week. The product was launched last week. Present Perfect They have solved the problem. The problem has been solved. Future They will announce the results tomorrow. The results will be announced tomorrow. ### Con modali

modal + be + past participle

  • The form must be completed today.
  • The issue can be solved.
  • The report should be revised.

docs.title: ⚖️ Active o Passive?
docs.text: Non esiste una scelta “sempre migliore”. Esiste una scelta più adatta al contesto.

docs.title: Active: più diretto, più chiaro, più energico
docs.text:
• The team finished the project early.
  • Our company developed the software internally.

docs.title: Passive: più neutro, più formale, più orientato al risultato
docs.text:
• The project was finished early.
  • The software was developed internally.

docs.title: Regola pratica
docs.text:
Usa il passive quando:

  • il risultato è più importante del soggetto
  • vuoi evitare di nominare l’agente
  • stai scrivendo in stile più formale o tecnico

docs.title: 1. Quando l’agente non interessa
docs.text:
• The work has been completed.
  • The documents were sent this morning.

docs.title: 2. Quando l’agente è ovvio
docs.text:
• The suspect was arrested.
  • The patient was taken to hospital.

docs.title: 3. In contesti professionali o istituzionali
docs.text:
• The decision was made after careful consideration.
  • The data were collected over a six-month period.
  • The final version will be shared next week.

docs.title: 4. Per essere meno diretti
docs.text:
• A mistake was made.
  • Several deadlines were missed.

Qui il passive può rendere il tono più diplomatico o meno accusatorio.

docs.title: Present Perfect Passive
docs.text:
• The issue has already been discussed.
  • All the files have been uploaded.

docs.title: Past Perfect Passive
docs.text:
• The room had been cleaned before the guests arrived.
  • The contract had been signed before the meeting started.

docs.title: Future Passive
docs.text:
• A decision will be taken tomorrow.
  • The final list will be published next week.

docs.title: Continuous Passive
docs.text:
Non è il passive più frequente, ma esiste.

  • The road is being repaired.
  • The proposal was being reviewed when we arrived.

docs.title: 🧩 Impersonal Passive
docs.text: Molto comune in inglese formale, giornalistico e accademico.

docs.title: Pattern frequenti
docs.text:
• It is said that...
  • It is believed that...
  • It is thought that...
  • It is expected that...
  • It has been reported that...

docs.title: Esempi
docs.text:
• It is believed that the company will expand next year.
  • It has been reported that several changes are being considered.

Questo tipo di struttura permette di:

  • evitare fonti troppo precise
  • mantenere un tono oggettivo
  • creare distanza dal contenuto

docs.title: 🧩 Get Passive
docs.text: Il get passive è più comune nel parlato e spesso più personale o dinamico.

docs.title: Confronto
docs.text:
• He was fired. → neutro
  • He got fired. → più colloquiale, più “sentito”

Altri esempi:

  • She got promoted last month.
  • My phone got damaged in the rain.
  • We got stuck in traffic.

In generale:

  • be passive → più neutro, standard, formale
  • get passive → più colloquiale, più vicino all’esperienza del soggetto

docs.title: 1. Usare il passive solo perché “suona avanzato”
docs.text:
❌ The company was deciding to change the strategy.

Meglio:

✅ The company decided to change the strategy.

docs.title: 2. Dimenticare been nei tempi composti
docs.text:
❌ The report has finished.

✅ The report has been finished.

Spesso però, in inglese naturale, è ancora meglio:

✅ The report has been completed.

docs.title: 3. Confondere passive e aggettivo
docs.text:
• The door is closed. → può essere passive o stato risultante
  • The door was closed by the security guard. → passive chiaro

docs.title: 4. Inserire “by someone” inutilmente
docs.text:
❌ The decision was made by the manager (quando è già ovvio o irrilevante)

Spesso basta:

✅ The decision was made.

docs.title: 📘 Reading
docs.text:
Leggi il testo e osserva come il passive sposta il focus verso processi, risultati e decisioni.

The project was completed ahead of schedule, and the final report has been shared with all stakeholders. Several recommendations were made during the final review, and it is expected that a revised version will be published next month. No major issues were identified, although one section is being updated at the moment.

docs.title: Domande di comprensione
docs.text:
1. What was completed ahead of schedule?
  2. Who received the report?
  3. What is expected next month?
  4. Which part is still being changed?
  5. Why is passive useful in this text?

docs.title: 🗣️ Speaking Practice
docs.text:
Rispondi oralmente usando il passive quando è naturale.

  1. How are decisions usually made in your workplace or field?
  2. How is information shared in your team or school?
  3. What products are widely used in your industry?
  4. What changes are being discussed at the moment in society or technology?
  5. Has anything important been changed in your city recently?

docs.title: Mini task
docs.text:
Descrivi un processo reale, per esempio:

  • how a product is developed
  • how an event is organised
  • how exams are assessed
  • how customer complaints are handled

docs.title: ✍️ Writing Practice
docs.text:
Scrivi 120–150 parole su uno di questi temi:

  1. Describe how a product or service is created or delivered.
  2. Explain how decisions are made in an organisation you know.
  3. Write a short report about a project that has been completed recently.

docs.title: Checklist
docs.text:
Controlla di aver usato:

  • almeno 4 passive structures
  • almeno 2 tempi diversi nel passive
  • almeno 1 modal passive
  • un tono coerente e naturale

docs.title: ✅ Self-check finale
docs.text:
Controlla se sai:

  • scegliere tra active e passive in base al focus
  • formare il passive in più tempi verbali
  • usare modal passive con sicurezza
  • riconoscere e usare impersonal passive
  • distinguere be passive e get passive
  • evitare passive innaturali o troppo scolastici

Se riesci a farlo, stai usando il passive in modo molto più vicino a un vero livello C1.

docs.title: 🎯 Lesson Goals
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • usare il passive voice come scelta di focus, stance e register, non come semplice trasformazione grammaticale
  • distinguere quando il passive rende il testo più efficace, più neutro, più istituzionale, più diplomatico o più impersonale
  • controllare diverse strutture avanzate: perfect passive, continuous passive, modal passive, impersonal passive, get passive e costruzioni con reporting verbs
  • riconoscere quando il passive aiuta a gestire la responsabilità, la distanza, l’evidenza o la prominenza informativa
  • evitare passive artificiosi, gonfi o poco idiomatici che spesso derivano da una falsa idea di “inglese avanzato”
  • produrre speaking e writing più maturi, con maggiore controllo tra directness, neutrality, diplomacy e formality

Questa unità non serve a “fare il passive meglio”. Serve a capire what the passive does to discourse.

docs.title: 🧠 Beyond transformation: passive as discourse management
docs.text:
Molti studenti associano il passive a un esercizio scolastico:

  • They approved the proposal.
  • The proposal was approved.

Ma a livello C1, la vera domanda è:

  • Why is the agent being backgrounded?
  • What is being foregrounded instead?
  • Does the passive sound objective, evasive, formal, or appropriately impersonal?
  • Would an active sentence be clearer, stronger, or more accountable?

Il passive, infatti, modifica almeno quattro dimensioni del discorso:

  1. information structure → cosa va in posizione iniziale
  2. agency visibility → quanto è chiaro chi agisce
  3. register → quanto il testo suona formale, tecnico o burocratico
  4. stance → se il parlante si avvicina, si distanzia o si protegge pragmaticamente

docs.title: ⚖️ Active vs Passive: same facts, different discourse effect
docs.text:
Confronta:

  • We made a mistake in the final version.
  • A mistake was made in the final version.

La seconda frase:

  • riduce la visibilità dell’agente
  • suona più distaccata
  • può essere più diplomatica
  • può anche sembrare meno trasparente

Questo significa che il passive non è mai neutro al 100%: crea sempre un effetto.

docs.title: Compare
docs.text:
• The board rejected the proposal.
  • The proposal was rejected.

Nel primo caso l’attenzione è sulla responsabilità del board. Nel secondo caso il focus è sull’esito.

docs.title: Practical implication
docs.text:
Un buon utente C1 sa scegliere:

  • active quando servono chiarezza, responsabilità, energia, precisione
  • passive quando servono oggettività, proceduralità, diplomazia, coesione tematica o depersonalizzazione

docs.title: 🧩 The core grammar, quickly but precisely
Canonical passive
docs.text:
be + past participle

  • The system is monitored continuously.
  • The issue was discussed yesterday.
  • The proposal has been revised.
  • The final version will be published soon.

docs.title: Modal passive
docs.text:
modal + be + past participle

  • The matter should be addressed immediately.
  • The figures can be checked later.
  • A final decision must be taken today.

docs.title: Perfect passive
docs.text:
• The project has been completed.
  • The documents had been prepared in advance.

docs.title: Continuous passive
docs.text:
• The website is being updated.
  • The terms were being negotiated when the meeting ended.

A livello avanzato, il punto non è solo saperle formare. È capire which one best matches the viewpoint you want.

docs.title: 🧠 Focus management: what goes first matters
docs.text:
In inglese, la posizione iniziale della frase è molto importante.

Con il passive puoi mettere in posizione tematica un elemento che vuoi mantenere come topic del discorso.

docs.title: Example sequence
docs.text:
• The report was submitted on Monday.
  • It was then reviewed by the compliance team.
  • Several issues were identified, and a revised version was requested.

Qui il passive aiuta a mantenere il discorso centrato sul report e sul processo, non sulle persone.

Questo è molto comune in:

  • report professionali
  • documentazione tecnica
  • presentazioni istituzionali
  • sintesi accademiche

docs.title: 1. The agent is unknown, irrelevant, or obvious
docs.text:
• My bike was stolen last night.
  • The patient was taken to hospital.
  • The building was designed in the 1990s.

docs.title: 2. The result matters more than the actor
docs.text:
• The data were analysed using three different models.
  • The contract has been signed.
  • The new policy was introduced in January.

docs.title: 3. You want a more formal or institutional tone
docs.text:
• The application has been received and is currently being processed.
  • All requests will be handled within five working days.

docs.title: 4. You want diplomatic distance
docs.text:
• Some deadlines were missed.
  • Certain assumptions were made too quickly.

Qui il passive può attenuare l’impatto accusatorio. Però va usato con attenzione: a volte suona professionale, altre volte evasivo.

docs.title: ⚠️ When the passive is a bad choice
docs.text: Uno studente avanzato deve anche saper non usare il passive.

docs.title: Avoid passive when:
docs.text:
• l’agente è il vero centro informativo
  • il testo diventa rigido o burocratico
  • la frase perde energia o chiarezza
  • vuoi mostrare responsabilità esplicita

docs.title: Compare
docs.text:
• We made the wrong assumption.
  • The wrong assumption was made.

La seconda frase è utile in alcuni contesti, ma in altri sembra una fuga dalla responsabilità.

docs.title: Better active examples
docs.text:
• Our team identified the issue quickly.
  • The researchers collected the data over six months.
  • The company changed the policy last year.

Qui l’active è più vivo, più diretto e spesso più credibile.

docs.title: 🧠 Impersonal passive and reporting structures
docs.text: Questa è una delle aree più interessanti del passive a livello C1.

docs.title: Common patterns
docs.text:
• It is believed that...
  • It is thought that...
  • It is assumed that...
  • It has been reported that...
  • It was claimed that...
  • It is expected that...

Queste strutture servono a:

  • introdurre informazione senza attribuirla in modo forte
  • creare distanza epistemica
  • mantenere un tono oggettivo o semi-oggettivo
  • evitare commitment troppo forte

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• It is believed that the market will recover gradually.
  • It has been suggested that the current model is no longer sustainable.
  • It was later discovered that several files had been deleted.

docs.title: Alternative advanced pattern
docs.text:
• The market is believed to be recovering gradually.
  • Several employees are thought to have left the company.
  • The policy is expected to be revised next quarter.

Queste strutture sono particolarmente utili in:

  • academic summaries
  • formal presentations
  • analytical writing
  • news-style reporting

docs.title: 🧩 Get passive: not just informal, but experiential
docs.text:
Molti manuali dicono solo:

  • be passive = formale
  • get passive = informale

È vero solo in parte.

Il get passive spesso aggiunge:

  • maggiore senso di evento
  • coinvolgimento del soggetto
  • idea di cambiamento o impatto vissuto
  • sfumatura più colloquiale o narrativa

docs.title: Compare
docs.text:
• He was fired last week. → neutro, reportivo

  • He got fired last week. → più narrativo, più personale

  • She was promoted in June. → neutro/professionale

  • She got promoted in June. → più colloquiale, più vicino all’esperienza della persona

docs.title: Typical get-passive examples
docs.text:
• get hired
  • get promoted
  • get paid
  • get hurt
  • get stuck
  • get invited
  • get caught

A livello C1 è utile saper distinguere non solo la grammatica, ma il tone profile di queste scelte.

docs.title: 🧠 Passive, adjectives, and resultative states
docs.text:
Una zona di confine importante è la differenza tra:

  • true passive
  • resultative adjective / state

docs.title: Compare
docs.text:
• The door was closed by security at 10 p.m. → evento, passive chiaro

  • The door is closed. → stato risultante / descrizione

  • The window was broken during the storm. → evento

  • The window is broken. → stato attuale

Questo conta molto nella comprensione e nella precisione descrittiva.

docs.title: 1. Overpassivisation
docs.text:
Alcuni studenti usano il passive troppo spesso per “suonare avanzati”. Il risultato può essere innaturale.

❌ Important decisions were taken by us in order to improve the strategy.

Meglio:

✅ We made several important decisions to improve the strategy.

docs.title: 2. Unnecessary by-phrases
docs.text:
❌ The report was written by the manager

Se il manager è già noto o irrilevante, il complemento d’agente è superfluo.

docs.title: 3. Wrong register assumptions
docs.text: Il passive non rende automaticamente una frase elegante. A volte la rende solo più pesante.

docs.title: 4. Confusion with intransitive verbs
docs.text:
Non tutti i verbi possono andare al passive.

❌ He was arrived late. ✅ He arrived late.

docs.title: 📘 Analysis Task
docs.text:
Leggi il testo e osserva cosa viene foregrounded e cosa viene backgrounded.

The final report was submitted on Friday and was circulated among senior staff later that day. Several concerns were raised during the review process, and a revised version is expected to be produced by the end of the month. It has also been suggested that additional data should be collected before any final decision is made.

docs.title: Analysis questions
docs.text:
1. Why is passive preferred in this text?
  2. Which agents are hidden or backgrounded?
  3. Which sentence sounds most institutional?
  4. Where would an active version be more effective?
  5. What effect does It has also been suggested that... create?

docs.title: Task 1 – Process explanation
docs.text:
Spiega oralmente uno di questi processi usando il passive quando è naturale:

  • how a proposal is approved
  • how a product is tested
  • how complaints are handled
  • how university applications are processed

docs.title: Task 2 – Diplomatic reporting
docs.text:
Riformula queste frasi in modo più istituzionale o diplomatico:

  • We made a few mistakes.
  • The team missed two deadlines.
  • They changed the schedule at the last minute.
  • Someone leaked the information.

docs.title: Task 3 – Active or passive?
docs.text:
Per ogni caso, spiega quale forma sceglieresti e perché:

  • formal report
  • apology email
  • news summary
  • meeting recap
  • storytelling in conversation

docs.title: ✍️ Writing Practice
docs.text: Scrivi 180–220 parole scegliendo uno dei seguenti compiti.

docs.title: Option A – Formal process description
docs.text: Describe how an internal review process is carried out in a company or institution.

docs.title: Option B – Analytical summary
docs.text: Write a short formal summary of a project in which the focus is on outcomes, actions taken, and decisions made.

docs.title: Option C – Style comparison
docs.text:
Write two short versions of the same event:

  1. one mostly in the active voice
  2. one using the passive voice strategically

Poi spiega brevemente quale versione suona più adatta a un professional context e perché.

docs.title: Writing checklist
docs.text:
Assicurati di includere:

  • almeno 6 passive structures
  • almeno 3 tempi diversi nel passive
  • almeno 1 impersonal passive
  • almeno 1 modal passive
  • almeno 1 scelta consapevole tra active e passive spiegabile in termini di focus o register

docs.title: 🧪 Error Clinic
docs.text:
Correggi o migliora queste frasi.

  1. The company was deciding to reduce costs.
  2. The report has finished yesterday.
  3. Important changes were made by the management, and they were announced by them later.
  4. He was arrived late, so the meeting was delayed.
  5. The product got developed in our lab.

docs.title: Possibili correzioni
docs.text:
1. The company decided to reduce costs.
  2. The report was finished yesterday. / The report has been completed.
  3. Important changes were made by management and announced later. oppure una versione attiva più chiara
  4. He arrived late, so the meeting was delayed.
  5. The product was developed in our lab.

docs.title: ✅ Final Self-check
docs.text:
Verifica se sai davvero fare queste cose:

  • scegliere tra active e passive per ragioni di focus e non solo di grammatica
  • usare il passive in tempi semplici, perfetti, continui e con modali
  • riconoscere quando il passive aumenta neutrality, diplomacy, formality o distance
  • usare con naturalezza strutture come It is believed that... e X is thought to...
  • distinguere be passive, get passive e resultative state
  • evitare passive rigidi, sovraccarichi o poco idiomatici

Se riesci a fare tutto questo con buona naturalezza, stai usando il passive in modo molto vicino a un vero livello C1.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità saprai:

  • trasformare il discorso diretto in indiretto con maggiore sicurezza
  • riportare affermazioni, domande, ordini e suggerimenti
  • adattare tempi, pronomi, riferimenti di tempo e luogo
  • usare diversi reporting verbs in modo più naturale
  • raccontare conversazioni, riunioni e scambi di informazioni in modo più fluido

docs.title: 🧠 Il salto da B2 a C1
docs.text:
A livello B2 spesso il reported speech viene trattato come un esercizio di trasformazione:

  • “I’m tired.” → He said that he was tired.

A livello C1 il punto non è solo cambiare la forma.

Il punto è saper:

  • riportare il contenuto essenziale
  • scegliere il verbo più adatto
  • decidere se fare o no il backshift
  • rendere il discorso più naturale, sintetico e informativo

In altre parole, non stai più “spostando i tempi”. Stai trasmettendo significato.

docs.title: Direct Speech
docs.text:
Riporta le parole esatte.

  • She said, “I need more time.”
  • He asked, “Are you ready?”

docs.title: Reported Speech
docs.text:
Riporta il contenuto del messaggio.

  • She said that she needed more time.
  • He asked if I was ready.

docs.title: Perché usare il reported speech?
docs.text:
Per:

  • raccontare conversazioni
  • riferire decisioni prese in una riunione
  • sintetizzare informazioni
  • scrivere report, email e aggiornamenti professionali

docs.title: 🔁 Backshifting: la regola di base
docs.text:
Quando il verbo introduttivo è al passato, i tempi spesso “scendono indietro”.

Direct Reported Present Simple Past Simple Present Continuous Past Continuous Past Simple Past Perfect Present Perfect Past Perfect will would can could may might

docs.title: Esempi
docs.text:
• “I work from home.” → He said that he worked from home.
  • “I am waiting.” → She said that she was waiting.
  • “I finished the report.” → He said that he had finished the report.
  • “I will call you.” → She said that she would call me.

docs.title: ⚠️ Ma il backshift non è automatico al 100%
docs.text: Non sempre bisogna cambiare il tempo.

docs.title: Quando spesso NON si fa backshift
1. Quando il contenuto è ancora vero
docs.text:
• “Rome is the capital of Italy.”
  • He said that Rome is the capital of Italy.

docs.title: 2. Quando vuoi mantenere il fatto come valido adesso
docs.text: • She said the office opens at 9.

docs.title: 3. Quando riporti notizie o affermazioni ancora attuali
docs.text:
• The spokesperson said that the company is reviewing the proposal.

A livello alto, la scelta dipende dal punto di vista del parlante.

docs.title: 🧠 Cambiamenti di pronomi, tempo e luogo
docs.text: Quando riporti qualcosa, non cambiano solo i tempi verbali.

docs.title: Pronomi
docs.text:
• I → he / she
  • we → they
  • my → his / her
  • our → their

docs.title: Espressioni di tempo
docs.text:
• now → then
  • today → that day
  • yesterday → the day before
  • tomorrow → the next day / the following day
  • last week → the week before
  • next month → the following month

docs.title: Espressioni di luogo
docs.text:
• here → there
  • this → that
  • these → those

docs.title: Esempio completo
docs.text:
• “I’m working here today.”
  • He said that he was working there that day.

docs.title: 🗣️ Reporting statements
docs.text:
Con le affermazioni usiamo spesso:

  • say (that)
  • tell + object + that
  • explain (that)
  • mention (that)
  • admit (that)
  • promise (that)

docs.title: Esempi
docs.text:
• She said that the plan was too expensive.
  • He told me that he needed help.
  • They explained that the delay was unavoidable.
  • She admitted that she had made a mistake.
  • He promised that he would call later.

docs.title: Say vs Tell
docs.text:
• say → non richiede oggetto immediato
  • tell → richiede quasi sempre una persona

✅ He said that he was tired. ✅ He told me that he was tired.

❌ He said me that...

docs.title: Yes / No questions
docs.text:
Usiamo if o whether.

  • “Are you coming?”

  • He asked if I was coming.

  • “Did you finish the task?”

  • She asked whether I had finished the task.

docs.title: Wh- questions
docs.text:
Manteniamo la parola interrogativa.

  • “Where do you live?”

  • He asked where I lived.

  • “Why did she leave?”

  • They asked why she had left.

docs.title: Attenzione all’ordine delle parole
docs.text:
Nel reported speech non usiamo l’ordine interrogativo.

❌ He asked where did I live. ✅ He asked where I lived.

docs.title: 🧾 Reporting commands, requests and warnings
docs.text:
Per ordini, richieste e avvertimenti si usa spesso:

  • tell + object + to + verb
  • ask + object + to + verb
  • warn + object + to / not to + verb

docs.title: Esempi
docs.text:
• “Close the door.” → He told me to close the door.
  • “Please send the file.” → She asked me to send the file.
  • “Don’t be late.” → He told me not to be late.
  • “Don’t touch that wire.” → She warned us not to touch that wire.

docs.title: 💡 Reporting suggestions and offers
docs.text: Non tutto si riporta con say.

docs.title: Suggest
docs.text:
• He suggested waiting a few more days.
  • She suggested that we postpone the meeting.

docs.title: Offer
docs.text:
• He offered to help.
  • She offered to drive us to the station.

docs.title: Promise
docs.text:
• They promised to send an update.
  • He promised that he would be more careful.

docs.title: 🧩 Reporting verbs più utili
docs.text: Verbo Uso tipico say dire tell dire a qualcuno explain spiegare mention menzionare admit ammettere deny negare suggest suggerire warn avvertire promise promettere offer offrire recommend raccomandare insist insistere

docs.title: Esempi
docs.text:
• She denied that she had copied the work.
  • He recommended taking a break.
  • They insisted that the figures were correct.

docs.title: 🧠 Riportare informazioni in modo naturale
docs.text:
A livello alto non riporti ogni parola.

Spesso selezioni solo ciò che conta.

docs.title: Direct speech
docs.text: “I know we wanted to finish everything today, but I’m still waiting for two documents, so I think we should move the deadline to next Monday.”

docs.title: Natural reported version
docs.text:
She explained that she was still waiting for two documents and suggested moving the deadline to the following Monday.

Questa è la vera abilità: sintetizzare senza perdere il senso.

docs.title: 🎧 Reading
docs.text: During the meeting, the manager explained that sales had improved in the previous quarter but admitted that costs were still too high. She also warned the team that some deadlines might need to be revised and suggested focusing on the most urgent tasks first.

docs.title: Comprensione
docs.text:
1. What had improved?
  2. What problem still existed?
  3. What did she warn the team about?
  4. What did she suggest?

docs.title: 🗣️ Speaking
docs.text:
Racconta una conversazione o una riunione usando almeno 5 strutture di reported speech.

Puoi parlare di:

  • una riunione di lavoro o di studio
  • una conversazione con un amico
  • un momento in cui qualcuno ti ha dato un consiglio
  • un problema che ti è stato spiegato

docs.title: Modello utile
docs.text:
• He told me that...
  • She explained that...
  • They asked whether...
  • He warned us not to...
  • She suggested...

docs.title: ✍️ Writing
docs.text: Scrivi un testo di 140–170 parole.

docs.title: Traccia
docs.text:
Racconta una riunione, una telefonata o una conversazione importante.

Nel testo includi:

  • almeno 4 reporting verbs diversi
  • almeno 1 domanda riportata
  • almeno 1 ordine, richiesta o suggerimento riportato
  • almeno 1 esempio di scelta corretta tra backshift e non-backshift

docs.title: ⚠️ Errori comuni
docs.text: Errore Corretto He said me that... He told me that... He asked where did I live He asked where I lived She suggested to wait She suggested waiting He told that he was tired He said that he was tired / He told me that... He said that he will come He said that he would come

docs.title: Completa
docs.text:
1. “I am busy.” → She said that she ___ busy.
  2. “Do you agree?” → He asked if I ___ .
  3. “Send me the file.” → She told me ___ the file.
  4. “Let’s wait.” → He suggested ___ .
  5. “We will help you.” → They promised they ___ help me.

docs.title: Trasforma
docs.text:
1. “I can’t stay long.”
  2. “Why are you late?”
  3. “Don’t open the window.”
  4. “We finished the project yesterday.”
  5. “I think the plan is too risky.”

docs.title: 🚀 Final Challenge
docs.text: Racconta un episodio complesso usando il reported speech.

docs.title: Obiettivo
docs.text:
Devi includere:

  • 6 reporting verbs diversi
  • 1 question report
  • 1 command / request report
  • 1 suggestion
  • 1 esempio di informazione sintetizzata, non tradotta parola per parola

docs.title: 🎯 Insight finale
docs.text:
A livelli alti, il reported speech non serve solo a trasformare frasi.

Serve a:

  • riportare idee
  • gestire informazioni
  • fare sintesi
  • dare il giusto tono al messaggio

Advanced English is not about repeating words. It is about reporting meaning with precision.

docs.title: 🎯 Lesson Goals
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • usare il reported speech come strumento di information management, non come semplice trasformazione grammaticale
  • distinguere tra faithful reporting, selective reporting e interpretive reporting
  • gestire con precisione backshift, no backshift, cambi di riferimento e sfumature di prospettiva
  • scegliere reporting verbs che trasmettono non solo il contenuto, ma anche attitude, intention, stance e speech act
  • riportare affermazioni, domande, ordini, suggerimenti, accuse, promesse, richieste e valutazioni con registro adeguato
  • sintetizzare riunioni, conversazioni, interviste, briefing e scambi complessi in un inglese più maturo e professionale

Questa unità non serve a “cambiare i tempi correttamente”. Serve a capire how reported speech reshapes meaning.

docs.title: 🧠 Beyond transformation: reported speech as interpretation
docs.text:
A scuola il reported speech viene spesso presentato così:

  • “I am tired.” → He said that he was tired.

Ma a livello C1 la vera domanda non è:

  • Can I backshift the tense correctly?

La vera domanda è:

  • What exactly am I choosing to transmit?
  • Am I reproducing the wording, or the meaning?
  • Am I remaining neutral, summarising, distancing, softening, or interpreting?
  • What does my choice of reporting verb imply about the original speaker?

Quando riporti il discorso, non trasferisci solo parole. Trasferisci:

  1. content → cosa è stato detto
  2. stance → come tu presenti quell’informazione
  3. evaluation → se il messaggio suona neutro, dubbio, forte, emotivo, diplomatico
  4. focus → quali elementi decidi di mettere in primo piano
  5. speaker intention → spiegare, promettere, accusare, negare, proporre, avvertire, ammettere

Il reported speech è quindi una forma di controlled reframing.

docs.title: 🧩 Direct speech, reported speech, and reporting distance
Direct speech
docs.text:
• She said, “I won’t sign this version.”

Qui hai:

  • wording esatto
  • maggiore vividness
  • effetto più drammatico o immediato

docs.title: Neutral reported speech
docs.text:
• She said that she wouldn’t sign that version.

Qui il contenuto rimane, ma il messaggio è meno teatrale e più integrato nel discorso.

docs.title: Interpretive reporting
docs.text:
• She made it clear that she was unwilling to approve the current version.

Questa non è una semplice trasformazione. È una reframing choice: il parlante interpreta il valore pragmatico del messaggio.

docs.title: 🔁 Backshift: not a rule, but a viewpoint choice
Traditional pattern
docs.text:
Quando il verbo introduttivo è al passato, spesso abbiamo:

Direct Reported Present Simple Past Simple Present Continuous Past Continuous Past Simple Past Perfect Present Perfect Past Perfect will would can could may might

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• “I work remotely.” → He said that he worked remotely.
  • “I’m waiting for a reply.” → She said that she was waiting for a reply.
  • “I finished it yesterday.” → He said that he had finished it the day before.
  • “I will call later.” → She said that she would call later.

docs.title: But at C1, backshift is not mechanical
docs.text:
Il backshift segnala spesso che stai guardando il contenuto from a past reporting point.

No-backshift, invece, può segnalare che il contenuto è:

  • ancora valido
  • presentato come ancora rilevante
  • trattato come fact rather than past statement
  • mantenuto vicino al punto di vista attuale del reporter

docs.title: Compare
docs.text:
• He said that the office was on the second floor.
  • He said that the office is on the second floor.

La seconda frase suggerisce che l’informazione è ancora vera adesso. La scelta quindi è semantica, non solo grammaticale.

docs.title: 🧠 Reference shift: pronouns, time, place, perspective
docs.text: Un buon reported speech richiede controllo dei riferimenti.

docs.title: Pronouns
docs.text:
• I → he / she
  • we → they
  • my → his / her
  • our → their

docs.title: Time
docs.text:
• now → then
  • today → that day
  • yesterday → the day before
  • tomorrow → the next / following day
  • last week → the previous week
  • next month → the following month

docs.title: Place / deixis
docs.text:
• here → there
  • this → that
  • these → those

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
• “We’re meeting here tomorrow.”
  • He said that they were meeting there the following day.

But advanced users also know that natural English sometimes keeps certain expressions unchanged if the context is still shared.

docs.title: 🗣️ Reporting verbs are never neutral
docs.text:
Molti studenti usano quasi solo say e tell. A livello C1 questo limita moltissimo precisione e nuance.

Confronta:

  • He said that the figures were wrong.
  • He pointed out that the figures were wrong.
  • He complained that the figures were wrong.
  • He admitted that the figures were wrong.
  • He insisted that the figures were wrong.
  • He claimed that the figures were wrong.

Il contenuto di base è simile, ma cambia completamente la lettura pragmatica.

docs.title: Neutral / informational
docs.text:
• say
  • tell
  • state
  • mention
  • report
  • note
  • explain
  • add

docs.title: Stronger stance / evaluation
docs.text:
• claim
  • argue
  • insist
  • maintain
  • deny
  • admit
  • concede
  • confirm
  • reveal
  • stress
  • warn
  • urge
  • announce
  • acknowledge

docs.title: Speech-act oriented
docs.text:
• promise
  • offer
  • threaten
  • suggest
  • recommend
  • advise
  • request
  • order
  • invite
  • refuse

A C1, saper scegliere il verbo giusto significa saper codificare speaker intention con precisione.

docs.title: 1. Verb + that-clause
docs.text:
• She explained that the system had failed.
  • He admitted that he had overlooked the issue.
  • They claimed that the figures were accurate.

docs.title: 2. Verb + object + that-clause
docs.text:
• He told me that the deadline had changed.
  • She assured us that everything was under control.

docs.title: 3. Verb + to-infinitive
docs.text:
• She agreed to revise the draft.
  • He promised to call later.
  • They refused to comment.
  • She offered to help.

docs.title: 4. Verb + object + to-infinitive
docs.text:
• He told me to wait.
  • She asked us to leave early.
  • They urged the team to act quickly.
  • The doctor advised him to rest.

docs.title: 5. Verb + -ing
docs.text:
• He suggested waiting.
  • She denied copying the report.
  • They admitted making a mistake.
  • He recommended taking a different approach.

docs.title: 6. Verb + preposition + -ing / noun
docs.text:
• She apologised for arriving late.
  • He complained about having too little time.
  • They accused him of leaking the document.

Il controllo di questi pattern è cruciale, perché il vero livello alto si vede spesso nella combinazione corretta verbo + struttura, non nella sola idea generale.

docs.title: ❓ Reporting questions: form, not just meaning
Yes / No questions
docs.text:
Usiamo if o whether:

  • “Are you available tomorrow?”
  • She asked if / whether I was available the next day.

docs.title: Wh- questions
docs.text:
Manteniamo la parola interrogativa:

  • “Why did the client reject it?”
  • He asked why the client had rejected it.

docs.title: The advanced point
docs.text:
Nel reported speech la domanda non mantiene la sintassi interrogativa.

❌ He asked why did the client reject it. ✅ He asked why the client had rejected it.

A livelli alti, però, la vera differenza è che puoi anche reframe the question function:

  • He wanted to know why the client had rejected it.
  • She wondered whether the delay could still be avoided.
  • They questioned whether the current strategy was sustainable.

Queste forme suonano spesso più naturali e sofisticate di un semplice asked.

docs.title: 🧾 Reporting commands, requests, warnings, and pressure
Standard patterns
docs.text:
• tell + object + to + verb
  • ask + object + to + verb
  • warn + object + not to + verb
  • advise + object + to + verb
  • urge + object + to + verb

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• She told us to leave the draft unchanged.
  • He asked me to resend the attachment.
  • They warned us not to share the file externally.
  • The consultant advised the team to simplify the process.
  • She urged him to speak to the client directly.

docs.title: Tone matters
docs.text:
Confronta:

  • He told us to stop.
  • He asked us to stop.
  • He urged us to stop.
  • He warned us to stop.

La differenza non è solo grammaticale: è una differenza di force, urgency, and stance.

docs.title: 💡 Suggesting, recommending, proposing
docs.text: Uno degli errori più comuni è trattare tutti i suggerimenti allo stesso modo.

docs.title: Useful structures
docs.text:
• He suggested waiting a bit longer.
  • She suggested that we postpone the launch.
  • They recommended reducing the scope.
  • He proposed moving the discussion to Friday.

docs.title: Wrong vs right
docs.text:
❌ He suggested to wait. ✅ He suggested waiting. ✅ He suggested that we wait.

A livello C1, puoi anche usare il reported speech per compress extended discussion into one interpretive summary:

After listening to the concerns, she suggested scaling the project back and focusing on the most realistic deliverables.

docs.title: 🧠 Selective reporting vs word-by-word reporting
docs.text: Questa è una delle competenze più importanti del livello C1.

docs.title: Word-by-word tendency
docs.text: Molti studenti provano a riportare quasi tutto in sequenza, mantenendo la struttura del parlato originale. Questo produce spesso testi pesanti, ripetitivi e poco eleganti.

docs.title: Selective reporting
docs.text:
Uno studente avanzato sa:

  • eliminare ridondanze
  • comprimere dettagli secondari
  • scegliere il reporting verb più informativo
  • organizzare logicamente il contenuto

docs.title: Original speech
docs.text: “I know we wanted to finish this week, but we’re still missing two approvals, and unless we get them by Friday, I don’t think we should commit to the launch date.”

docs.title: Weak report
docs.text: She said that they wanted to finish that week but that they were still missing two approvals and that if they didn’t get them by Friday, she didn’t think they should commit to the launch date.

docs.title: Stronger report
docs.text:
She explained that two approvals were still missing and argued that the team should avoid committing to the launch date unless they were received by Friday.

La seconda versione è più sintetica, più precisa e più professionale.

docs.title: 🧠 Reported speech and stance
docs.text: Quando riporti, stai anche prendendo posizione rispetto a ciò che è stato detto.

docs.title: Compare
docs.text:
• He said that the plan would work.
  • He claimed that the plan would work.
  • He argued that the plan would work.
  • He maintained that the plan would work.
  • He admitted that the plan might not work.

Ogni verbo orienta il lettore in modo diverso.

docs.title: Practical consequence
docs.text:
Nel writing professionale, accademico o giornalistico, la scelta del reporting verb è spesso un modo per segnalare:

  • affidabilità
  • distanza critica
  • neutralità
  • scetticismo
  • allineamento o disallineamento

docs.title: 🧩 Reported speech in professional and academic settings
Meeting summary
docs.text:
• The director stated that the budget would remain unchanged.
  • Several team members pointed out that the timeline was unrealistic.
  • The client requested that the revised proposal be submitted by Monday.

docs.title: Analytical / academic tone
docs.text:
• The author argues that the current framework is insufficient.
  • Recent studies have shown that the effect may be overstated.
  • It has been suggested that the results were influenced by external factors.

docs.title: News-style reporting
docs.text:
• Officials confirmed that an investigation was under way.
  • Witnesses reported seeing smoke shortly before the explosion.
  • The company denied having breached any regulations.

A C1, reported speech è anche una competenza di genre awareness.

docs.title: 🎧 Reading
docs.text: In the briefing, the operations manager acknowledged that the rollout had taken longer than expected but insisted that the delays had prevented more serious problems later on. She also pointed out that several assumptions had been made too early and advised the regional teams to review the implementation schedule before committing to any new deadlines.

docs.title: Analyse the discourse
docs.text:
1. Which verb shows openness about a problem?
  2. Which verb shows a defensive or assertive stance?
  3. Which phrase suggests criticism without sounding aggressive?
  4. Which verb introduces practical advice?

docs.title: Task 1 — Recount a difficult conversation
docs.text:
Racconta una conversazione complessa avuta al lavoro, all’università o in un contesto personale.

Devi includere:

  • almeno 6 reporting verbs diversi
  • almeno 1 question report
  • almeno 1 request / order / advice report
  • almeno 1 example of selective reporting

docs.title: Task 2 — Meeting summary
docs.text:
Immagina di dover spiegare a un collega assente cosa è successo in una riunione.

Obiettivo:

  • non ripetere tutto
  • organizzare le informazioni
  • segnalare accordi, obiezioni, suggerimenti e decisioni

Useful frames:

  • She made it clear that...
  • He pointed out that...
  • They questioned whether...
  • The team agreed that...
  • She warned us not to...
  • He denied that...
  • They proposed...

docs.title: ✍️ Writing
docs.text: Scrivi un testo di 180–220 parole.

docs.title: Prompt
docs.text:
Write a formal summary of a meeting, interview, discussion, or disagreement.

Your text should:

  • report information accurately
  • include a range of reporting verbs
  • show clear control of time reference
  • distinguish between facts, opinions, concerns, and recommendations
  • sound natural and professionally structured

docs.title: Success criteria
docs.text:
Include at least:

  • 8 different reporting structures
  • 1 example of no backshift with a clear reason
  • 1 reported question
  • 1 reported request / order / suggestion
  • 1 example where you compress long direct speech into a concise summary

docs.title: ⚠️ Error Clinic
docs.text: Errore Correzione Nota He said me that... He told me that... tell richiede un oggetto She suggested to wait She suggested waiting suggest + -ing He asked where did I go He asked where I had gone niente inversione interrogativa He said that he will come He said that he would come backshift standard She told that the plan was risky She said that the plan was risky / She told us that... tell richiede un destinatario He explained me the problem He explained the problem to me pattern del verbo They insisted to leave early They insisted on leaving early / insisted that we should leave early struttura diversa

docs.title: Subtle C1 pitfalls
docs.text:
1. usare un reporting verb troppo forte o troppo debole rispetto al contesto
  2. fare backshift anche quando il contenuto è chiaramente ancora vero
  3. non distinguere tra reporting meaning e reporting wording
  4. produrre sequenze troppo letterali, poco sintetiche e poco idiomatiche

docs.title: A. Choose the best reporting verb
docs.text:
1. He ______ that the figures had been checked twice. (explained / complained / denied)
  2. She ______ that she had overlooked the attachment. (admitted / promised / ordered)
  3. They ______ us not to share the draft outside the team. (warned / suggested / mentioned)
  4. He ______ whether the deadline could still be changed. (wondered / insisted / announced)

docs.title: B. Rewrite naturally
docs.text:
Transform without sounding literal.

  1. “I’m not saying the strategy is wrong, but I think we should reconsider the timing.”
  2. “Can you send me the revised version by the end of the day?”
  3. “Don’t mention this to the client yet.”
  4. “We’ve been trying to solve the issue for weeks, but we still don’t have enough data.”

docs.title: C. Compress the message
docs.text:
Turn this into one strong reported sentence:

“I know everyone is under pressure, and I appreciate the effort, but unless we clarify responsibilities now, we’re going to run into exactly the same problem again next month.”

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text:
You attended a tense strategy meeting. Different people:

  • defended the current plan
  • questioned the timeline
  • admitted some errors
  • suggested alternatives
  • warned about risks
  • asked for more data
  • promised follow-up actions

docs.title: Your task
docs.text:
Write or say a clear, high-level summary of what happened.

To succeed, you must show:

  • precision in reporting verbs
  • control of time reference
  • good balance between detail and synthesis
  • awareness of stance and tone
  • language that sounds genuinely C1, not translated word by word

docs.title: 🎯 Final Insight
docs.text:
At lower levels, reported speech is mostly about grammar. At C1, it becomes a tool for:

  • reframing meaning
  • managing information
  • positioning yourself toward what others said
  • writing and speaking with analytical control

Advanced users do not merely repeat what was said. They decide how it should be represented.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità saprai:

  • collegare idee complesse in modo più chiaro e maturo
  • usare connettori diversi per addizione, contrasto, causa, risultato, concessione e conclusione
  • evitare ripetizioni e collegamenti troppo elementari come and / but / because / so
  • costruire paragrafi e interventi orali più coerenti
  • organizzare un ragionamento in inglese con maggiore controllo

Questa unità ti aiuta a fare un salto importante: non dire solo frasi corrette, ma costruire discorsi ben strutturati.

docs.title: 🧠 Il salto da B2 a C1
docs.text:
A livelli più bassi, molti studenti collegano le idee così:

  • I like the course because it is useful and it is practical.
  • It is difficult but it is interesting.

Queste frasi sono corrette, ma spesso risultano poco varie.

A livello C1, invece, inizi a gestire relazioni più precise tra le idee:

  • The course is demanding; however, it is extremely practical.
  • Although the material is complex, the explanations are clear.
  • The process took longer than expected. As a result, the launch was postponed.

Il punto non è usare parole “più difficili” a caso. Il punto è scegliere il connettore giusto per esprimere il rapporto logico corretto.

docs.title: 1. Addizione
docs.text:
Servono ad aggiungere un’idea.

  • moreover
  • furthermore
  • in addition
  • also
  • besides

docs.title: Esempi
docs.text:
• The proposal is realistic. Moreover, it is financially sustainable.
  • The course improves grammar. In addition, it helps students speak more naturally.
  • The task was challenging. Besides, we had very little time.

docs.title: 2. Contrasto
docs.text:
Servono a contrapporre due idee.

  • however
  • nevertheless
  • on the other hand
  • whereas
  • while

docs.title: Esempi
docs.text:
• The plan is ambitious. However, it may be too expensive.
  • The first option is cheaper. On the other hand, it is less flexible.
  • Some people prefer remote work, whereas others work better in an office.

docs.title: 3. Concessione
docs.text:
Servono a mostrare contrasto con un’idea che “ammetti” prima.

  • although
  • even though
  • though
  • despite
  • in spite of

docs.title: Esempi
docs.text:
• Although the schedule was tight, the team met the deadline.
  • Even though I was tired, I finished the report.
  • Despite the delay, the event was successful.
  • In spite of the criticism, the project continued.

docs.title: 4. Causa
docs.text:
Servono a spiegare perché qualcosa accade.

  • because
  • since
  • as
  • due to
  • owing to

docs.title: Esempi
docs.text:
• We changed the strategy because the first one was ineffective.
  • Since demand increased, the company hired more staff.
  • The flight was cancelled due to bad weather.

docs.title: 5. Risultato / conseguenza
docs.text:
Servono a mostrare l’effetto di una causa.

  • therefore
  • as a result
  • consequently
  • for this reason
  • so

docs.title: Esempi
docs.text:
• The data was incomplete; therefore, the analysis was limited.
  • He was not well prepared. As a result, the presentation was confusing.
  • The instructions were unclear. Consequently, several mistakes were made.

docs.title: 6. Conclusione / sintesi
docs.text:
Servono a chiudere un discorso.

  • in conclusion
  • to sum up
  • overall
  • all things considered

docs.title: Esempi
docs.text:
• In conclusion, the solution is effective but expensive.
  • Overall, the experience was positive.
  • To sum up, the advantages clearly outweigh the disadvantages.

docs.title: ⚠️ Connector o struttura grammaticale?
docs.text: Non tutti funzionano allo stesso modo.

docs.title: Connettori che introducono una frase completa
docs.text:
• although + clause
  • however + clause
  • therefore + clause

✅ Although the task was difficult, we completed it. ✅ The task was difficult. However, we completed it.

docs.title: Connettori che richiedono un nome o una forma in -ing
docs.text:
• despite + noun / -ing
  • in spite of + noun / -ing
  • due to + noun

✅ Despite the pressure, she remained calm. ✅ Despite working long hours, he stayed productive.

❌ Despite it was difficult...

Per quella frase devi usare:

✅ Although it was difficult...

docs.title: 1. Because + so
docs.text: ❌ Because it was late, so we left. ✅ Because it was late, we left. ✅ It was late, so we left.

docs.title: 2. Although + but
docs.text: ❌ Although it was expensive, but I bought it. ✅ Although it was expensive, I bought it. ✅ It was expensive, but I bought it.

docs.title: 3. Despite + frase completa
docs.text: ❌ Despite it was raining, we went out. ✅ Although it was raining, we went out. ✅ Despite the rain, we went out.

docs.title: 4. Ripetere sempre gli stessi connettori
docs.text:
Molti studenti usano quasi solo:

  • and
  • but
  • because
  • so

A livello C1 devi ampliare il repertorio.

docs.title: Base
docs.text: • I like the course because it is useful and it is practical.

docs.title: Più naturale
docs.text: • I like the course because it is useful. Moreover, it is highly practical.

docs.title: Base
docs.text: • It was difficult but I finished it.

docs.title: Più naturale
docs.text:
• Although it was difficult, I managed to finish it.
  • It was difficult; however, I still managed to finish it.

docs.title: Base
docs.text: • We didn’t have enough information, so the decision was delayed.

docs.title: Più naturale
docs.text:
• We did not have enough information. As a result, the decision was delayed.
  • The available information was insufficient; therefore, the decision was postponed.

docs.title: 🧱 Costruire un paragrafo ben collegato
docs.text:
Un paragrafo efficace spesso segue questa logica:

  1. idea principale
  2. sviluppo / spiegazione
  3. contrasto o limite
  4. conclusione

docs.title: Esempio
docs.text:
Learning English is increasingly important in both academic and professional contexts. In addition, it allows people to communicate more easily across cultures. However, reaching a high level requires time, patience and consistent practice. In conclusion, although the process can be demanding, the long-term benefits are considerable.

Osserva che ogni frase ha un rapporto logico chiaro con la precedente.

docs.title: 🎧 Reading focus
docs.text:
Leggi il testo e nota i connettori.

Many people believe that remote work is the future. In addition, companies often see it as a way to reduce costs. However, working remotely is not ideal for everyone, as some employees find collaboration more difficult online. Overall, the best solution may be a flexible model that combines both approaches.

docs.title: Domande
docs.text:
1. Quale connettore aggiunge un secondo vantaggio?
  2. Quale introduce un limite o contrasto?
  3. Quale espressione sintetizza la posizione finale del testo?

docs.title: 🗣️ Speaking task
docs.text:
Parla per 2–3 minuti su uno dei seguenti temi:

  • the advantages and disadvantages of studying online
  • working from home vs working in an office
  • why learning English is important today

docs.title: Devi usare almeno:
docs.text:
• 1 connettore di addizione
  • 1 di contrasto
  • 1 di causa o risultato
  • 1 conclusione

docs.title: ✍️ Writing task
docs.text:
Scrivi un testo di 150–180 parole su questo tema:

Is online learning better than traditional classroom learning?

docs.title: Includi:
docs.text:
• un’introduzione con la tua idea principale
  • almeno un vantaggio
  • almeno uno svantaggio
  • una conclusione chiara
  • almeno 5 connectors diversi

docs.title: 🧪 Practice
docs.text:
Completa con un connettore adatto.

  1. The course is demanding. ____, it is extremely useful.
  2. ____ the price was high, many people bought the product.
  3. The instructions were unclear. ____, several mistakes were made.
  4. The company expanded quickly. ____, it opened two new offices.
  5. ____, the project was successful despite the difficulties.

docs.title: 🔄 Transformation
docs.text:
Riscrivi le frasi usando il connettore indicato.

  1. It was expensive but I bought it. (although)
  2. We missed the train, so we arrived late. (therefore)
  3. The plan is practical and it is affordable. (moreover)
  4. It rained, but the event continued. (however)
  5. The team was under pressure, but they finished on time. (despite / although)

docs.title: ✅ Review finale
docs.text:
Controlla se sai:

  • distinguere addizione, contrasto, causa, risultato, concessione e conclusione
  • usare correttamente although e however
  • non confondere despite con although
  • costruire frasi e paragrafi con relazioni logiche chiare
  • evitare ripetizioni e connettori troppo elementari

Se riesci a farlo con buona varietà e precisione, stai sviluppando una vera competenza da C1.

docs.title: 🎯 Lesson Goals
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • usare i connectors come strumenti di thought organisation, non come semplici parole da inserire tra due frasi
  • distinguere tra logical relation, rhetorical effect e register choice
  • collegare idee con maggiore precisione attraverso addition, contrast, concession, cause, result, reformulation, exemplification e conclusion
  • costruire paragrafi e interventi orali con una progressione argomentativa più matura
  • evitare sia l’eccesso di semplicità (and / but / because) sia l’uso artificiale di connettori “sofisticati” ma mal collocati
  • controllare il ritmo del discorso scritto e parlato in modo davvero coerente con il livello C1
