docs.text: Hi, my name is ... I'm ... years old and I'm from ... I live in ... and I work / study ... Every day I wake up at ... and I go to ... by ... In my room / in my area there is ... and there are ... I can ..., but I can't ... very well. What about you? Where are you from? What do you do?

docs.title: ✍️ Writing Task – Guided Production
docs.text: Scrivi un testo di 120–150 parole. Il tema è:

docs.title: About me: my life, my routine and my town
docs.text:
Il testo deve includere:

  • who you are
  • where you are from / where you live
  • what you do
  • your typical day
  • a short description of your room or town
  • what you can and can't do
  • one personal preference or opinion

docs.title: Suggested structure
docs.text:
Paragraph 1 – Personal profile Name, age, country, city, job/studies

Paragraph 2 – Daily routine Morning, transport, work/study, evening

Paragraph 3 – Description + ability Room/town, places or objects, abilities

docs.title: Useful connectors
docs.text:
• and
  • but
  • then
  • after that
  • usually
  • also

docs.title: 🛠️ Error Clinic – Typical Italian Learner Problems
1. Age
docs.text: ❌ I have 28 years. ✅ I am 28 years old.

docs.title: 2. Third person singular
docs.text: ❌ She work in a bank. ✅ She works in a bank.

docs.title: 3. Questions without auxiliary
docs.text:
❌ Where you live? ✅ Where do you live?

❌ What he does? ✅ What does he do?

docs.title: 4. Incorrect modal structure
docs.text:
❌ I can to swim. ✅ I can swim.

❌ She doesn't can drive. ✅ She can't drive.

docs.title: 5. Singular/plural confusion with there is / there are
docs.text: ❌ There is two parks near my house. ✅ There are two parks near my house.

docs.title: 6. Overuse of "is" before main verbs
docs.text:
❌ He is work in an office. ✅ He works in an office.

👉 Se questi errori compaiono ancora spesso, il passaggio ad A2 è possibile solo dopo un breve consolidamento.

docs.title: 🔊 Pronunciation and Fluency Review
docs.text:
Ripeti in gruppi di senso, non parola per parola:

  • I'm from Italy and I live in Turin.
  • I work in a café near the station.
  • There are two chairs next to the desk.
  • Can you speak English quite well?
  • I can cook, but I can't drive yet.

docs.title: Mini fluency drill
docs.text:
Leggi tre volte questa micro-presentazione, ogni volta un po’ più fluentemente:

Hi, I'm Sara. I'm 26 years old and I'm from Italy. I work in a bookshop. Every day I wake up at 7, have breakfast and go to work by bus. In my room there is a desk and there are a lot of books. I can speak English a little and I can cook quite well, but I can't drive.

docs.title: 📋 Final Self-Assessment
docs.text:
Segna Yes / Almost / Not yet.

Can do statement Yes Almost Not yet I can introduce myself clearly. I can ask basic personal questions. I can describe my daily routine. I can describe a room or area with there is / there are. I can talk about abilities with can / can't. I can write a short text about my life. I can avoid the most common A1 grammar mistakes.

docs.title: How to interpret your result
docs.text:
• Mostly Yes → you are ready to move into A2 work
  • Many Almost → you can continue, but review weak points in speaking and writing
  • Many Not yet → repeat selected A1 units before moving on

docs.title: 🚀 Bridge to A2
docs.text:
Nel livello A2 inizierai a fare qualcosa di nuovo: non solo descrivere il presente, ma anche parlare del passato, organizzare meglio il discorso e gestire scambi più naturali.

Per arrivarci bene, alla fine di A1 devi avere tre basi forti:

  1. frasi corrette
  2. domande corrette
  3. mini-produzioni continue di 1–2 minuti

Se queste basi ci sono, A2 diventa molto più fluido e molto meno frustrante.

A1 ends when basic language becomes usable, not when rules are simply memorised.

docs.title: 🔵 Livello A2 – Elementary
docs.text:
Benvenuto nel livello A2.

In questo blocco inizi a usare l’inglese in modo più attivo e autonomo. Dopo aver costruito le basi del livello A1, qui impari a parlare con più sicurezza di esperienze, azioni in corso, progetti futuri, confronti e situazioni quotidiane più articolate.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo del livello
docs.text:
Alla fine del livello A2 sarai in grado di:

  • descrivere azioni presenti e situazioni temporanee
  • raccontare eventi passati in modo semplice ma chiaro
  • parlare di piani futuri, intenzioni e decisioni spontanee
  • confrontare persone, luoghi e oggetti
  • usare un lessico più ricco in contesti reali come viaggi, cibo, acquisti e vita quotidiana
  • sostenere brevi conversazioni con maggiore fluidità

docs.title: 🧭 Due modalità di studio
docs.text: Questo corso offre due modalità selezionabili dal toggle in alto nel sito:

docs.title: Base
docs.text:
La modalità Base è pensata per chi vuole:

  • studiare in modo progressivo e lineare
  • consolidare le strutture principali del livello A2
  • usare esempi chiari e controllati
  • acquisire sicurezza prima di passare a contenuti più densi

docs.title: Professional
docs.text:
La modalità Professional è pensata per chi vuole un percorso più ricco e strutturato, con:

  • spiegazioni più approfondite
  • più lessico e collocazioni naturali
  • confronto tra strutture simili
  • errori tipici degli italiani
  • speaking drills, writing guidato, review e self-check finali

👉 Puoi passare da una modalità all’altra in qualsiasi momento usando il selettore BASE / PRO nella barra superiore.

docs.title: 📚 Cosa imparerai in A2
docs.text:
Nel livello A2 lavorerai su:

  • present continuous
  • contrasto tra present simple e present continuous
  • past simple
  • lessico e narrazione su viaggi e vacanze
  • comparativi e superlativi
  • cibo, shopping e quantità
  • quantifiers
  • futuro con will e going to
  • present perfect per esperienze
  • revisione integrata delle strutture principali del livello

docs.title: ⚠️ Cosa aspettarti a questo livello
docs.text:
A livello A2 è normale:

  • capire molto meglio l’inglese scritto che quello parlato veloce
  • riuscire a comunicare, ma con esitazioni
  • confondere ancora tempi verbali simili
  • fare errori con verbi irregolari, preposizioni e quantificatori
  • avere bisogno di molto riuso prima di fissare bene le strutture

Anche questo è del tutto normale: stai passando da un inglese “di sopravvivenza” a un inglese più realmente comunicativo.

docs.title: 💡 Come usare bene questo livello
docs.text:
Per ottenere risultati migliori:

  • confronta sempre i tempi verbali, non studiarli come blocchi isolati
  • ripeti ad alta voce esempi e mini-dialoghi
  • trasforma ogni esercizio in frasi personali
  • usa la modalità Professional quando vuoi consolidare davvero accuratezza e naturalezza
  • ripassa regolarmente le unità precedenti, soprattutto dopo le review

docs.title: 📖 Unità del livello A2
docs.text:
1. Present Continuous
  2. Present Simple vs Present Continuous
  3. Past Simple
  4. Travel and Holidays
  5. Comparatives and Superlatives
  6. Food and Shopping
  7. Quantifiers
  8. Future: Will / Going to
  9. Experiences (Have you ever...?)
  10. A2 Review

docs.title: ✅ Traguardo finale A2
docs.text:
Alla fine di questo blocco dovresti riuscire a:

  • raccontare esperienze passate e recenti in modo semplice
  • parlare di programmi futuri e intenzioni personali
  • descrivere differenze e fare confronti
  • gestire conversazioni quotidiane più ricche su viaggio, cibo, acquisti e vita personale
  • sostenere brevi scambi con maggiore continuità e meno dipendenza dalla traduzione mentale

👉 Inizia con Unità 11 oppure usa il toggle in alto per scegliere la modalità di studio che preferisci.

docs.title: Unità 11 – Present Continuous
docs.text:
Modalità Base attiva. Questa unità ti aiuta a usare il Present Continuous per parlare di azioni in corso, cioè di ciò che sta succedendo adesso o in questo momento. Per la versione estesa usa il toggle BASE / PRO nella barra in alto.

Modalità Professional attiva. In questa unità non impari solo una nuova forma verbale: impari a scegliere tra routine e azione in corso, a gestire la forma -ing con precisione e a descrivere situazioni reali in modo più naturale e controllato.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • usare il Present Continuous in forma affermativa, negativa e interrogativa
  • descrivere azioni in corso nel momento in cui parli
  • fare domande come What are you doing?
  • distinguere tra una routine abituale e un’azione temporanea

docs.title: Forma affermativa
docs.text:
Usiamo il Present Continuous per parlare di qualcosa che sta succedendo adesso.

Subject + to be + verb-ing

  • I am working.
  • You are studying.
  • She is reading.
  • They are watching TV.

👉 Il verbo principale prende la forma -ing.

docs.title: Forma negativa
docs.text:
Subject + to be + not + verb-ing

  • I am not working.
  • He is not sleeping.
  • We are not studying.

Forme contratte più comuni:

  • I'm not
  • isn't
  • aren't

Esempi:

  • She isn't listening.
  • They aren't eating.

docs.title: Forma interrogativa
docs.text:
To be + subject + verb-ing?

  • Are you working?
  • Is she studying?
  • Are they coming?

Risposte brevi:

  • Yes, I am.
  • No, I'm not.
  • Yes, she is.
  • No, they aren't.

docs.title: 🔁 Present Simple vs Present Continuous
docs.text:
Confronta:

  • I work every day. → routine
  • I am working now. → adesso

Altri esempi:

  • She studies in the evening. → abitudine
  • She is studying now. → in questo momento

👉 now / right now / at the moment aiutano spesso a capire che serve il Present Continuous.

docs.title: Verbi comuni
docs.text:
• work
  • study
  • eat
  • read
  • write
  • sleep
  • watch
  • talk
  • listen
  • cook
  • drive
  • wait

docs.title: Time expressions
docs.text:
• now
  • right now
  • at the moment
  • today
  • this week

docs.title: ✍️ Spelling con -ing
docs.text:
Osserva questi cambiamenti:

  • work → working
  • read → reading
  • make → making
  • write → writing
  • sit → sitting
  • run → running

Regole base:

  • di solito aggiungi -ing: work → working
  • se il verbo finisce in -e, togli la -e: make → making
  • in alcuni verbi brevi raddoppi la consonante finale: sit → sitting

docs.title: 🎧 Listening / Reading
docs.text:
Dialogue:

A: Hi Anna, what are you doing? B: I'm doing my English homework. A: Are you studying alone? B: No, I'm studying with my brother. A: Is he doing the same exercise? B: No, he isn't. He's writing an email.

docs.title: Comprensione
docs.text:
1. What is Anna doing?
  2. Is she studying alone?
  3. What is her brother doing?

docs.title: 🗣️ Speaking
docs.text:
Ripeti:

  • I am studying now.
  • She is writing an email.
  • We are waiting for the bus.
  • They aren't sleeping.
  • Are you listening?

Ora completa oralmente:

  • I am ___ right now.
  • My friend is ___ at the moment.
  • We are ___ today.

Fai domande:

  • What are you doing?
  • Are you working now?
  • Is your teacher speaking English?

docs.title: ✍️ Writing
docs.text:
Scrivi 6–8 frasi su cosa sta succedendo intorno a te adesso.

Puoi includere:

  • cosa stai facendo tu
  • cosa fanno altre persone
  • cosa non sta succedendo
  • dove ti trovi

docs.title: Modello utile
docs.text: I am sitting at my desk. I am studying English. My phone is not ringing. People are walking outside. My family is having dinner.

docs.title: 🔊 Pronunciation
docs.text:
Attenzione alla finale -ing:

  • working → /ˈwɜːrkɪŋ/
  • reading → /ˈriːdɪŋ/
  • sitting → /ˈsɪtɪŋ/

👉 Il suono finale è /ɪŋ/, non “ingh” italiano.

docs.title: ⚠️ Errori comuni (italiani)
docs.text:
❌ I am work now. ✅ I am working now.

❌ She working. ✅ She is working.

❌ Are studying? ✅ Are you studying?

❌ I work now. (se parli di questo momento) ✅ I am working now.

docs.title: ✅ Review & Practice
docs.text:
Completa con la forma corretta del verbo.

  1. I am ___ (read).
  2. She is ___ (study).
  3. They are ___ (watch) TV.
  4. We are ___ (sit) in class.
  5. He is ___ (make) dinner.
  6. Are you ___ (listen)?

docs.title: 🔄 Trasforma
docs.text:
Trasforma usando il Present Continuous.

  1. I work. → (now) ______
  2. She reads a book. → (at the moment) ______
  3. They eat lunch. → (right now) ______
  4. He writes an email. → negativa ______
  5. You study English. → domanda ______

docs.title: 🚀 Mini task
docs.text:
Guarda una stanza, una foto o una scena intorno a te e descrivi 3–5 azioni in corso.

Esempio:

  • A woman is talking on the phone.
  • Two children are playing.
  • A man is drinking coffee.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • costruire il Present Continuous con maggiore sicurezza in frasi affermative, negative e interrogative
  • distinguere con chiarezza tra Present Simple e Present Continuous
  • descrivere scene, attività temporanee e azioni in corso con lessico più ricco
  • evitare gli errori più frequenti su to be, verb-ing e ordine delle parole
  • sostenere brevi micro-interazioni realistiche usando domande e risposte spontanee

docs.title: 🧭 Quando usiamo il Present Continuous
docs.text: Il Present Continuous si usa soprattutto per tre funzioni centrali a questo livello.

docs.title: 1. Azioni in corso nel momento in cui parli
docs.text:
• I'm talking to you now.
  • She is studying at the moment.
  • They are waiting outside.

docs.title: 2. Situazioni temporanee, non permanenti
docs.text:
• I'm staying with a friend this week.
  • He is working from home today.
  • We are taking an online course this month.

docs.title: 3. Scene che osservi e descrivi
docs.text:
• A man is running in the park.
  • Two children are playing near the door.
  • Someone is speaking on the phone.

👉 A questo stadio, questa terza funzione è molto importante perché allena il passaggio da frasi isolate a descrizione continua.

docs.title: Forma affermativa
docs.text:
Subject + am / is / are + verb-ing

  • I am reading.
  • You are listening.
  • He is cooking.
  • We are waiting.

docs.title: Forma negativa
docs.text:
Subject + am / is / are + not + verb-ing

  • I'm not reading.
  • She isn't listening.
  • They aren't waiting.

docs.title: Forma interrogativa
docs.text:
Am / Is / Are + subject + verb-ing?

  • Are you reading?
  • Is he cooking?
  • Are they waiting?

docs.title: Risposte brevi
docs.text:
• Yes, I am.
  • No, I'm not.
  • Yes, he is.
  • No, they aren't.

👉 L'errore più comune non è il verbo principale, ma dimenticare il verbo to be.

docs.title: ✍️ Formazione della forma -ing
docs.text: Questa parte merita attenzione, perché qui nascono moltissimi errori ortografici.

docs.title: Regola 1: aggiungi semplicemente -ing
docs.text:
• work → working
  • read → reading
  • play → playing
  • listen → listening

docs.title: Regola 2: togli la -e finale
docs.text:
• make → making
  • write → writing
  • come → coming
  • take → taking

docs.title: Regola 3: raddoppia la consonante finale in alcuni verbi brevi
docs.text:
• sit → sitting
  • run → running
  • swim → swimming
  • stop → stopping

docs.title: Nota utile
docs.text:
Non tutti i verbi finali in consonante raddoppiano. Per esempio:

  • open → opening
  • visit → visiting
  • happen → happening

👉 All'inizio conviene memorizzare i casi più frequenti, non tutte le eccezioni teoriche.

docs.title: 🔁 Present Simple vs Present Continuous
docs.text: Questa è la vera competenza-obiettivo della unità.

docs.title: Present Simple = abitudine / routine / fatto generale
docs.text:
• I work in a bank.
  • She studies English every Tuesday.
  • They live in Rome.

docs.title: Present Continuous = adesso / periodo temporaneo / scena in corso
docs.text:
• I am working at home today.
  • She is studying right now.
  • They are living with friends this month.

docs.title: Coppie a confronto
docs.text:
• He works in a restaurant. → lavoro abituale

  • He is working in the kitchen now. → azione in corso

  • I read before bed. → abitudine

  • I am reading an email now. → adesso

  • We go to the office every day. → routine

  • We are going to a meeting right now. → azione in corso

👉 Molti studenti italiani usano troppo il Present Simple perché in italiano il presente copre spesso entrambe le funzioni. In inglese la distinzione va resa in modo più esplicito.

docs.title: Action verbs for everyday situations
docs.text:
• work
  • study
  • read
  • write
  • cook
  • drive
  • wait
  • look for
  • listen to
  • talk to
  • clean
  • watch
  • use
  • carry
  • wear

docs.title: Time markers
docs.text:
• now
  • right now
  • at the moment
  • today
  • this week
  • these days

docs.title: Useful classroom / work phrases
docs.text:
• I'm taking notes.
  • She's checking her email.
  • We're working on a project.
  • They're waiting for the lesson to start.
  • I'm not using my phone.

docs.title: 🎧 Reading – Scene Description
docs.text:
Leggi il testo.

It is 8:30 in the morning and the office is already busy. Julia is talking to a client on the phone. Her colleague Mark is answering emails and drinking coffee at his desk. Two interns are carrying some boxes into the meeting room. The manager isn't sitting in his office; he is speaking to a visitor near the reception desk. Outside the building, people are waiting for the bus and a man is riding his bike in the rain.

docs.title: Comprensione
docs.text:
1. What is Julia doing?
  2. What is Mark doing?
  3. Where are the interns going?
  4. Is the manager sitting in his office?
  5. What is happening outside the building?

docs.title: Language notice
docs.text:
Nel testo trovi:

  • azioni singole in corso
  • più persone descritte nello stesso momento
  • una forma negativa naturale: isn't sitting

Questa è la base per imparare a descrivere una scena in modo fluido.

docs.title: A. Controlled practice
docs.text:
Rispondi oralmente.

  1. What are you doing right now?
  2. Are you sitting or standing?
  3. Is anyone talking near you?
  4. What are people in your house doing?
  5. What are you wearing today?

docs.title: B. Pair-work simulation
docs.text:
Studente A domanda, Studente B risponde.

  • What are you doing at the moment?
  • Are you working on something important?
  • Is your phone ringing?
  • Are you listening to music?
  • What are your friends doing today?

Poi invertite i ruoli.

docs.title: C. Scene narration
docs.text:
Guarda una foto o immagina una scena in un caffè, in un ufficio o in una stazione. Parla per 45–60 secondi e descrivi cosa sta succedendo.

Per aiutarti:

  • One person is ...
  • Another person is ...
  • Some people are ...
  • Nobody is ...
  • In the background, ...

docs.title: Task 1 – Around me now
docs.text:
Scrivi 8–10 frasi su ciò che sta succedendo intorno a te in questo momento.

Devi includere:

  • almeno 5 verbi diversi
  • almeno 1 frase negativa
  • almeno 1 domanda
  • almeno 2 time markers

docs.title: Task 2 – Temporary situation
docs.text:
Scrivi un breve testo su una situazione temporanea della tua vita.

Esempi:

  • this week
  • these days
  • this month
  • today

Modello:

This week I am working on a new project. I am spending a lot of time at my computer. I am also studying English in the evening. My colleagues are helping me, but I am not sleeping very much.

docs.title: 🔊 Pronunciation
docs.text: Lavora soprattutto su due punti.

docs.title: 1. Finale -ing
docs.text:
• working
  • talking
  • studying
  • waiting

Il finale è /ɪŋ/.

docs.title: 2. Contrazioni naturali
docs.text:
Nel parlato reale sentirai spesso:

  • I'm working.
  • She's waiting.
  • They're coming.
  • He isn't listening.

👉 Allenati a produrre anche le contrazioni, non solo la forma completa.

docs.title: Errore 1: dimenticare to be
docs.text: ❌ She studying now. ✅ She is studying now.

docs.title: Errore 2: usare il verbo base dopo be
docs.text: ❌ I am study English. ✅ I am studying English.

docs.title: Errore 3: usare il Present Simple per un'azione in corso
docs.text: ❌ I work now. ✅ I am working now.

docs.title: Errore 4: spelling della forma -ing
docs.text: ❌ writting / makeing / swiming ✅ writing / making / swimming

docs.title: Errore 5: ordine errato nella domanda
docs.text: ❌ What you are doing? ✅ What are you doing?

docs.title: A. Completa con la forma corretta
docs.text:
1. She is ___ (write) an email.
  2. We are ___ (wait) for the teacher.
  3. I am ___ (sit) near the window.
  4. He isn't ___ (work) today.
  5. Are they ___ (study) for the test?
  6. My friends are ___ (run) in the park.

docs.title: B. Scegli: Present Simple o Present Continuous?
docs.text:
1. I usually work / am working from 9 to 5.
  2. Today I work / am working from home.
  3. She reads / is reading a lot in general.
  4. She reads / is reading a report right now.
  5. We go / are going to the gym every Monday.
  6. We go / are going to the gym now.

docs.title: C. Trasforma
docs.text:
1. He works in an office. → (now)
  2. They are studying. → negative
  3. You are listening to music. → question
  4. I write emails every day. → (at the moment)
  5. She is making dinner. → question

docs.title: ✅ Self-check
docs.text:
Metti una spunta se sai fare queste cose:

  • [ ] descrivere ciò che stai facendo adesso
  • [ ] fare domande corrette con What are you doing?
  • [ ] usare bene am / is / are nel Present Continuous
  • [ ] formare correttamente i verbi in -ing più frequenti
  • [ ] distinguere tra routine e azione in corso
  • [ ] descrivere una scena con più persone e più azioni

docs.title: 🚀 Mini task finale
docs.text:
Osserva un luogo reale o immaginario: una sala d'attesa, un bar, una classe, un ufficio.

Scrivi o registra una descrizione di 60–90 parole.

Devi includere:

  • almeno 4 persone o soggetti
  • almeno 6 verbi al Present Continuous
  • almeno 1 frase negativa
  • almeno 1 confronto con una routine abituale

Esempio di chiusura:

Normally the office is quiet in the early morning, but today everyone is working quickly and people are moving from one room to another.

docs.title: Unità 12 – Present Simple vs Present Continuous
docs.text:
Modalità Base attiva. Questa unità ti aiuta a distinguere tra Present Simple e Present Continuous: il primo per routine, abitudini e fatti generali; il secondo per azioni in corso o situazioni temporanee. Per la versione estesa usa il toggle BASE / PRO nella barra in alto.

Modalità Professional attiva. In questa unità sviluppi una competenza decisiva: scegliere tra Present Simple e Present Continuous non in modo meccanico, ma in base al significato. L’obiettivo non è solo “sapere la regola”, ma capire se stai parlando di una routine, di una situazione stabile, di un’azione in corso o di un periodo temporaneo.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • distinguere tra routine e azioni in corso
  • scegliere tra Present Simple e Present Continuous
  • usare correttamente avverbi ed espressioni di tempo
  • evitare gli errori più comuni quando parli di “adesso” e di “sempre”

docs.title: Present Simple
docs.text:
Usiamo il Present Simple per:

  • abitudini
  • routine
  • fatti generali
  • situazioni stabili

Esempi:

  • I work in a hotel.
  • She drinks coffee every morning.
  • They live in Naples.
  • We usually study in the evening.

docs.title: Present Continuous
docs.text:
Usiamo il Present Continuous per:

  • azioni che stanno succedendo adesso
  • situazioni temporanee
  • attività in corso in questo periodo

Esempi:

  • I am working now.
  • She is drinking tea at the moment.
  • They are living with friends this month.
  • We are studying for an exam this week.

docs.title: ⚖️ Differenza chiave
docs.text:
Present Simple Present Continuous routine adesso / in questo periodo every day now usually at the moment always today / this week

Confronta:

  • I work from home. → in generale

  • I am working from home today. → oggi, temporaneamente

  • She studies English on Mondays. → routine

  • She is studying English right now. → in questo momento

docs.title: Con il Present Simple
docs.text:
• every day
  • usually
  • always
  • often
  • sometimes
  • never
  • on Mondays
  • in the morning

docs.title: Con il Present Continuous
docs.text:
• now
  • right now
  • at the moment
  • today
  • this week
  • these days

docs.title: 🎧 Listening / Reading
docs.text:
Dialogue:

A: What do you do in the evenings? B: I usually study English after dinner. A: And what are you doing now? B: I’m doing my homework. A: Do you always study at home? B: No, not always. Sometimes I study at the library.

docs.title: Comprensione
docs.text:
1. What does B usually do in the evenings?
  2. What is B doing now?
  3. Does B always study at home?

docs.title: 🗣️ Speaking
docs.text:
Ripeti:

  • I work every day.
  • I am working now.
  • She usually takes the bus.
  • She is taking the bus today.

Ora completa oralmente:

  • I usually ___ in the morning.
  • I am ___ right now.
  • My friend always ___ on Sundays.
  • My friend is ___ today.

Fai domande:

  • What do you usually do after work?
  • What are you doing now?
  • Do you usually study in the evening?
  • Are you studying English at the moment?

docs.title: ✍️ Writing
docs.text:
Scrivi 8 frasi:

  • 4 frasi con il Present Simple sulla tua routine
  • 4 frasi con il Present Continuous su adesso o su questo periodo

docs.title: Modello utile
docs.text:
I usually wake up at seven. I drink coffee every morning. I work from Monday to Friday. I often read before bed.

I am sitting at my desk now. I am writing in English. I am using my computer. I am studying this lesson today.

docs.title: 🔊 Pronunciation
docs.text:
Ascolta la differenza tra:

  • work / working
  • live / living
  • study / studying

👉 Nel Present Continuous la parte importante è la pronuncia chiara di am / is / are e della finale -ing.

docs.title: ⚠️ Errori comuni (italiani)
docs.text:
❌ I am working every day. ✅ I work every day.

❌ I work now. ✅ I am working now.

❌ She is usually drinking coffee in the morning. ✅ She usually drinks coffee in the morning.

❌ They living in Rome this month. ✅ They are living in Rome this month.

docs.title: ✅ Review & Practice
docs.text:
Scegli la forma corretta:

  1. I (work / am working) in a bank.
  2. I (work / am working) now.
  3. She (usually goes / is usually going) to school by bus.
  4. We (study / are studying) for a test this week.
  5. They (live / are living) in Milan, but this month they (stay / are staying) with friends.

docs.title: 🔄 Trasforma
docs.text:
Trasforma le frasi secondo l’indicazione.

  1. I work in an office. → (now) ________
  2. She is studying now. → (every day) ________
  3. They play football on Saturdays. → (today) ________
  4. We are having lunch now. → (usually) ________

docs.title: 🎯 Mini task
docs.text:
Parla per 1 minuto e descrivi:

  • cosa fai di solito durante la settimana
  • cosa stai facendo adesso
  • una cosa che fai sempre
  • una cosa che stai facendo solo in questo periodo

In English, time changes the verb choice.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • distinguere con precisione tra abitudine, fatto generale, azione in corso e situazione temporanea
  • selezionare il tempo verbale corretto usando segnali di significato, non solo parole-chiave
  • produrre frasi, domande e brevi descrizioni più naturali e coerenti
  • evitare gli errori tipici degli italofoni quando l’italiano usa un solo presente
  • sostenere una breve conversazione confrontando ciò che fai di solito con ciò che stai facendo ora

docs.title: 🧭 La distinzione fondamentale
docs.text: In italiano il presente può coprire sia il valore abituale sia quello dell’azione in corso. In inglese, invece, la scelta tra Present Simple e Present Continuous è spesso obbligatoria.

docs.title: Present Simple
docs.text:
Usalo per:

  • routine e abitudini
  • fatti generali
  • situazioni permanenti o relativamente stabili
  • orari e schemi abituali

Esempi:

  • I work in customer support.
  • She usually has lunch at one.
  • They live near the station.
  • The shop opens at nine.

docs.title: Present Continuous
docs.text:
Usalo per:

  • azioni che stanno succedendo adesso
  • situazioni temporanee
  • attività in corso in questo periodo
  • descrizioni di una scena nel momento in cui la osservi

Esempi:

  • I’m talking to a client right now.
  • She’s staying with her sister this week.
  • We’re preparing for an exam these days.
  • A man is waiting outside the building.

docs.title: 🧠 Decisione rapida: quale tempo scelgo?
docs.text:
Quando costruisci una frase, chiediti:

  1. Sto parlando di sempre / di solito / ogni settimana? → Present Simple

  2. Sto parlando di adesso / in questo momento / oggi / questa settimana? → Present Continuous

  3. Sto descrivendo una situazione stabile? → Present Simple

  4. Sto descrivendo una situazione temporanea o eccezionale? → Present Continuous

Questa decisione è più affidabile delle sole parole-chiave.

docs.title: 1. Routine vs now
docs.text:
• I take the train to work. → abitudine
  • I’m taking a taxi today. → eccezione / oggi

docs.title: 2. Stable situation vs temporary situation
docs.text:
• She lives in Turin. → situazione stabile
  • She’s living in Turin for a few months. → situazione temporanea

docs.title: 3. Habitual action vs action in progress
docs.text:
• We study after dinner. → abitudine
  • We’re studying for tomorrow’s test. → attività in corso

docs.title: 4. General truth vs current scene
docs.text:
• People use smartphones every day. → fatto generale
  • Everyone is using their phone in this room. → scena osservata ora

docs.title: Segnali frequenti del Present Simple
docs.text:
• always
  • usually
  • often
  • sometimes
  • never
  • every day
  • every week
  • on Fridays
  • in the morning

docs.title: Segnali frequenti del Present Continuous
docs.text:
• now
  • right now
  • at the moment
  • today
  • this week
  • these days
  • for the time being

👉 Attenzione: la parola-chiave aiuta, ma il significato resta più importante.

docs.title: 🚫 Verbi che normalmente non usiamo al Continuous
docs.text:
A questo livello è utile iniziare a notare che alcuni verbi si usano più spesso al Present Simple, anche se parlano del presente.

Esempi tipici:

  • know
  • like
  • love
  • hate
  • understand
  • want
  • need
  • believe

Per esempio:

  • I know the answer. ✅

  • I am knowing the answer. ❌

  • She likes jazz. ✅

  • She is liking jazz. ❌

Non serve ancora studiare tutte le categorie teoriche, ma è importante riconoscere i casi più frequenti.

docs.title: 🎧 Reading – A realistic contrast
docs.text:
Leggi il testo.

Marco works for a small travel company and usually starts work at 9:00. He answers emails, speaks to customers and checks bookings during the day. This week, however, things are a little different. He is working from home because his office is being repainted. Right now, he is sitting at his kitchen table, talking to a customer in Madrid and looking for a hotel room online. His sister is making coffee, and the dog is sleeping under the chair.

docs.title: Comprensione
docs.text:
1. What does Marco usually do at work?
  2. Why is he working from home this week?
  3. What is he doing right now?
  4. What is his sister doing?
  5. Which actions describe routine, and which describe the current moment?

docs.title: Language focus
docs.text:
Nel testo trovi tre livelli diversi:

  • routine generale: works, starts, answers, speaks, checks
  • periodo temporaneo: is working from home
  • scena del momento: is sitting, is talking, is looking

Questa distinzione è esattamente la competenza da sviluppare.

docs.title: A. Personal contrast drill
docs.text:
Rispondi oralmente in coppia di frasi.

  1. What do you usually do in the morning?
  2. What are you doing right now?
  3. Where do you usually study or work?
  4. Where are you studying or working today?
  5. Who do you usually talk to during the day?
  6. Who are you talking to these days more often?

docs.title: B. Expand your answer
docs.text:
Per ogni risposta, aggiungi un dettaglio.

Esempio:

  • I usually work in an office.
  • This week I’m working from home because the office is closed.

docs.title: C. One-minute contrast talk
docs.text:
Parla per 60–90 secondi su questo tema:

My usual weekday and my day today

Struttura consigliata:

  • Normally, I...
  • I usually...
  • Today, I’m...
  • Right now, I’m...
  • This week, I’m...

docs.title: Task 1 – Routine vs current situation
docs.text:
Scrivi 10–12 frasi:

  • 5–6 frasi sulla tua routine normale
  • 5–6 frasi su cosa sta succedendo oggi, adesso o in questo periodo

docs.title: Task 2 – Guided paragraph
docs.text:
Scrivi un breve testo di 120–150 parole dal titolo:

My life in general and my life this week

Devi includere:

  • dove studi o lavori di solito
  • cosa fai normalmente durante il giorno
  • cosa stai facendo questa settimana di diverso dal solito
  • almeno 3 frasi al Present Simple
  • almeno 3 frasi al Present Continuous

docs.title: 🔄 Transformation drill
docs.text:
Trasforma mantenendo il significato richiesto.

  1. I work in a café. → (today / from home)
  2. She is studying in the library now. → (every afternoon)
  3. They go to the gym on Tuesdays. → (right now / at the gym)
  4. We are eating at a restaurant this week. → (usually / at home)
  5. He drives to work every day. → (today / by train)

Poi controlla:

  • hai cambiato anche il time marker?
  • hai cambiato correttamente il verbo?
  • la frase è naturale?

docs.title: 🧩 Error Clinic
docs.text:
Correggi gli errori.

  1. I am usually going to bed at eleven.
  2. She works at the moment.
  3. We are knowing the answer.
  4. They live with friends this month and they like it.
  5. What do you do right now?
  6. He is taking the bus every day.

docs.title: Soluzioni ragionate
docs.text:
1. I usually go to bed at eleven. Routine → Present Simple.

  2. She is working at the moment. Azione in corso → Present Continuous.

  3. We know the answer. Know normalmente non va al continuous.

  4. They are living with friends this month and they like it. Situazione temporanea + verbo di stato.

  5. What are you doing right now? “Right now” richiede il Continuous.

  6. He takes the bus every day. Abitudine → Present Simple.

docs.title: 🔊 Pronunciation and rhythm
docs.text:
Quando confronti i due tempi, allena il ritmo della frase:

  • I work here.

  • I’m working here today.

  • She lives in Rome.

  • She’s living in Rome this month.

👉 Nel Continuous fai attenzione a:

  • contrazioni naturali: I’m, she’s, we’re
  • finale -ing chiara
  • accento sulle parole informative, non su ogni parola

docs.title: ✅ Self-check
docs.text:
Riesci a fare tutto questo?

  • parlare di una tua abitudine con il Present Simple
  • parlare di un’azione che sta succedendo ora con il Present Continuous
  • spiegare la differenza tra situazione stabile e temporanea
  • evitare errori come I am working every day o I work now
  • usare almeno un paio di esempi personali naturali

Se riesci, la distinzione di base tra i due tempi sta diventando davvero tua.

docs.title: 🏁 Final Challenge
docs.text:
Registra o pronuncia ad alta voce un mini intervento di 90 secondi su:

What I usually do and what is different today

Prova a includere:

  • 4 frasi con il Present Simple
  • 4 frasi con il Present Continuous
  • 1 contrasto esplicito: Usually..., but today...
  • 1 situazione temporanea: This week...

Good English often depends on one question: Is this generally true, or is it happening now?

docs.title: Unità 13 – Past Simple
docs.text:
Modalità Base attiva. Questa unità ti aiuta a usare il Past Simple per parlare di azioni concluse nel passato. Imparerai a raccontare cosa hai fatto ieri o la settimana scorsa, a usare alcuni verbi irregolari molto frequenti e a fare domande semplici sul passato. Per la versione estesa usa il toggle BASE / PRO nella barra in alto.

Modalità Professional attiva. In questa unità non impari solo a “mettere il verbo al passato”: impari a raccontare eventi conclusi in modo più preciso, a gestire il contrasto tra forma affermativa e strutture con did / didn't, e a costruire piccoli racconti credibili con sequenza temporale, dettagli e verbi irregolari ad alta frequenza.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • parlare di azioni concluse nel passato
  • usare il Past Simple con verbi regolari e irregolari comuni
  • fare domande e risposte brevi con did
  • raccontare in modo semplice cosa hai fatto ieri o nel weekend

docs.title: Forma affermativa
docs.text:
Per parlare di un'azione finita nel passato usiamo il Past Simple.

Subject + past form of the verb

  • I worked yesterday.
  • She visited her grandmother.
  • We watched a film.
  • They played football.

Con i verbi regolari, di solito aggiungiamo -ed:

  • work → worked
  • watch → watched
  • play → played
  • clean → cleaned

docs.title: Verbi irregolari comuni
docs.text:
Alcuni verbi non prendono -ed e cambiano forma:

  • go → went
  • eat → ate
  • drink → drank
  • see → saw
  • have → had
  • do → did
  • buy → bought
  • come → came

Esempi:

  • I went to work.
  • We had dinner at home.
  • She bought a book.
  • They came late.

docs.title: Forma negativa
docs.text:
Per la forma negativa usiamo did not / didn't + verbo base.

Subject + didn't + base verb

  • I didn't work yesterday.
  • She didn't go out.
  • We didn't watch TV.

👉 Dopo didn't il verbo torna alla forma base.

docs.title: Forma interrogativa
docs.text:
Per fare domande usiamo Did + subject + base verb?

  • Did you work yesterday?
  • Did she go to school?
  • Did they eat at home?

Risposte brevi:

  • Yes, I did.
  • No, I didn't.
  • Yes, she did.
  • No, they didn't.

docs.title: Time expressions
docs.text:
• yesterday
  • yesterday morning
  • last night
  • last week
  • last month
  • last year
  • two days ago
  • an hour ago
  • on Saturday
  • in 2024

docs.title: Useful verbs
docs.text:
• work
  • study
  • play
  • watch
  • visit
  • travel
  • cook
  • clean
  • call
  • start
  • finish
  • go
  • eat
  • drink
  • see
  • have

docs.title: 🔁 Regular vs irregular
docs.text:
Confronta:

  • work → worked
  • play → played
  • visit → visited

ma:

  • go → went
  • eat → ate
  • see → saw

👉 I verbi irregolari vanno memorizzati poco alla volta, ma sono molto frequenti.

docs.title: 🎧 Listening / Reading
docs.text:
Text:

Yesterday was busy. I woke up at 7:00, had breakfast and went to work. I worked until 5:30. In the evening, I met a friend, ate at a small restaurant and came home at 10:00. I didn't watch TV because I was tired.

docs.title: Comprensione
docs.text:
1. What time did the person wake up?
  2. Where did the person go in the morning?
  3. Who did the person meet in the evening?
  4. Did the person watch TV?

docs.title: 🗣️ Speaking
docs.text:
Ripeti:

  • I worked yesterday.
  • I went out last night.
  • She ate at home.
  • We didn't study.
  • Did you call your friend?

Ora completa oralmente:

  • Yesterday I ___
  • Last weekend I ___
  • I didn't ___ yesterday.
  • Did you ___ last night?

Mini speaking task:

Parla per 30–45 secondi su what you did yesterday.

docs.title: ✍️ Writing
docs.text:
Scrivi 8–10 frasi su quello che hai fatto ieri o lo scorso weekend.

Puoi includere:

  • quando ti sei svegliato
  • dove sei andato
  • cosa hai mangiato
  • chi hai visto
  • cosa non hai fatto

docs.title: Modello utile
docs.text: Yesterday I woke up at 7:30. I had coffee and went to work. I worked all morning. In the afternoon I visited my parents. In the evening I didn't go out.

docs.title: 🔊 Pronunciation
docs.text:
Attenzione alla pronuncia di -ed:

  • worked → /t/
  • played → /d/
  • wanted → /ɪd/

👉 -ed non si pronuncia sempre allo stesso modo.

docs.title: ⚠️ Errori comuni (italiani)
docs.text:
❌ I go yesterday. ✅ I went yesterday.

❌ I didn't went. ✅ I didn't go.

❌ Did you went out? ✅ Did you go out?

❌ I have lunch with my friend yesterday. ✅ I had lunch with my friend yesterday.

👉 Con did / didn't usi il verbo base, non il passato.

docs.title: ✅ Review & Practice
docs.text:
Completa con il Past Simple corretto.

  1. I ___ (work) yesterday.
  2. She ___ (go) to the supermarket.
  3. We ___ (not watch) TV.
  4. ___ you ___ (study) last night?
  5. They ___ (have) lunch at 1:00.
  6. He ___ (see) a good film.

docs.title: 🔄 Trasforma
docs.text:
Trasforma al passato.

  1. I work every day. → ________
  2. She goes to school. → ________
  3. We don't study on Sundays. → ________
  4. Do they eat at home? → ________

docs.title: 🎯 Mini task
docs.text:
Parla per 1 minuto e racconta:

  • cosa hai fatto ieri
  • dove sei andato
  • con chi sei stato
  • cosa non hai fatto

docs.title: 🧠 Self-check
docs.text:
Riesci a:

  • usare worked / watched / visited correttamente?
  • ricordare alcuni verbi irregolari come went / ate / had / saw?
  • fare domande con Did you...??
  • evitare errori come didn't went?

Se sì, sei pronto per continuare.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • usare il Past Simple per raccontare eventi conclusi in ordine logico
  • distinguere con sicurezza tra forma al passato e base verb dopo did / didn't
  • usare un primo nucleo essenziale di verbi irregolari ad alta frequenza
  • fare domande più naturali su esperienze recenti e attività concluse
  • produrre un breve racconto orale o scritto sul tuo ieri, sul tuo weekend o su una giornata speciale

docs.title: 🧭 Quando usiamo il Past Simple
docs.text: Il Past Simple si usa per azioni e situazioni concluse in un momento finito del passato.

docs.title: 1. Azioni concluse
docs.text:
• I finished work at 6.
  • She called her sister yesterday.
  • We visited a museum last weekend.

docs.title: 2. Sequenza di azioni
docs.text:
• I got up, had breakfast, took the bus and arrived at work.
  • He opened the door, turned on the light and sat down.

docs.title: 3. Eventi legati a un tempo passato chiaro
docs.text:
• They moved to Milan in 2022.
  • I saw him two days ago.
  • We met on Saturday.

👉 Il punto centrale è questo: l'azione è finita e il riferimento temporale è chiuso.

docs.title: Forma affermativa
docs.text:
Subject + past form

  • I worked late.
  • She arrived early.
  • They went home.
  • We had dinner together.

docs.title: Forma negativa
docs.text:
Subject + did not / didn't + base verb

  • I didn't work late.
  • She didn't arrive early.
  • They didn't go home.
  • We didn't have dinner together.

docs.title: Forma interrogativa
docs.text:
Did + subject + base verb?

  • Did you work late?
  • Did she arrive early?
  • Did they go home?
  • Did you have dinner together?

docs.title: Risposte brevi
docs.text:
• Yes, I did.
  • No, I didn't.
  • Yes, she did.
  • No, they didn't.

👉 La difficoltà maggiore per molti studenti non è l'affermativa, ma ricordare che con did / didn't il verbo torna sempre alla forma base.

docs.title: 🔁 Il contrasto decisivo: went vs did go?
docs.text:
Confronta bene:

  • I went out last night. ✅
  • Did you go out last night? ✅
  • I didn't go out last night. ✅

Ma:

  • Did you went out...? ❌
  • I didn't went out. ❌

Perché? Perché il passato è già espresso da did. Il verbo dopo resta base:

  • go
  • eat
  • see
  • have
  • buy
  • come

Questo vale con tutti i verbi, regolari e irregolari.

docs.title: Time markers tipici del Past Simple
docs.text:
• yesterday
  • yesterday morning / afternoon / evening
  • last night
  • last week
  • last month
  • last year
  • on Monday
  • on Saturday evening
  • two days ago
  • a few minutes ago
  • in 2023

docs.title: High-frequency regular verbs
docs.text:
• work
  • watch
  • play
  • visit
  • clean
  • start
  • finish
  • phone / call
  • help
  • open

docs.title: High-frequency irregular verbs
docs.text:
• go → went
  • come → came
  • have → had
  • do → did
  • eat → ate
  • drink → drank
  • see → saw
  • buy → bought
  • get → got
  • make → made
  • meet → met
  • take → took

docs.title: ✍️ Formazione del passato regolare
docs.text: La regola di base è aggiungere -ed, ma ci sono alcune variazioni ortografiche utili.

docs.title: Aggiunta semplice di -ed
docs.text:
• work → worked
  • play → played
  • clean → cleaned

docs.title: Verbi che finiscono in -e
docs.text:
• live → lived
  • arrive → arrived
  • like → liked

docs.title: Verbi che finiscono in consonante + y
docs.text:
• study → studied
  • carry → carried
  • try → tried

docs.title: Alcuni verbi brevi raddoppiano la consonante
docs.text:
• stop → stopped
  • plan → planned

👉 A questo livello ti basta riconoscere i modelli più frequenti e produrre correttamente i verbi ad alta utilità.

docs.title: 🎧 Reading – A day that went wrong
docs.text:
Leggi il testo.

Yesterday started badly. I woke up late because I didn't hear my alarm. I got dressed quickly and left the house without breakfast. At the bus stop I met a colleague, but our bus didn't arrive on time, so we took a taxi. I reached the office at 9:20 and my manager asked, “What happened?” In the evening I planned to go to the gym, but I felt tired and stayed at home instead. I made dinner, watched a series and went to bed early.

docs.title: Comprensione
docs.text:
1. Why did the speaker wake up late?
  2. Did the bus arrive on time?
  3. How did the speaker get to the office?
  4. What did the manager ask?
  5. Did the speaker go to the gym?
  6. Which verbs in the text are irregular?

docs.title: Language focus
docs.text:
Nel testo trovi:

  • sequenza narrativa: woke up, got dressed, left, met, took, reached
  • negativa al passato: didn't hear, didn't arrive
  • cambio di piano: planned to go, but stayed at home instead

Questo è il tipo di controllo che ti serve per passare da frase isolata a mini-racconto.

docs.title: A. Controlled drill
docs.text:
Rispondi oralmente con una frase completa.

  1. What time did you get up yesterday?
  2. What did you have for breakfast?
  3. Did you work or study yesterday?
  4. Who did you talk to?
  5. Did anything unexpected happen?
  6. What did you do in the evening?

docs.title: B. Expand with details
docs.text:
Aggiungi un dettaglio di tempo, luogo o compagnia.

Esempio:

  • I went to the supermarket.
  • I went to the supermarket after work with my brother.

docs.title: C. One-minute story
docs.text:
Parla per 60–90 secondi su uno di questi temi:

  • My day yesterday
  • My last weekend
  • A day that went wrong

Struttura consigliata:

  • First, I...
  • Then, I...
  • After that, I...
  • In the evening, I...
  • Finally, I...

docs.title: Task 1 – Guided paragraph
docs.text:
Scrivi un testo di 120–150 parole dal titolo:

What I did yesterday

Devi includere:

  • almeno 4 verbi regolari al Past Simple
  • almeno 4 verbi irregolari al Past Simple
  • almeno 2 forme negative con didn't
  • almeno 1 domanda che qualcuno ti ha fatto o che hai fatto tu

docs.title: Task 2 – Better storytelling
docs.text:
Rivedi il testo e controlla se hai inserito:

  • una sequenza chiara degli eventi
  • connettivi semplici come first, then, after that, later, finally
  • dettagli concreti di luogo, tempo o persone
  • almeno un piccolo problema o cambiamento di piano

docs.title: 🔊 Pronunciation
docs.text: La finale -ed ha tre pronunce principali.

docs.title: /t/
docs.text:
• worked
  • watched
  • finished

docs.title: /d/
docs.text:
• played
  • cleaned
  • called

docs.title: /ɪd/
docs.text:
• wanted
  • needed
  • visited

👉 Pronunciare bene -ed migliora molto l'intelligibilità, soprattutto quando racconti eventi in sequenza.

docs.title: ⚠️ Error clinic – errori tipici degli italiani
docs.text:
❌ I go to the cinema yesterday. ✅ I went to the cinema yesterday.

❌ Did you went there alone? ✅ Did you go there alone?

❌ I didn't saw him. ✅ I didn't see him.

❌ She studied English and then she didn't went out. ✅ She studied English and then she didn't go out.

❌ Yesterday I was go to work. ✅ Yesterday I went to work.

docs.title: Perché succedono questi errori?
docs.text:
Per tre motivi molto comuni:

  1. trasferimento diretto dall'italiano
  2. confusione tra passato nel verbo e passato nell'ausiliare did
  3. scarsa automatizzazione dei verbi irregolari più frequenti

docs.title: 🔄 Transformation drill
docs.text:
Trasforma seguendo l'indicazione.

  1. I go to work by bus. → (yesterday)
  2. She doesn't call her parents. → (last night)
  3. Do they eat at home? → (yesterday evening)
  4. We see our friends every weekend. → (last Saturday)
  5. He buys lunch at the station. → (two days ago)
  6. I don't have time for breakfast. → (yesterday morning)

Poi controlla:

  • hai cambiato il verbo principale al passato solo dove serve?
  • con did / didn't hai lasciato il verbo base?
  • hai aggiunto un riferimento temporale chiaro?

docs.title: ✅ Review & Practice
docs.text:
Completa con la forma corretta.

  1. We ___ (leave) the office at 6:30.
  2. She ___ (not come) to class yesterday.
  3. ___ you ___ (see) Luca last weekend?
  4. I ___ (have) dinner at home and then I ___ (watch) a film.
  5. They ___ (buy) train tickets online.
  6. He ___ (study) all afternoon but he ___ (not finish) the report.

docs.title: 🧠 Self-check
docs.text:
Riesci a:

  • raccontare una breve sequenza di eventi al passato?
  • usare correttamente went / had / saw / bought / met?
  • fare domande con Did you...? senza usare il passato due volte?
  • scrivere un piccolo racconto con ordine logico e dettagli?

Se sì, la base del racconto al passato è pronta.

docs.title: Unità 14 – Travel and Holidays
docs.text:
Modalità Base attiva. Questa unità ti aiuta a parlare di viaggi e vacanze usando il Past Simple in modo semplice e naturale. Imparerai a descrivere dove sei andato, dove hai alloggiato, cosa hai visitato e cosa hai fatto durante una vacanza. Per la versione estesa usa il toggle BASE / PRO nella barra in alto.

Modalità Professional attiva. In questa unità impari a raccontare un viaggio in modo più credibile e più ricco: non solo dove sei andato, ma anche come ti sei mosso, che esperienza hai avuto, cosa è andato bene o male e come organizzare un piccolo travel story con sequenza, dettagli e lessico davvero utile in conversazione.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • parlare di un viaggio o di una vacanza passata
  • usare il Past Simple per raccontare eventi conclusi
  • usare parole utili per hotel, trasporti, città e attività turistiche
  • fare domande semplici sulle vacanze di un'altra persona

docs.title: Past Simple per raccontare un viaggio
docs.text:
Usiamo il Past Simple per parlare di azioni finite nel passato.

  • I went to Spain last summer.
  • We stayed in a small hotel.
  • She visited two museums.
  • They travelled by train.

docs.title: Sequenza di eventi
docs.text:
Per raccontare una vacanza in ordine logico usiamo connettivi semplici:

  • first
  • then
  • after that
  • later
  • finally

Esempio:

First, we arrived at the hotel. Then we went to the beach. After that, we had lunch. Later, we visited the old town. Finally, we went back to the hotel.

docs.title: Domande utili
docs.text:
Per fare domande al passato usiamo did.

  • Where did you go?
  • How did you travel?
  • Where did you stay?
  • What did you do?
  • Did you enjoy it?

docs.title: Travel words
docs.text:
• travel
  • trip
  • holiday
  • journey
  • ticket
  • suitcase
  • airport
  • station
  • hotel
  • hostel

docs.title: Transport
docs.text:
• plane
  • train
  • bus
  • car
  • taxi
  • underground
  • ferry

docs.title: Places and activities
docs.text:
• beach
  • mountains
  • city centre
  • museum
  • restaurant
  • monument
  • take photos
  • visit
  • stay
  • walk around
  • buy souvenirs

docs.title: 🎧 Listening / Reading
docs.text:
Text:

Last August I went to Lisbon with two friends. We travelled by plane and stayed in a hotel near the city centre. During the day we visited museums, walked around the old town and took lots of photos. In the evening we ate in small restaurants and listened to live music. We really enjoyed the trip.

docs.title: Comprensione
docs.text:
1. Where did the person go?
  2. Who did the person go with?
  3. How did they travel?
  4. Where did they stay?
  5. What did they do in the evening?

docs.title: 🗣️ Speaking
docs.text:
Ripeti:

  • I went to Lisbon last summer.
  • We travelled by plane.
  • We stayed in a hotel.
  • We visited the old town.
  • We took a lot of photos.

Ora completa oralmente:

  • Last summer I went to ___
  • I travelled by ___
  • I stayed in ___
  • I visited ___
  • In the evening I ___

Mini task:

Parla per 40–60 secondi su una vacanza che hai fatto o che vorresti fare.

docs.title: ✍️ Writing
docs.text:
Scrivi 8–10 frasi su una vacanza passata.

Puoi includere:

  • dove sei andato
  • con chi sei andato
  • come hai viaggiato
  • dove hai alloggiato
  • cosa hai fatto
  • se ti è piaciuta

docs.title: Modello utile
docs.text: Last summer I went to Rome with my family. We travelled by train. We stayed in a small hotel. We visited many monuments. We ate in nice restaurants. We enjoyed the holiday very much.

docs.title: 🔊 Pronunciation
docs.text:
Attenzione alla pronuncia di alcuni verbi al passato:

  • stayed → /steɪd/
  • visited → /ˈvɪzɪtɪd/
  • walked → /wɔːkt/
  • travelled → /ˈtrævəld/

👉 La finale -ed non si pronuncia sempre allo stesso modo.

docs.title: ⚠️ Errori comuni (italiani)
docs.text:
❌ I go to Paris last year. ✅ I went to Paris last year.

❌ We didn't travelled by plane. ✅ We didn't travel by plane.

❌ I stayed in hotel near the beach. ✅ I stayed in a hotel near the beach.

❌ Did you went with your family? ✅ Did you go with your family?

docs.title: ✅ Review & Practice
docs.text:
Completa con la forma corretta.

  1. We ___ (go) to Greece last summer.
  2. She ___ (stay) in a hostel.
  3. They ___ (travel) by bus.
  4. I ___ (visit) a museum.
  5. ___ you ___ (enjoy) the holiday?
  6. We ___ not ___ (buy) souvenirs.

docs.title: 🔄 Trasforma
docs.text:
Trasforma le frasi al passato.

  1. I travel by train. → ________
  2. We stay in a hotel. → ________
  3. They visit a museum. → ________
  4. Do you enjoy the trip? → ________

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • raccontare una vacanza o un viaggio breve con una sequenza chiara di eventi
  • usare il Past Simple con verbi ad alta frequenza legati al travel English
  • distinguere parole vicine come trip, travel, journey, holiday
  • fare e rispondere a domande più naturali su trasporto, alloggio, attività ed esperienza personale
  • produrre un breve resoconto orale o scritto con dettagli concreti e opinioni semplici ma efficaci

docs.title: 🧭 Language focus – talking about travel naturally
1. Raccontare fatti conclusi
docs.text:
Il Past Simple resta il tempo centrale per descrivere una vacanza finita.

  • I went to Porto for a long weekend.
  • We stayed in a small guesthouse near the river.
  • I visited two museums and tried local food.
  • We came back on Sunday evening.

docs.title: 2. Aggiungere ordine e coerenza
docs.text:
Per raccontare bene non basta una lista di frasi: serve una progressione.

  • first
  • then
  • after that
  • later
  • in the evening
  • on the last day
  • finally

Confronta:

Lista debole:

I went to Prague. I stayed in a hotel. I visited the centre. I ate a lot.

Versione migliore:

First, I flew to Prague and checked into a small hotel near the centre. Then I walked around the Old Town and visited a museum. In the evening I tried local food in a traditional restaurant. On the last day I bought a few souvenirs and flew back home.

👉 La seconda versione non è più “difficile” grammaticalmente, ma è molto più professionale e naturale.

docs.title: trip / travel / journey / holiday
docs.text:
• trip = viaggio singolo, spesso breve o definito
  • It was a business trip.

  • We went on a school trip.

  • travel = viaggiare in generale, come attività o concetto

  • I love travel.

  • We travelled around Portugal.

  • journey = spostamento da un luogo a un altro, attenzione al tragitto

  • The train journey was long.

  • We had a comfortable journey.

  • holiday = vacanza

  • We went on holiday in June.
  • It was a relaxing holiday.

👉 In molti contesti italiani “viaggio” copre tutto, ma in inglese la scelta della parola cambia il significato.

docs.title: Transport and movement
docs.text:
• travel by plane / train / car / bus
  • catch a flight
  • miss a train
  • book a ticket
  • rent a car
  • arrive at the airport
  • leave early
  • come back late

docs.title: Accommodation
docs.text:
• stay in a hotel
  • stay at a guesthouse
  • book a room
  • check in
  • check out
  • share a room
  • have breakfast included

docs.title: Activities
docs.text:
• visit a museum
  • walk around the city centre
  • go sightseeing
  • take photos
  • try local food
  • go to the beach
  • buy souvenirs
  • spend time outdoors

docs.title: Useful opinion language
docs.text:
• It was great.
  • It was relaxing.
  • It was crowded.
  • It was expensive.
  • The weather was amazing.
  • The hotel was comfortable.
  • The journey was tiring.

docs.title: 🎧 Reading – A short city break
docs.text:
Leggi il testo.

In October I went to Budapest with a friend for four days. We travelled by plane and stayed in a small hotel not far from the centre. On the first day we walked around the city and took a boat trip on the Danube. The weather was cold but sunny, so we spent a lot of time outdoors. On the second day we visited a museum and tried some local dishes in a traditional restaurant. Unfortunately, on the last day our flight was delayed, so we waited at the airport for three hours. Even so, we really enjoyed the trip and decided to go back one day.

docs.title: Comprensione
docs.text:
1. Where did the speakers go?
  2. How long did they stay there?
  3. Where did they stay?
  4. What did they do on the first day?
  5. What problem did they have on the last day?
  6. Which sentences express opinion rather than only fact?

docs.title: Language focus
docs.text:
Il testo funziona bene perché contiene:

  • fatti: went, travelled, stayed, visited
  • sequenza: on the first day, on the second day, on the last day
  • valutazione: the weather was cold but sunny, we really enjoyed the trip
  • piccolo imprevisto: our flight was delayed

Questi elementi trasformano una lista di azioni in un vero racconto.

docs.title: A. Short answers → full answers
docs.text:
Rispondi prima in modo breve, poi amplia.

  1. Where did you go on your last holiday?
  2. Who did you go with?
  3. How did you travel?
  4. Where did you stay?
  5. What did you do during the day?
  6. Did anything go wrong?
  7. Would you like to go back?

Esempio:

  • I went to Naples.
  • I went to Naples with two friends for a long weekend, and we stayed near the seafront.

docs.title: B. Build a travel story
docs.text:
Usa questa scaletta:

  • destination
  • transport
  • accommodation
  • activities
  • food
  • one positive point
  • one problem or surprise
  • final opinion

docs.title: C. 90-second task
docs.text:
Parla per 60–90 secondi su uno di questi temi:

  • My last holiday
  • A trip I really enjoyed
  • A trip that had a problem

docs.title: Task 1 – Guided paragraph
docs.text:
Scrivi un testo di 120–150 parole su una vacanza passata.

Devi includere:

  • almeno 5 verbi al Past Simple
  • almeno 4 parole del travel vocabulary
  • almeno 3 connettivi temporali
  • almeno 1 opinione personale
  • almeno 1 piccolo problema o imprevisto

docs.title: Task 2 – Upgrade your writing
docs.text:
Controlla se il tuo testo risponde a queste domande:

  • Il lettore capisce dove sei andato e quando?
  • Hai spiegato come hai viaggiato?
  • Hai incluso due o tre attività specifiche?
  • Hai dato una valutazione personale, non solo fatti?
  • Hai usato almeno un dettaglio concreto su hotel, cibo, luogo o trasporto?

docs.title: Final -ed
docs.text:
• stayed → /d/
  • walked → /t/
  • visited → /ɪd/

docs.title: Word stress utile
docs.text:
• HOtel
  • AIRport
  • MUseum
  • souveNIR

docs.title: Rhythm tip
docs.text:
Le frasi di viaggio suonano più naturali se raggruppi le informazioni:

  • We stayed in a small hotel near the centre.
  • We travelled by train and arrived in the afternoon.
  • On the last day, we bought a few souvenirs.

docs.title: ⚠️ Error clinic – errori tipici degli italiani
docs.text:
❌ I made a travel to London. ✅ I took a trip to London. / I travelled to London.

❌ We did a holiday in Greece. ✅ We went on holiday to Greece.

❌ I stayed in an hotel. ✅ I stayed in a hotel.

❌ We didn't visited the museum. ✅ We didn't visit the museum.

❌ Did you enjoyed the trip? ✅ Did you enjoy the trip?

❌ I and my friend went to Paris. ✅ My friend and I went to Paris.

👉 A questo livello la correttezza grammaticale conta, ma conta anche scegliere le espressioni davvero idiomatiche.

docs.title: ✅ Review & Practice
docs.text:
Completa con la forma corretta.

  1. We ___ (go) to Vienna in March.
  2. She ___ (book) a room online.
  3. They ___ (travel) by train and ___ (arrive) late.
  4. I ___ not ___ (buy) any souvenirs.
  5. ___ you ___ (stay) near the city centre?
  6. Our flight ___ (be) delayed.
  7. We ___ (have) dinner in a traditional restaurant.
  8. He ___ (take) lots of photos.

docs.title: 🔄 Transformation drill
docs.text:
Riscrivi le frasi in modo più naturale.

  1. I went to Madrid. I stayed there. I visited things. → _______

  2. We travelled by plane. It was good. → _______

  3. I did a trip with my family. → _______

  4. Did you enjoyed your holiday? → _______

  5. We didn't went to the beach because it rained. → _______

docs.title: 🧠 Think & Speak
docs.text:
Rispondi in modo personale.

  • Do you prefer city breaks or beach holidays? Why?
  • Do you like travelling alone or with other people?
  • What makes a trip memorable for you?
  • Is good food important when you travel?

docs.title: Speaking final
docs.text:
Racconta una vacanza in 90 secondi seguendo questo schema:

  • where you went
  • who you went with
  • how you travelled
  • where you stayed
  • what you did
  • one good thing
  • one problem
  • your final opinion

docs.title: Writing final
docs.text:
Scrivi un breve travel post intitolato:

A holiday I still remember

Obiettivo: testo chiaro, concreto e naturale, non solo corretto.

docs.title: Unità 15 – Comparatives and Superlatives
docs.text:
Modalità Base attiva. Questa unità ti aiuta a fare confronti semplici in inglese: parlare di cose più grandi, più economiche, più interessanti o dire qual è la migliore o la più bella. Per la versione estesa usa il toggle BASE / PRO nella barra in alto.

Modalità Professional attiva. In questa unità non impari solo a dire che una cosa è “più grande” o “più costosa”: impari a confrontare in modo credibile, a scegliere tra comparative, superlative e as ... as, a usare forme irregolari ad alta frequenza come better, worse e further, e a costruire opinioni più naturali su città, servizi, prodotti, viaggi ed esperienze.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • confrontare due persone, oggetti o luoghi usando il comparative
  • dire qual è il massimo o il minimo usando il superlative
  • distinguere tra aggettivi brevi e lunghi
  • usare alcune forme irregolari molto comuni come better e worse
  • esprimere preferenze semplici in frasi quotidiane

docs.title: Comparatives: confronto tra due cose
docs.text: Usiamo il comparative per confrontare due elementi.

docs.title: Aggettivi brevi
docs.text:
Struttura:

adjective + -er + than

  • Rome is bigger than Milan.
  • This bag is cheaper than that one.
  • My brother is taller than me.

docs.title: Aggettivi lunghi
docs.text:
Con molti aggettivi di due o più sillabe usiamo more + adjective + than.

  • This exercise is more difficult than the last one.
  • London is more expensive than Lisbon.
  • Her job is more interesting than mine.

docs.title: Superlatives: il massimo in un gruppo
docs.text: Usiamo il superlative per dire che una cosa è al top o al bottom di un gruppo.

docs.title: Aggettivi brevi
docs.text:
Struttura:

the + adjective + -est

  • Rome is the biggest city in Italy.
  • This is the cheapest option.
  • He is the tallest student in the class.

docs.title: Aggettivi lunghi
docs.text:
Struttura:

the most + adjective

  • This is the most interesting lesson.
  • It is the most expensive hotel in the area.
  • She is the most organised person in the team.

docs.title: Forme irregolari comuni
docs.text:
Alcuni aggettivi non seguono la regola standard.

  • good → better → the best
  • bad → worse → the worst
  • far → farther / further → the farthest / the furthest

Esempi:

  • This restaurant is better than that one.
  • Today is worse than yesterday.
  • Which station is farther from here?

docs.title: Common adjectives for comparison
docs.text:
• big
  • small
  • tall
  • short
  • fast
  • slow
  • cheap
  • expensive
  • easy
  • difficult
  • good
  • bad
  • beautiful
  • interesting
  • comfortable
  • busy

docs.title: Useful expressions
docs.text:
• bigger than
  • smaller than
  • more expensive than
  • more interesting than
  • the best
  • the worst
  • the cheapest
  • the most comfortable

docs.title: 🎧 Listening / Reading
docs.text:
Text:

My town is smaller than Rome, but it is quieter and cleaner. The public transport is worse, but the streets are less crowded. There are two hotels in the centre. The Grand Hotel is more expensive than the City Hotel, but it is also more comfortable. In my opinion, spring is the best season to visit because the weather is warmer and the town is more beautiful.

docs.title: Comprensione
docs.text:
1. Is the town bigger or smaller than Rome?
  2. What is worse in the town?
  3. Which hotel is more expensive?
  4. Which hotel is more comfortable?
  5. Which season is the best to visit?

docs.title: 🗣️ Speaking
docs.text:
Ripeti:

  • Rome is bigger than Milan.
  • This phone is cheaper than that one.
  • My house is smaller than yours.
  • It is the best café in the area.
  • This exercise is more difficult than the last one.

Ora completa oralmente:

  • My city is ___ than ___
  • This restaurant is ___ than ___
  • English is ___ than ___
  • In my opinion, ___ is the best ___

Mini task:

Confronta per 40–60 secondi:

  • due città
  • due ristoranti
  • due telefoni o due computer
  • due modi di viaggiare

docs.title: ✍️ Writing
docs.text:
Scrivi 8–10 frasi in cui confronti persone, luoghi o oggetti.

Puoi includere:

  • due città che conosci
  • due mezzi di trasporto
  • due ristoranti o hotel
  • due materie scolastiche o attività

docs.title: Modello utile
docs.text: Rome is bigger than Florence. Florence is smaller, but it is more elegant. Travelling by train is more relaxing than travelling by car. In my opinion, spring is the best season for a city break.

docs.title: 🔊 Pronunciation
docs.text:
Attenzione alla pronuncia di alcune forme:

  • bigger → /ˈbɪɡər/
  • cheaper → /ˈtʃiːpər/
  • easier → /ˈiːziər/
  • best → /best/
  • worst → /wɜːst/

👉 Nella lingua parlata, la terminazione -er deve essere chiara ma naturale.

docs.title: ⚠️ Errori comuni (italiani)
docs.text:
❌ more bigger ✅ bigger

❌ more cheap ✅ cheaper

❌ the more expensive hotel ✅ the most expensive hotel

❌ Milan is more small than Rome. ✅ Milan is smaller than Rome.

❌ This is the better restaurant in town. ✅ This is the best restaurant in town.

👉 Con gli aggettivi corti in genere non usiamo more.

docs.title: 🔁 Schema semplice
docs.text: Tipo Forma aggettivo corto bigger aggettivo lungo more interesting superlativo corto biggest superlativo lungo most interesting irregolare better / best

docs.title: ✅ Review & Practice
docs.text:
Completa con la forma corretta.

  1. My house is ___ than yours. (small)
  2. This bag is the ___ one here. (cheap)
  3. English is ___ than German for me. (easy)
  4. This is the ___ film of the year. (interesting)
  5. Today is ___ than yesterday. (good)
  6. That restaurant is the ___ in town. (bad)

docs.title: 🔄 Trasforma
docs.text:
Trasforma secondo l'indicazione.

  1. big → ______ (comparativo)
  2. expensive → ______ (comparativo)
  3. easy → ______ (comparativo)
  4. good → ______ (comparativo)
  5. beautiful → ______ (superlativo)
  6. bad → ______ (superlativo)

docs.title: 🧠 Think & Speak
docs.text:
Parla liberamente:

  • Qual è la città più bella che conosci?
  • È meglio viaggiare in treno o in aereo?
  • Qual è il posto più tranquillo vicino a casa tua?
  • Quale stagione è la migliore per te e perché?

docs.title: 🧠 Self-check
docs.text:
Riesci a:

  • confrontare due cose con -er than o more ... than?
  • usare correttamente the ... -est e the most ...?
  • ricordare forme irregolari come better e the best?
  • parlare per un minuto facendo confronti semplici ma chiari?

Se sì, la base dei confronti in inglese è pronta.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • usare con sicurezza comparatives e superlatives in contesti reali
  • distinguere tra aggettivi brevi, lunghi e forme irregolari
  • usare strutture utili come much / a lot / a bit + comparative e as ... as
  • evitare errori molto comuni come more easier, the better, than me / than I am in contesti pratici
  • produrre confronti più ricchi e motivati in conversazione e in scrittura

docs.title: 🧭 Language focus – how comparison really works
1. Comparative: confronto tra due elementi
docs.text:
Il comparative serve a mettere in relazione due persone, oggetti, luoghi o situazioni.

  • Rome is bigger than Bologna.
  • This laptop is lighter than my old one.
  • A hotel is usually more expensive than a hostel.
  • Working from home is sometimes easier than commuting every day.

Il punto importante non è solo la forma, ma il criterio del confronto:

  • size → bigger / smaller
  • price → cheaper / more expensive
  • quality → better / worse
  • comfort → more comfortable
  • speed → faster / slower

👉 Un buon confronto specifica sempre che cosa stai comparando e spesso in base a quale criterio.

docs.title: 2. Superlative: il massimo all'interno di un gruppo
docs.text:
Il superlative identifica un elemento come il più alto, il più basso, il migliore o il peggiore di un gruppo.

  • This is the cheapest restaurant in the area.
  • It was the most interesting talk of the day.
  • She is the best player on the team.
  • Winter is the worst season for me.

Spesso il superlativo è seguito da:

  • in + place / group
  • the best café in town
  • of + plural / period / set
  • the most useful lesson of the course

docs.title: 🧠 Formazione: short adjectives, long adjectives, irregular forms
Short adjectives
docs.text:
Di solito usano:

  • -er than
  • the -est

Esempi:

  • tall → taller → the tallest
  • fast → faster → the fastest
  • cheap → cheaper → the cheapest
  • nice → nicer → the nicest

docs.title: Final -e
docs.text:
• nice → nicer → the nicest
  • large → larger → the largest

docs.title: Consonant + y → ier / iest
docs.text:
• easy → easier → the easiest
  • busy → busier → the busiest
  • happy → happier → the happiest

docs.title: Double consonant in some short words
docs.text:
• big → bigger → the biggest
  • hot → hotter → the hottest
  • thin → thinner → the thinnest

docs.title: Long adjectives
docs.text:
Molti aggettivi più lunghi usano:

  • more + adjective + than
  • the most + adjective

Esempi:

  • more useful
  • more reliable
  • more comfortable
  • the most interesting
  • the most convenient

docs.title: Irregular forms
docs.text:
• good → better → the best
  • bad → worse → the worst
  • far → farther / further → the farthest / the furthest

Esempi:

  • This option is better than the other one.
  • The weather today is worse than yesterday.
  • Which stop is further from the station?

docs.title: 🔍 Beyond the basics: very useful comparison patterns
1. much / a lot / far + comparative
docs.text:
Usiamo queste parole per rafforzare il confronto.

  • This exercise is much easier than the previous one.
  • Travelling by train is a lot more relaxing than driving.
  • My new phone is far better than my old one.

docs.title: 2. a bit / a little / slightly + comparative
docs.text:
Per un confronto più moderato.

  • Today is a bit colder than yesterday.
  • This neighbourhood is slightly quieter at night.
  • The second option is a little more expensive.

docs.title: 3. as ... as
docs.text:
Per dire che due cose sono uguali o quasi uguali sotto un certo aspetto.

  • My sister is as tall as me.
  • This café is as expensive as the one near the station.
  • The exam wasn't as difficult as I expected.

Forme utili:

  • as good as
  • as fast as
  • not as easy as
  • not as crowded as

👉 A2 richiede già di saper esprimere non solo “più di”, ma anche “uguale a” e “non così ... come”.

docs.title: Adjectives for city, travel and everyday life
docs.text:
• crowded
  • quiet
  • noisy
  • safe
  • dangerous
  • cheap
  • expensive
  • reliable
  • convenient
  • comfortable
  • modern
  • traditional
  • friendly
  • busy
  • clean
  • dirty

docs.title: Adjectives for study and work
docs.text:
• easy
  • difficult
  • useful
  • practical
  • effective
  • boring
  • interesting
  • clear
  • organised
  • flexible

docs.title: Natural comparison chunks
docs.text:
• much better than
  • a bit cheaper than
  • more reliable than
  • not as easy as
  • the best option for
  • the worst part of
  • one of the most interesting
  • far more useful than

docs.title: 🎧 Reading – Choosing the best place to stay
docs.text:
Leggi il testo.

We looked at three places for our weekend in Valencia. The first hotel was the cheapest, but it was also the farthest from the centre. The second one was smaller but much more comfortable, and the staff seemed friendlier. The third option was the most modern and the closest to the station, but it was also the most expensive. In the end, we chose the second hotel because it wasn't as cheap as the first one, but it was far better in terms of comfort and location. Sometimes the cheapest option isn't the best one.

docs.title: Comprensione
docs.text:
1. Which hotel was the cheapest?
  2. Which one was the farthest from the centre?
  3. Which hotel was the most modern?
  4. Why did they choose the second hotel?
  5. Find one sentence with not as ... as.
  6. Find one sentence where price and quality are contrasted.

docs.title: Language focus
docs.text:
Nel testo trovi:

  • superlatives: the cheapest, the farthest, the most modern, the most expensive
  • moderated comparison: smaller but much more comfortable
  • equal/unequal comparison: wasn't as cheap as
  • value judgement: the cheapest option isn't the best one

Questo è il tipo di lingua che rende i confronti utili e credibili.

docs.title: A. Controlled comparison
docs.text:
Rispondi con una frase completa.

  1. Is your city bigger or smaller than Rome?
  2. Is travelling by train better than travelling by bus for you?
  3. Which is more useful: learning vocabulary or doing grammar exercises?
  4. What is the best place to study in your house?
  5. Which season is the worst for commuting?

docs.title: B. Expand the answer
docs.text:
Aggiungi almeno un motivo.

Esempio:

  • Travelling by train is better than travelling by bus.
  • Travelling by train is usually better than travelling by bus because it is more comfortable and more reliable.

docs.title: C. One-minute task
docs.text:
Parla per 60–90 secondi su uno di questi temi:

  • Two cities you know
  • Two ways of travelling
  • Two restaurants or cafés
  • Studying at home vs studying in a library

Obiettivo:

  • usa almeno 3 comparatives
  • usa almeno 1 superlative
  • usa almeno 1 structure with as ... as

docs.title: Task 1 – Comparison paragraph
docs.text:
Scrivi un testo di 120–150 parole dal titolo:

Which option is better?

Scegli uno di questi argomenti:

  • city A vs city B
  • hotel A vs hotel B
  • travelling by train vs travelling by car
  • studying online vs studying in class

Devi includere:

  • almeno 4 comparatives
  • almeno 2 superlatives
  • almeno 1 irregular form (better, worse, best, worst)
  • almeno 1 structure with as ... as o not as ... as
  • almeno 2 reasons per giustificare la tua opinione

docs.title: Task 2 – Make it more natural
docs.text:
Rivedi il testo e controlla se hai inserito:

  • criteri chiari di confronto (prezzo, qualità, tempo, comfort, distanza)
  • connettivi semplici come but, because, however, in my opinion
  • almeno una frase che non sia solo grammaticale, ma anche valutativa
  • It is more expensive, but it is much more reliable.

docs.title: 🔊 Pronunciation
docs.text:
Fai attenzione a queste forme:

  • cheaper → /ˈtʃiːpər/
  • easier → /ˈiːziər/
  • quieter → /ˈkwaɪətər/
  • best → /best/
  • worst → /wɜːst/

docs.title: Pronunciation note
docs.text:
Con alcune forme come easier, busier, quieter, il rischio è “mangiare” una sillaba o italianizzare il suono. Meglio dire la parola in due tempi e poi accelerare:

  • ea-sier
  • bu-sier
  • qui-e-ter

docs.title: ⚠️ Error clinic – errori tipici degli italiani
docs.text:
❌ more easier ✅ easier

❌ the better hotel in town ✅ the best hotel in town

❌ This exercise is more easy than that one. ✅ This exercise is easier than that one.

❌ Milan is more small than Rome. ✅ Milan is smaller than Rome.

❌ My brother is more tall than me. ✅ My brother is taller than me.

❌ This phone is as better as the other one. ✅ This phone is as good as the other one.

docs.title: Perché succedono questi errori?
docs.text:
Per tre ragioni molto comuni:

  1. trasferimento diretto dall'italiano (più facile → more easy)
  2. incertezza su quando usare -er e quando usare more
  3. confusione tra comparative e superlative

docs.title: 🔄 Transformation drill
docs.text:
Trasforma seguendo l'indicazione.

  1. This hotel is cheap. → (comparative)
  2. The blue car is fast. → (superlative in a group of three cars)
  3. My town is quiet. → (comparative with Rome)
  4. This exercise is difficult. → (negative equality: not as ... as)
  5. That restaurant is good. → (comparative)
  6. This is a bad option. → (superlative)

Poi controlla:

  • hai scelto -er o more in modo corretto?
  • hai usato the con il superlativo?
  • hai evitato forme sbagliate come more easier o the better?

docs.title: ✅ Review & Practice
docs.text:
Completa con la forma corretta.

  1. My new flat is much ___ than the old one. (bright)
  2. This route is ___ than the other one, but it is also safer. (long)
  3. It is the ___ restaurant in the neighbourhood. (popular)
  4. Today is ___ than yesterday, so let's stay at home. (bad)
  5. The bus isn't as ___ as the train. (fast)
  6. Which is the ___ way to get to the airport? (easy)
  7. This task is a bit ___ than the last one. (complicated)
  8. In my opinion, autumn is the ___ season for travelling. (good)

docs.title: 🧠 Self-check
docs.text:
Riesci a:

  • fare confronti naturali con -er than e more ... than?
  • usare correttamente the best, the worst, the most useful?
  • esprimere sfumature con much, a bit, as ... as, not as ... as?
  • spiegare perché una cosa è migliore, peggiore, più conveniente o meno adatta?

Se sì, la lingua del confronto sta diventando davvero operativa.

docs.title: Unità 16 – Food and Shopping
docs.text:
Modalità Base attiva. Questa unità ti aiuta a parlare di cibo, bevande, spesa e ordini al ristorante in inglese. Imparerai a distinguere tra nomi countable e uncountable, a usare some / any, e a fare richieste semplici in modo naturale. Per la versione estesa usa il toggle BASE / PRO nella barra in alto.

Modalità Professional attiva.
