In questa unità non impari solo il lessico del cibo: impari a gestire situazioni reali in cui devi fare la spesa, ordinare al ristorante, chiedere quantità, chiarire preferenze e risolvere piccoli problemi. L'obiettivo è usare con naturalezza countable / uncountable nouns, some / any / much / many / a lot of, i containers più comuni e formule di richiesta educate e credibili.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • parlare di cibo, bevande e acquisti quotidiani
  • distinguere tra nomi countable e uncountable
  • usare some, any e a lot of in frasi semplici
  • chiedere e dire quantità di base
  • ordinare al ristorante o comprare prodotti in un negozio o supermercato

docs.title: Countable nouns
docs.text:
I nomi countable si possono contare.

  • an apple
  • two apples
  • three sandwiches
  • a bottle
  • four eggs

Possiamo usare:

  • a / an
  • numeri
  • many

Esempi:

  • I want an apple.
  • We need three tomatoes.
  • There are many shops in this street.

docs.title: Uncountable nouns
docs.text:
I nomi uncountable non si contano normalmente uno per uno.

  • water
  • milk
  • bread
  • rice
  • sugar
  • coffee
  • cheese

Di solito non usiamo a / an.

  • I want water.
  • We need some rice.
  • There isn't any milk.

docs.title: Some / Any
docs.text:
Usiamo some soprattutto nelle frasi affermative.

  • I need some bread.
  • She bought some apples.

Usiamo any soprattutto nelle frasi negative e interrogative.

  • We don't have any coffee.
  • Do you have any eggs?

docs.title: A lot of
docs.text:
A lot of si usa sia con countable sia con uncountable.

  • We bought a lot of fruit.
  • There are a lot of customers today.

docs.title: Useful question patterns
docs.text:
• Can I have a coffee, please?
  • How much is it?
  • How many apples do you want?
  • Do you have any water?
  • Would you like anything else?

docs.title: Food
docs.text:
• bread
  • rice
  • pasta
  • meat
  • chicken
  • fish
  • fruit
  • vegetables
  • salad
  • soup
  • cheese
  • eggs

docs.title: Drinks
docs.text:
• water
  • coffee
  • tea
  • juice
  • milk

docs.title: Shopping and eating out
docs.text:
• shop
  • supermarket
  • market
  • menu
  • bill
  • waiter
  • customer
  • order
  • buy
  • pay
  • price
  • change

docs.title: Quantity words
docs.text:
• some
  • any
  • a lot of
  • much
  • many
  • a bottle of
  • a kilo of
  • a cup of
  • a glass of

docs.title: 🎧 Listening / Reading
docs.text:
Dialogue 1 – at a café

A: Good afternoon. What would you like? B: Can I have a sandwich and a bottle of water, please? A: Of course. Anything else? B: Yes, a coffee, please. A: Sure. That's £8.

Dialogue 2 – at the supermarket

A: Do we have any milk at home? B: No, we don't. We also need some bread and eggs. A: How many eggs do we need? B: A dozen, please.

docs.title: 🗣️ Speaking
docs.text:
Ripeti:

  • I need some bread.
  • We don't have any milk.
  • Can I have a coffee, please?
  • How much is this?
  • I'd like two apples and a bottle of water.

👉 Ora parla di te:

  • What food do you usually buy?
  • What do you drink in the morning?
  • What do you order in a café?
  • What do you need at home today?

👉 Fai domande:

  • Do you have any rice?
  • How many tomatoes do we need?
  • Can I pay by card?

docs.title: ✍️ Writing
docs.text:
Scrivi 8–10 frasi su una di queste situazioni:

  1. la tua spesa settimanale
  2. un ordine al bar o al ristorante
  3. cosa c'è e cosa manca nella tua cucina

Puoi usare questo modello:

I usually buy some fruit, bread and milk. I don't buy much meat. At the café, I usually order a coffee and a sandwich. At home, we don't have any rice today.

docs.title: 🔊 Pronunciation
docs.text:
Attenzione a queste parole:

  • bread → /bred/
  • fruit → /fruːt/
  • juice → /dʒuːs/
  • rice → /raɪs/
  • sugar → /ˈʃʊɡə(r)/

👉 Non leggere tutto come in italiano. In inglese molte parole comuni hanno suoni molto diversi dalla grafia italiana.

docs.title: ⚠️ Errori comuni (italiani)
docs.text:
❌ I want a bread ✅ I want some bread / I want bread

❌ We need a milk ✅ We need some milk

❌ How much apples? ✅ How many apples?

❌ I buyed some food ✅ I bought some food

❌ I want a water ✅ I want some water / Can I have a bottle of water?

docs.title: 🧠 Pattern fondamentali
docs.text:
• some + plural / uncountable
  • any + plural / uncountable
  • How much + uncountable?
  • How many + plural countable?
  • Can I have ... , please?
  • I'd like ...

Esempi:

  • I'd like some soup.
  • Do you have any cheese?
  • How many bananas do you want?
  • How much rice do we need?

docs.title: ❓ Domande utili
docs.text:
• What would you like?
  • Can I help you?
  • Do you need anything else?
  • How much is it?
  • How many do you want?
  • Can I pay by card?

docs.title: ✅ Review & Practice
docs.text:
Completa con some / any / much / many:

  1. We need ______ bread.
  2. Do you have ______ eggs?
  3. How ______ milk do you drink?
  4. How ______ apples do you want?
  5. There aren't ______ customers today.

Trasforma in domanda:

  1. You have some coffee. → ______
  2. They need three bottles of water. → ______
  3. She wants some cheese. → ______

docs.title: 🧠 Think & Speak
docs.text:
Parla per 1 minuto:

  • cosa compri di solito al supermercato
  • cosa c'è nel tuo frigorifero
  • cosa ordini quando esci
  • quali cibi ti piacciono e quali no

docs.title: 🚀 Mini task
docs.text:
Simula un dialogo al ristorante.

Ruolo A: cliente Ruolo B: cameriere

Usa almeno queste espressioni:

  • Can I have ...?
  • I'd like ...
  • Anything else?
  • That's all, thank you.
  • Can I have the bill, please?

docs.title: 💬 Extra practice
docs.text:
Rispondi in modo completo:

  • What do you usually buy at the supermarket?
  • Do you drink much coffee?
  • How many meals do you eat a day?
  • Is there any food you never eat?

docs.title: 🎯 Challenge
docs.text:
Scrivi un mini-dialogo di 10–12 battute tra un cliente e un commesso oppure tra un cliente e un cameriere. Usa:

  • almeno 4 parole di cibo o bevande
  • some / any
  • una domanda con How much o How many
  • una richiesta educata con Can I have ...? o I'd like ...

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • distinguere con sicurezza tra countable e uncountable nouns
  • usare correttamente some, any, much, many, a lot of e diversi quantity expressions
  • ordinare cibo e bevande in modo naturale e cortese
  • fare acquisti in inglese chiedendo prezzi, quantità, confezioni e disponibilità
  • evitare errori molto comuni come a bread, two informations, much apples o I'd like a water

docs.title: 🧭 Language focus – food, quantity and real-life interaction
1. Countable vs uncountable: non è solo grammatica, è significato
docs.text:
I countable nouns si possono contare come unità separate:

  • an apple
  • two bananas
  • three bottles
  • a sandwich
  • several customers

Gli uncountable nouns indicano spesso sostanze, cibi “non separati”, bevande o concetti visti come massa:

  • water
  • milk
  • bread
  • rice
  • cheese
  • sugar
  • coffee

Questa distinzione influenza:

  • articoli (a / an)
  • plurale
  • scelta tra much e many
  • scelta tra How much e How many

Confronta:

  • an apple / two apples
  • some rice / some cheese
  • many apples
  • much rice

👉 In contesto reale, il problema non è solo sapere la regola, ma vedere il nome come unità o come sostanza.

docs.title: 2. Quando un nome uncountable può diventare countable
docs.text:
In contesti quotidiani, alcuni uncountable nouns diventano countable quando parliamo di porzioni, contenitori o tipi.

  • two coffees = two cups of coffee
  • a water = molto colloquiale in alcuni contesti, ma meglio per studenti: a bottle of water
  • three cheeses = three types of cheese

Per un corso ben controllato, preferiamo forme più chiare:

  • a cup of coffee
  • a glass of water
  • a bottle of milk
  • a loaf of bread
  • a piece of cake

docs.title: 3. Some, any, much, many, a lot of
Some
docs.text:
Usato soprattutto in frasi affermative e offerte/richieste educate.

  • We need some eggs.
  • I'd like some water.
  • Would you like some coffee?

docs.title: Any
docs.text:
Usato soprattutto in domande e negazioni.

  • Do you have any fresh bread?
  • We don't have any rice left.
  • Is there any milk in the fridge?

docs.title: Much / many
docs.text:
• many + countable plural

  • much + uncountable

  • How many oranges do we need?

  • How much pasta is there?

Nel parlato quotidiano, in frasi affermative si preferisce spesso a lot of:

  • We bought a lot of vegetables.
  • She drinks a lot of coffee.

👉 much in frase affermativa semplice spesso suona più formale o meno naturale:

  • We drink much coffee. ❌/strano
  • We drink a lot of coffee. ✅

docs.title: 4. Containers and quantity expressions
docs.text:
Queste strutture rendono il linguaggio molto più naturale:

  • a bottle of water
  • a carton of milk
  • a loaf of bread
  • a packet of rice
  • a kilo of apples
  • a jar of jam
  • a cup of tea
  • a slice of pizza
  • a piece of cake

Esempi:

  • We need a carton of milk and a loaf of bread.
  • Can I have a slice of cake?
  • I'd like two bottles of water.

docs.title: 5. Functional language: ordering and shopping politely
docs.text:
In situazioni reali, la grammatica deve andare insieme alla cortesia.

Formule molto utili:

  • I'd like ...
  • Can I have ... , please?
  • Could I get ...?
  • Do you have any ...?
  • How much is this?
  • How much are these?
  • That's all, thank you.
  • Can I pay by card?
  • Can I have the bill, please?

Confronta:

  • I want a coffee. → corretto ma diretto
  • I'd like a coffee, please. → più naturale e cortese
  • Can I have a coffee, please? → molto comune

docs.title: Countable
docs.text:
• a tomato
  • two tomatoes
  • many tomatoes
  • How many tomatoes do we need?

docs.title: Uncountable
docs.text:
• some pasta
  • much pasta
  • a lot of pasta
  • How much pasta do we need?

docs.title: Mixed use with containers
docs.text:
• a bottle of water
  • three cups of coffee
  • two pieces of cake
  • a bowl of soup

docs.title: Food and drink
docs.text:
• bread
  • rice
  • pasta
  • flour
  • butter
  • yoghurt
  • cereal
  • jam
  • tuna
  • soup
  • steak
  • beans
  • oil
  • vinegar
  • sparkling water
  • still water

docs.title: Shopping language
docs.text:
• receipt
  • cash desk
  • discount
  • full price
  • change
  • wallet
  • card machine
  • shopping list
  • offer
  • out of stock

docs.title: Restaurant language
docs.text:
• starter
  • main course
  • dessert
  • side dish
  • menu
  • table for two
  • reservation
  • vegetarian option
  • bill / check

docs.title: Situation 1 – supermarket
docs.text: We need to do a quick shop before dinner. There isn't any pasta at home and we only have a little rice left. Let's get some vegetables, a loaf of bread, two bottles of water and a carton of milk. We should also buy some fruit because there isn't much in the fridge.

docs.title: Situation 2 – restaurant
docs.text: Good evening. We'd like a table for two, please. Could we also have the menu? For starters, we'll have soup and a salad. For the main course, I'd like the grilled chicken with vegetables, and my friend would like the pasta. Can we also get a bottle of still water?

docs.title: 🔍 Notice the language
docs.text:
Osserva queste scelte:

  • there isn't any pasta → negazione con uncountable noun
  • a little rice → piccola quantità positiva
  • some vegetables → quantità non specificata, ma presente
  • there isn't much in the fridge → quantità ridotta
  • we'd like → forma più morbida e naturale di we want

docs.title: Drill 1 – Say it naturally
docs.text:
Trasforma le idee in inglese naturale:

  1. Vorrei un caffè e una fetta di torta.
  2. Non abbiamo latte a casa.
  3. Quante uova ci servono?
  4. Posso pagare con la carta?
  5. Abbiamo bisogno di una bottiglia d'acqua.

docs.title: Drill 2 – Expand the sentence
docs.text:
Parti da una frase breve e rendila più naturale.

  • I want pasta. → I'd like some pasta, please.

  • We need milk. → We need a carton of milk.

  • Buy apples. → Could you buy a kilo of apples?

docs.title: Drill 3 – Mini role-play
docs.text:
At the market

A: Can I help you? B: Yes, I'd like ... A: How much / how many ...? B: ... please. A: Anything else? B: That's all, thank you.

docs.title: Task 1 – My shopping list
docs.text:
Scrivi un breve testo di 120–150 parole in cui descrivi:

  • cosa devi comprare
  • cosa manca a casa
  • quali quantità ti servono
  • dove vai a fare la spesa
  • cosa compri più spesso e cosa raramente

Cerca di usare:

  • almeno 3 uncountable nouns
  • almeno 3 countable nouns
  • some / any / a lot of
  • una domanda indiretta o una richiesta cortese

docs.title: Task 2 – At the restaurant
docs.text:
Scrivi un mini-dialogo di 10–12 battute tra cliente e cameriere. Inserisci:

  • una richiesta iniziale
  • un ordine completo
  • una domanda su prezzo o disponibilità
  • una chiusura educata

docs.title: 🔊 Pronunciation and fluency
docs.text:
Parole da curare:

  • vegetables → /ˈvedʒtəblz/
  • chocolate → /ˈtʃɒklət/
  • water → /ˈwɔːtə(r)/
  • menu → /ˈmenjuː/
  • sugar → /ˈʃʊɡə(r)/
  • receipt → /rɪˈsiːt/

docs.title: Rhythm tip
docs.text:
Nelle richieste cortesi, il ritmo è importante:

  • I'd like a coffee, please.
  • Can I have the bill, please?
  • Could we get some water?

Dire bene il ritmo rende l'inglese più naturale anche se il livello grammaticale è ancora intermedio basso.

docs.title: Errore 1: articolo con uncountable nouns
docs.text:
❌ a bread ✅ some bread / a loaf of bread

❌ a rice ✅ some rice / a packet of rice

❌ a cheese ✅ some cheese / a piece of cheese

docs.title: Errore 2: much / many
docs.text:
❌ much apples ✅ many apples

❌ many water ✅ much water / a lot of water

docs.title: Errore 3: richiesta troppo diretta
docs.text:
❌ I want a table. ✅ I'd like a table, please.

❌ Give me water. ✅ Can I have some water, please?

docs.title: Errore 4: quantità senza contenitore
docs.text:
❌ two breads ✅ two loaves of bread / two pieces of bread

❌ three waters ✅ three bottles of water

docs.title: A. Choose the correct option
docs.text:
1. We don't have much / many eggs.
  2. I'd like some / any soup, please.
  3. How much / many bottles do you need?
  4. There isn't some / any milk left.
  5. She bought a lot of / much bananas.

docs.title: B. Complete the sentence
docs.text:
1. Can I have ______ bottle of water, please?
  2. We need __ bread and ____ eggs.
  3. How ______ coffee do you drink?
  4. Do you have ______ vegetarian options?
  5. I'd like ______ slice of cake.

docs.title: C. Rewrite naturally
docs.text:
1. I want a water.
  2. Give me the menu.
  3. We need much apples.
  4. There are not some eggs.
  5. I buyed bread yesterday.

docs.title: 🧠 Self-check
docs.text:
Riesci a fare tutto questo senza guardare gli appunti?

  • ordinare in un bar o ristorante in modo educato
  • distinguere How much e How many
  • usare some e any correttamente
  • parlare della spesa settimanale in 8–10 frasi
  • correggere da solo errori come a bread o much apples

Se sì, la base A2 di questa area comunicativa è davvero solida.

docs.title: 🚀 Final task
docs.text:
Preparati a parlare per 90 secondi su questo tema:

"What do you usually buy, eat and order during a normal week?"

Nel tuo intervento prova a includere:

  • almeno 8 parole di lessico sul cibo
  • 2 countable nouns e 2 uncountable nouns
  • una frase negativa con any
  • una domanda con How much / How many
  • una richiesta educata con I'd like o Can I have

Obiettivo: non solo usare la grammatica giusta, ma parlare in modo più fluido, realistico e credibile.

docs.title: Unità 17 – Quantifiers (some, any, much, many)
docs.text:
Modalità Base attiva. Questa unità ti aiuta a parlare di quantità in inglese in modo corretto e naturale. Imparerai a usare some, any, much e many, a distinguere tra nomi numerabili e non numerabili e a evitare errori molto comuni. Per la versione estesa usa il toggle BASE / PRO nella barra in alto.

Modalità Professional attiva. In questa unità lavori sui quantifiers in modo più realistico e preciso. Non ti limiti a scegliere tra some, any, much e many: impari a usarli in frasi naturali, a distinguere registro e frequenza d'uso, a fare richieste educate, a parlare di disponibilità, scarsità e abbondanza, e a evitare errori tipici degli italiani con i nomi countable e uncountable.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • usare some e any nelle frasi affermative, negative e interrogative
  • usare much e many con i nomi giusti
  • parlare di quantità in contesti quotidiani
  • fare domande semplici su quantità e disponibilità
  • evitare errori comuni come much apples o I don't have some money

docs.title: Some
docs.text:
Usiamo some soprattutto nelle frasi affermative.

  • I have some water.
  • She bought some apples.
  • We need some sugar.

A volte some si usa anche in offerte o richieste gentili.

  • Would you like some coffee?
  • Can I have some bread, please?

docs.title: Any
docs.text:
Usiamo any soprattutto nelle frasi negative e nelle domande.

  • I don't have any money.
  • There isn't any milk.
  • Do you have any questions?
  • Is there any juice in the fridge?

docs.title: Much vs Many
docs.text:
Usiamo many con i nomi countable al plurale.

  • many books
  • many people
  • many shops
  • many emails

Usiamo much con i nomi uncountable.

  • much water
  • much time
  • much money
  • much food

Esempi:

  • We don't have much time.
  • There are many students in the class.
  • How many eggs do we need?
  • How much rice is there?

docs.title: How much / How many
docs.text:
• How many + countable plural
  • How much + uncountable

Esempi:

  • How many chairs are there?
  • How many apples do you want?
  • How much coffee do you drink?
  • How much money do you need?

docs.title: Countable nouns
docs.text:
• apples
  • bottles
  • chairs
  • books
  • bags
  • sandwiches
  • students
  • questions

docs.title: Uncountable nouns
docs.text:
• water
  • coffee
  • money
  • food
  • rice
  • sugar
  • time
  • information

docs.title: Useful quantity phrases
docs.text:
• some water
  • some apples
  • any milk
  • any problems
  • much time
  • many people
  • a lot of food
  • a lot of books

docs.title: 🎧 Listening / Reading
docs.text:
Dialogue 1 – at home

A: Do we have any milk? B: No, we don't have any milk. A: What about coffee? B: We have some coffee, but not much.

Dialogue 2 – at the supermarket

A: How many apples do you want? B: Six, please. A: And how much cheese? B: About 200 grams.

docs.title: 🗣️ Speaking
docs.text:
Ripeti:

  • I have some water.
  • I don't have any money.
  • Do you have any bread?
  • There aren't many people here.
  • We don't have much time.

👉 Ora parla di te:

  • What food do you have at home?
  • Do you have any homework today?
  • How much coffee do you drink?
  • How many books do you read in a year?

👉 Fai domande:

  • Do you have any questions?
  • How much time do we have?
  • How many students are there in your class?

docs.title: ✍️ Writing
docs.text:
Scrivi 8–10 frasi usando questi elementi:

  1. some
  2. any
  3. much
  4. many
  5. How much...?
  6. How many...?

Puoi seguire questo modello:

I have some tea at home, but I don't have any coffee. I don't have much free time during the week. There are many good cafés near my office. How many emails do you write every day?

docs.title: 🔊 Pronunciation
docs.text:
Attenzione a queste parole:

  • some → /sʌm/
  • any → /ˈeni/
  • many → /ˈmeni/
  • much → /mʌtʃ/
  • money → /ˈmʌni/

👉 much e money non si pronunciano come in italiano. Fai attenzione anche alla differenza tra many e money.

docs.title: ⚠️ Errori comuni (italiani)
docs.text:
❌ I don't have some money ✅ I don't have any money

❌ I have much apples ✅ I have many apples

❌ I have many money ✅ I have much money / I have a lot of money

❌ How much books do you have? ✅ How many books do you have?

❌ There isn't many water ✅ There isn't much water

docs.title: 🧠 Pattern fondamentali
docs.text:
• some → di solito in affermativa
  • any → di solito in negativa e interrogativa
  • many → con countable plural
  • much → con uncountable
  • How many...? → countable plural
  • How much...? → uncountable

Esempi:

  • We need some eggs.
  • We don't need any eggs.
  • How many eggs do we need?
  • How much milk is there?

docs.title: ✅ Review & Practice
docs.text:
Completa con some, any, much o many:

  1. We need ___ bread.
  2. I don't have ___ time.
  3. Are there ___ students in the room?
  4. Do you have ___ questions?
  5. There are ___ books on the table.
  6. We don't have ___ milk.

docs.title: 🔄 Trasforma
docs.text:
Trasforma le frasi:

  1. I have coffee. → I have ___ coffee.
  2. I don't have money. → I don't have ___ money.
  3. apples → many ___
  4. water → much ___
  5. How ___ chairs are there?
  6. How ___ sugar do we need?

docs.title: 🚀 Mini task
docs.text:
Sei a casa e stai preparando una lista della spesa. Scrivi 6–8 frasi usando:

  • some
  • any
  • much
  • many
  • How much...?
  • How many...?

Esempio:

We need some fruit and some pasta. We don't have any milk. How many eggs do we need? We don't need much sugar.

docs.title: 💬 Self-check
docs.text:
Sai:

  • usare some e any correttamente?
  • distinguere much e many?
  • fare domande con How much e How many?
  • riconoscere i nomi countable e uncountable più comuni?

Se la risposta è sì, sei pronto per la prossima unità.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • usare con sicurezza some, any, much, many e a lot of in contesti reali
  • distinguere bene tra countable e uncountable nouns e scegliere il quantifier corretto
  • fare domande naturali su quantità e disponibilità con How much e How many
  • usare some anche in offerte e richieste, non solo in frasi affermative
  • parlare di quantità in casa, al supermercato, al lavoro e nello studio con più precisione e naturalezza

docs.title: 🧭 Language focus – quantifiers in real English
1. Some e any: la regola base è utile, ma non basta
docs.text:
La regola iniziale è questa:

  • some → soprattutto in frasi affermative
  • any → soprattutto in negative e domande

Esempi base:

  • We have some bread.
  • We don't have any bread.
  • Do we have any bread?

Questa regola è corretta, ma in inglese reale some compare anche in richieste e offerte quando il parlante pensa che la risposta sarà positiva o vuole essere cortese.

  • Would you like some tea?
  • Can I have some water, please?
  • Could you give me some information?

👉 Quindi non pensare a some solo come “affermativa”: pensa anche a quantità presente o desiderata in modo concreto e cortese.

docs.title: 2. Much e many: corretto grammaticalmente, ma attenzione alla naturalezza
docs.text:
La regola formale:

  • many + countable plural
  • much + uncountable

Esempi:

  • many people
  • many emails
  • much time
  • much money

Ma nel parlato quotidiano, nelle frasi affermative, spesso preferiamo a lot of.

Confronta:

  • I have many friends in London. ✅
  • I have a lot of friends in London. ✅ più naturale nel parlato
  • We drink much coffee. ⚠️ possibile, ma poco naturale
  • We drink a lot of coffee. ✅ molto più naturale

👉 Usa much soprattutto:

  • in frasi negative: We don't have much time.
  • in domande: How much money do you need?
  • in contesti più formali o scritti

docs.title: 3. Countable vs uncountable: la scelta del quantifier dipende dal nome
Countable nouns
docs.text:
Si possono contare singolarmente:

  • a chair / two chairs
  • a student / three students
  • a sandwich / four sandwiches
  • an idea / many ideas

Con questi usiamo:

  • many
  • a few
  • numeri
  • How many...?

docs.title: Uncountable nouns
docs.text:
Non si contano normalmente come unità separate:

  • water
  • milk
  • rice
  • information
  • advice
  • furniture
  • luggage
  • homework

Con questi usiamo:

  • much
  • a little
  • some
  • How much...?

👉 Errori tipici da evitare:

  • informations ❌ → information ✅
  • advices ❌ → advice ✅
  • furnitures ❌ → furniture ✅
  • homeworks ❌ → homework ✅

docs.title: 4. Some, any, much, many in four common situations
At home
docs.text:
• We have some pasta, but we don't have any rice.
  • There isn't much milk left.
  • How many eggs are in the fridge?

docs.title: At the supermarket
docs.text:
• Do we need any fruit?
  • We need some tomatoes and a lot of water.
  • How much cheese should I buy?

docs.title: At work
docs.text:
• I don't have much time this morning.
  • We don't have many people available today.
  • Do you have any updates on the project?

docs.title: In study situations
docs.text:
• I don't have much homework tonight.
  • She has a lot of notes, but not many books.
  • Do you have any questions about the exercise?

docs.title: Some / Any
docs.text:
• some coffee
  • some emails
  • any sugar
  • any problems

docs.title: Much / Many
docs.text:
• much time
  • much money
  • many students
  • many mistakes

docs.title: More natural spoken alternatives
docs.text:
• a lot of time
  • a lot of money
  • a lot of students
  • a lot of mistakes

docs.title: Key question patterns
docs.text:
• How much time do we have?
  • How much water do you drink?
  • How many lessons do you have this week?
  • How many messages did you get?

docs.title: Common countable nouns
docs.text:
• bottles
  • chairs
  • files
  • questions
  • mistakes
  • lessons
  • buses
  • meetings
  • customers
  • coins

docs.title: Common uncountable nouns
docs.text:
• water
  • coffee
  • money
  • time
  • advice
  • information
  • furniture
  • traffic
  • homework
  • luggage

docs.title: Useful collocations
docs.text:
• some free time
  • some useful information
  • any questions
  • any problems
  • much experience
  • much traffic
  • many options
  • many opportunities
  • a lot of work
  • a lot of energy

docs.title: Text 1 – before a dinner party
docs.text: We need to check what we have at home before people arrive. There is some bread and there are some bottles of water, but there isn't much juice left. We don't have any ice cream, and there aren't many snacks in the cupboard. How many guests are coming? If there are ten people, we need a lot of food.

docs.title: Text 2 – at work
docs.text: I'm quite busy this week. I don't have much free time, and I still have a lot of work to finish. There are many emails in my inbox, but there isn't much useful information in some of them. Do you have any time this afternoon to help me?

docs.title: 🔍 Notice the language
docs.text:
Osserva queste scelte:

  • there is some bread / there are some bottles → some con uncountable e con plural countable
  • there isn't much juice → much con uncountable in negativa
  • there aren't many snacks → many con plural countable in negativa
  • a lot of food / a lot of work → molto naturale nel parlato
  • any time this afternoon → any in domanda su disponibilità

docs.title: Drill 1 – Choose and justify
docs.text:
Completa e spiega la scelta:

  1. We don't have ___ chairs for everyone.
  2. Is there ___ milk in the fridge?
  3. I don't have ___ time today.
  4. There are ___ good restaurants in this area.
  5. Could I have ___ information about the course?

docs.title: Drill 2 – Make it more natural
docs.text:
Rendi più naturale la frase.

  • We have much work today. → We have a lot of work today.

  • I want some informations. → I need some information.

  • How much students are there? → How many students are there?

  • I don't have some money. → I don't have any money.

docs.title: Drill 3 – Short role-plays
docs.text:
Role-play A – office

A: Do you have any time after lunch? B: I don't have much time, but I can stay for twenty minutes.

Role-play B – shopping

A: How many bottles of water do we need? B: Let's get four. We don't have any at home.

Role-play C – study

A: Do you have any questions about the homework? B: Yes, I have a few questions, but not many.

docs.title: Task 1 – What we have and what we need
docs.text:
Scrivi un testo di 120–150 parole su una di queste situazioni:

  1. preparare una cena a casa
  2. organizzare una piccola riunione di lavoro
  3. preparare una settimana di studio

Nel testo prova a usare:

  • some almeno 3 volte
  • any almeno 2 volte
  • much almeno 2 volte
  • many almeno 2 volte
  • a lot of almeno 2 volte
  • almeno 4 nomi uncountable corretti

docs.title: Task 2 – Quantity questions
docs.text:
Scrivi 8 domande realistiche con How much o How many, per esempio su:

  • cibo
  • tempo
  • denaro
  • lavoro
  • studio
  • viaggi

docs.title: 🔊 Pronunciation
docs.text:
Attenzione a queste parole:

  • some → /sʌm/
  • much → /mʌtʃ/
  • many → /ˈmeni/
  • money → /ˈmʌni/
  • any → /ˈeni/

👉 Allenati a distinguere:

  • many /ˈmeni/
  • money /ˈmʌni/

Sono parole molto comuni e spesso gli italiani le confondono nella pronuncia.

docs.title: ⚠️ Error clinic – errori tipici degli italiani
docs.text:
❌ I don't have some time ✅ I don't have any time

❌ There are much people here ✅ There are many people here

❌ How much chairs do we need? ✅ How many chairs do we need?

❌ I have many homeworks ✅ I have a lot of homework / I have a lot of assignments

❌ Can you give me some informations? ✅ Can you give me some information?

❌ We haven't many milk ✅ We don't have much milk

docs.title: 🧩 Transformation practice
docs.text:
Riscrivi in inglese corretto e naturale:

  1. Non abbiamo molto tempo oggi.
  2. Ci sono molte persone fuori.
  3. Hai qualche domanda?
  4. Quante lezioni hai questa settimana?
  5. Quanta acqua bevi ogni giorno?
  6. Ho molte cose da fare e non molto tempo.
  7. Vorrei qualche informazione sul corso.
  8. Non ci sono molti autobus la sera.

docs.title: ✅ Final review
docs.text:
Completa con la forma migliore:

  1. We need ___ help with this report.
  2. There aren't ___ chairs in this room.
  3. Do you have ___ advice for me?
  4. I don't have ___ free time this month.
  5. There are ___ useful websites for English learners.
  6. How ___ luggage do you usually take on holiday?
  7. How ___ exercises do you do every week?
  8. We bought ___ food for the weekend.

docs.title: 🧠 Self-check
docs.text:
Sai:

  • distinguere rapidamente tra countable e uncountable nouns?
  • usare some e any anche in richieste e offerte?
  • scegliere tra much / many / a lot of in modo naturale?
  • fare domande corrette con How much e How many?
  • correggere errori come informations, much people, some money in negativa?

Se sì, la base grammaticale dei quantifiers è solida e puoi passare alla prossima unità con maggiore controllo e naturalezza.

docs.title: Unità 18 – Future (will / going to)
docs.text:
Modalità Base attiva. Questa unità ti aiuta a parlare del futuro in inglese usando will e be going to. Imparerai a distinguere tra decisioni spontanee, piani già decisi e semplici frasi sul futuro, evitando errori molto comuni. Per la versione estesa usa il toggle BASE / PRO nella barra in alto.

Modalità Professional attiva. In questa unità lavori sul futuro in modo più realistico e preciso. Non ti limiti a distinguere tra will e be going to: impari a parlare di intenzioni, decisioni spontanee, previsioni basate su evidenze, promesse, offerte e piani già organizzati, con lessico naturale e maggiore controllo dell'uso reale.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • parlare di piani futuri con be going to
  • esprimere decisioni prese sul momento con will
  • fare domande e frasi negative sul futuro
  • usare espressioni di tempo come tomorrow, next week, tonight, later
  • evitare errori comuni come I will to go o I going to study

docs.title: Be going to → piani e intenzioni
docs.text:
Usiamo be going to quando parliamo di qualcosa che abbiamo già deciso o che intendiamo fare.

  • I am going to study tonight.
  • She is going to visit her grandparents.
  • We are going to travel next month.

Schema:

  • I am going to + verbo
  • He / She is going to + verbo
  • We / They are going to + verbo

Esempi:

  • I am going to cook dinner.
  • They are going to watch a film.
  • He is going to start a new course.

docs.title: Will → decisioni spontanee
docs.text:
Usiamo will quando decidiamo qualcosa nel momento in cui parliamo.

  • I will help you.
  • OK, I will call her now.
  • Don't worry, we will wait.

Schema:

  • I / You / He / She / We / They will + verbo

Forma contratta:

  • I'll help.
  • You'll see.
  • We'll go later.

docs.title: Going to
docs.text:
• I am not going to work tomorrow.
  • She isn't going to come.
  • Are you going to study tonight?
  • Is he going to buy a new phone?

docs.title: Will
docs.text:
• I will not call him now.
  • She won't be late.
  • Will you help me?
  • Will they come with us?

docs.title: Differenza base
docs.text:
Struttura Uso principale be going to piano / intenzione già decisa will decisione presa sul momento

Esempi:

  • I am going to visit Rome next weekend. → l'ho già deciso
  • OK, I will come with you. → lo decido adesso

docs.title: Future time expressions
docs.text:
• tomorrow
  • tonight
  • later
  • next week
  • next month
  • this weekend
  • in the evening
  • soon

docs.title: Useful verbs
docs.text:
• study
  • work
  • call
  • help
  • travel
  • visit
  • buy
  • start
  • stay
  • cook

docs.title: Useful phrases
docs.text:
• make a plan
  • call later
  • help someone
  • visit a friend
  • stay at home
  • travel by train
  • start a course
  • buy a ticket

docs.title: 🎧 Listening / Reading
docs.text:
Dialogue 1 – making plans

A: What are you going to do this weekend? B: I am going to visit my sister. A: Nice. Are you going to stay there all weekend? B: No, just for one day.

Dialogue 2 – spontaneous decision

A: I can't open this box. B: Wait, I will help you. A: Thanks. B: No problem.

docs.title: 🗣️ Speaking
docs.text:
Ripeti:

  • I am going to study tonight.
  • We are going to travel next month.
  • I will help you.
  • I will call you later.
  • Are you going to come?
  • Will you be at home tonight?

👉 Ora parla di te:

  • What are you going to do tomorrow?
  • Are you going to study this evening?
  • What are you going to do next weekend?
  • When will you call a friend or a family member?

👉 Fai domande:

  • Are you going to work tomorrow?
  • Will you help me?
  • Are they going to travel this summer?
  • Will she call later?

docs.title: ✍️ Writing
docs.text:
Scrivi 8 frasi:

  • 4 con be going to
  • 4 con will

Puoi seguire questo modello:

I am going to study English tonight. We are going to visit our friends on Saturday. She is going to buy a new bag next week. They are going to stay at home tomorrow.

I will help you with your homework. I will call you later. We will wait here. He will open the window.

docs.title: 🔊 Pronunciation
docs.text:
Attenzione a queste forme naturali:

  • going to → spesso si sente gonna nel parlato informale
  • I will → I'll
  • we will → we'll
  • will not → won't

Esempi:

  • I'm going to go. → nel parlato informale può suonare come I'm gonna go
  • I'll call you later.
  • We won't be late.

docs.title: ⚠️ Errori comuni (italiani)
docs.text:
❌ I will to go ✅ I will go

❌ I going to study ✅ I am going to study

❌ I will going ✅ I will go

❌ She going to work tomorrow ✅ She is going to work tomorrow

❌ Are you going study tonight? ✅ Are you going to study tonight?

docs.title: 🧠 Pattern fondamentali
docs.text:
• be going to + verbo base
  • will + verbo base
  • be cambia in base al soggetto
  • dopo will non usiamo mai to

Esempi:

  • I am going to study.
  • He is going to work.
  • They are going to travel.
  • I will help.
  • We will call later.

docs.title: ✅ Review & Practice
docs.text:
Completa con am / is / are / will:

  1. I ___ going to study tonight.
  2. She ___ going to call later.
  3. We ___ help you.
  4. They ___ going to travel next month.
  5. I ___ open the door.
  6. He ___ going to stay at home.

docs.title: 🔄 Trasforma
docs.text:
Trasforma le frasi:

  1. I study tonight. → I am going to ______
  2. I help you. → I will ______
  3. She visit her aunt next week. → She is going to ______
  4. We call you later. → We will ______
  5. They stay at home tomorrow. → They are going to ______

docs.title: 🧠 Think & Speak
docs.text:
Parla per 1 minuto di:

  • i tuoi piani per domani
  • cosa farai nel weekend
  • una decisione che puoi prendere sul momento oggi

docs.title: 🚀 Mini task
docs.text:
Scrivi o racconta un breve testo su:

My plans for next week

Prova a usare almeno:

  • 3 frasi con going to
  • 2 frasi con will
  • 4 espressioni di tempo futuro

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • usare be going to per piani, intenzioni e previsioni con evidenza presente
  • usare will per decisioni spontanee, offerte, promesse e previsioni generali
  • fare domande e frasi negative con buona sicurezza e naturalezza
  • distinguere casi in cui entrambe le forme sono possibili ma con sfumature diverse
  • parlare dei tuoi progetti futuri in modo più fluido, preciso e realistico

docs.title: 🧭 Language focus – talking about the future naturally
1. Be going to: piani, intenzioni, segnali evidenti
docs.text:
Usiamo be going to quando:

  • abbiamo già un'intenzione o un piano
  • parliamo di qualcosa che abbiamo deciso prima del momento in cui parliamo
  • facciamo una previsione basata su qualcosa che vediamo o sappiamo già

Esempi:

  • I'm going to study after dinner.
  • We're going to move to a bigger flat next year.
  • She's going to apply for a new job.
  • Look at those clouds. It's going to rain.

👉 Qui la previsione non è “solo un'opinione”: c'è un segnale concreto.

docs.title: 2. Will: decisione del momento, offerta, promessa, previsione generale
docs.text:
Usiamo will quando:

  • decidiamo qualcosa mentre stiamo parlando
  • facciamo un'offerta
  • promettiamo qualcosa
  • esprimiamo una previsione, spesso come opinione o valutazione personale

Esempi:

  • The phone is ringing. I'll answer it.
  • I'll help you with your bags.
  • I won't tell anyone.
  • I think our team will win.
  • I'm sure you'll enjoy the course.

👉 will suona molto naturale con espressioni come:

  • I think...
  • I guess...
  • probably
  • maybe
  • I'm sure...

docs.title: 3. The core contrast: prior plan vs decision now
docs.text:
Confronta:

  • I'm going to cook tonight. → l'ho già deciso

  • OK, I'll cook tonight. → lo decido adesso

  • We're going to visit Laura on Sunday. → visita già programmata

  • Fine, we'll visit Laura on Sunday. → decisione presa nel momento della conversazione

Questa differenza è centrale. In tanti casi entrambe le forme sono grammaticalmente possibili, ma il significato cambia.

docs.title: Will for opinion / personal prediction
docs.text:
• I think it will be a good year.
  • She'll probably pass the exam.
  • I don't think they'll arrive on time.

docs.title: Going to for visible evidence / strong signs
docs.text:
• Look at the traffic. We're going to be late.
  • He's driving too fast. He's going to have an accident.
  • The glass is full. It's going to fall.

👉 Regola utile:

  • will = previsione mentale / opinione
  • going to = previsione con base osservabile o già evidente

docs.title: Be going to
docs.text:
Affermativa

  • I am going to leave early.
  • She is going to start a new project.
  • They are going to travel in June.

Negativa

  • I'm not going to stay long.
  • He isn't going to buy it.
  • We aren't going to change the date.

Interrogativa

  • Are you going to call her?
  • Is she going to join us?
  • Are they going to book the hotel?

docs.title: Will
docs.text:
Affermativa

  • I will call you later.
  • She will help us.
  • They will arrive soon.

Negativa

  • I won't forget.
  • He won't agree.
  • We won't be late.

Interrogativa

  • Will you send me the file?
  • Will she be there?
  • Will they understand?

docs.title: 📚 Vocabulary expansion – future plans and decisions
Planning and organisation
docs.text:
• make a plan
  • arrange a meeting
  • book a hotel
  • buy a ticket
  • cancel a trip
  • change the date
  • start a course
  • move house
  • apply for a job
  • save money

docs.title: Promises and offers
docs.text:
• help you
  • call you back
  • send the document
  • wait here
  • carry your bag
  • explain everything
  • check it later
  • remind you tomorrow

docs.title: Future time expressions
docs.text:
• tomorrow morning
  • tomorrow evening
  • later today
  • next weekend
  • next month
  • in a few days
  • in the summer
  • soon
  • one day
  • in the future

docs.title: Text 1 – plans and intentions
docs.text: I'm going to have a busy weekend. On Saturday morning I'm going to clean the house and then I'm going to meet a friend for lunch. In the afternoon we're going to look at a flat because we may move later this year. On Sunday I'm going to visit my parents. I already told them, so it's a real plan, not just an idea.

docs.title: Text 2 – spontaneous decisions and predictions
docs.text: The weather looks terrible. It's going to rain soon, so I'm going to take an umbrella. I think the buses will be late because traffic is already heavy. If that happens, I'll call the office and I'll tell them I'm running late. Don't worry, everything will be fine.

docs.title: 🔍 Notice the language
docs.text:
Osserva queste scelte:

  • I'm going to have a busy weekend → intenzione / piano
  • we're going to look at a flat → attività organizzata
  • It's going to rain → previsione con evidenza
  • I think the buses will be late → opinione personale
  • I'll call the office → decisione del momento / reazione
  • everything will be fine → previsione rassicurante / promessa implicita

docs.title: Drill 1 – Choose the best form
docs.text:
Scegli tra will e going to e spiega perché.

  1. I think she ___ enjoy the film.
  2. Look at those black clouds. It ___ rain.
  3. We ___ visit our cousins next weekend. We already bought the train tickets.
  4. The phone is ringing. I ___ answer it.
  5. Don't worry. I ___ help you with the report.

docs.title: Drill 2 – Rephrase with a different meaning
docs.text:
Confronta le due versioni e spiega la differenza.

  • I'm going to cook dinner tonight.

  • I'll cook dinner tonight.

  • We're going to leave early tomorrow.

  • We'll leave early tomorrow.

  • She's going to call him later.

  • She'll call him later.

👉 L'obiettivo non è solo “scegliere il tempo giusto”, ma capire la prospettiva del parlante.

docs.title: Drill 3 – Short role-plays
docs.text:
Role-play A – at home

A: We don't have any bread for breakfast. B: OK, I'll go to the bakery now.

Role-play B – planning the weekend

A: What are you going to do on Saturday? B: I'm going to visit my grandparents and then I'm going to meet some friends.

Role-play C – prediction

A: Do you think the exam will be difficult? B: I think it will be challenging, but manageable.

Role-play D – office situation

A: The client just emailed us. B: I'll reply now. A: Good. I'm going to prepare the updated file.

docs.title: Task 1 – Plans, decisions, predictions
docs.text:
Scrivi un testo di 120–150 parole intitolato:

My plans for the next few weeks

Nel testo prova a includere:

  • almeno 4 frasi con going to
  • almeno 4 frasi con will
  • una previsione personale con I think... will...
  • una previsione con evidenza con going to
  • almeno 5 espressioni di tempo futuro

docs.title: Task 2 – Real-life mini scenarios
docs.text:
Completa queste situazioni con una o due frasi naturali:

  1. Your friend looks tired.
  2. You already bought a ticket for Friday.
  3. Someone asks for help with a heavy bag.
  4. The sky is very dark.
  5. You decide to send an email right now.

docs.title: Contractions
docs.text:
Nel parlato quotidiano le contrazioni sono frequentissime:

  • I will → I'll
  • you will → you'll
  • he will → he'll
  • we will → we'll
  • will not → won't

docs.title: Going to in connected speech
docs.text:
Nel parlato informale going to spesso si riduce a un suono simile a gonna.

  • I'm going to call her. → informally: I'm gonna call her.

👉 In un corso professionale è utile riconoscerlo quando ascolti, ma nello scritto standard continuiamo a usare going to.

docs.title: ⚠️ Errori comuni (italiani)
docs.text:
❌ I will to call you later ✅ I will call you later

❌ I going to buy a new phone ✅ I am going to buy a new phone

❌ She will goes there tomorrow ✅ She will go there tomorrow

❌ We are going to going out ✅ We are going to go out

❌ I think it is going to be fine, because that's my opinion only ✅ I think it will be fine

❌ Look at the sky! It will rain. ✅ Look at the sky! It is going to rain.

👉 L'ultima coppia non è “sempre sbagliata” in assoluto, ma going to è la scelta più naturale quando la previsione nasce da un segnale evidente.

docs.title: 🧠 Pattern fondamentali
docs.text:
• be going to + verbo base → piano, intenzione, previsione con evidenza
  • will + verbo base → decisione spontanea, offerta, promessa, previsione/opinione
  • dopo will non usiamo mai to
  • con going to il verbo be è obbligatorio e cambia con il soggetto

Mini-schema:

  • I'm going to study.
  • She's going to start a new job.
  • We'll help you.
  • I think they'll arrive soon.
  • Look out! You're going to drop it.

docs.title: ✅ Review & Practice
docs.text:
Completa con will o be going to nella forma corretta.

  1. I think our team ___ win.
  2. Look at that child! He ___ fall.
  3. We already booked everything. We ___ travel in July.
  4. The doorbell is ringing. I ___ open the door.
  5. Don't worry, I ___ keep your secret.
  6. She bought the ingredients. She ___ cook tonight.
  7. I don't think they ___ agree.
  8. Are you ___ apply for that job?

docs.title: 🔄 Transformation work
docs.text:
Trasforma o completa le frasi mantenendo il significato.

  1. I decided yesterday to study more. → I ___ study more.

  2. The phone is ringing. I answer it now. → I ___ answer it.

  3. There are dark clouds in the sky. Rain is imminent. → It ___ rain.

  4. I promise I keep this information private. → I ___ keep this information private.

  5. We already arranged the meeting for Monday. → We ___ meet on Monday.

docs.title: 🧠 Think & Speak
docs.text:
Parla per 90 secondi su questi punti:

  • tre cose che hai già deciso di fare nelle prossime settimane
  • una decisione che prenderesti subito in una situazione di emergenza
  • due previsioni sul tuo lavoro, sui tuoi studi o sulla tua vita personale
  • una promessa che puoi fare a te stesso o a un'altra persona

Prova a usare sia will sia going to in modo consapevole.

docs.title: Speaking or writing task
docs.text:
Prepara una presentazione breve intitolata:

My future plans and what I think will happen

Deve includere:

  • piani già decisi
  • intenzioni personali
  • almeno due decisioni spontanee immaginate
  • almeno due previsioni
  • un'offerta o una promessa

Obiettivo: parlare o scrivere in modo più naturale, non solo “applicare una regola”.

docs.title: Unità 19 – Experiences (Have you ever...?)
docs.text:
Modalità Base attiva. Questa unità ti aiuta a parlare di esperienze di vita usando il Present Perfect con have you ever...?, have / haven't e never. Imparerai a fare domande semplici, rispondere in modo naturale e distinguere tra esperienza generale e passato con data precisa. Per la versione estesa usa il toggle BASE / PRO nella barra in alto.

Modalità Professional attiva. In questa unità lavori sul Present Perfect of experience in modo molto più preciso e naturale. Non ti limiti a dire yes / no: impari a chiedere, approfondire, raccontare esperienze, collegare il Present Perfect al Past Simple, usare ever / never / before, scegliere i participi corretti e sostenere mini-conversazioni autentiche.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • fare domande con Have you ever...?
  • parlare di esperienze personali con have / haven't
  • usare ever e never in modo corretto
  • distinguere Present Perfect e Past Simple in situazioni semplici
  • evitare errori comuni come Have you ever went?

docs.title: Present Perfect for experiences
docs.text:
Usiamo il Present Perfect quando parliamo di un'esperienza nella vita, senza dire quando è successa.

Schema:

  • have / has + past participle

Esempi:

  • I have been to Spain.
  • She has tried Japanese food.
  • We have seen that film.
  • Have you ever visited London?

👉 Qui l'idea importante è: esperienza nella vita, non il momento preciso.

docs.title: Questions with Have you ever...?
docs.text:
Usiamo ever soprattutto nelle domande per chiedere se qualcuno ha fatto qualcosa almeno una volta nella vita.

Schema:

  • Have you ever + past participle...?

Esempi:

  • Have you ever been to Paris?
  • Have you ever eaten sushi?
  • Have you ever travelled alone?
  • Have you ever seen snow?

Risposte brevi:

  • Yes, I have.
  • No, I haven't.

Risposte più complete:

  • Yes, I have. I have been there twice.
  • No, I haven't, but I'd like to.

docs.title: Negative with never
docs.text:
Usiamo never per dire che un'esperienza non è mai successa.

  • I have never tried Indian food.
  • He has never travelled by plane.
  • We have never been to Scotland.

⚠️ Never è già negativo, quindi non usiamo don't / didn't.

❌ I haven't never tried sushi. ✅ I have never tried sushi.

docs.title: Been and gone
docs.text:
Per esperienze di viaggio, a livello base, la forma più utile è:

  • have been to = essere stato/a in un posto

Esempi:

  • I have been to Rome.
  • She has been to Berlin.
  • Have you ever been to London?

docs.title: Present Perfect vs Past Simple
docs.text:
Tempo Uso Present Perfect esperienza generale, senza tempo preciso Past Simple azione finita in un momento preciso

Confronta:

  • I have been to Rome. → esperienza nella vita

  • I went to Rome last year. → dici quando

  • Have you ever eaten sushi? → esperienza generale

  • When did you eat sushi? → domanda sul momento preciso

👉 Se dici yesterday, last year, in 2022, di solito usi il Past Simple.

docs.title: Experiences
docs.text:
• travel abroad
  • visit a museum
  • eat sushi
  • try a new sport
  • see a famous place
  • meet a famous person
  • fly by plane
  • stay in a hotel
  • climb a mountain
  • speak English with a stranger

docs.title: Useful expressions
docs.text:
• ever
  • never
  • before
  • in my life
  • once
  • twice
  • many times

docs.title: Dialogue 1
docs.text: A: Have you ever been abroad? B: Yes, I have. I have been to Spain and Portugal. A: Really? When did you go to Portugal? B: I went there last summer.

docs.title: Dialogue 2
docs.text: A: Have you ever tried Korean food? B: No, I haven't. I have never tried it. A: Would you like to? B: Yes, definitely.

docs.title: 🗣️ Speaking
docs.text:
Ripeti ad alta voce:

  • Have you ever been to another country?
  • Have you ever eaten sushi?
  • Have you ever travelled alone?
  • I have never tried scuba diving.
  • Yes, I have.
  • No, I haven't.

Ora prova con idee tue:

  • Have you ever ______?
  • I have ______.
  • I have never ______.

Mini speaking task:

Fai 5 domande a un compagno o rispondi da solo.

Temi possibili:

  • food
  • travel
  • music
  • sport
  • cinema
  • study

docs.title: Task 1 – My experiences
docs.text:
Scrivi 8 frasi:

  • 4 cose che hai fatto
  • 4 cose che non hai mai fatto

Esempio:

I have been to Florence. I have travelled by plane. I have seen the sea in winter. I have tried Japanese food. I have never visited New York. I have never driven a motorbike. I have never met a famous actor. I have never gone camping alone.

docs.title: Task 2 – Short interview
docs.text: Scrivi 5 domande con Have you ever...? e 5 possibili risposte.

docs.title: 🔊 Pronunciation
docs.text:
Attenzione a queste forme:

  • have → spesso debole /həv/
  • ever → /ˈevə(r)/
  • been → spesso /biːn/ oppure /bɪn/

Chunk utili:

  • Have you ever...
  • I've never...
  • Have you been to...?

Ripeti in modo fluido:

  • Have you ever been to London?
  • I've never tried that.
  • Have you ever spoken English abroad?

docs.title: ⚠️ Errori comuni (italiani)
docs.text:
❌ Have you ever went to Rome? ✅ Have you ever gone to Rome? / Have you ever been to Rome?

❌ I have see that film. ✅ I have seen that film.

❌ I have been in London. ✅ I have been to London.

❌ I didn't ever try sushi. ✅ I have never tried sushi.

❌ Have you ever did that? ✅ Have you ever done that?

docs.title: Complete the sentences
docs.text:
1. Have you ever ___ (eat) sushi?
  2. I have never ___ (be) to Germany.
  3. She has ___ (see) that film.
  4. We have ___ (try) Thai food.
  5. Have they ever ___ (travel) abroad?

docs.title: Choose the correct form
docs.text:
1. I have been / went to Rome.
  2. Have you ever see / seen snow?
  3. She has never try / tried Indian food.
  4. We went / have gone there last year.
  5. Have you ever been / gone to Paris?

docs.title: Transform
docs.text:
1. I went to London last year. → I have ______ to London.
  2. I didn't try sushi in my life. → I have never ______ sushi.
  3. Did you ever visit Spain? → Have you ever ______ Spain?

docs.title: 🧠 Think & Speak
docs.text:
Parla di:

  • 3 posti che hai visitato
  • 3 esperienze che hai fatto
  • 3 cose che non hai mai fatto ma vuoi fare

Modello utile:

  • I have been to...
  • I have tried...
  • I have never..., but I'd like to...

docs.title: 🚀 Mini task
docs.text:
Intervista una persona con 6 domande.

Usa almeno questi temi:

  • travel
  • food
  • films
  • sport
  • study
  • free time

Poi scrivi un mini report:

Marta has been to Spain, but she has never travelled alone. She has tried sushi, but she hasn't tried Indian food.

docs.title: 🎯 Self-check
docs.text:
Sai:

  • fare domande con Have you ever...?
  • usare ever e never
  • formare il participio passato dei verbi più comuni
  • distinguere esperienza generale e passato con data precisa

Se sì, sei pronto per la review finale di A2.

Experiences make conversations more interesting.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • usare il Present Perfect per parlare di esperienze di vita con sicurezza
  • costruire domande naturali con Have you ever...?
  • rispondere in modo più ricco con dettagli, follow-up e reazioni
  • distinguere bene experience e finished past event
  • usare in modo corretto been, gone, ever, never, before e i participi irregolari più frequenti

docs.title: 🧭 Language focus – Present Perfect for life experience
1. What the tense really does
docs.text:
Quando usiamo il Present Perfect per le esperienze, non stiamo raccontando una storia in ordine cronologico. Stiamo dicendo che una certa esperienza fa parte della nostra vita fino ad ora.

  • I've been to Berlin.
  • She's tried Turkish food.
  • Have you ever spoken English with native speakers?

Il punto centrale non è quando, ma se è successo almeno una volta nella tua vita.

👉 Per questo il Present Perfect risponde bene a domande come:

  • Have you ever...?
  • Have you tried...?
  • Have you been to...?
  • Have you done that before?

docs.title: 2. The basic structure
docs.text:
Affermativa

  • I have visited Dublin.
  • She has travelled alone.
  • We have tried that restaurant.

Negativa

  • I haven't been there.
  • He hasn't tried sushi.
  • They haven't travelled abroad.

Interrogativa

  • Have you ever visited Dublin?
  • Has she ever flown business class?
  • Have they tried Moroccan food?

Short answers

  • Yes, I have.
  • No, I haven't.
  • Yes, she has.
  • No, they haven't.

docs.title: Ever
docs.text:
Usiamo ever soprattutto nelle domande:

  • Have you ever been to Greece?
  • Have you ever eaten snails?
  • Have you ever done anything like this?

Può comparire anche in frasi come:

  • It's the best film I've ever seen.

docs.title: Never
docs.text:
Usiamo never per dire “mai nella vita fino ad ora”.

  • I've never tried horse riding.
  • She's never flown alone.
  • We've never stayed in a five-star hotel.

docs.title: Before
docs.text:
Before è utile quando vogliamo dire che qualcosa è già successo prima di questo momento.

  • I've seen this film before.
  • Have you met him before?
  • We haven't been here before.

👉 never è più assoluto; before dice semplicemente che l'esperienza è o non è già successa in precedenza.

docs.title: 🧠 Been vs gone
docs.text: Questa distinzione merita attenzione.

docs.title: Have been to
docs.text:
Significa: la persona è stata in un posto e poi è tornata.

  • I've been to Madrid twice.
  • Have you ever been to Amsterdam?
  • She's been to Japan, but only once.

docs.title: Have gone to
docs.text:
Significa: la persona è andata lì e, nel momento in cui parliamo, è ancora là oppure è in viaggio.

  • Tom has gone to the bank. (= non è qui adesso)
  • My parents have gone to Sicily for the weekend.

👉 Per parlare di esperienze di vita, nella maggior parte dei casi userai have been to, non have gone to.

docs.title: 🔄 Present Perfect vs Past Simple – the real contrast
Present Perfect: experience without time reference
docs.text:
• Have you ever visited London?
  • I've tried Korean food.
  • She's never seen snow.

docs.title: Past Simple: specific finished moment
docs.text:
• When did you visit London?
  • I tried Korean food last month.
  • She saw snow when she was a child.

👉 Nelle conversazioni reali spesso succede questo:

  1. prima chiedi l'esperienza con Present Perfect
  2. poi chiedi il dettaglio con Past Simple

Esempio naturale:

  • Have you ever been to Ireland?
  • Yes, I have.
  • Really? When did you go?
  • I went there in 2023.
  • Did you like it?
  • Yes, I loved it.

Questa sequenza è fondamentale.

docs.title: 🧱 High-frequency past participles
docs.text:
Ecco i participi che servono davvero in questa unità:

Base form Past participle be been go gone see seen eat eaten do done try tried visit visited travel travelled / traveled fly flown ride ridden speak spoken write written take taken make made meet met

👉 Per chi studia dall'italiano, gli errori più frequenti sono:

  • I have went
  • I have saw
  • I have wrote
  • Have you ever did...?

docs.title: 📚 Vocabulary expansion – experiences worth talking about
Travel and movement
docs.text:
• travel abroad
  • go on a road trip
  • miss a flight
  • stay in a hostel
  • take a night train
  • visit a capital city
  • cross a border
  • get lost in a new city

docs.title: Food and culture
docs.text:
• try street food
  • eat something spicy
  • cook a foreign dish
  • go to a food market
  • visit a museum
  • watch a show in English
  • attend a concert
  • see a famous landmark

docs.title: Personal development
docs.text:
• speak in public
  • take an exam in English
  • make a speech
  • work with international clients
  • study abroad
  • move to another city
  • meet someone from another culture

Useful follow-up expressions:

  • once / twice / several times
  • a few years ago
  • when I was younger
  • the first time
  • actually
  • to be honest
  • not yet

docs.title: Dialogue 1 – from experience to detail
docs.text: Emma: Have you ever travelled on your own? Leo: Yes, I have. I went to Lisbon by myself two years ago. Emma: Really? Were you nervous? Leo: A little, yes, but it was a great experience. Emma: Have you ever stayed in a hostel? Leo: No, I haven't. I've always stayed in small hotels.

docs.title: Dialogue 2 – food and reactions
docs.text: Marta: Have you ever tried Ethiopian food? Chris: No, I haven't, but I've heard it's excellent. Marta: I've had it twice. It's really good. Chris: Where did you try it? Marta: In London, last autumn.

docs.title: Dialogue 3 – work and study
docs.text: A: Have you ever had to use English at work? B: Yes, I have. I've spoken to clients from Germany and the Netherlands. A: Was it difficult at first? B: Yes, it was, but it got easier with practice.

docs.title: Drill 1 – question formation
docs.text:
Transform the prompts into full questions:

  • you / ever / be / abroad
  • you / ever / fly / alone
  • you / ever / try / Indian food
  • you / ever / speak / English with a stranger
  • you / ever / lose / your passport

docs.title: Drill 2 – short answer + extension
docs.text:
Answer using this pattern:

  • Yes, I have. / No, I haven't. + one extra detail

Examples:

  • Have you ever travelled by plane?

  • Yes, I have. I've flown to Barcelona twice.

  • Have you ever tried surfing?

  • No, I haven't, but I'd like to.

docs.title: Drill 3 – experience interview
docs.text:
Ask and answer at least 8 questions about:

  • travel
  • food
  • films
  • English
  • work
  • sport
  • unusual experiences
  • future wishes connected to experiences

docs.title: Task 1 – Experience profile
docs.text:
Scrivi un testo di 120–150 parole su di te.

Devi includere:

  • 4 esperienze che hai già fatto
  • 3 cose che non hai mai fatto
  • 2 dettagli al Past Simple su una delle esperienze
  • almeno 5 participi passati diversi

Possible opening:

I have had several interesting experiences in my life. I have travelled abroad, tried different kinds of food and spoken English with foreign visitors. However, there are still many things I haven't done yet...

docs.title: Task 2 – Interview report
docs.text:
Immagina di aver intervistato un amico con domande Have you ever...?. Scrivi un report di 90–120 parole.

Model:

Giulia has travelled abroad several times, but she has never flown outside Europe. She has tried Japanese food and she has been to Spain, but she hasn't visited Portugal yet...

docs.title: Weak forms and rhythm
docs.text:
Nella lingua parlata, have spesso non è forte.

  • Have you ever...? → suona spesso come /həv juː ˈevə/
  • I've never... → ritmo compatto e veloce

docs.title: Sound focus
docs.text:
• been → /biːn/ o /bɪn/
  • ever → stress sulla prima sillaba
  • written / ridden / eaten / spoken → attenzione alla sillaba accentata e alla consonante finale

Repeat in chunks:

  • Have you ever been there?
  • I've never done that before.
  • Have you ever spoken English at work?
  • It's the best meal I've ever had.

docs.title: ⚠️ Error clinic – typical Italian mistakes
docs.text:
❌ Have you ever went to London? ✅ Have you ever been to London? / Have you ever gone to London? (grammatical, ma meno naturale per esperienza)

❌ I have saw that film. ✅ I have seen that film.

❌ I have wrote an email in English. ✅ I have written an email in English.

❌ I am never been to Berlin. ✅ I have never been to Berlin.

❌ Did you ever try sushi? ✅ Have you ever tried sushi?

❌ I have been in Paris. ✅ I have been to Paris.

❌ I have gone to Rome last year. ✅ I went to Rome last year.

👉 Regola d'oro:

  • Present Perfect → esperienza / collegamento con il presente
  • Past Simple → momento finito e specifico nel passato

docs.title: A. Complete with the correct participle
docs.text:
1. Have you ever ___ (ride) a horse?
  2. I have never ___ (write) a formal email in English.
  3. She has ___ (meet) several international clients.
  4. We have ___ (take) a night train.
  5. Have they ever ___ (fly) abroad?

docs.title: B. Choose the best option
docs.text:
1. Have you ever been / went to Scotland?
  2. I have never seen / saw snow.
  3. We went / have gone there in 2022.
  4. She has tried / tried sushi last week.
  5. Have you ever did / done anything like that?

docs.title: C. Correct the mistakes
docs.text:
1. I have went to Spain.
  2. Have you ever saw this film?
  3. I have never be there.
  4. She has gone to Paris last year.
  5. Did you ever travelled alone?

docs.title: D. Build a mini-conversation
docs.text:
Start with Present Perfect, continue with Past Simple:

  • Have you ever ______?
  • Yes, I have. / No, I haven't.
  • When did you ______?
  • I __ in ____.
  • Did you enjoy it?
  • Yes, I did / No, I didn't.

docs.title: 🧠 Think, react, extend
docs.text:
Answer these naturally, not with one-word replies:

  • Have you ever done something that scared you?
  • Have you ever eaten something you didn't like at all?
  • Have you ever had to speak English unexpectedly?
  • Have you ever missed a train or a flight?
  • Have you ever changed your opinion after travelling somewhere?

Useful reaction language:

  • Actually, yes, I have.
  • Not yet, but I'd love to.
  • No, never.
  • Yes, once, and it was...
  • Yes, a couple of times.
  • No, but I'm curious to try.

docs.title: Part 1
docs.text: Crea una mini intervista di 10 domande con Have you ever...?

docs.title: Part 2
docs.text:
Scegli 3 risposte positive e trasformale in una breve storia con dettagli al Past Simple.

Example:

Have you ever travelled alone? Yes, I have. I went to Lisbon in 2024. I stayed in a small hotel near the centre and I met people from different countries. It was challenging at first, but I really enjoyed it.

docs.title: 🎯 Self-assessment
docs.text:
Sai:

  • usare Have you ever...? con buona fluidità
  • scegliere il participio corretto dei verbi più frequenti
  • passare da Present Perfect a Past Simple per chiedere dettagli
  • evitare gli errori classici su been / gone, ever / never, gone / went
  • sostenere una breve conversazione autentica sulle esperienze

Se sì, sei pronto per la review finale di A2.

Interesting conversations often begin with real experiences.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità saprai:

  • ripassare le strutture fondamentali di A2
  • parlare di routine, passato, esperienze e futuro
  • gestire una conversazione semplice ma completa
  • scrivere un testo breve e coerente

docs.title: 🧠 Grammar Review
docs.text:
In A2 hai studiato:

  • Present Simple per abitudini e routine
  • Present Continuous per azioni in corso o situazioni temporanee
  • Past Simple per eventi conclusi nel passato
  • will / going to per futuro, decisioni e piani
  • some / any / much / many / a lot of
  • comparatives and superlatives
  • Present Perfect per esperienze di vita

👉 In questa unità usi tutto insieme.

docs.title: 📚 Vocabulary Review
docs.text:
Sai parlare di:

  • vita quotidiana
  • lavoro e studio
  • viaggi e vacanze
  • cibo e spesa
  • città e servizi
  • esperienze personali
  • piani futuri

docs.title: 📝 TEST 1 – Grammar
docs.text:
Completa le frasi con la forma corretta del verbo o della parola.

  1. I usually ___ to work by bus. (go)
  2. Look! The children ___ in the garden. (play)
  3. Last weekend we ___ a museum. (visit)
  4. I have never ___ Japanese food. (try)
  5. She is going to ___ her grandparents tomorrow. (see)
  6. I think it ___ rain later. (will)
  7. There aren’t ___ eggs in the fridge.
  8. How ___ milk do we need?
  9. This exercise is ___ than the last one. (easy)
  10. It’s the ___ restaurant in the area. (good)

docs.title: 📝 TEST 2 – Transformations
docs.text:
Trasforma le frasi come indicato.

  1. I work in an office. → (now)
  2. She goes to school every day. → (yesterday)
  3. We travelled to Spain. → (experience)
  4. They have a meeting tomorrow. → (plan with going to)
  5. There is some water. → (negative)

docs.title: 🗣️ SPEAKING REVIEW
docs.text:
Parla per 2 minuti e includi:

  • chi sei
  • dove vivi
  • cosa fai ogni giorno
  • cosa stai facendo in questo periodo
  • cosa hai fatto recentemente
  • un’esperienza importante
  • un piano per il futuro

docs.title: Traccia utile
docs.text:
Puoi seguire questo ordine:

  1. personal information
  2. daily routine
  3. today / this week
  4. yesterday / last weekend
  5. life experiences
  6. future plans

docs.title: ✍️ WRITING TASK
docs.text:
Scrivi un testo di 10–12 frasi su di te.

Il testo deve includere:

  • routine quotidiana
  • un’attività che stai facendo in questo periodo
  • qualcosa che hai fatto nel passato
  • un’esperienza
  • un piano futuro

docs.title: Modello possibile
docs.text: My name is _. I live in . I usually _. These days I am . Last weekend I _. I have already . Next week I am going to _. I think I will .

docs.title: 🧠 THINK & SPEAK
docs.text:
Rispondi oralmente:

  • What do you usually do in the morning?
  • What are you doing these days?
  • What did you do last weekend?
  • Have you ever travelled abroad?
  • What are you going to do next weekend?
  • Which place in your town is the most interesting?

docs.title: 🔊 PRONUNCIATION CHECK
docs.text:
Leggi ad alta voce:

  • I work every day.
  • I am working now.
  • I worked yesterday.
  • I have visited London.
  • I’m going to study tonight.
  • I think it will be fine.

Fai attenzione a:

  • worked /t/
  • visited /ɪd/
  • going to nella lingua parlata rapida

docs.title: ⚠️ Errori comuni
docs.text:
❌ I work now. ✅ I am working now.

❌ I have went to Spain. ✅ I have gone to Spain.

❌ I will to call you. ✅ I will call you.

❌ I don’t have some money. ✅ I don’t have any money.

❌ She is more taller than me. ✅ She is taller than me.

docs.title: 🚀 FINAL TASK
docs.text:
Immagina di incontrare una persona nuova in un corso di inglese.

Devi:

  • presentarti
  • parlare della tua routine
  • raccontare una cosa fatta nel weekend
  • descrivere un’esperienza della tua vita
  • dire un piano per il mese prossimo
  • fare almeno 3 domande all’altra persona

docs.title: 🎯 Autovalutazione
docs.text:
Metti ✔️ se sai fare queste cose:

  • parlare per 2 minuti con poche pause
  • usare presente, passato e futuro in modo comprensibile
  • fare domande semplici e naturali
  • scrivere un testo breve e chiaro
  • capire una conversazione lenta su temi quotidiani

Se hai almeno 4/5, sei pronto per iniziare il livello B1.

docs.title: 💬 Next Step
docs.text:
Nel livello B1 inizierai a:

  • esprimere opinioni in modo più articolato
  • gestire conversazioni più lunghe
  • usare i modali con più sicurezza
  • parlare di ipotesi e situazioni immaginarie
  • scrivere testi più completi

If you can talk about your life in the present, past, and future, you are ready to move beyond beginner English.

docs.title: 🎯 Lesson Goals
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • integrare in modo coerente le principali strutture grammaticali studiate in A2
  • parlare con maggiore continuità di abitudini, situazioni temporanee, esperienze, eventi passati e progetti futuri
  • sostenere una breve conversazione reale con domande, risposte e follow-up
  • scrivere un testo personale chiaro, coeso e grammaticalmente controllato
  • riconoscere e correggere gli errori più frequenti di uno studente italofono a livello A2

Questa non è una semplice revisione: è una unità di consolidamento. L’obiettivo non è ricordare una regola isolata, ma saper combinare più strutture nella stessa produzione orale e scritta.

docs.title: 🧠 A2 Grammar Map
docs.text: Durante il livello A2 hai imparato a distinguere tra tempi e strutture con funzioni diverse.

docs.title: 1. Present Simple
docs.text:
Usalo per:

  • routine e abitudini
  • fatti generali
  • situazioni stabili

Examples

  • I usually get up at seven.
  • My sister works in a pharmacy.
  • We live in a small town.

docs.title: 2. Present Continuous
docs.text:
Usalo per:

  • azioni in corso nel momento in cui parli
  • situazioni temporanee
  • attività attuali ma non necessariamente in questo preciso istante

Examples

  • I’m doing my homework now.
  • She’s staying with friends this week.
  • We’re studying for our exams these days.

docs.title: 3. Past Simple
docs.text:
Usalo per:

  • azioni concluse nel passato
  • eventi con tempo definito o chiaramente finito

Examples

  • I visited Rome last year.
  • They arrived late yesterday.
  • We didn’t go out on Sunday.

docs.title: 4. Future: will / going to
docs.text:
Usa going to per:

  • piani e intenzioni già presenti
  • previsioni basate su ciò che vedi o sai già

Usa will per:

  • decisioni spontanee
  • promesse, offerte, predizioni-opinioni

Examples

  • I’m going to start a new course next month.
  • Look at those clouds. It’s going to rain.
  • I’ll help you with that.
  • I think you’ll enjoy the film.

docs.title: 5. Present Perfect for experiences
docs.text:
Usalo per parlare di esperienze di vita quando non dici quando è successo.

Examples

  • I’ve been to Portugal.
  • She has never tried sushi.
  • Have you ever travelled alone?

Quando invece aggiungi un tempo concluso, usa il Past Simple.

  • I went to Portugal in 2023.

docs.title: 6. Quantifiers
docs.text:
Hai lavorato su:

  • some / any
  • much / many
  • a lot of
  • countable / uncountable nouns

docs.title: 7. Comparatives and superlatives
docs.text:
Hai imparato a confrontare persone, oggetti, luoghi e situazioni.

  • bigger than
  • more interesting than
  • the best
  • the most useful

docs.title: 🔍 Review Strategy
docs.text:
Per parlare in modo più naturale, chiediti sempre:

  1. Sto parlando di un’abitudine, di adesso, del passato o del futuro?
  2. Sto raccontando un’esperienza generale o un evento con data/tempo finito?
  3. Sto usando il verbo nella forma giusta dopo ausiliari e modali?
  4. La frase è grammaticalmente corretta ma anche naturale?

Questo passaggio è fondamentale: a livello A2 non basta più produrre frasi isolate corrette; bisogna iniziare a selezionare la struttura in base al significato.

docs.title: 🧪 Diagnostic Check – Mixed Grammar
docs.text:
Completa con la forma corretta.

  1. My brother usually ___ at 8:00, but today he ___ late. (start / work)
  2. We ___ to Florence last month and we ___ a great time. (go / have)
  3. I’ve never ___ in a five-star hotel. (stay)
  4. Look at that car! It ___ too fast. (go)
  5. I think people ___ more online in the future. (shop)
  6. There isn’t ___ cheese left, but there are still ___ tomatoes. (much / a few / many)
  7. This exercise is ___ than the previous one, but Unit 15 was the ___. (easy / difficult)
  8. ___ you ever ___ a famous person? (meet)
  9. What ___ you ___ right now? (do)
  10. We ___ visit our cousins next weekend; we already bought the train tickets. (going to)

docs.title: 🔁 Controlled Transformation Drill
docs.text:
Trasforma le frasi senza cambiare il significato principale.

  1. I usually study in the evening. → (now)
  2. She went to London in April. → (experience)
  3. I have never eaten Indian food. → (question form)
  4. They plan to move next year. → (going to)
  5. I’ll carry that bag for you. → (function: offer)
  6. There are a lot of people in the shop. → (singular noun phrase impossible? explain mentally why not)
  7. This restaurant is more cheap than that one. → (correct the sentence)
  8. I didn’t went out yesterday. → (correct the sentence)

docs.title: Part A – Short answers
docs.text:
Rispondi in 1–2 frasi.

  • What do you usually do after work or school?
  • What are you doing differently this month?
  • Where did you go last weekend?
  • Have you ever visited another country?
  • What are you going to do next Saturday?

docs.title: Part B – Extended answers
docs.text:
Rispondi in 3–4 frasi.

  • Describe your typical weekday and explain what you like or dislike about it.
  • Talk about a recent weekend. Where did you go? Who were you with? What happened?
  • Describe an experience you have had that was interesting, surprising or difficult.
  • Talk about a plan you already have for the near future.

docs.title: Part C – Integrated speaking task
docs.text:
Parla per 2–3 minuti su questo tema:

Your life now, a memorable past event, and your plans for the near future.

Il tuo intervento dovrebbe includere:

  • presentazione personale
  • routine abituale
  • attività attuali o temporanee
  • un evento passato con dettagli
  • un’esperienza di vita
  • uno o due progetti futuri
  • almeno una comparazione

Example opening

My name is Elena and I live in Bologna. I usually work during the day and study English in the evening. These days I’m preparing for a job interview, so I’m practising more than usual. Last month I visited Naples with two friends, and we had a great time...

docs.title: 👂 Conversation Task
docs.text: Lavora in coppia o simula entrambe le parti.

docs.title: Situation
docs.text: Stai parlando con un’altra persona in una scuola di lingue durante la pausa.

docs.title: You must:
docs.text:
• introduce yourself
  • ask about daily life
  • ask one question about current activities
  • ask one question about past experiences
  • ask one question about future plans
  • react naturally to the answers

docs.title: Useful follow-up expressions
docs.text:
• Really?
  • That sounds interesting.
  • How often do you do that?
  • When did that happen?
  • Are you going to do it again?
  • Why do you like it?

Questa parte è importante perché molti studenti A2 sanno rispondere, ma non sanno portare avanti la conversazione.

docs.title: ✍️ Writing Task – Final A2 Profile
docs.text: Scrivi un testo di 120–150 parole intitolato:

docs.title: My life now and my plans for the future
docs.text:
Il testo deve includere:

  • informazioni personali essenziali
  • routine e abitudini
  • qualcosa che stai facendo in questo periodo
  • un evento passato con dettagli
  • almeno un’esperienza di vita
  • un progetto futuro già pianificato
  • un’opinione o una preferenza
  • almeno una comparazione

docs.title: Writing checklist
docs.text:
Controlla di aver usato:

  • almeno 1 Present Simple
  • almeno 1 Present Continuous
  • almeno 2 frasi al Past Simple
  • almeno 1 Present Perfect
  • almeno 1 future form
  • almeno 1 comparative or superlative
  • connettori base: and, but, because, so, then, after that

docs.title: 🛠️ Error Clinic – Italian Learner Focus
docs.text:
Correggi gli errori.

  1. I am work in a bank.
  2. I have went to London twice.
  3. Yesterday I didn’t saw him.
  4. I’m going to study and then I will to cook.
  5. There are much people in the room.
  6. This book is more easier than the other one.
  7. Have you ever been in Paris?
  8. I do shopping every Saturday.
  9. She is knowing the answer.
  10. I have been to Spain last summer.

docs.title: Commento didattico
docs.text:
Gli errori più frequenti in questa fase sono:

  • uso eccessivo di be davanti al verbo principale
  • doppia marcatura del passato: did + past form
  • confusione tra Present Perfect e Past Simple
  • comparativi doppi: more easier
  • uso innaturale di quantifiers e collocations

docs.title: 🔊 Pronunciation and Fluency Check
docs.text:
Leggi ad alta voce cercando ritmo e continuità:

  • I usually work from Monday to Friday.
  • I’m studying more than usual this month.
  • We visited some friends last weekend.
  • I’ve never tried Japanese food.
  • I’m going to travel in August.
  • I think it’ll be a good experience.

docs.title: Focus points
docs.text:
• worked /t/
  • visited /ɪd/
  • tried con dittongo finale chiaro
  • contrazione naturale: I’m, I’ve, it’ll
  • ritmo della frase, non parola per parola

docs.title: ✅ Final Performance Task
docs.text: Simula una conversazione reale di 3 minuti con una nuova conoscenza.

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text: Ti trovi a un evento internazionale o in una scuola di lingue. Devi parlare in inglese in modo naturale e semplice.

docs.title: Your mission
docs.text:
1. Present yourself.
  2. Ask at least 4 questions.
  3. Talk about your routine.
  4. Mention something you are doing these days.
  5. Describe one past event.
  6. Mention one life experience.
  7. Say what you are going to do soon.
  8. Compare two places, activities or options.

Se riesci a fare tutto questo con frasi abbastanza corrette e comprensibili, il tuo A2 è ben consolidato.

docs.title: 🎯 Self-Assessment
docs.text:
Metti ✔️ se sei capace di:

  • usare presente, passato e futuro in una stessa conversazione
  • fare e capire domande semplici ma naturali
