  • parlare di esperienze usando Have you ever...?
  • descrivere programmi con going to
  • confrontare due cose in modo corretto
  • scrivere un testo breve ma coeso
  • riconoscere e correggere alcuni errori tipici

docs.title: Interpreta il risultato
docs.text:
• 6–7 ✔️ → A2 solido, pronto per B1
  • 4–5 ✔️ → A2 quasi consolidato, utile una revisione mirata
  • 0–3 ✔️ → conviene ripassare alcune unità prima di proseguire

docs.title: 🚀 Bridge to B1
docs.text:
Nel livello B1 inizierai a fare un salto importante.

Non studierai solo nuove regole: imparerai a:

  • sostenere conversazioni più lunghe
  • esprimere opinioni e motivazioni
  • raccontare con più dettaglio
  • usare strutture come modali, condizionali e discorso più naturale
  • scrivere testi più organizzati

Il vero passaggio da A2 a B1 non è “sapere più grammatica”, ma usare la grammatica per dire qualcosa di più ricco e personale.

When you can connect present, past, future, experience, quantity and comparison in one conversation, you are ready to move beyond elementary English.

docs.title: 🟡 Livello B1 – Intermediate
docs.text:
Benvenuto nel livello B1.

Questo è il livello in cui smetti di usare l’inglese solo per “sopravvivere” e inizi a usarlo per comunicare davvero. Dopo A1 e A2, qui lavori su tempi verbali più sensibili al significato, opinioni, condizionali, modali, email, lessico più naturale e strutture che ti rendono più autonomo nel parlato e nello scritto.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo del livello
docs.text:
Alla fine del livello B1 sarai in grado di:

  • sostenere conversazioni reali su vita quotidiana, studio e lavoro
  • raccontare esperienze e parlare di cambiamenti nel tempo
  • distinguere con più precisione tra tempi verbali simili
  • esprimere opinioni, accordo e disaccordo in modo più naturale
  • scrivere email e messaggi chiari, appropriati e ben organizzati
  • usare un inglese più flessibile e meno dipendente dalla traduzione parola per parola

docs.title: 🧭 Due modalità di studio
docs.text: Questo corso offre due modalità selezionabili dal toggle in alto nel sito:

docs.title: Base
docs.text:
La modalità Base è pensata per chi vuole:

  • consolidare le strutture principali del livello B1 in modo ordinato
  • lavorare con spiegazioni chiare e progressive
  • rafforzare comprensione, grammatica e uso controllato
  • acquisire sicurezza prima di passare a un uso più ricco e sfumato della lingua

docs.title: Professional
docs.text:
La modalità Professional è pensata per chi vuole un percorso più ricco e strutturato, con:

  • spiegazioni più profonde e contrastive
  • uso reale delle strutture in contesto
  • lessico più naturale, collocazioni e register
  • errori tipici degli italiani
  • speaking drills, writing guidato, review integrate e self-check finali

👉 Puoi passare da una modalità all’altra in qualsiasi momento usando il selettore BASE / PRO nella barra superiore.

docs.title: 📚 Cosa imparerai in B1
docs.text:
Nel livello B1 lavorerai su:

  • present perfect e contrasto con past simple
  • forme del futuro e differenze d’uso
  • first conditional
  • linguaggio per esprimere opinioni e argomentare
  • lessico e strutture per lavoro e studio
  • modali come must, should e have to
  • email e messaggi in registro quotidiano e professionale
  • phrasal verbs ad alta frequenza
  • revisione integrata delle strutture più importanti del livello

docs.title: ⚠️ Cosa aspettarti a questo livello
docs.text:
A livello B1 è normale:

  • capire bene il senso generale ma avere ancora dubbi sulle sfumature
  • parlare con discreta continuità, ma con errori di accuratezza
  • confondere strutture corrette ma simili nel significato
  • sentire il bisogno di più lessico per risultare naturale
  • riuscire a comunicare bene, ma non sempre nel modo più preciso o idiomatico

È un passaggio importante: stai entrando in un inglese più autonomo, più utile e più vicino alla comunicazione reale.

docs.title: 💡 Come usare bene questo livello
docs.text:
Per ottenere risultati migliori:

  • confronta sempre strutture simili invece di studiarle isolate
  • osserva il significato e l’intenzione comunicativa, non solo la forma grammaticale
  • riusa attivamente il lessico nuovo in frasi personali
  • leggi e ripeti ad alta voce i dialoghi e i modelli scritti
  • usa la modalità Professional quando vuoi lavorare su naturalezza, precisione e produzione autonoma

docs.title: 📖 Unità del livello B1
docs.text:
1. Present Perfect (Real Use)
  2. Present Perfect vs Past Simple
  3. Future Forms (Will / Going to / Present Continuous)
  4. First Conditional (If...)
  5. Giving Opinions
  6. Work and Study
  7. Modals: Must, Should, Have to
  8. Emails and Messages
  9. Phrasal Verbs
  10. B1 Review

docs.title: ✅ Traguardo finale B1
docs.text:
Alla fine di questo blocco dovresti riuscire a:

  • partecipare a conversazioni reali con più continuità e autonomia
  • spiegare esperienze, progetti, opinioni e obblighi in modo chiaro
  • scrivere messaggi ed email efficaci in contesti quotidiani e semi-professionali
  • scegliere strutture più appropriate in base al significato, non solo alla regola
  • sostenere un inglese funzionale e credibile in studio, lavoro e vita quotidiana

👉 Inizia con Unità 21 oppure usa il toggle in alto per scegliere la modalità di studio che preferisci.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità saprai:

  • usare il Present Perfect in modo semplice ma corretto
  • collegare un’azione passata al presente
  • parlare di esperienze, risultati e cambiamenti
  • distinguere tra Present Perfect e Past Simple nei casi più comuni

docs.title: Struttura
docs.text:
👉 subject + have / has + past participle

  • I have worked
  • She has studied
  • They have travelled

Forme brevi molto comuni:

  • I’ve finished
  • She’s gone out
  • We’ve seen it

docs.title: 1. Esperienze di vita
docs.text:
Usa il Present Perfect quando parli di un’esperienza e non dici quando.

  • I have been to Spain.
  • She has eaten sushi.
  • We have seen that film.

docs.title: 2. Risultati nel presente
docs.text:
L’azione è passata, ma il risultato è importante adesso.

  • I have lost my keys.
  • He has broken his glasses.
  • We have finished our homework.

docs.title: 3. Cambiamenti nel tempo
docs.text:
• My English has improved.
  • The city has changed a lot.
  • She has become more confident.

docs.title: ⚖️ Present Perfect vs Past Simple
docs.text:
Present Perfect Past Simple esperienza o risultato azione finita nel passato non dici quando dici quando legame con il presente tempo passato concluso

Examples

  • I have been to Rome. ✅
  • I went to Rome last year. ✅
  • I have been to Rome last year. ❌

docs.title: Past participles frequenti
docs.text:
• be → been
  • go → gone
  • do → done
  • eat → eaten
  • see → seen
  • write → written
  • make → made
  • take → taken
  • speak → spoken
  • buy → bought

docs.title: Signal words
docs.text:
• already = già
  • just = appena
  • yet = ancora / già (in domande e negative)
  • ever = mai
  • never = mai

Examples

  • I have already finished.
  • She has just arrived.
  • We haven’t started yet.
  • Have you ever tried Indian food?
  • I have never flown business class.

docs.title: 🎧 Reading / Dialogue
docs.text:
A: Have you finished your report yet? B: Yes, I’ve just sent it.

A: Have you ever worked with international clients? B: Yes, I have. I’ve worked with clients from Germany and Spain.

A: Really? When did you start? B: I started two years ago.

docs.title: 🗣️ Speaking
docs.text:
Ripeti:

  • I’ve just finished.
  • We haven’t decided yet.
  • She has already called.
  • Have you ever tried it?

Ora completa con idee tue:

  • I have already ...
  • I have just ...
  • I haven’t ... yet.
  • I have never ...

Fai domande:

  • Have you ever travelled alone?
  • Have you finished your work yet?
  • Have you ever met a famous person?

docs.title: ✍️ Writing
docs.text:
Scrivi 8 frasi usando il Present Perfect.

Usa almeno queste parole:

  • already
  • just
  • yet
  • ever
  • never

Esempio:

I’ve already done my homework. I haven’t called my friend yet. Have you ever visited London?

docs.title: 🔊 Pronunciation
docs.text:
Nel parlato naturale spesso si usano le contrazioni:

  • I have → I’ve
  • you have → you’ve
  • she has → she’s
  • we have → we’ve

Attenzione anche a:

  • finished → /ˈfɪnɪʃt/
  • worked → /wɜːrkt/
  • lived → /lɪvd/

docs.title: ⚠️ Errori comuni (italiani)
docs.text:
❌ I have went to London. ✅ I have gone to London.

❌ I have done it yesterday. ✅ I did it yesterday.

❌ I am finished. ✅ I have finished.

❌ Have you ever been in New York? ✅ Have you ever been to New York?

docs.title: ✅ Review & Practice
docs.text:
Completa con il participio corretto:

  1. I have ___ (eat) lunch.
  2. She has ___ (go) home.
  3. We have ___ (see) that film.
  4. He hasn’t ___ (finish) yet.
  5. They have ___ (write) the email.

Scegli il tempo corretto:

  1. I have seen / saw him yesterday.
  2. Have you ever been / did you go to Ireland?
  3. She has just arrived / just arrived five minutes ago.
  4. We didn’t finish / haven’t finished yet.

docs.title: 🧠 Think & Speak
docs.text:
Parla di:

  • 3 cose che hai già fatto oggi
  • 2 cose che non hai ancora fatto
  • 2 esperienze che hai fatto nella tua vita

docs.title: 🚀 Mini task
docs.text:
Parla per 1–2 minuti:

👉 What have you done this week?

Prova a usare:

  • already
  • just
  • yet
  • ever / never

English becomes more natural when you connect the past to the present.

docs.title: 🎯 Lesson Goals
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • usare il Present Perfect in modo naturale per parlare di esperienze, risultati, cambiamenti e periodi non conclusi
  • distinguere con sicurezza tra Present Perfect e Past Simple in base al significato, non solo alla regola meccanica
  • usare in modo corretto e fluente already, just, yet, ever, never, for, since
  • sostenere una conversazione B1 con follow-up naturali, domande di approfondimento e riferimenti temporali coerenti
  • evitare gli errori più comuni degli italofoni, soprattutto con participi irregolari, traduzione letterale e scelta sbagliata del tempo verbale

Questa è una delle unità centrali del passaggio da A2 a B1. Il punto non è soltanto “sapere la regola”, ma capire quando il presente è ancora coinvolto e quando invece l’evento appartiene a un passato chiuso.

docs.title: 🧠 Core concept: past event, present relevance
docs.text:
Il Present Perfect collega un fatto passato a una situazione presente. In altre parole, l’azione è avvenuta prima di adesso, ma è ancora rilevante adesso.

👉 Formula di base:

subject + have / has + past participle

  • I have finished the report.
  • She has changed jobs.
  • We have never tried that app.

Nel parlato reale userai spesso le contrazioni:

  • I’ve finished.
  • She’s changed jobs.
  • We’ve never tried it.

docs.title: 1. Main uses of the Present Perfect
A. Experiences
docs.text:
Parli di cose che hai fatto nella vita senza specificare un momento preciso.

  • I’ve been to Japan twice.
  • Have you ever worked abroad?
  • She has never driven on the left.

Qui il focus non è quando, ma se l’esperienza esiste nel tuo bagaglio personale.

docs.title: B. Results visible now
docs.text:
L’azione è passata, ma il risultato è presente.

  • I’ve lost my keys. (so I can’t open the door now)
  • We’ve finished the presentation. (it is ready now)
  • He’s broken his phone. (it doesn’t work now)

docs.title: C. Change over time
docs.text:
Parli di evoluzione, sviluppo o trasformazione.

  • My pronunciation has improved a lot.
  • The company has grown بسرعة.
  • This area has become more expensive.

docs.title: D. Unfinished time periods
docs.text:
Il periodo di riferimento non è concluso.

  • I’ve had three meetings today.
  • She hasn’t called me this week.
  • We’ve sold a lot this month.

👉 today / this week / this month / this year possono richiedere il Present Perfect se il periodo è ancora in corso.

docs.title: E. States or actions continuing until now
docs.text:
Con for e since puoi parlare di situazioni iniziate nel passato e ancora vere adesso.

  • I’ve lived here for six years.
  • She has worked here since 2021.
  • We’ve known each other for a long time.

docs.title: 2. Present Perfect vs Past Simple
docs.text: Questa è la distinzione più importante di tutta l’unità.

docs.title: Usa il Present Perfect quando:
docs.text:
• il tempo non è specificato
  • il periodo non è finito
  • il risultato è importante adesso
  • l’esperienza conta più del momento preciso

docs.title: Usa il Past Simple quando:
docs.text:
• il tempo è definito o implicito come concluso
  • parli di un evento “storico” della tua narrazione
  • vuoi dire quando è successo qualcosa

docs.title: Compare
docs.text:
• I’ve seen that film. (esperienza generale)

  • I saw that film last weekend. (momento definito)

  • She has changed a lot. (cambiamento fino a oggi)

  • She changed schools in 2022. (evento chiuso)

  • We’ve had two meetings today. (oggi non è finito)

  • We had two meetings yesterday. (ieri è finito)

docs.title: Quick test
docs.text:
Chiediti:

  1. Sto dicendo quando?
  2. Il periodo è finito?
  3. Mi interessa il risultato presente?
  4. Sto parlando dell’esperienza in generale?

Se la risposta 1 è sì, di solito vai verso Past Simple. Se prevalgono 2, 3 o 4, spesso serve il Present Perfect.

docs.title: already
docs.text:
Indica che qualcosa è successo prima del previsto o prima di quanto necessario.

  • I’ve already sent the email.
  • She has already eaten.

docs.title: just
docs.text:
Indica un’azione appena conclusa.

  • We’ve just arrived.
  • I’ve just spoken to Marta.

docs.title: yet
docs.text:
Si usa soprattutto in negative e domande.

  • I haven’t finished yet.
  • Have you booked the tickets yet?

docs.title: ever / never
docs.text:
Parlano di esperienza di vita.

  • Have you ever tried scuba diving?
  • I’ve never been to Iceland.

docs.title: for / since
docs.text:
• for = durata

  • since = punto iniziale

  • I’ve studied English for ten years.

  • I’ve studied English since primary school.

docs.title: 4. Been vs Gone
docs.text: Questa differenza crea molti errori.

docs.title: been
docs.text:
La persona è andata e poi è tornata.

  • I’ve been to Berlin three times.
  • She has been to that restaurant before.

docs.title: gone
docs.text:
La persona è andata ed è ancora lì, oppure non è qui nel momento in cui parli.

  • Tom has gone to the bank. (he isn’t here now)
  • My parents have gone on holiday.

docs.title: 5. Frequent past participles you really need
Irregular verbs
docs.text:
• be → been
  • begin → begun
  • break → broken
  • do → done
  • eat → eaten
  • go → gone
  • know → known
  • see → seen
  • speak → spoken
  • take → taken
  • write → written
  • drive → driven
  • choose → chosen
  • give → given
  • make → made

docs.title: High-frequency chunks
docs.text:
• I’ve already finished.
  • I haven’t decided yet.
  • Have you ever heard of it?
  • We’ve just arrived.
  • She’s worked here since May.

docs.title: 🎧 Reading: workplace conversation
docs.text: Elena: Have you finished the client presentation yet? Marco: Yes, I’ve just sent the final version to the team. Elena: Great. Have you included the new sales figures? Marco: Yes, I have. We’ve had excellent results this quarter. Elena: Really? Marco: Yes. We’ve signed three new contracts this month. Elena: That’s impressive. Have you ever worked on a project like this before? Marco: Not exactly. I worked on a similar campaign last year, but this one has been much more international.

docs.title: Comprehension
docs.text:
1. Has Marco finished the presentation?
  2. Why is this month used with the Present Perfect?
  3. Which sentence refers to a finished past time?
  4. Why does Marco say I worked on a similar campaign last year and not I’ve worked?

docs.title: A. Personal experience
docs.text:
Rispondi in modo completo:

  • Have you ever done something difficult in English?
  • Have you ever travelled alone?
  • Have you ever changed your routine because of work or study?
  • Have you ever learned a skill online?

docs.title: B. Result now
docs.text:
Completa e spiega la conseguenza presente:

  • I’ve lost ... so now ...
  • We’ve finished ... so now ...
  • She’s changed ... so now ...

docs.title: C. for / since
docs.text:
Parla di te:

  • I’ve lived in ... for ...
  • I’ve known ... since ...
  • I’ve studied English for ...
  • I haven’t ... since ...

docs.title: D. Follow-up questions
docs.text:
Dopo una risposta con il Present Perfect, fai una domanda di approfondimento al Past Simple.

Example:

  • A: Have you ever worked abroad?
  • B: Yes, I have.
  • A: Where did you work?
  • A: How long did you stay there?

Questa combinazione è una firma del parlato naturale a livello B1.

docs.title: ✍️ Writing Task
docs.text:
Scrivi un testo di 140–180 parole sul tema:

How your life has changed in the last few years

Nel testo prova a usare:

  • almeno 3 Present Perfect per cambiamenti o risultati
  • almeno 2 esempi con for / since
  • almeno 1 Past Simple per un evento con tempo definito
  • almeno 2 segnali tra already, just, yet, ever, never

docs.title: Writing frame
docs.text:
Puoi organizzare il testo così:

  1. Introduzione generale
  2. Cambiamenti nello studio, lavoro o routine
  3. Nuove esperienze fatte o non ancora fatte
  4. Conclusione personale

docs.title: 🔊 Pronunciation and connected speech
docs.text:
Nel parlato naturale, have e has sono spesso deboli e poco marcati.

  • I’ve finished it.
  • She’s just called.
  • We’ve already seen it.
  • They’ve never tried it.

Allenati a pronunciare i blocchi, non le parole isolate.

docs.title: Drill
docs.text:
Ripeti:

  • I’ve just sent it.
  • We’ve already booked it.
  • She hasn’t answered yet.
  • Have you ever seen it?
  • I’ve lived here for years.

docs.title: ⚠️ Typical Italian mistakes
docs.text:
❌ I have went there. ✅ I have gone there.

❌ I have seen him yesterday. ✅ I saw him yesterday.

❌ I am here since 2020. ✅ I have been here since 2020.

❌ I know her since high school. ✅ I have known her since high school.

❌ Have you ever been in London? ✅ Have you ever been to London?

❌ I have finished just now yesterday. ✅ I’ve just finished.

docs.title: Why these happen
docs.text:
Molti di questi errori nascono da:

  • traduzione letterale dall’italiano
  • confusione tra participio e past simple
  • mancata distinzione tra tempo definito e rilevanza presente
  • uso scorretto di for / since

docs.title: 🔄 Transformation Drill
docs.text:
Trasforma correttamente.

  1. I finished my homework. → risultato presente
  2. She started working here in 2021. → situazione ancora vera
  3. We didn’t decide. → con yet
  4. He went to Paris three times in his life. → esperienza
  5. I know Anna from 2018. → con since

docs.title: Possible answers
docs.text:
1. I’ve finished my homework.
  2. She has worked here since 2021.
  3. We haven’t decided yet.
  4. He has been to Paris three times.
  5. I’ve known Anna since 2018.

docs.title: ✅ Mixed Practice
docs.text:
Scegli la forma corretta.

  1. I’ve lost / I lost my phone, so I can’t call her now.
  2. Did you ever try / Have you ever tried Korean food?
  3. We’ve had / We had two meetings yesterday.
  4. She has worked / worked here since 2022.
  5. I’ve already sent / already sent the file.
  6. Tom has been / has gone to the doctor, so he isn’t here.
  7. I haven’t finished / didn’t finish yet.
  8. They’ve lived / lived here for ten years.

docs.title: 🧠 Self-check
docs.text:
Riesci a:

  • spiegare la differenza tra I’ve been to Rome e I went to Rome last year?
  • usare bene already / just / yet senza tradurre parola per parola?
  • costruire frasi corrette con for / since?
  • passare dal Present Perfect al Past Simple in una conversazione naturale?
  • evitare errori come have went, I am here since, have seen yesterday?

Se la risposta è “quasi sempre sì”, sei sulla strada giusta per il livello B1.

docs.title: 🚀 Final Speaking Task
docs.text:
Parla per 2–3 minuti su uno di questi temi:

  1. What has changed in your life recently?
  2. What have you already done this week, and what haven’t you done yet?
  3. What experiences have helped you grow as a person?

docs.title: Target language to include
docs.text:
• Present Perfect for experiences
  • Present Perfect for results
  • one example with for
  • one example with since
  • one Past Simple follow-up with a specific time reference

At B1 level, grammar becomes useful when you choose it for meaning, not just because you remember the rule.

docs.title: Unità 22 – Present Perfect vs Past Simple
Unità 22 – Present Perfect vs Past Simple
🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità saprai:

  • distinguere tra Present Perfect e Past Simple
  • capire se il focus è su quando è successa un’azione o sul suo legame con il presente
  • usare correttamente parole chiave come already, just, yet, ever, never, yesterday, last week
  • evitare gli errori più comuni nella scelta del tempo verbale

docs.title: Usa il Present Perfect quando:
docs.text:
• non dici quando è successo qualcosa
  • parli di un’esperienza di vita
  • il risultato è importante adesso
  • il periodo di tempo non è finito

Examples

  • I have seen that film.
  • She has just arrived.
  • We have finished the project.
  • I have had three meetings today.

docs.title: Usa il Past Simple quando:
docs.text:
• dici quando è successo qualcosa
  • parli di un momento passato finito
  • racconti un evento concluso

Examples

  • I saw that film last weekend.
  • She arrived ten minutes ago.
  • We finished the project yesterday.
  • I had three meetings yesterday.

docs.title: ⚖️ Confronto diretto
docs.text: Present Perfect Past Simple I have been to London. I went to London in 2022. She has finished her work. She finished her work at 6. We have seen this series. We saw this series last month. I have spoken to him today. I spoke to him yesterday.

docs.title: Present Perfect
docs.text:
• already
  • just
  • yet
  • ever
  • never
  • today
  • this week
  • this month

docs.title: Past Simple
docs.text:
• yesterday
  • last week
  • last month
  • in 2021
  • two days ago
  • when I was a child

docs.title: 🎧 Dialogue
docs.text:
A: Have you finished the report yet? B: Yes, I have. I’ve just sent it.

A: Great. When did you send it? B: I sent it five minutes ago.

A: Have you ever written a report in English before? B: Yes, I have, but I wrote my first one only last year.

docs.title: 🗣️ Speaking
docs.text:
Completa le frasi:

  • I have already ...
  • I haven’t ... yet.
  • I saw ... yesterday.
  • I went to ... last ...
  • Have you ever ...?

Fai domande a un compagno:

  • Have you ever travelled abroad alone?
  • Have you finished your homework yet?
  • When did you last watch a film in English?
  • Did you study English yesterday?

docs.title: ✍️ Writing
docs.text:
Scrivi 8 frasi:

  • 4 con il Present Perfect
  • 4 con il Past Simple

Usa almeno:

  • one sentence with already
  • one with yet
  • one with ever
  • one with yesterday
  • one with last week

Esempio:

I have already done my homework. I haven’t called my friend yet. I saw my teacher yesterday. We visited Rome last week.

docs.title: 🔊 Pronunciation
docs.text:
Fai attenzione alla differenza tra:

  • I’ve finished
  • I finished

Nel parlato veloce, I’ve è molto breve. Allenati a sentire e produrre la differenza.

docs.title: ⚠️ Errori comuni (italiani)
docs.text:
❌ I have done it yesterday. ✅ I did it yesterday.

❌ I have seen him last week. ✅ I saw him last week.

❌ Have you ever went there? ✅ Have you ever been there?

❌ I went to London many times. ✅ I have been to London many times.

docs.title: ✅ Review & Practice
docs.text:
Scegli la forma corretta:

  1. I have seen / saw that film yesterday.
  2. She has just arrived / just arrived.
  3. We finished / have finished the meeting ten minutes ago.
  4. Have you ever been / Did you ever go to Scotland?
  5. I have had / had two coffees today.
  6. He went / has gone to Paris in 2023.

docs.title: 🔄 Trasforma
docs.text:
Trasforma usando il tempo corretto:

  1. I saw that film. → I have ______ that film.
  2. She finished the task. → She has ______ the task.
  3. We went to Spain last year. → We have ______ to Spain.

docs.title: 🧠 Think & Speak
docs.text:
Parla per 1–2 minuti di:

  • 3 esperienze che hai fatto nella vita
  • 3 azioni con un tempo preciso nel passato
  • una cosa che hai già fatto oggi
  • una cosa che non hai ancora fatto

The best way to choose the tense is to ask: When? or Relevant now?

docs.title: Unità 22 – Present Perfect vs Past Simple
🎯 Lesson Goals
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • distinguere con sicurezza tra Present Perfect e Past Simple in base al significato comunicativo
  • scegliere il tempo corretto in conversazioni reali, email, racconti personali e contesti professionali semplici
  • usare in modo accurato already, just, yet, ever, never, for, since, today, this week, yesterday, last year
  • passare in modo naturale da una domanda generale al Present Perfect a un follow-up specifico al Past Simple
  • evitare gli errori più tipici degli italofoni, specialmente quelli dovuti alla traduzione letterale del passato prossimo italiano

Questa unità è decisiva a livello B1, perché qui non basta conoscere due tempi: bisogna capire che cosa vuoi comunicare.

docs.title: 🧠 The real question: finished past or present connection?
docs.text: La differenza non è soltanto grammaticale. È soprattutto una differenza di prospettiva.

docs.title: Usa il Present Perfect quando il passato è collegato al presente
docs.text:
Il fatto è accaduto prima di adesso, ma conta ancora adesso.

  • I’ve lost my wallet. (so I can’t pay now)
  • We’ve finished the presentation. (it is ready now)
  • She has changed jobs. (this is her current situation)
  • I’ve been to Dublin twice. (life experience)

docs.title: Usa il Past Simple quando il fatto appartiene a un passato chiuso
docs.text:
Il tempo è definito, concluso o narrativamente chiuso.

  • I lost my wallet yesterday.
  • We finished the presentation at 6 p.m.
  • She changed jobs in January.
  • I went to Dublin in 2022.

👉 In breve:

  • Present Perfect = past + present relevance
  • Past Simple = completed past

docs.title: A. Experience vs specific occasion
docs.text:
• I’ve been to Berlin. (experience in life)
  • I went to Berlin last summer. (specific trip)

docs.title: B. Result now vs finished action then
docs.text:
• She has broken her glasses. (they are broken now)
  • She broke her glasses last week. (past event)

docs.title: C. Unfinished time vs finished time
docs.text:
• I’ve had three calls today. (today is not finished)

  • I had three calls yesterday. (yesterday is finished)

  • We’ve made good progress this month.

  • We made good progress last month.

docs.title: D. General question vs detail question
docs.text:
• Have you ever worked with foreign clients?
  • Yes, I have.
  • When did you start?
  • I started in 2021.

Questo passaggio da general experience a specific past detail è una struttura chiave del parlato naturale.

docs.title: Usually with Present Perfect
docs.text:
• already
  • just
  • yet
  • ever
  • never
  • so far
  • recently
  • today
  • this week
  • this month
  • this year
  • for
  • since

docs.title: Usually with Past Simple
docs.text:
• yesterday
  • last night
  • last week
  • last month
  • in 2019
  • three days ago
  • when I was at university
  • on Monday

docs.title: Watch out: today / this week / this month
docs.text:
Questi marcatori possono richiedere il Present Perfect perché il periodo è ancora aperto.

  • I’ve had a busy day today.
  • She hasn’t replied this week.

Ma se il contesto rende il periodo chiuso, puoi trovare anche il Past Simple in un racconto narrativo.

docs.title: 3. Italian interference: why this is difficult
docs.text:
In italiano diciamo spesso:

  • Ho visto quel film ieri.
  • Ho finito due ore fa.
  • Hai mai visitato Londra?

In inglese, però, il sistema è diverso.

docs.title: English choices
docs.text:
• I saw that film yesterday.
  • I finished two hours ago.
  • Have you ever visited London?

L’errore nasce perché il passato prossimo italiano copre usi che in inglese si dividono tra Present Perfect e Past Simple.

docs.title: 4. for / since and the idea of duration
docs.text:
Quando una situazione è iniziata nel passato e continua ancora adesso, spesso userai il Present Perfect.

  • I’ve worked here for six years.
  • She has lived in Milan since 2020.
  • We’ve known each other for a long time.

docs.title: Difference
docs.text:
• for = duration
  • since = starting point

Non dire:

❌ I live here since 2020. ✅ I’ve lived here since 2020.

docs.title: 5. Dialogue in context
docs.text:
Sara: Have you sent the client email yet? Luca: Yes, I’ve just sent it.

Sara: Great. Did you attach the updated price list? Luca: Yes, I did. I added it a few minutes ago.

Sara: Perfect. Have you ever dealt with this client before? Luca: Yes, I have. I worked with them last year on a smaller project.

Sara: And how has the collaboration been so far? Luca: It has been very positive. They’ve already approved two of our proposals.

docs.title: Comprehension
docs.text:
1. Which sentence shows a result relevant now?
  2. Which sentence refers to a specific finished moment?
  3. Why is so far compatible with the Present Perfect?
  4. Why does Luca say I worked with them last year?

docs.title: Present Perfect patterns
docs.text:
• I’ve already finished ...
  • I haven’t ... yet.
  • Have you ever ...?
  • We’ve just received ...
  • She has never tried ...
  • I’ve worked here for ...
  • He has lived here since ...

docs.title: Past Simple patterns
docs.text:
• I did it yesterday.
  • We met in 2022.
  • She called me an hour ago.
  • They moved house last month.
  • I started learning English when I was ...

docs.title: A. Life experience + follow-up
docs.text:
Ask and answer:

  • Have you ever taken an important exam in English?
  • Have you ever had a difficult interview?
  • Have you ever travelled for work or study?
  • Have you ever changed your routine because of a project or deadline?

After each yes answer, ask:

  • When did it happen?
  • Where did it happen?
  • Who were you with?
  • How did it go?

docs.title: B. Present result vs past event
docs.text:
Complete both versions:

  • I’ve lost ... / I lost ...
  • We’ve finished ... / We finished ...
  • She’s changed ... / She changed ...

Explain the difference in meaning.

docs.title: C. Today vs yesterday
docs.text:
Speak for one minute comparing:

  • what you’ve done today
  • what you did yesterday

docs.title: ✍️ Writing Task
docs.text:
Scrivi un testo di 160–190 parole sul tema:

A recent experience and what it means for you now

Nel testo usa:

  • almeno 4 Present Perfect
  • almeno 4 Past Simple
  • almeno 2 esempi con already / just / yet / ever / never / so far
  • almeno 1 esempio con for o since

docs.title: Suggested structure
docs.text:
1. Introduci l’esperienza in generale
  2. Spiega che cosa è successo concretamente e quando
  3. Descrivi il risultato o l’effetto nel presente
  4. Concludi con una riflessione personale

docs.title: 🔊 Pronunciation and rhythm
docs.text:
Confronta il ritmo di queste coppie:

  • I’ve finished it. / I finished it yesterday.
  • She’s called me. / She called me this morning.
  • We’ve seen it before. / We saw it last weekend.

Nel parlato reale, le contrazioni rendono il Present Perfect più rapido e compatto.

docs.title: Drill
docs.text:
Ripeti a voce alta:

  • I’ve just sent it.
  • We haven’t decided yet.
  • Have you ever worked abroad?
  • I saw him two days ago.
  • She’s already done it.

docs.title: Error 1
docs.text: ❌ I have seen him yesterday. ✅ I saw him yesterday.

docs.title: Error 2
docs.text: ❌ I have finished two hours ago. ✅ I finished two hours ago.

docs.title: Error 3
docs.text: ❌ Did you ever go to New York? ✅ Have you ever been to New York?

docs.title: Error 4
docs.text: ❌ I am here since 2021. ✅ I’ve been here since 2021.

docs.title: Error 5
docs.text: ❌ I went to London many times. ✅ I’ve been to London many times.

docs.title: Error 6
docs.text: ❌ Have you finished the report yesterday? ✅ Did you finish the report yesterday?

docs.title: ✅ Review & Practice
docs.text:
Choose the correct option.

  1. I have met / met her last year.
  2. We have just received / just received the documents.
  3. Did you ever try / Have you ever tried Japanese food?
  4. She has worked / worked here since 2022.
  5. I have had / had two meetings today.
  6. He has called / called me an hour ago.
  7. They have moved / moved to Rome in 2021.
  8. I haven’t finished / didn’t finish the task yet.

docs.title: Correct the mistakes
docs.text:
1. I have done it yesterday.
  2. She has arrived ten minutes ago.
  3. Have you ever went to Ireland?
  4. I know him since primary school.
  5. We saw already that film.

docs.title: 🧠 Final Speaking Challenge
docs.text:
Parla per 2–3 minuti su uno di questi temi:

  • an experience that has changed the way you think
  • something you’ve already achieved this year
  • a place you’ve visited more than once and one specific trip there

Try to include:

  • one general life experience
  • one specific past event with a clear time reference
  • one present result
  • one example with for or since

At B1 level, accuracy is important, but meaning is more important: choose the tense that expresses your perspective clearly.

docs.title: Unità 23 – Future Forms (Will / Going to / Present Continuous)
Unità 23 – Future Forms (Will / Going to / Present Continuous)
🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità saprai:

  • usare will, going to e Present Continuous per parlare del futuro
  • scegliere la forma corretta in base al contesto
  • esprimere piani, decisioni spontanee e appuntamenti organizzati
  • evitare gli errori più comuni legati alle forme future

docs.title: 🧠 Grammar in context
docs.text:
In inglese non esiste una sola forma del futuro. Le tre strutture più utili a questo livello sono:

  • will
  • be going to
  • Present Continuous con valore di futuro

La scelta dipende dal significato.

docs.title: 1. Will
docs.text:
Usa will per:

  • decisioni prese nel momento in cui parli
  • offerte e promesse
  • previsioni personali

docs.title: Struttura
docs.text:
subject + will + base form

  • I will call you later.
  • We will help you.
  • She will be here soon.

docs.title: Forma negativa
docs.text:
• I will not call.
  • She won’t come.

docs.title: Domanda
docs.text:
• Will you help me?
  • Will they arrive on time?

docs.title: 2. Going to
docs.text:
Usa be going to per:

  • piani o intenzioni già decise
  • previsioni basate su evidenze presenti

docs.title: Struttura
docs.text:
subject + be + going to + base form

  • I am going to study tonight.
  • They are going to move house.
  • It is going to rain.

docs.title: Forma negativa
docs.text:
• I am not going to stay.
  • We aren’t going to buy it.

docs.title: Domanda
docs.text:
• Are you going to travel this summer?
  • Is she going to apply for the job?

docs.title: 3. Present Continuous for future
docs.text: Usa il Present Continuous per appuntamenti e programmi già organizzati.

docs.title: Struttura
docs.text:
subject + be + verb-ing

  • I am meeting Anna tomorrow.
  • We are flying to Madrid on Friday.
  • He is seeing the dentist this afternoon.

Spesso trovi un riferimento di tempo:

  • tomorrow
  • tonight
  • next week
  • on Monday
  • this evening

docs.title: ⚖️ Differenza chiave
docs.text: Forma Uso principale will decisione spontanea, promessa, previsione personale going to piano già deciso, previsione con evidenza Present Continuous appuntamento o programma organizzato

docs.title: Decision now → will
docs.text: A: The phone is ringing. B: I’ll answer it.

docs.title: Prior plan → going to
docs.text: I’m going to start a new course next month.

docs.title: Arrangement → Present Continuous
docs.text: We’re meeting the client at 10 tomorrow.

docs.title: Prediction with evidence → going to
docs.text: Look at those clouds. It’s going to rain.

docs.title: Personal prediction → will
docs.text: I think our team will win.

docs.title: 🎧 Reading / Dialogue
docs.text:
A: What are you doing this weekend? B: I’m visiting my cousins on Saturday.

A: Nice. Are you going to stay there all weekend? B: No, just one night.

A: Oh, I forgot to book the restaurant. B: Don’t worry, I’ll do it now.

A: Great. Do you think it will be busy? B: Yes, I think it will.

docs.title: 🗣️ Speaking
docs.text:
Ripeti:

  • I’ll call you later.
  • I’m going to study tonight.
  • We’re meeting at six.
  • It’s going to rain.

Ora completa con idee tue:

  • I’ll ...
  • I’m going to ...
  • I’m ... tomorrow.
  • I think ... will ...

docs.title: ✍️ Writing
docs.text:
Scrivi 9 frasi:

  • 3 con will
  • 3 con going to
  • 3 con Present Continuous per il futuro

Usa almeno tre espressioni di tempo diverse.

docs.title: 🔊 Pronunciation
docs.text:
Nel parlato naturale si usano spesso le contrazioni:

  • I will → I’ll
  • we will → we’ll
  • I am going to → I’m going to
  • we are meeting → we’re meeting

Nel parlato informale potresti sentire anche gonna, ma per ora è meglio saperla riconoscere prima di usarla attivamente.

docs.title: ⚠️ Errori comuni (italiani)
docs.text:
❌ I will to call you. ✅ I will call you.

❌ I going to travel in August. ✅ I am going to travel in August.

❌ I am go tomorrow. ✅ I am going tomorrow.

❌ I will meeting my friend later. ✅ I am meeting my friend later.

❌ It will rain look at the sky. ✅ It’s going to rain. Look at the sky.

docs.title: ✅ Review & Practice
docs.text:
Scegli la forma corretta:

  1. I think she will / is going to like the gift.
  2. We will / are going to move to a new flat next month. (già deciso)
  3. I will / am meeting Laura at 5 p.m. (appuntamento fissato)
  4. The bag looks heavy. I will / am going to help you.
  5. Look at the traffic. We will / are going to be late.

Completa:

  1. Don’t worry. I ___ help you.
  2. We are ___ to start a new project.
  3. She is ___ the manager tomorrow morning.
  4. I think they ___ arrive late.

docs.title: 🧠 Think & Speak
docs.text:
Parla di:

  • tre cose che farai questa settimana
  • un piano già deciso
  • un appuntamento già organizzato
  • una decisione che puoi prendere adesso

docs.title: 🚀 Mini task
docs.text:
Parla per 2 minuti dei tuoi piani per i prossimi giorni.

Cerca di usare:

  • will
  • going to
  • Present Continuous

Real English uses different future forms for different meanings.

docs.title: Unità 23 – Future Forms (Will / Going to / Present Continuous)
🎯 Lesson Goals
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • scegliere tra will, be going to e Present Continuous in base al significato comunicativo, non solo alla regola formale
  • parlare di decisioni spontanee, intenzioni già formulate, appuntamenti organizzati e previsioni con maggiore precisione
  • riconoscere le sfumature tra futuro “pensato”, futuro “organizzato” e futuro “deciso sul momento”
  • usare strutture molto frequenti nel parlato reale, come I’ll, I’m going to, I’m meeting..., I think... will..., It’s going to...
  • evitare gli errori tipici degli italofoni, specialmente la tendenza a usare una sola forma per tutti i contesti

Questa unità è fondamentale a livello B1 perché il parlante deve iniziare a usare il futuro in modo flessibile e naturale. Il punto non è memorizzare tre etichette, ma capire che ogni forma trasmette una prospettiva diversa.

docs.title: 🧠 Core idea: future meaning depends on viewpoint
docs.text:
In inglese il futuro non si esprime con un solo “tempo verbale”. Si sceglie una forma in base a come il parlante vede l’evento.

Chiediti sempre:

  1. È una decisione che sto prendendo adesso?
  2. È un piano già deciso?
  3. È un appuntamento o un programma già organizzato?
  4. È una previsione basata sulla mia opinione o su un’evidenza visibile?

Le tre forme di questa unità coprono proprio questi significati.

docs.title: 1. Will: decision now, promise, offer, prediction
Formula
docs.text:
subject + will + base form

  • I will call you later.
  • We’ll send the file this afternoon.
  • She won’t agree.
  • Will they come?

docs.title: A. Spontaneous decisions
docs.text:
La decisione nasce nel momento in cui parli.

  • The phone is ringing. I’ll get it.
  • You look tired. I’ll make some coffee.
  • We don’t have any bread. I’ll go and buy some.

docs.title: B. Offers and promises
docs.text:
• I’ll help you with that report.
  • I won’t tell anyone.
  • I’ll call you as soon as I arrive.

docs.title: C. Personal predictions
docs.text:
Parli di ciò che pensi o immagini.

  • I think the meeting will be useful.
  • I’m sure she’ll enjoy the course.
  • They probably won’t finish on time.

docs.title: Common discourse markers
docs.text:
• I think...
  • I’m sure...
  • probably
  • maybe
  • I promise...
  • don’t worry, I’ll...

docs.title: 2. Be going to: prior intention and visible evidence
Formula
docs.text:
subject + be + going to + base form

  • I’m going to apply for the job.
  • They’re going to move in June.
  • It isn’t going to work.
  • Are you going to join us?

docs.title: A. Prior plan or intention
docs.text:
La decisione è stata presa prima del momento in cui parli.

  • I’m going to learn Spanish this year.
  • We’re going to redesign the website.
  • She’s going to look for a new flat.

Qui non è necessario che tutto sia già prenotato: basta che ci sia un’intenzione chiara.

docs.title: B. Prediction based on present evidence
docs.text:
• Look at those clouds. It’s going to rain.
  • He’s driving too fast. He’s going to have an accident.
  • This project is going to be expensive.

La differenza con will è che qui il parlante si basa su un segnale osservabile o su una situazione già evidente.

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
subject + be + verb-ing

  • I’m meeting the supplier tomorrow.
  • We’re having dinner with Clara on Friday.
  • She isn’t working next Monday.
  • Are you seeing the doctor this week?

docs.title: Main use
docs.text:
Il Present Continuous si usa per programmi organizzati, spesso con elementi concreti già fissati: orario, luogo, persone coinvolte, prenotazione, agenda.

  • I’m flying to Amsterdam on Tuesday morning.
  • We’re meeting the client at 11:30.
  • They’re staying at a hotel near the station.

docs.title: Key nuance
docs.text:
going to = piano / intenzione Present Continuous = appuntamento / arrangiamento già impostato

Compare:

  • I’m going to see the dentist soon. (intenzione / piano)
  • I’m seeing the dentist at 3 on Thursday. (appuntamento fissato)

docs.title: 4. The real contrast: same future, different angle
A. Plan vs arrangement
docs.text:
• I’m going to visit Paris this summer. (intenzione o piano generale)
  • I’m flying to Paris on 12 July. (organizzazione concreta)

docs.title: B. Opinion vs evidence
docs.text:
• I think sales will improve next quarter. (opinione)
  • Sales are going to improve. We’ve already signed three major contracts. (evidenza / base concreta)

docs.title: C. Decision now vs decision before
docs.text:
• We need someone to take notes. I’ll do it. (decisione spontanea)
  • I’m going to take notes during the meeting. (decisione già presa prima)

docs.title: D. Arrangement vs promise
docs.text:
• I’m meeting Julia after work. (programma fissato)
  • I’ll meet Julia after work if she’s free. (decisione o proposta che emerge ora / non ancora organizzata)

docs.title: 5. Time expressions that help the choice
docs.text:
Spesso il futuro si accompagna a espressioni come:

  • tonight
  • tomorrow
  • this evening
  • next week
  • on Friday
  • in two days
  • later
  • soon
  • at 8:30

Attenzione: la presenza di una time expression non basta da sola a scegliere la forma.

  • I’ll call you tomorrow. ✅
  • I’m going to call you tomorrow. ✅
  • I’m calling you tomorrow. ✅

Sono tutte possibili, ma il significato cambia:

  • I’ll call you tomorrow → decisione / promessa
  • I’m going to call you tomorrow → intenzione
  • I’m calling you tomorrow → piano già inserito nella tua agenda mentale o reale

docs.title: With will
docs.text:
• I’ll do it.
  • I’ll let you know.
  • I’ll be there.
  • I won’t forget.
  • Do you think it’ll work?

docs.title: With going to
docs.text:
• I’m going to start next month.
  • We’re going to change the plan.
  • She isn’t going to accept that.
  • What are you going to do after the course?

docs.title: With future Present Continuous
docs.text:
• I’m meeting the team at 9.
  • We’re leaving early tomorrow.
  • Are you coming with us tonight?
  • They’re staying with friends.

docs.title: 🎧 Reading: planning a work trip
docs.text: Nadia: Have you booked your trip to Berlin yet? Leo: Yes, I have. I’m flying on Wednesday morning and I’m meeting the client in the afternoon. Nadia: Great. Where are you staying? Leo: I’m staying near Alexanderplatz. Nadia: Nice. What are you going to discuss with the client? Leo: We’re going to talk about the new contract and the delivery schedule. Nadia: Sounds important. Do you think they’ll accept the proposal? Leo: I think they will, but they’re probably going to ask for a few changes. Nadia: That makes sense. Don’t worry, I’ll help you prepare before you leave.

docs.title: Comprehension
docs.text:
1. Which future form is used for Leo’s travel arrangements?
  2. Which sentence expresses Nadia’s offer?
  3. Which form is used for Leo’s opinion about the proposal?
  4. Why is going to used in they’re probably going to ask for a few changes?

docs.title: A. Decision now
docs.text:
React immediately to these situations:

  • Your colleague forgot the slides.
  • Your friend looks stressed.
  • There is no one at reception.
  • A customer is waiting for an answer.

Use I’ll... in complete sentences.

docs.title: B. Prior intentions
docs.text:
Answer with going to:

  • What are you going to improve in your English this month?
  • What are you going to do this weekend?
  • Are you going to change anything in your routine?
  • What are you going to study next?

docs.title: C. Arrangements
docs.text:
Answer with Present Continuous:

  • Who are you meeting this week?
  • What are you doing on Friday evening?
  • Are you travelling anywhere soon?
  • When are you seeing your teacher / manager / client next?

docs.title: D. Predictions
docs.text:
Make one sentence with will and one with going to for each topic:

  • the weather
  • your work or studies
  • technology
  • your English progress

Then explain why you chose each form.

docs.title: ✍️ Writing task
docs.text:
Write 140–180 words about your plans for the next two weeks.

Include:

  • at least 2 sentences with will
  • at least 3 sentences with going to
  • at least 3 sentences with Present Continuous for future arrangements
  • one prediction based on opinion
  • one prediction based on evidence

Suggested opening:

The next two weeks are going to be quite busy for me. I’m meeting...

docs.title: Common contractions
docs.text:
• I will → I’ll
  • you will → you’ll
  • we will → we’ll
  • is going to → ’s going to
  • are going to → ’re going to

docs.title: Natural spoken rhythm
docs.text:
• I’ll call you later.
  • We’re going to start at nine.
  • I’m meeting them after lunch.

Nel parlato informale potresti sentire gonna, ma a questo livello è più utile riconoscerla e capire quando compare, senza forzarne l’uso in modo artificiale.

docs.title: ⚠️ Error clinic: typical Italian mistakes
docs.text:
❌ I will to send the email. ✅ I will send the email.

❌ I going to change job. ✅ I’m going to change jobs. / I’m going to change my job situation.

❌ Tomorrow I am go to Milan. ✅ Tomorrow I’m going to Milan. / Tomorrow I’m going to go to Milan.

❌ I will meeting the customer at 4. ✅ I’m meeting the customer at 4.

❌ It will rain, look at the sky. ✅ It’s going to rain. Look at the sky.

❌ I think it’s going to be a good idea. (possible, but often Italians overuse going to for personal opinion) ✅ I think it will be a good idea.

docs.title: Why these mistakes happen
docs.text: Molti italiani cercano un equivalente unico di “futuro semplice”, ma l’inglese ragiona in modo più sfumato. Non devi tradurre il tempo verbale italiano: devi esprimere l’intenzione comunicativa.

docs.title: 🔁 Transformation drill
docs.text:
Riformula la frase scegliendo la forma più naturale.

  1. I have already decided: visit my aunt this weekend.
  2. The phone is ringing: answer it.
  3. Appointment with the accountant at 11 tomorrow.
  4. Look at the score: our team lose.
  5. My opinion: the new system be useful.
  6. We have tickets and a hotel reservation: stay in Rome for three nights.

docs.title: ✅ Mixed Review
docs.text:
Choose the best option.

  1. I think the presentation will / is going to / is presenting be successful.
  2. We will meet / are going to meet / are meeting the supplier at 2 p.m. tomorrow.
  3. Don’t worry, I will / am going to / am help you with the report.
  4. Look at that child on the bike! He will fall / is going to fall / is falling.
  5. I will start / am going to start / am starting a new online course next month; I’ve already enrolled.
  6. What will you do / are you going to do / are you doing after class tonight? (more than one answer may be possible — explain the nuance)

docs.title: 🧠 Think & Speak
docs.text:
Parla per 2–3 minuti di:

  • un progetto personale che hai intenzione di iniziare
  • tre appuntamenti o programmi già organizzati
  • due decisioni che potresti prendere sul momento in una situazione reale
  • due previsioni: una basata su opinione e una basata su evidenza

Obiettivo: usare tutte e tre le forme in modo consapevole e naturale.

docs.title: 🚀 Final task
docs.text:
Prepare a short spoken presentation titled:

My plans, arrangements and predictions for the next month

Include:

  • one opening sentence with overview
  • two examples with will
  • two with going to
  • two with Present Continuous
  • one contrast sentence that shows you know the difference

Example frame:

I’m going to focus more on my English next month. I’m meeting my teacher twice a week, and I’m starting a new reading routine. I think this will help me become more confident...

At B1 level, speaking naturally about the future means choosing the form that matches your intention, your evidence and your degree of organisation.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità saprai:

  • usare il First Conditional per parlare di situazioni future possibili
  • costruire frasi corrette con if + Present Simple, will + verbo base
  • distinguere tra condizione e risultato
  • evitare gli errori più comuni, soprattutto l’uso scorretto di will dopo if

docs.title: 🧠 Grammar in context
docs.text:
Il First Conditional si usa per parlare di una situazione futura reale o possibile e della sua conseguenza.

Formula base:

If + Present Simple, will + base form

  • If I study, I will pass the exam.
  • If it rains, we will stay at home.
  • If you call me, I will help you.

Questa struttura esprime una relazione logica:

  • condizione → cosa deve succedere
  • risultato → cosa succederà come conseguenza

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
If + subject + Present Simple, subject + will + base form

  • If she arrives early, we will start on time.
  • If they don’t hurry, they will miss the train.
  • If I have time, I will finish the report.

docs.title: Ordine alternativo
docs.text:
Puoi anche invertire l’ordine delle due parti:

  • If I have time, I will call you.
  • I will call you if I have time.

Quando la frase comincia con if, si usa la virgola.

docs.title: Negativa nella condizione
docs.text:
• If I don’t study, I won’t pass.
  • If she doesn’t come, we will start without her.

docs.title: Negativa nel risultato
docs.text:
• If it rains, we won’t go out.
  • If you don’t ask, they won’t know.

docs.title: Negativa in entrambe le parti
docs.text: • If we don’t leave now, we won’t arrive on time.

docs.title: 3. Forma interrogativa
docs.text:
Si usa spesso per fare domande su conseguenze future.

  • What will you do if it rains?
  • Will they come if we invite them?
  • What will happen if you miss the bus?
  • If he doesn’t study, will he fail the test?

docs.title: 4. Quando si usa
docs.text: Usa il First Conditional per:

docs.title: A. Situazioni future possibili
docs.text:
• If I see Anna tomorrow, I will tell her.
  • If we go by train, we will arrive earlier.

docs.title: B. Avvisi e conseguenze
docs.text:
• If you touch that, you will break it.
  • If you don’t sleep enough, you will feel tired.

docs.title: C. Promesse e decisioni legate a una condizione
docs.text:
• If you need help, I will stay.
  • If they agree, we will start next week.

docs.title: 📚 Examples in context
docs.text:
• If I finish work early, I will go to the gym.
  • If we don’t book now, we will lose the discount.
  • If you study every day, you will improve quickly.
  • If the weather is good, we will have lunch outside.
  • If he doesn’t answer, I will send a message.

docs.title: 🧩 Time expressions often used
docs.text:
Nel First Conditional trovi spesso parole come:

  • tomorrow
  • tonight
  • next week
  • later
  • soon
  • in the future
  • this afternoon

Esempi:

  • If she arrives tomorrow, we will talk.
  • If I have time later, I will call you.

docs.title: ⚠️ Regola fondamentale
docs.text:
Dopo if non si usa will.

❌ If I will study, I will pass. ✅ If I study, I will pass.

❌ If it will rain, we will stay home. ✅ If it rains, we will stay home.

Ricorda:

  • if-part = Present Simple
  • main clause = will + verbo base

docs.title: 🎧 Reading / Dialogue
docs.text:
Dialogue 1

A: What will you do if the meeting finishes early? B: If it finishes early, I will go back to the office.

A: And what if the client has more questions? B: Then we will stay longer.

Dialogue 2

A: Will you go out if it rains? B: No, I won’t. If it rains, I will stay home.

docs.title: 🗣️ Speaking
docs.text:
Ripeti:

  • If I study, I will pass.
  • If it rains, we will stay home.
  • If you call me, I will answer.
  • If she doesn’t hurry, she will be late.

Ora completa a voce:

  • If I have free time, I will ...
  • If my friend needs help, I will ...
  • If it is sunny this weekend, I will ...
  • If I don’t feel well, I will ...

docs.title: ✍️ Writing
docs.text:
Scrivi 8 frasi con il First Conditional.

Usa almeno questi temi:

  • studio
  • lavoro
  • salute
  • famiglia
  • tempo libero
  • viaggi

Esempio:

If I finish my tasks early, I will go for a walk.

docs.title: 🔊 Pronunciation
docs.text:
Nel parlato naturale:

  • I will → spesso I’ll
  • we will → we’ll
  • he will → he’ll

Esempi:

  • If I have time, I’ll help you.
  • If they arrive early, we’ll start.

Allenati a leggere:

  • If you need me, I’ll be here.
  • If it rains, we’ll cancel the picnic.

docs.title: ⚠️ Errori comuni (italiani)
docs.text:
❌ If I will see her, I will tell her. ✅ If I see her, I will tell her.

❌ If he will not study, he will fail. ✅ If he doesn’t study, he will fail.

❌ If I study, I pass the exam. ✅ If I study, I will pass the exam.

❌ If I will have time, I call you. ✅ If I have time, I will call you.

docs.title: Completa con la forma corretta
docs.text:
1. If I ______ (finish) early, I will come.
  2. If she ______ (not hurry), she will miss the bus.
  3. If we ______ (book) now, we will save money.
  4. If it ______ (rain), we will stay inside.
  5. If they ______ (not study), they will fail.

docs.title: Trasforma
docs.text:
1. I will help you. / you ask me
  2. We will stay home. / it rain
  3. She will be happy. / she pass the exam
  4. They will miss the train. / they not leave now

docs.title: 🧠 Think & Speak
docs.text:
Rispondi a voce:

  • What will you do if you are tired tonight?
  • What will you do if you have free time this weekend?
  • What will happen if you don’t study English regularly?
  • What will you buy if you go shopping tomorrow?

docs.title: ✅ Self-check
docs.text:
Controlla se sai:

  • formare frasi con if + Present Simple
  • usare will nel risultato
  • fare domande con il First Conditional
  • evitare will dopo if
  • parlare di conseguenze future possibili

Se riesci a farlo con sicurezza, la unità è acquisita.

docs.title: 🎯 Lesson Goals
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • usare il First Conditional per parlare di conseguenze future realistiche e prevedibili
  • distinguere tra una semplice frase sul futuro e una struttura che esprime condizione + risultato
  • costruire ipotesi pratiche, avvisi, offerte, piani e decisioni legate a circostanze specifiche
  • usare con naturalezza schemi molto frequenti nel parlato reale, come If you need anything, I’ll..., If the weather improves, we’ll..., If we don’t leave now, we’ll...
  • evitare gli errori tipici degli italofoni, soprattutto will dopo if, l’uso meccanico del presente al posto del futuro e la punteggiatura incerta

A livello B1 il First Conditional non serve solo a “fare frasi con if”, ma a ragionare in modo chiaro su conseguenze, scenari probabili, problemi pratici, piani alternativi e risultati attesi.

docs.title: 🧠 Core idea: a real future condition with a likely consequence
docs.text:
Il First Conditional si usa quando immagini una situazione futura possibile, concreta, plausibile e ne indichi la conseguenza.

Struttura canonica:

If + Present Simple, will + base form

  • If we leave now, we’ll catch the train.
  • If you don’t revise, you’ll make mistakes.
  • If the client agrees, we’ll sign the contract.

Il significato è questo:

  • la situazione non è certa al 100%
  • ma è realistica
  • e il risultato è presentato come una conseguenza logica

Non stai descrivendo un’abitudine generale, e non stai nemmeno immaginando una fantasia improbabile. Stai parlando di un possibile futuro reale.

docs.title: Basic formula
docs.text:
If + subject + Present Simple, subject + will + base form

  • If I have time, I’ll call you.
  • If she arrives late, we’ll start without her.
  • If they don’t answer, we’ll send an email.

docs.title: Alternative word order
docs.text:
Puoi invertire le due parti:

  • If I have time, I’ll call you.
  • I’ll call you if I have time.

La differenza non è grammaticale ma di enfasi:

  • mettendo if-clause all’inizio, metti in primo piano la condizione
  • mettendo prima il risultato, metti in primo piano l’azione principale

docs.title: Comma rule
docs.text:
• If-clause first → di norma metti la virgola
  • Main clause first → di norma niente virgola

Corretto:

  • If the meeting ends early, we’ll have lunch together.
  • We’ll have lunch together if the meeting ends early.

docs.title: 2. Why Present Simple after if?
docs.text:
Questo è il punto che crea più problemi agli italiani.

In italiano diciamo spesso:

  • Se pioverà, resteremo a casa.

In inglese standard, invece, dopo if si usa il Present Simple, non will.

✅ If it rains, we’ll stay at home. ❌ If it will rain, we’ll stay at home.

La logica inglese è questa:

  • la subordinata con if introduce la condizione
  • la principale esprime la conseguenza futura

Quindi:

  • if-clause = present form
  • result clause = future/result form

docs.title: A. Plans and practical consequences
docs.text:
• If we leave at six, we’ll avoid traffic.
  • If you book today, you’ll get a lower price.
  • If I finish this report, I’ll send it tonight.

Qui il First Conditional è molto utile per organizzare azioni concrete.

docs.title: B. Warnings and advice with consequences
docs.text:
• If you don’t back up your files, you’ll lose your work.
  • If you eat too fast, you’ll feel sick.
  • If he keeps interrupting people, they won’t listen to him.

Qui la struttura esprime un rapporto chiaro tra comportamento e risultato.

docs.title: C. Offers and support
docs.text:
• If you need help, I’ll stay after class.
  • If you want, I’ll check the document for you.
  • If there’s a problem, I’ll call the hotel.

Questa funzione è molto comune nel parlato collaborativo e professionale.

docs.title: D. Promises and reassurance
docs.text:
• If you tell me the truth, I won’t get angry.
  • If everything goes well, I’ll let you know immediately.
  • If you can’t come, I’ll explain the situation to the others.

docs.title: E. Cause-effect reasoning
docs.text:
• If sales increase, we’ll hire more staff.
  • If the weather improves, the event will be more successful.
  • If people work remotely more often, city centres may change.

docs.title: 4. First Conditional vs zero and second conditional
docs.text: Per usare bene il First Conditional, devi sentirne i confini.

docs.title: First Conditional = future possibility
docs.text:
• If it rains tomorrow, we’ll stay home.

Parli di una situazione futura specifica.

docs.title: Zero Conditional = general truth / routine consequence
docs.text:
• If it rains, the streets get slippery.

Questa frase non parla di domani: parla di una verità generale.

docs.title: Second Conditional = hypothetical / less real
docs.text:
• If it rained tomorrow, we would stay home.

Questa forma suggerisce maggiore distanza o minore probabilità, oppure uno stile più ipotetico.

A livello B1 è importante capire che il First Conditional è la forma più naturale per scenari futuri concreti e realistici.

docs.title: 5. The result clause is not always only will
docs.text:
La struttura base è if + present, will + verb, ma il risultato può anche contenere altri modali o strutture quando il significato lo richiede.

Esempi naturali:

  • If you finish early, can you call me?
  • If the traffic is bad, we might be late.
  • If you want to improve, you must practise regularly.
  • If she arrives before me, tell her to wait.

Per questa unità la struttura principale resta con will, ma è utile iniziare a vedere che nel mondo reale la seconda parte può adattarsi al messaggio.

docs.title: 6. High-frequency patterns in real English
docs.text: Questi schemi compaiono continuamente:

docs.title: If you need..., I’ll...
docs.text:
• If you need anything, I’ll be in my office.
  • If you need more information, I’ll send it later.

docs.title: If we don’t..., we’ll...
docs.text:
• If we don’t leave now, we’ll be late.
  • If we don’t act quickly, we’ll lose the opportunity.

docs.title: If ... happens, I’ll...
docs.text:
• If anything changes, I’ll text you.
  • If there’s a problem, I’ll deal with it.

docs.title: If the weather / traffic / meeting ...
docs.text:
• If the weather is good, we’ll eat outside.
  • If the traffic is heavy, we’ll take the train.
  • If the meeting runs late, we’ll cancel lunch.

Imparare questi pattern ti rende subito più sciolto.

docs.title: Work
docs.text:
• If the manager approves the budget, we’ll start the project next month.
  • If the supplier delays the delivery, we’ll need a backup plan.
  • If you send me the final version today, I’ll review it tonight.

docs.title: Study
docs.text:
• If I revise regularly, I’ll feel more confident in the exam.
  • If she misses another lesson, she’ll fall behind.
  • If we finish the exercises early, we’ll do some speaking practice.

docs.title: Travel
docs.text:
• If our flight is delayed, we’ll miss the connection.
  • If the hotel is too expensive, we’ll look for another one.
  • If it stops raining, we’ll go for a walk by the sea.

docs.title: Everyday life
docs.text:
• If I get home early, I’ll cook dinner.
  • If you don’t charge your phone, it’ll die.
  • If they invite us, we’ll probably go.

docs.title: 🎧 Reading: practical decision-making
docs.text:
Situation: planning a weekend trip

Marta: What shall we do if the weather is bad on Saturday?

Leo: If it rains, we’ll visit the museum instead of going hiking.

Marta: Good idea. And what if the trains are delayed?

Leo: If that happens, we’ll take the earlier one on Sunday.

Marta: Should we book the hotel now?

Leo: Yes. If we book tonight, we’ll probably get a better rate.

docs.title: Why this dialogue is useful
docs.text:
Qui il First Conditional serve per:

  • fare piani alternativi
  • gestire imprevisti
  • ragionare in anticipo
  • proporre soluzioni concrete

docs.title: Drill 1: repeat with rhythm
docs.text:
• If I have time, I’ll help.
  • If she calls, I’ll answer.
  • If they don’t hurry, they’ll miss it.
  • If the weather improves, we’ll go out.
  • If you ask clearly, people will understand.

docs.title: Drill 2: complete the result
docs.text:
Completa a voce:

  • If I finish work early, I’ll ...
  • If my train is cancelled, I’ll ...
  • If I don’t sleep enough, I’ll ...
  • If my friend needs help, I’ll ...
  • If the lesson ends early, we’ll ...

docs.title: Drill 3: complete the condition
docs.text:
• I’ll call you if ...
  • We’ll stay home if ...
  • She’ll be disappointed if ...
  • They’ll buy it if ...
  • I won’t go if ...

docs.title: Drill 4: respond quickly
docs.text:
A: What will you do if your laptop stops working? B: If my laptop stops working, I’ll use my backup device.

A: What will happen if you don’t prepare? B: If I don’t prepare, I’ll probably make mistakes.

docs.title: Task 1: personal scenarios
docs.text:
Scrivi 8 frasi vere su di te usando il First Conditional.

Usa almeno questi temi:

  • lavoro o studio
  • soldi
  • salute
  • weekend
  • tecnologia
  • relazioni

Esempio:

If I have a free evening this week, I’ll finish the book I started last month.

docs.title: Task 2: practical email-style writing
docs.text:
Scrivi un breve testo di 120–150 parole in cui spieghi il piano per un evento o una riunione. Usa almeno 6 frasi con il First Conditional.

Puoi usare idee come:

  • ritardo dei partecipanti
  • problemi tecnici
  • maltempo
  • cambio di programma
  • necessità di aiuto

docs.title: 🔊 Pronunciation and connected speech
docs.text:
Nel parlato reale will spesso si riduce:

  • I will → I’ll
  • we will → we’ll
  • he will → he’ll
  • she will → she’ll
  • they will → they’ll

Esempi:

  • If I have time, I’ll call you.
  • If we leave now, we’ll be fine.
  • If she arrives early, she’ll join us.

Allenati sul ritmo della frase intera, non parola per parola:

  • If I have time, I’ll help you.
  • If the traffic is light, we’ll get there early.
  • If you don’t ask, you won’t know.

docs.title: Errore 1: will dopo if
docs.text: ❌ If I will see him, I’ll tell him. ✅ If I see him, I’ll tell him.

docs.title: Errore 2: presente al posto del risultato futuro
docs.text:
❌ If I study, I pass the exam. ✅ If I study, I’ll pass the exam.

Questa frase potrebbe sembrare comprensibile, ma non è la forma standard per parlare di una conseguenza futura specifica.

docs.title: Errore 3: uso meccanico della stessa struttura ovunque
docs.text:
A volte serve il Zero Conditional, non il First.

  • If people don’t drink water, they get thirsty. ✅

Qui non stai parlando di una situazione futura singola, ma di una verità generale.

docs.title: Errore 4: confusione tra if e when
docs.text:
• if = se accade
  • when = quando accadrà / nel momento in cui accade

Confronta:

  • If he calls, I’ll tell him. → non so se chiamerà
  • When he calls, I’ll tell him. → penso che chiamerà, è più probabile/certo

Questa differenza è molto utile in contesti realistici.

docs.title: A. Complete with the correct form
docs.text:
1. If we ______ (leave) now, we will catch the bus.
  2. If she ______ (not study), she will not pass.
  3. If the weather ______ (improve), we will go out.
  4. If I ______ (have) a question, I will ask.
  5. If they ______ (miss) the deadline, the client will complain.
  6. If you ______ (not back up) your files, you will lose them.
  7. If he ______ (finish) early, he will join us.
  8. If the shop ______ (be) closed, we will order online.

docs.title: B. Rewrite using the First Conditional
docs.text:
1. You ask me. I help you.
  2. We don’t book now. We lose our seats.
  3. She arrives on time. We start at nine.
  4. I don’t feel better. I go to the doctor.
  5. The presentation goes well. We celebrate.

docs.title: C. Choose the best option
docs.text:
1. If it rains tomorrow, we stay / will stay inside.
  2. If he will call / calls, tell him I’m busy.
  3. If they don’t hurry, they miss / will miss the train.
  4. If I see the problem, I fix / will fix it.
  5. If the price goes up, we won’t buy / don’t buy it.

docs.title: 🧠 Think and speak
docs.text:
Rispondi in modo sviluppato, non con una frase sola.

  • What will you do if your plans change at the last minute?
  • What will happen if people in your city use cars less?
  • If you have a stressful week, how will you relax?
  • If you get a good professional opportunity, what will you do?
  • If you don’t practise English regularly, what will happen?

Obiettivo: collegare grammatica e ragionamento reale.

docs.title: Scenario task
docs.text:
Stai organizzando un piccolo evento, una lezione, una riunione o un viaggio. Devi spiegare cosa succederà in diversi scenari.

Parla o scrivi usando almeno 8 frasi con il First Conditional.

Devi includere:

  • un problema possibile
  • una soluzione
  • un’alternativa
  • una promessa di supporto
  • una conseguenza negativa se non si agisce

Esempio di apertura:

If everyone arrives on time, we’ll start at 9:00. If someone has technical problems, I’ll help before the meeting begins. If the internet connection fails, we’ll switch to a backup plan.

docs.title: ✅ Self-check
docs.text:
Controlla se riesci a:

  • formare il First Conditional senza errori strutturali
  • usare correttamente if + Present Simple
  • evitare will dopo if
  • distinguere First Conditional da Zero e Second Conditional
  • usare la struttura per piani, avvisi, offerte e conseguenze realistiche
  • parlare per 1–2 minuti di scenari futuri possibili con buona fluidità

Se tutto questo ti riesce con naturalezza, la unità è davvero acquisita a livello B1.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità saprai:

  • esprimere opinioni personali in modo chiaro e naturale
  • dire se sei d’accordo o non sei d’accordo
  • dare una breve motivazione con because
  • usare formule semplici ma efficaci per partecipare a una conversazione
  • evitare errori comuni come I am agree o I think yes

docs.title: 🧠 Grammar in context
docs.text:
In inglese esprimere un’opinione è una competenza fondamentale, soprattutto a livello B1, perché permette di partecipare davvero a una conversazione.

Le strutture più comuni sono:

  • I think...
  • I don’t think...
  • I believe...
  • In my opinion...

Esempi:

  • I think this idea is useful.
  • I don’t think this plan will work.
  • I believe English is essential for work.
  • In my opinion, this is the best solution.

docs.title: Formule base
docs.text:
• I think this is a good idea.
  • I think the film is too long.
  • I don’t think this restaurant is very good.
  • In my opinion, public transport should be cheaper.

docs.title: Perché sono importanti
docs.text:
Queste strutture ti aiutano a:

  • iniziare una conversazione
  • rispondere a una domanda personale
  • commentare un’idea, una notizia, un film, una decisione
  • esprimere accordo o disaccordo in modo educato

docs.title: Agreement
docs.text:
• I agree.
  • I agree with you.
  • You’re right.
  • I think so.
  • Exactly.

docs.title: Disagreement
docs.text:
• I don’t agree.
  • I don’t think so.
  • I’m not sure.
  • I see your point, but...
  • I think you’re wrong. (più diretto)

docs.title: 3. Giving a reason
docs.text:
Per rendere la tua opinione più naturale, aggiungi una motivazione con because.

  • I think this app is useful because it saves time.
  • I don’t think it’s a good idea because it’s too expensive.
  • In my opinion, online classes are effective because they are flexible.

docs.title: Opinion expressions
docs.text:
• I think...
  • I don’t think...
  • In my opinion...
  • I believe...
  • For me...

docs.title: Reaction expressions
docs.text:
• Really?
  • That’s true.
  • Maybe.
  • I’m not sure.
  • It depends.

docs.title: 🎧 Reading / Dialogue
docs.text:
Dialogue 1

A: What do you think of remote work? B: I think it’s great because it saves time. A: I agree, but I think people also need face-to-face contact. B: Yes, that’s true.

Dialogue 2

A: In my opinion, travelling by train is better than flying. B: I’m not sure. I think flying is faster. A: That’s true, but trains are usually more comfortable.

docs.title: 🗣️ Speaking
docs.text:
Ripeti:

  • I think this is a good idea.
  • I don’t think it will work.
  • I agree with you.
  • I don’t think so.
  • In my opinion, this is the best option.

Ora completa a voce:

  • I think English is important because ...
  • In my opinion, social media is ...
  • I don’t think ... because ...
  • I agree that ...
  • I’m not sure if ...

docs.title: ✍️ Writing
docs.text:
Scrivi 8 frasi per esprimere opinioni su questi temi:

  • work
  • study
  • technology
  • travel
  • food
  • free time

Usa almeno:

  • 3 frasi con I think...
  • 2 con I don’t think...
  • 1 con In my opinion...
  • 2 con because

Esempio:

I think learning English is important because it opens many opportunities.

docs.title: 🔊 Pronunciation
docs.text:
Attenzione a queste espressioni:

  • I think → /aɪ θɪŋk/
  • I agree → /aɪ əˈɡriː/
  • I don’t think so → ritmo naturale e collegato

Allenati a dire:

  • I think it’s useful.
  • I don’t think so.
  • I agree with you.
  • In my opinion, it’s better.

docs.title: ⚠️ Errori comuni (italiani)
docs.text:
❌ I am agree ✅ I agree

❌ I think yes ✅ I think so

❌ I don’t agree with this idea because is expensive ✅ I don’t agree with this idea because it is expensive

❌ In my opinion is better ✅ In my opinion, it is better

docs.title: ✅ Review & Practice
docs.text:
Completa:

  1. I ___ this is a good idea.
  2. I don’t ___ so.
  3. I ___ with you.
  4. In my ___, this is better.
  5. I think it’s useful ___ it saves time.

docs.title: 🔄 Transform
docs.text:
Trasforma le frasi usando un’espressione di opinione:

  1. This plan is good. → I think ______
  2. This course is useful. → In my opinion, ______
  3. It is not a good idea. → I don’t think ______
  4. Online meetings are practical. → I believe ______

docs.title: 🧠 Think & Speak
docs.text:
Rispondi oralmente:

  • What do you think of studying online?
  • Do you think technology helps people?
  • Do you agree that English is necessary for work?
  • What is your opinion about living in a big city?

docs.title: 🚀 Mini task
docs.text:
Parla per 2 minuti su questo tema:

“What makes a good place to live?”

Prova a includere:

  • 3 opinioni
  • 2 ragioni con because
  • 1 punto di accordo o disaccordo

English becomes more real when you can explain what you think and why you think it.

docs.title: 🎯 Lesson Goals
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • esprimere opinioni in modo più sfumato e naturale, non solo con il semplice I think
  • mostrare accordo, disaccordo, riserva, dubbio e parziale concessione con formule utili nel parlato reale
  • sostenere un’opinione con ragioni, esempi e contrasti
  • partecipare a conversazioni su temi quotidiani, studio, lavoro, tecnologia e società in modo più maturo
  • evitare errori tipici degli italofoni, soprattutto I am agree, I think yes, uso eccessivo di I think e mancanza di coesione nel discorso

A livello B1 non basta “dire la propria idea”: bisogna saperla formulare in modo chiaro, credibile e conversazionale.

docs.title: 🧠 Core idea: opinions are not only grammar, but interaction
docs.text:
Esprimere un’opinione in inglese significa saper fare almeno quattro cose:

  1. dire cosa pensi
  2. mostrare il grado di certezza
  3. reagire all’opinione degli altri
  4. spiegare perché

Per questo motivo, a livello B1 è utile andare oltre il trio base:

  • I think...
  • I agree.
  • I don’t agree.

Queste formule funzionano, ma risultano limitate se usate sempre uguali. In conversazione reale servono varietà, tono e collegamenti logici.

docs.title: 1. Degrees of opinion: strong, neutral, cautious
A. Neutral opinion
docs.text:
• I think this solution is practical.
  • I think the course is well organised.
  • In my opinion, this option is safer.

Queste forme sono dirette ma non aggressive.

docs.title: B. Stronger opinion
docs.text:
• I strongly believe that communication is more important than perfect grammar.
  • I’m convinced that remote work will remain common.
  • There’s no doubt in my mind that practice matters more than theory.

Queste strutture mostrano maggiore sicurezza.

docs.title: C. More cautious / diplomatic opinion
docs.text:
• I suppose it depends on the situation.
  • I’m not entirely sure, but I think this could work.
  • From my point of view, this seems a bit unrealistic.
  • I’m not convinced that this is the best approach.

Queste formule sono molto utili quando vuoi sembrare più equilibrato o professionale.

docs.title: 2. The most useful opinion structures at B1
Core forms
docs.text:
• I think...
  • I don’t think...
  • I believe...
  • In my opinion...
  • From my point of view...
  • As far as I’m concerned...

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• I think this app is useful for beginners.
  • I don’t think that policy is fair.
  • I believe people learn faster when they practise regularly.
  • From my point of view, working from home is efficient but not ideal for everyone.
  • As far as I’m concerned, public transport should be cheaper and more reliable.

docs.title: Register note
docs.text:
• I think... = neutro, comune, molto frequente
  • I believe... = leggermente più formale o più forte
  • In my opinion... = chiaro e organizzato, utile anche nello scritto
  • From my point of view... = naturale in contesti più discussivi

docs.title: 3. Agreeing and disagreeing naturally
docs.text: A livello B1 è importante non sembrare troppo bruschi.

docs.title: Direct agreement
docs.text:
• I agree.
  • I agree with you.
  • Exactly.
  • That’s true.
  • I think so too.

docs.title: Softer disagreement
docs.text:
• I’m not sure I agree.
  • I see your point, but...
  • That may be true, but...
  • I’m not convinced.
  • I don’t really see it that way.

docs.title: Stronger disagreement
docs.text:
• I don’t agree.
  • I don’t think that’s right.
  • I think that view is too simplistic.

Per una conversazione civile e naturale, di solito è meglio iniziare con una formula più morbida prima di contraddire.

Esempio:

  • I see your point, but I think cost is still a major problem.

docs.title: 4. Giving reasons and support
docs.text: Un’opinione da sola spesso suona debole. A B1 devi imparare ad aggiungere supporto.

docs.title: Connectors that help
docs.text:
• because
  • so
  • that’s why
  • for example
  • however
  • on the other hand

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• I think online learning is effective because it gives people more flexibility.
  • I don’t think this plan will succeed, because it ignores the cost.
  • Public transport is useful. That’s why cities should invest in it.
  • I like remote work. However, I think teams still need some in-person meetings.
  • For example, many employees are more productive when they can work from home.

docs.title: 5. Opinion + contrast = more mature speaking
docs.text:
Una delle abilità più importanti a livello B1 è riconoscere che due idee possono essere vere contemporaneamente.

Esempi:

  • I think social media can be useful, but it can also be distracting.
  • In my opinion, city life is exciting. On the other hand, it can be stressful.
  • I agree that technology saves time; however, it sometimes reduces real interaction.
