• “Rome is the capital of Italy.”
  • He said that Rome is the capital of Italy.

docs.title: 2. Quando vuoi mantenere il fatto come valido adesso
docs.text: • She said the office opens at 9.

docs.title: 3. Quando riporti notizie o affermazioni ancora attuali
docs.text:
• The spokesperson said that the company is reviewing the proposal.

A livello alto, la scelta dipende dal punto di vista del parlante.

docs.title: 🧠 Cambiamenti di pronomi, tempo e luogo
docs.text: Quando riporti qualcosa, non cambiano solo i tempi verbali.

docs.title: Pronomi
docs.text:
• I → he / she
  • we → they
  • my → his / her
  • our → their

docs.title: Espressioni di tempo
docs.text:
• now → then
  • today → that day
  • yesterday → the day before
  • tomorrow → the next day / the following day
  • last week → the week before
  • next month → the following month

docs.title: Espressioni di luogo
docs.text:
• here → there
  • this → that
  • these → those

docs.title: Esempio completo
docs.text:
• “I’m working here today.”
  • He said that he was working there that day.

docs.title: 🗣️ Reporting statements
docs.text:
Con le affermazioni usiamo spesso:

  • say (that)
  • tell + object + that
  • explain (that)
  • mention (that)
  • admit (that)
  • promise (that)

docs.title: Esempi
docs.text:
• She said that the plan was too expensive.
  • He told me that he needed help.
  • They explained that the delay was unavoidable.
  • She admitted that she had made a mistake.
  • He promised that he would call later.

docs.title: Say vs Tell
docs.text:
• say → non richiede oggetto immediato
  • tell → richiede quasi sempre una persona

✅ He said that he was tired. ✅ He told me that he was tired.

❌ He said me that...

docs.title: Yes / No questions
docs.text:
Usiamo if o whether.

  • “Are you coming?”

  • He asked if I was coming.

  • “Did you finish the task?”

  • She asked whether I had finished the task.

docs.title: Wh- questions
docs.text:
Manteniamo la parola interrogativa.

  • “Where do you live?”

  • He asked where I lived.

  • “Why did she leave?”

  • They asked why she had left.

docs.title: Attenzione all’ordine delle parole
docs.text:
Nel reported speech non usiamo l’ordine interrogativo.

❌ He asked where did I live. ✅ He asked where I lived.

docs.title: 🧾 Reporting commands, requests and warnings
docs.text:
Per ordini, richieste e avvertimenti si usa spesso:

  • tell + object + to + verb
  • ask + object + to + verb
  • warn + object + to / not to + verb

docs.title: Esempi
docs.text:
• “Close the door.” → He told me to close the door.
  • “Please send the file.” → She asked me to send the file.
  • “Don’t be late.” → He told me not to be late.
  • “Don’t touch that wire.” → She warned us not to touch that wire.

docs.title: 💡 Reporting suggestions and offers
docs.text: Non tutto si riporta con say.

docs.title: Suggest
docs.text:
• He suggested waiting a few more days.
  • She suggested that we postpone the meeting.

docs.title: Offer
docs.text:
• He offered to help.
  • She offered to drive us to the station.

docs.title: Promise
docs.text:
• They promised to send an update.
  • He promised that he would be more careful.

docs.title: 🧩 Reporting verbs più utili
docs.text: Verbo Uso tipico say dire tell dire a qualcuno explain spiegare mention menzionare admit ammettere deny negare suggest suggerire warn avvertire promise promettere offer offrire recommend raccomandare insist insistere

docs.title: Esempi
docs.text:
• She denied that she had copied the work.
  • He recommended taking a break.
  • They insisted that the figures were correct.

docs.title: 🧠 Riportare informazioni in modo naturale
docs.text:
A livello alto non riporti ogni parola.

Spesso selezioni solo ciò che conta.

docs.title: Direct speech
docs.text: “I know we wanted to finish everything today, but I’m still waiting for two documents, so I think we should move the deadline to next Monday.”

docs.title: Natural reported version
docs.text:
She explained that she was still waiting for two documents and suggested moving the deadline to the following Monday.

Questa è la vera abilità: sintetizzare senza perdere il senso.

docs.title: 🎧 Reading
docs.text: During the meeting, the manager explained that sales had improved in the previous quarter but admitted that costs were still too high. She also warned the team that some deadlines might need to be revised and suggested focusing on the most urgent tasks first.

docs.title: Comprensione
docs.text:
1. What had improved?
  2. What problem still existed?
  3. What did she warn the team about?
  4. What did she suggest?

docs.title: 🗣️ Speaking
docs.text:
Racconta una conversazione o una riunione usando almeno 5 strutture di reported speech.

Puoi parlare di:

  • una riunione di lavoro o di studio
  • una conversazione con un amico
  • un momento in cui qualcuno ti ha dato un consiglio
  • un problema che ti è stato spiegato

docs.title: Modello utile
docs.text:
• He told me that...
  • She explained that...
  • They asked whether...
  • He warned us not to...
  • She suggested...

docs.title: ✍️ Writing
docs.text: Scrivi un testo di 140–170 parole.

docs.title: Traccia
docs.text:
Racconta una riunione, una telefonata o una conversazione importante.

Nel testo includi:

  • almeno 4 reporting verbs diversi
  • almeno 1 domanda riportata
  • almeno 1 ordine, richiesta o suggerimento riportato
  • almeno 1 esempio di scelta corretta tra backshift e non-backshift

docs.title: ⚠️ Errori comuni
docs.text: Errore Corretto He said me that... He told me that... He asked where did I live He asked where I lived She suggested to wait She suggested waiting He told that he was tired He said that he was tired / He told me that... He said that he will come He said that he would come

docs.title: Completa
docs.text:
1. “I am busy.” → She said that she ___ busy.
  2. “Do you agree?” → He asked if I ___ .
  3. “Send me the file.” → She told me ___ the file.
  4. “Let’s wait.” → He suggested ___ .
  5. “We will help you.” → They promised they ___ help me.

docs.title: Trasforma
docs.text:
1. “I can’t stay long.”
  2. “Why are you late?”
  3. “Don’t open the window.”
  4. “We finished the project yesterday.”
  5. “I think the plan is too risky.”

docs.title: 🚀 Final Challenge
docs.text: Racconta un episodio complesso usando il reported speech.

docs.title: Obiettivo
docs.text:
Devi includere:

  • 6 reporting verbs diversi
  • 1 question report
  • 1 command / request report
  • 1 suggestion
  • 1 esempio di informazione sintetizzata, non tradotta parola per parola

docs.title: 🎯 Insight finale
docs.text:
A livelli alti, il reported speech non serve solo a trasformare frasi.

Serve a:

  • riportare idee
  • gestire informazioni
  • fare sintesi
  • dare il giusto tono al messaggio

Advanced English is not about repeating words. It is about reporting meaning with precision.

docs.title: 🎯 Lesson Goals
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • usare il reported speech come strumento di information management, non come semplice trasformazione grammaticale
  • distinguere tra faithful reporting, selective reporting e interpretive reporting
  • gestire con precisione backshift, no backshift, cambi di riferimento e sfumature di prospettiva
  • scegliere reporting verbs che trasmettono non solo il contenuto, ma anche attitude, intention, stance e speech act
  • riportare affermazioni, domande, ordini, suggerimenti, accuse, promesse, richieste e valutazioni con registro adeguato
  • sintetizzare riunioni, conversazioni, interviste, briefing e scambi complessi in un inglese più maturo e professionale

Questa unità non serve a “cambiare i tempi correttamente”. Serve a capire how reported speech reshapes meaning.

docs.title: 🧠 Beyond transformation: reported speech as interpretation
docs.text:
A scuola il reported speech viene spesso presentato così:

  • “I am tired.” → He said that he was tired.

Ma a livello C1 la vera domanda non è:

  • Can I backshift the tense correctly?

La vera domanda è:

  • What exactly am I choosing to transmit?
  • Am I reproducing the wording, or the meaning?
  • Am I remaining neutral, summarising, distancing, softening, or interpreting?
  • What does my choice of reporting verb imply about the original speaker?

Quando riporti il discorso, non trasferisci solo parole. Trasferisci:

  1. content → cosa è stato detto
  2. stance → come tu presenti quell’informazione
  3. evaluation → se il messaggio suona neutro, dubbio, forte, emotivo, diplomatico
  4. focus → quali elementi decidi di mettere in primo piano
  5. speaker intention → spiegare, promettere, accusare, negare, proporre, avvertire, ammettere

Il reported speech è quindi una forma di controlled reframing.

docs.title: 🧩 Direct speech, reported speech, and reporting distance
Direct speech
docs.text:
• She said, “I won’t sign this version.”

Qui hai:

  • wording esatto
  • maggiore vividness
  • effetto più drammatico o immediato

docs.title: Neutral reported speech
docs.text:
• She said that she wouldn’t sign that version.

Qui il contenuto rimane, ma il messaggio è meno teatrale e più integrato nel discorso.

docs.title: Interpretive reporting
docs.text:
• She made it clear that she was unwilling to approve the current version.

Questa non è una semplice trasformazione. È una reframing choice: il parlante interpreta il valore pragmatico del messaggio.

docs.title: 🔁 Backshift: not a rule, but a viewpoint choice
Traditional pattern
docs.text:
Quando il verbo introduttivo è al passato, spesso abbiamo:

Direct Reported Present Simple Past Simple Present Continuous Past Continuous Past Simple Past Perfect Present Perfect Past Perfect will would can could may might

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• “I work remotely.” → He said that he worked remotely.
  • “I’m waiting for a reply.” → She said that she was waiting for a reply.
  • “I finished it yesterday.” → He said that he had finished it the day before.
  • “I will call later.” → She said that she would call later.

docs.title: But at C1, backshift is not mechanical
docs.text:
Il backshift segnala spesso che stai guardando il contenuto from a past reporting point.

No-backshift, invece, può segnalare che il contenuto è:

  • ancora valido
  • presentato come ancora rilevante
  • trattato come fact rather than past statement
  • mantenuto vicino al punto di vista attuale del reporter

docs.title: Compare
docs.text:
• He said that the office was on the second floor.
  • He said that the office is on the second floor.

La seconda frase suggerisce che l’informazione è ancora vera adesso. La scelta quindi è semantica, non solo grammaticale.

docs.title: 🧠 Reference shift: pronouns, time, place, perspective
docs.text: Un buon reported speech richiede controllo dei riferimenti.

docs.title: Pronouns
docs.text:
• I → he / she
  • we → they
  • my → his / her
  • our → their

docs.title: Time
docs.text:
• now → then
  • today → that day
  • yesterday → the day before
  • tomorrow → the next / following day
  • last week → the previous week
  • next month → the following month

docs.title: Place / deixis
docs.text:
• here → there
  • this → that
  • these → those

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
• “We’re meeting here tomorrow.”
  • He said that they were meeting there the following day.

But advanced users also know that natural English sometimes keeps certain expressions unchanged if the context is still shared.

docs.title: 🗣️ Reporting verbs are never neutral
docs.text:
Molti studenti usano quasi solo say e tell. A livello C1 questo limita moltissimo precisione e nuance.

Confronta:

  • He said that the figures were wrong.
  • He pointed out that the figures were wrong.
  • He complained that the figures were wrong.
  • He admitted that the figures were wrong.
  • He insisted that the figures were wrong.
  • He claimed that the figures were wrong.

Il contenuto di base è simile, ma cambia completamente la lettura pragmatica.

docs.title: Neutral / informational
docs.text:
• say
  • tell
  • state
  • mention
  • report
  • note
  • explain
  • add

docs.title: Stronger stance / evaluation
docs.text:
• claim
  • argue
  • insist
  • maintain
  • deny
  • admit
  • concede
  • confirm
  • reveal
  • stress
  • warn
  • urge
  • announce
  • acknowledge

docs.title: Speech-act oriented
docs.text:
• promise
  • offer
  • threaten
  • suggest
  • recommend
  • advise
  • request
  • order
  • invite
  • refuse

A C1, saper scegliere il verbo giusto significa saper codificare speaker intention con precisione.

docs.title: 1. Verb + that-clause
docs.text:
• She explained that the system had failed.
  • He admitted that he had overlooked the issue.
  • They claimed that the figures were accurate.

docs.title: 2. Verb + object + that-clause
docs.text:
• He told me that the deadline had changed.
  • She assured us that everything was under control.

docs.title: 3. Verb + to-infinitive
docs.text:
• She agreed to revise the draft.
  • He promised to call later.
  • They refused to comment.
  • She offered to help.

docs.title: 4. Verb + object + to-infinitive
docs.text:
• He told me to wait.
  • She asked us to leave early.
  • They urged the team to act quickly.
  • The doctor advised him to rest.

docs.title: 5. Verb + -ing
docs.text:
• He suggested waiting.
  • She denied copying the report.
  • They admitted making a mistake.
  • He recommended taking a different approach.

docs.title: 6. Verb + preposition + -ing / noun
docs.text:
• She apologised for arriving late.
  • He complained about having too little time.
  • They accused him of leaking the document.

Il controllo di questi pattern è cruciale, perché il vero livello alto si vede spesso nella combinazione corretta verbo + struttura, non nella sola idea generale.

docs.title: ❓ Reporting questions: form, not just meaning
Yes / No questions
docs.text:
Usiamo if o whether:

  • “Are you available tomorrow?”
  • She asked if / whether I was available the next day.

docs.title: Wh- questions
docs.text:
Manteniamo la parola interrogativa:

  • “Why did the client reject it?”
  • He asked why the client had rejected it.

docs.title: The advanced point
docs.text:
Nel reported speech la domanda non mantiene la sintassi interrogativa.

❌ He asked why did the client reject it. ✅ He asked why the client had rejected it.

A livelli alti, però, la vera differenza è che puoi anche reframe the question function:

  • He wanted to know why the client had rejected it.
  • She wondered whether the delay could still be avoided.
  • They questioned whether the current strategy was sustainable.

Queste forme suonano spesso più naturali e sofisticate di un semplice asked.

docs.title: 🧾 Reporting commands, requests, warnings, and pressure
Standard patterns
docs.text:
• tell + object + to + verb
  • ask + object + to + verb
  • warn + object + not to + verb
  • advise + object + to + verb
  • urge + object + to + verb

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• She told us to leave the draft unchanged.
  • He asked me to resend the attachment.
  • They warned us not to share the file externally.
  • The consultant advised the team to simplify the process.
  • She urged him to speak to the client directly.

docs.title: Tone matters
docs.text:
Confronta:

  • He told us to stop.
  • He asked us to stop.
  • He urged us to stop.
  • He warned us to stop.

La differenza non è solo grammaticale: è una differenza di force, urgency, and stance.

docs.title: 💡 Suggesting, recommending, proposing
docs.text: Uno degli errori più comuni è trattare tutti i suggerimenti allo stesso modo.

docs.title: Useful structures
docs.text:
• He suggested waiting a bit longer.
  • She suggested that we postpone the launch.
  • They recommended reducing the scope.
  • He proposed moving the discussion to Friday.

docs.title: Wrong vs right
docs.text:
❌ He suggested to wait. ✅ He suggested waiting. ✅ He suggested that we wait.

A livello C1, puoi anche usare il reported speech per compress extended discussion into one interpretive summary:

After listening to the concerns, she suggested scaling the project back and focusing on the most realistic deliverables.

docs.title: 🧠 Selective reporting vs word-by-word reporting
docs.text: Questa è una delle competenze più importanti del livello C1.

docs.title: Word-by-word tendency
docs.text: Molti studenti provano a riportare quasi tutto in sequenza, mantenendo la struttura del parlato originale. Questo produce spesso testi pesanti, ripetitivi e poco eleganti.

docs.title: Selective reporting
docs.text:
Uno studente avanzato sa:

  • eliminare ridondanze
  • comprimere dettagli secondari
  • scegliere il reporting verb più informativo
  • organizzare logicamente il contenuto

docs.title: Original speech
docs.text: “I know we wanted to finish this week, but we’re still missing two approvals, and unless we get them by Friday, I don’t think we should commit to the launch date.”

docs.title: Weak report
docs.text: She said that they wanted to finish that week but that they were still missing two approvals and that if they didn’t get them by Friday, she didn’t think they should commit to the launch date.

docs.title: Stronger report
docs.text:
She explained that two approvals were still missing and argued that the team should avoid committing to the launch date unless they were received by Friday.

La seconda versione è più sintetica, più precisa e più professionale.

docs.title: 🧠 Reported speech and stance
docs.text: Quando riporti, stai anche prendendo posizione rispetto a ciò che è stato detto.

docs.title: Compare
docs.text:
• He said that the plan would work.
  • He claimed that the plan would work.
  • He argued that the plan would work.
  • He maintained that the plan would work.
  • He admitted that the plan might not work.

Ogni verbo orienta il lettore in modo diverso.

docs.title: Practical consequence
docs.text:
Nel writing professionale, accademico o giornalistico, la scelta del reporting verb è spesso un modo per segnalare:

  • affidabilità
  • distanza critica
  • neutralità
  • scetticismo
  • allineamento o disallineamento

docs.title: 🧩 Reported speech in professional and academic settings
Meeting summary
docs.text:
• The director stated that the budget would remain unchanged.
  • Several team members pointed out that the timeline was unrealistic.
  • The client requested that the revised proposal be submitted by Monday.

docs.title: Analytical / academic tone
docs.text:
• The author argues that the current framework is insufficient.
  • Recent studies have shown that the effect may be overstated.
  • It has been suggested that the results were influenced by external factors.

docs.title: News-style reporting
docs.text:
• Officials confirmed that an investigation was under way.
  • Witnesses reported seeing smoke shortly before the explosion.
  • The company denied having breached any regulations.

A C1, reported speech è anche una competenza di genre awareness.

docs.title: 🎧 Reading
docs.text: In the briefing, the operations manager acknowledged that the rollout had taken longer than expected but insisted that the delays had prevented more serious problems later on. She also pointed out that several assumptions had been made too early and advised the regional teams to review the implementation schedule before committing to any new deadlines.

docs.title: Analyse the discourse
docs.text:
1. Which verb shows openness about a problem?
  2. Which verb shows a defensive or assertive stance?
  3. Which phrase suggests criticism without sounding aggressive?
  4. Which verb introduces practical advice?

docs.title: Task 1 — Recount a difficult conversation
docs.text:
Racconta una conversazione complessa avuta al lavoro, all’università o in un contesto personale.

Devi includere:

  • almeno 6 reporting verbs diversi
  • almeno 1 question report
  • almeno 1 request / order / advice report
  • almeno 1 example of selective reporting

docs.title: Task 2 — Meeting summary
docs.text:
Immagina di dover spiegare a un collega assente cosa è successo in una riunione.

Obiettivo:

  • non ripetere tutto
  • organizzare le informazioni
  • segnalare accordi, obiezioni, suggerimenti e decisioni

Useful frames:

  • She made it clear that...
  • He pointed out that...
  • They questioned whether...
  • The team agreed that...
  • She warned us not to...
  • He denied that...
  • They proposed...

docs.title: ✍️ Writing
docs.text: Scrivi un testo di 180–220 parole.

docs.title: Prompt
docs.text:
Write a formal summary of a meeting, interview, discussion, or disagreement.

Your text should:

  • report information accurately
  • include a range of reporting verbs
  • show clear control of time reference
  • distinguish between facts, opinions, concerns, and recommendations
  • sound natural and professionally structured

docs.title: Success criteria
docs.text:
Include at least:

  • 8 different reporting structures
  • 1 example of no backshift with a clear reason
  • 1 reported question
  • 1 reported request / order / suggestion
  • 1 example where you compress long direct speech into a concise summary

docs.title: ⚠️ Error Clinic
docs.text: Errore Correzione Nota He said me that... He told me that... tell richiede un oggetto She suggested to wait She suggested waiting suggest + -ing He asked where did I go He asked where I had gone niente inversione interrogativa He said that he will come He said that he would come backshift standard She told that the plan was risky She said that the plan was risky / She told us that... tell richiede un destinatario He explained me the problem He explained the problem to me pattern del verbo They insisted to leave early They insisted on leaving early / insisted that we should leave early struttura diversa

docs.title: Subtle C1 pitfalls
docs.text:
1. usare un reporting verb troppo forte o troppo debole rispetto al contesto
  2. fare backshift anche quando il contenuto è chiaramente ancora vero
  3. non distinguere tra reporting meaning e reporting wording
  4. produrre sequenze troppo letterali, poco sintetiche e poco idiomatiche

docs.title: A. Choose the best reporting verb
docs.text:
1. He ______ that the figures had been checked twice. (explained / complained / denied)
  2. She ______ that she had overlooked the attachment. (admitted / promised / ordered)
  3. They ______ us not to share the draft outside the team. (warned / suggested / mentioned)
  4. He ______ whether the deadline could still be changed. (wondered / insisted / announced)

docs.title: B. Rewrite naturally
docs.text:
Transform without sounding literal.

  1. “I’m not saying the strategy is wrong, but I think we should reconsider the timing.”
  2. “Can you send me the revised version by the end of the day?”
  3. “Don’t mention this to the client yet.”
  4. “We’ve been trying to solve the issue for weeks, but we still don’t have enough data.”

docs.title: C. Compress the message
docs.text:
Turn this into one strong reported sentence:

“I know everyone is under pressure, and I appreciate the effort, but unless we clarify responsibilities now, we’re going to run into exactly the same problem again next month.”

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text:
You attended a tense strategy meeting. Different people:

  • defended the current plan
  • questioned the timeline
  • admitted some errors
  • suggested alternatives
  • warned about risks
  • asked for more data
  • promised follow-up actions

docs.title: Your task
docs.text:
Write or say a clear, high-level summary of what happened.

To succeed, you must show:

  • precision in reporting verbs
  • control of time reference
  • good balance between detail and synthesis
  • awareness of stance and tone
  • language that sounds genuinely C1, not translated word by word

docs.title: 🎯 Final Insight
docs.text:
At lower levels, reported speech is mostly about grammar. At C1, it becomes a tool for:

  • reframing meaning
  • managing information
  • positioning yourself toward what others said
  • writing and speaking with analytical control

Advanced users do not merely repeat what was said. They decide how it should be represented.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità saprai:

  • collegare idee complesse in modo più chiaro e maturo
  • usare connettori diversi per addizione, contrasto, causa, risultato, concessione e conclusione
  • evitare ripetizioni e collegamenti troppo elementari come and / but / because / so
  • costruire paragrafi e interventi orali più coerenti
  • organizzare un ragionamento in inglese con maggiore controllo

Questa unità ti aiuta a fare un salto importante: non dire solo frasi corrette, ma costruire discorsi ben strutturati.

docs.title: 🧠 Il salto da B2 a C1
docs.text:
A livelli più bassi, molti studenti collegano le idee così:

  • I like the course because it is useful and it is practical.
  • It is difficult but it is interesting.

Queste frasi sono corrette, ma spesso risultano poco varie.

A livello C1, invece, inizi a gestire relazioni più precise tra le idee:

  • The course is demanding; however, it is extremely practical.
  • Although the material is complex, the explanations are clear.
  • The process took longer than expected. As a result, the launch was postponed.

Il punto non è usare parole “più difficili” a caso. Il punto è scegliere il connettore giusto per esprimere il rapporto logico corretto.

docs.title: 1. Addizione
docs.text:
Servono ad aggiungere un’idea.

  • moreover
  • furthermore
  • in addition
  • also
  • besides

docs.title: Esempi
docs.text:
• The proposal is realistic. Moreover, it is financially sustainable.
  • The course improves grammar. In addition, it helps students speak more naturally.
  • The task was challenging. Besides, we had very little time.

docs.title: 2. Contrasto
docs.text:
Servono a contrapporre due idee.

  • however
  • nevertheless
  • on the other hand
  • whereas
  • while

docs.title: Esempi
docs.text:
• The plan is ambitious. However, it may be too expensive.
  • The first option is cheaper. On the other hand, it is less flexible.
  • Some people prefer remote work, whereas others work better in an office.

docs.title: 3. Concessione
docs.text:
Servono a mostrare contrasto con un’idea che “ammetti” prima.

  • although
  • even though
  • though
  • despite
  • in spite of

docs.title: Esempi
docs.text:
• Although the schedule was tight, the team met the deadline.
  • Even though I was tired, I finished the report.
  • Despite the delay, the event was successful.
  • In spite of the criticism, the project continued.

docs.title: 4. Causa
docs.text:
Servono a spiegare perché qualcosa accade.

  • because
  • since
  • as
  • due to
  • owing to

docs.title: Esempi
docs.text:
• We changed the strategy because the first one was ineffective.
  • Since demand increased, the company hired more staff.
  • The flight was cancelled due to bad weather.

docs.title: 5. Risultato / conseguenza
docs.text:
Servono a mostrare l’effetto di una causa.

  • therefore
  • as a result
  • consequently
  • for this reason
  • so

docs.title: Esempi
docs.text:
• The data was incomplete; therefore, the analysis was limited.
  • He was not well prepared. As a result, the presentation was confusing.
  • The instructions were unclear. Consequently, several mistakes were made.

docs.title: 6. Conclusione / sintesi
docs.text:
Servono a chiudere un discorso.

  • in conclusion
  • to sum up
  • overall
  • all things considered

docs.title: Esempi
docs.text:
• In conclusion, the solution is effective but expensive.
  • Overall, the experience was positive.
  • To sum up, the advantages clearly outweigh the disadvantages.

docs.title: ⚠️ Connector o struttura grammaticale?
docs.text: Non tutti funzionano allo stesso modo.

docs.title: Connettori che introducono una frase completa
docs.text:
• although + clause
  • however + clause
  • therefore + clause

✅ Although the task was difficult, we completed it. ✅ The task was difficult. However, we completed it.

docs.title: Connettori che richiedono un nome o una forma in -ing
docs.text:
• despite + noun / -ing
  • in spite of + noun / -ing
  • due to + noun

✅ Despite the pressure, she remained calm. ✅ Despite working long hours, he stayed productive.

❌ Despite it was difficult...

Per quella frase devi usare:

✅ Although it was difficult...

docs.title: 1. Because + so
docs.text: ❌ Because it was late, so we left. ✅ Because it was late, we left. ✅ It was late, so we left.

docs.title: 2. Although + but
docs.text: ❌ Although it was expensive, but I bought it. ✅ Although it was expensive, I bought it. ✅ It was expensive, but I bought it.

docs.title: 3. Despite + frase completa
docs.text: ❌ Despite it was raining, we went out. ✅ Although it was raining, we went out. ✅ Despite the rain, we went out.

docs.title: 4. Ripetere sempre gli stessi connettori
docs.text:
Molti studenti usano quasi solo:

  • and
  • but
  • because
  • so

A livello C1 devi ampliare il repertorio.

docs.title: Base
docs.text: • I like the course because it is useful and it is practical.

docs.title: Più naturale
docs.text: • I like the course because it is useful. Moreover, it is highly practical.

docs.title: Base
docs.text: • It was difficult but I finished it.

docs.title: Più naturale
docs.text:
• Although it was difficult, I managed to finish it.
  • It was difficult; however, I still managed to finish it.

docs.title: Base
docs.text: • We didn’t have enough information, so the decision was delayed.

docs.title: Più naturale
docs.text:
• We did not have enough information. As a result, the decision was delayed.
  • The available information was insufficient; therefore, the decision was postponed.

docs.title: 🧱 Costruire un paragrafo ben collegato
docs.text:
Un paragrafo efficace spesso segue questa logica:

  1. idea principale
  2. sviluppo / spiegazione
  3. contrasto o limite
  4. conclusione

docs.title: Esempio
docs.text:
Learning English is increasingly important in both academic and professional contexts. In addition, it allows people to communicate more easily across cultures. However, reaching a high level requires time, patience and consistent practice. In conclusion, although the process can be demanding, the long-term benefits are considerable.

Osserva che ogni frase ha un rapporto logico chiaro con la precedente.

docs.title: 🎧 Reading focus
docs.text:
Leggi il testo e nota i connettori.

Many people believe that remote work is the future. In addition, companies often see it as a way to reduce costs. However, working remotely is not ideal for everyone, as some employees find collaboration more difficult online. Overall, the best solution may be a flexible model that combines both approaches.

docs.title: Domande
docs.text:
1. Quale connettore aggiunge un secondo vantaggio?
  2. Quale introduce un limite o contrasto?
  3. Quale espressione sintetizza la posizione finale del testo?

docs.title: 🗣️ Speaking task
docs.text:
Parla per 2–3 minuti su uno dei seguenti temi:

  • the advantages and disadvantages of studying online
  • working from home vs working in an office
  • why learning English is important today

docs.title: Devi usare almeno:
docs.text:
• 1 connettore di addizione
  • 1 di contrasto
  • 1 di causa o risultato
  • 1 conclusione

docs.title: ✍️ Writing task
docs.text:
Scrivi un testo di 150–180 parole su questo tema:

Is online learning better than traditional classroom learning?

docs.title: Includi:
docs.text:
• un’introduzione con la tua idea principale
  • almeno un vantaggio
  • almeno uno svantaggio
  • una conclusione chiara
  • almeno 5 connectors diversi

docs.title: 🧪 Practice
docs.text:
Completa con un connettore adatto.

  1. The course is demanding. ____, it is extremely useful.
  2. ____ the price was high, many people bought the product.
  3. The instructions were unclear. ____, several mistakes were made.
  4. The company expanded quickly. ____, it opened two new offices.
  5. ____, the project was successful despite the difficulties.

docs.title: 🔄 Transformation
docs.text:
Riscrivi le frasi usando il connettore indicato.

  1. It was expensive but I bought it. (although)
  2. We missed the train, so we arrived late. (therefore)
  3. The plan is practical and it is affordable. (moreover)
  4. It rained, but the event continued. (however)
  5. The team was under pressure, but they finished on time. (despite / although)

docs.title: ✅ Review finale
docs.text:
Controlla se sai:

  • distinguere addizione, contrasto, causa, risultato, concessione e conclusione
  • usare correttamente although e however
  • non confondere despite con although
  • costruire frasi e paragrafi con relazioni logiche chiare
  • evitare ripetizioni e connettori troppo elementari

Se riesci a farlo con buona varietà e precisione, stai sviluppando una vera competenza da C1.

docs.title: 🎯 Lesson Goals
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • usare i connectors come strumenti di thought organisation, non come semplici parole da inserire tra due frasi
  • distinguere tra logical relation, rhetorical effect e register choice
  • collegare idee con maggiore precisione attraverso addition, contrast, concession, cause, result, reformulation, exemplification e conclusion
  • costruire paragrafi e interventi orali con una progressione argomentativa più matura
  • evitare sia l’eccesso di semplicità (and / but / because) sia l’uso artificiale di connettori “sofisticati” ma mal collocati
  • controllare il ritmo del discorso scritto e parlato in modo davvero coerente con il livello C1

Questa unità non riguarda solo il lessico. Riguarda il modo in cui strutturi il pensiero in inglese.

docs.title: 🧠 Beyond linking: connectors as discourse architecture
docs.text:
A livelli bassi, i connettori vengono spesso trattati come equivalenti decorativi:

  • and = aggiunta
  • but = contrasto
  • because = causa
  • so = risultato

A livello C1, però, un buon connettore fa molto di più:

  1. segnala che tipo di rapporto logico c’è tra le idee
  2. aiuta il lettore o l’ascoltatore a seguire il ragionamento
  3. definisce il tono del discorso
  4. contribuisce alla densità argomentativa del testo
  5. mostra controllo di register e rhetorical stance

Confronta:

  • The proposal is interesting, but it is risky.
  • The proposal is interesting; however, it carries significant risks.
  • Although the proposal is interesting, it carries significant risks.
  • The proposal is interesting. Nevertheless, its implementation would involve substantial risk.

Le frasi sono simili nel contenuto, ma non nell’effetto. Cambia:

  • la struttura del ragionamento
  • il grado di formalità
  • la forza del contrasto
  • il focus finale della frase

docs.title: 🧩 A more precise map of connector functions
1. Addition
docs.text: Non tutta l’addizione è uguale.

docs.title: Neutral addition
docs.text:
• also
  • too
  • as well

docs.title: More formal / written addition
docs.text:
• moreover
  • furthermore
  • in addition

docs.title: Additive reinforcement / side note
docs.text:
• besides
  • what is more

docs.title: Compare
docs.text:
• The solution is cheap. Also, it is easy to implement.
  • The solution is cost-effective. Moreover, it can be implemented rapidly.
  • I don’t think the timing is ideal. Besides, we still lack key information.

Moreover and furthermore often sound more formal and argumentative. Besides can sound slightly more subjective or dismissive.

docs.title: 2. Contrast
docs.text: Qui il punto non è solo “dire il contrario”, ma scegliere che tipo di contrasto vuoi creare.

docs.title: Simple contrast
docs.text:
• but
  • however

docs.title: Stronger / more formal contrast
docs.text:
• nevertheless
  • nonetheless

docs.title: Balanced comparison
docs.text:
• whereas
  • while
  • by contrast
  • on the other hand

docs.title: Compare
docs.text:
• The plan is ambitious, but it may be unrealistic.
  • The plan is ambitious; however, it may prove unrealistic.
  • The plan is ambitious. Nevertheless, it lacks operational clarity.
  • The first strategy focuses on speed, whereas the second prioritises long-term stability.

However is flexible and very common. Nevertheless / nonetheless often signal a stronger rhetorical push against the previous point. Whereas is especially useful for structured contrast.

docs.title: 3. Concession
docs.text:
La concessione è diversa dal semplice contrasto.

Quando usi un concessive connector, concedi un punto prima di introdurre la tua posizione principale.

  • although
  • even though
  • though
  • despite
  • in spite of
  • admittedly
  • granted

docs.title: Compare
docs.text:
• Although the plan is expensive, it may still be worth funding.
  • Despite the high cost, the plan may still be worth funding.
  • Admittedly, the process is time-consuming; however, the outcome justifies the effort.
  • Granted, the first version was easier to use, but the updated one is more secure.

A livello C1, admittedly e granted sono molto utili perché introducono concessione con una sfumatura argomentativa più matura.

docs.title: 4. Cause and reason
docs.text:
• because
  • since
  • as
  • given that
  • considering that
  • due to
  • owing to

docs.title: Nuance
docs.text:
• because = chiaro, diretto, neutro
  • since / as = spesso più discorsivi o meno “pesanti”
  • given that = più riflessivo / argomentativo
  • due to / owing to = nominal style, più comune in scrittura formale

docs.title: Compare
docs.text:
• We postponed the meeting because several people were unavailable.
  • Given that several people were unavailable, postponing the meeting seemed sensible.
  • The delay was caused due to a technical failure.

⚠️ Meglio:

  • The delay was caused by a technical failure.
  • The delay occurred due to a technical failure.

docs.title: 5. Result and consequence
docs.text:
• so
  • therefore
  • thus
  • as a result
  • consequently
  • for this reason
  • hence

docs.title: Nuance
docs.text:
• so = comune, parlato, diretto
  • therefore / thus / consequently = più formali e logici
  • as a result = molto chiaro, spesso naturale anche in testi semi-formali
  • hence = più marcato e più scritto

docs.title: Compare
docs.text:
• We ran out of time, so we postponed the discussion.
  • We ran out of time; therefore, the discussion was postponed.
  • The evidence was incomplete. As a result, no final decision was made.

docs.title: 6. Reformulation and clarification
docs.text:
Questa è una zona molto importante a livello C1. Non stai solo collegando idee: stai anche guidando l’interpretazione.

  • in other words
  • that is to say
  • to put it differently
  • more specifically
  • namely

docs.title: Esempi
docs.text:
• The strategy lacks internal coherence. In other words, the different parts do not support one another.
  • The company needs a more targeted approach; more specifically, it needs to define its core audience.
  • The proposal has one main weakness, namely its unrealistic timeline.

docs.title: 7. Exemplification
docs.text:
• for example
  • for instance
  • such as
  • to illustrate
  • in particular

Questi connettori servono a sostenere un’idea con un caso concreto. A C1 è importante non limitarsi a dare opinioni astratte.

docs.title: 8. Conclusion and synthesis
docs.text:
• in conclusion
  • to sum up
  • overall
  • all things considered
  • on balance
  • ultimately

docs.title: Nuance
docs.text:
• in conclusion = molto chiaro e scolastico
  • overall = sintetico e frequente
  • all things considered / on balance = valutazione ponderata
  • ultimately = conclusione con senso di punto finale o esito ultimo

docs.title: ⚠️ Connector choice is also register choice
docs.text:
Un errore comune è pensare che più un connettore suona “difficile”, meglio è.

Non è così.

docs.title: Compare
docs.text:
• I was tired, but I finished the task.
  • I was tired; nevertheless, I completed the task.

La seconda frase non è “sempre migliore”. È solo più formale e più marcata.

In una chat, in una riunione informale o in un racconto personale, una forma semplice può essere la scelta migliore. Nel writing argomentativo o professionale, invece, maggiore varietà e precisione sono spesso un vantaggio.

La vera competenza C1 non è usare sempre forme difficili. È scegliere what fits the communicative situation best.

docs.title: 🧠 Concession vs contrast: a core C1 distinction
docs.text: Molti studenti trattano however e although come quasi equivalenti. Non lo sono.

docs.title: Contrast
docs.text:
• The idea is attractive. However, it may not be feasible.

Qui hai due enunciati separati. Il contrasto arriva dopo.

docs.title: Concession
docs.text:
• Although the idea is attractive, it may not be feasible.

Qui la prima parte è “concessa” e la seconda ha più peso argomentativo.

La concessione è spesso più elegante quando vuoi dire:

  • I recognise this point, but my main position is still different.

Questa sfumatura è centrale in discussione, debate, essay e report writing.

docs.title: 🧪 Structural control: clause connectors vs phrase connectors
Clause connectors
docs.text:
Richiedono una frase completa:

  • although
  • because
  • since
  • whereas
  • while

✅ Although the process was slow, the results were impressive.

docs.title: Sentence adverbials / linking adverbials
docs.text:
Collegano frasi o enunciati:

  • however
  • therefore
  • moreover
  • nevertheless
  • consequently

✅ The process was slow. However, the results were impressive.

docs.title: Preposition-based connectors
docs.text:
Richiedono nome o -ing:

  • despite
  • in spite of
  • due to
  • because of

✅ Despite the delay, the launch went ahead. ✅ Despite working under pressure, the team remained focused.

❌ Despite the process was slow...

docs.title: Versione base
docs.text: • I like working remotely because it is flexible and it saves time.

docs.title: Upgrade 1
docs.text: • I like working remotely because it is flexible. In addition, it saves a considerable amount of time.

docs.title: Upgrade 2
docs.text: • Remote work offers considerable flexibility; moreover, it can reduce commuting time significantly.

docs.title: Upgrade 3
docs.text:
• Although remote work offers clear advantages, its effectiveness depends on the type of work involved.

Qui non stai solo sostituendo parole. Stai cambiando il discourse shape della frase.

docs.title: 🧱 Building a C1 paragraph
docs.text:
Un buon paragrafo argomentativo spesso segue questo schema:

  1. main claim
  2. support / development
  3. concession or limitation
  4. refined conclusion

docs.title: Example paragraph
docs.text:
Online learning has become increasingly popular in recent years, largely because it offers a level of flexibility that traditional classrooms often cannot provide. In addition, it allows students to access material from almost anywhere. That said, online environments are not equally effective for all learners, as some people struggle without direct interaction or external structure. On balance, online learning is highly valuable, provided that it is designed carefully and supported by clear guidance.

Osserva i passaggi:

  • affermazione
  • sviluppo
  • limite
  • conclusione ponderata

Questo è molto più forte di una sequenza piatta di frasi scollegate.

docs.title: 🎧 Reading and connector analysis
docs.text:
Leggi il testo e analizza la funzione di ciascun connettore.

Many organisations are moving towards hybrid work models because they want to combine flexibility with in-person collaboration. In addition, employees often report higher satisfaction when they have some control over their schedules. That said, hybrid systems can be difficult to manage effectively, especially when communication is inconsistent. Consequently, companies need clear expectations and reliable processes. Overall, hybrid work can be highly effective, provided that it is implemented thoughtfully.

docs.title: Analisi guidata
docs.text:
1. because → introduce una ragione
  2. In addition → aggiunge un secondo punto a sostegno
  3. That said → introduce un limite senza annullare ciò che precede
  4. Consequently → presenta una conseguenza logica
  5. Overall → sintetizza la valutazione finale

That said è particolarmente utile a livelli alti perché segnala una transizione più naturale e dialogica rispetto a un semplice however.

docs.title: 🗣️ Speaking task – structured reasoning
docs.text:
Parla per 3–4 minuti su uno dei seguenti temi:

  • Is remote work better than office work?
  • Should universities focus more on practical skills?
  • Is technology making communication easier or more superficial?

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
Nel tuo intervento devi includere:

  • 1 concessione
  • 1 contrasto
  • 1 causa
  • 1 risultato
  • 1 conclusione ponderata
  • almeno 6 connectors diversi

docs.title: Useful speaking connectors
docs.text:
• To begin with...
  • That said...
  • At the same time...
  • As a result...
  • On balance...
  • Ultimately...

docs.title: ✍️ Writing task – argumentative paragraph set
docs.text:
Scrivi un testo di 220–260 parole su questo tema:

Should companies prioritise flexibility over control?

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• introduzione con posizione chiara
  • sviluppo con almeno due argomenti
  • concessione reale
  • esempio concreto
  • conclusione valutativa, non banale
  • almeno 8 connectors diversi

docs.title: Focus di revisione
docs.text:
Controlla se:

  • i connettori sono vari ma naturali
  • ogni connettore esprime davvero il rapporto logico corretto
  • non hai usato connettori troppo “pesanti” in ogni frase
  • la progressione del testo è chiara
  • la conclusione non ripete soltanto l’introduzione

docs.title: 1. Double marking
docs.text: ❌ Although it was difficult, but we continued. ✅ Although it was difficult, we continued. ✅ It was difficult, but we continued.

docs.title: 2. Because / so overlap
docs.text: ❌ Because the data was incomplete, so we postponed the decision. ✅ Because the data was incomplete, we postponed the decision. ✅ The data was incomplete, so we postponed the decision.

docs.title: 3. Misusing despite
docs.text: ❌ Despite the system was slow, it worked. ✅ Although the system was slow, it worked. ✅ Despite the slow system, it worked.

docs.title: 4. Register mismatch
docs.text:
⚠️ In un essay o report, usare solo and / but / so può far sembrare il testo troppo elementare.

⚠️ In una conversazione informale, usare moreover o nevertheless in ogni frase può sembrare innaturale.

docs.title: 5. Connector overload
docs.text:
Un testo con troppi connettori marcati diventa artificiale.

C1 non significa “mettere un connettore in ogni frase”. Significa creare una progressione logica leggibile e controllata.

docs.title: 🔄 Transformation practice
docs.text:
Riscrivi le frasi in modo più maturo, mantenendo il significato.

  1. The proposal is interesting, but it is risky.
  2. I support the idea because it could improve efficiency.
  3. It was expensive, but many people still bought it.
  4. We did not have enough evidence, so we delayed the decision.
  5. It is useful and it is practical and it is easy to apply.

docs.title: Obiettivo
docs.text:
Usa almeno una volta:

  • however / nevertheless
  • although / despite
  • as a result / therefore
  • moreover / in addition

docs.title: 🧠 Final synthesis: from sentences to discourse
docs.text:
Il vero salto verso C1 è questo:

  • A2/B1 → colleghi frasi
  • B2 → organizzi paragrafi
  • C1 → controlli the internal logic of discourse

Un buon connettore:

  • non è un ornamento
  • non è una parola “da esame” inserita a forza
  • non sostituisce il contenuto

È un segnale preciso di come vuoi che l’altra persona interpreti il rapporto tra le idee.

Quando impari a usare bene i connectors, il tuo inglese diventa:

  • più chiaro
  • più credibile
  • più strutturato
  • più persuasivo
  • più vicino a un vero livello C1

docs.title: ✅ Self-check
docs.text:
Chiediti se sai:

  • distinguere contrasto da concessione
  • scegliere tra however, although, despite, therefore, moreover, overall in base alla funzione
  • controllare struttura grammaticale e punteggiatura
  • evitare doppioni come although + but o because + so
  • costruire un paragrafo con sviluppo logico e non solo con frasi messe una dopo l’altra
  • adattare il tipo di connettore al registro richiesto

Se riesci a farlo con precisione e naturalezza, stai sviluppando una competenza discorsiva autenticamente C1.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità saprai:

  • capire che cosa sono le collocations e perché contano davvero a livello avanzato
  • riconoscere combinazioni naturali di parole in contesti di lavoro, studio e vita quotidiana
  • evitare errori tipici da traduzione letterale dall’italiano
  • usare gruppi lessicali più credibili nel parlato e nello scritto
  • migliorare la naturalezza del tuo inglese senza complicarlo artificialmente

Questa unità ti aiuta a fare un salto importante: non basta conoscere molte parole. A livello C1 devi saperle combinare nel modo in cui un parlante esperto si aspetta.

docs.title: 🧠 Il salto da B2 a C1
docs.text:
A livello B2 spesso lo studente conosce il significato delle parole, ma le usa in combinazioni poco naturali:

  • do a decision
  • make a photo
  • strong rain
  • say me

Le parole, prese singolarmente, sembrano giuste. Il problema è che non vanno insieme.

A livello C1, invece, inizi a pensare in blocchi:

  • make a decision
  • take a photo
  • heavy rain
  • tell me

Il risultato è un inglese più fluido, più preciso e meno “tradotto”.

docs.title: 🧩 Che cos’è una collocation?
docs.text:
Una collocation è una combinazione di parole che tende a comparire insieme con alta frequenza.

Non è una regola matematica, ma una preferenza forte della lingua.

docs.title: Esempi semplici
docs.text:
• make a mistake
  • take responsibility
  • heavy traffic
  • strong argument
  • raise awareness
  • meet expectations

In teoria potresti usare altre parole. In pratica, alcune combinazioni suonano molto più naturali di altre.

docs.title: 🔎 I tipi più comuni di collocations
1. Verb + noun
docs.text:
• make a decision
  • take action
  • draw a conclusion
  • reach an agreement
  • gain experience

docs.title: 2. Adjective + noun
docs.text:
• strong evidence
  • major issue
  • key factor
  • heavy workload
  • serious problem

docs.title: 3. Verb + preposition
docs.text:
• depend on
  • focus on
  • deal with
  • cope with
  • belong to

docs.title: 4. Adverb + adjective
docs.text:
• highly unlikely
  • deeply concerned
  • widely available
  • perfectly clear
  • closely related

docs.title: 5. Fixed / semi-fixed expressions
docs.text:
• at the end of the day
  • in the long run
  • as far as I know
  • to some extent
  • by and large

docs.title: ⚠️ Errori tipici degli italiani
docs.text:
Errore comune Forma naturale do a decision make a decision make a photo take a photo do a mistake make a mistake say me tell me strong rain heavy rain big traffic heavy traffic do progress make progress arrive to a conclusion reach / draw a conclusion

Molti di questi errori nascono perché in italiano usiamo verbi o aggettivi diversi, ma in inglese la combinazione standard è un’altra.

docs.title: 🧠 Collocations utili per lavoro e studio
Work / business
docs.text:
• meet a deadline
  • take responsibility
  • raise a concern
  • reach a target
  • give feedback
  • launch a product

docs.title: Study / learning
docs.text:
• take notes
  • do research
  • conduct a study
  • develop a skill
  • broaden your knowledge
  • gain confidence

docs.title: Discussion / argumentation
docs.text:
• express an opinion
  • make a point
  • support a claim
  • challenge an idea
  • raise a question
  • draw attention to

docs.title: Esempio 1
docs.text:
❌ I did an important decision last year.

✅ I made an important decision last year.

docs.title: Esempio 2
docs.text:
❌ The company did progress very fast.

✅ The company made rapid progress.

docs.title: Esempio 3
docs.text:
❌ We discussed about the problem and said our opinions.

✅ We discussed the problem and expressed our opinions.

docs.title: Esempio 4
docs.text:
❌ This created a strong rain of criticism.

✅ This led to heavy criticism.

docs.title: 🧠 Strategia giusta per impararle
docs.text:
Non studiare parole isolate.

Meglio imparare:

  • decision → make a decision
  • progress → make progress
  • awareness → raise awareness
  • responsibility → take responsibility

Quando trovi una parola nuova, chiediti sempre:

  1. con quale verbo compare spesso?
  2. con quale aggettivo compare spesso?
  3. in che contesto viene usata?
  4. è formale, neutra o colloquiale?

docs.title: 📘 Reading
docs.text:
Leggi il testo e nota le collocations.

The team made significant progress after taking immediate action. Instead of making excuses, they took responsibility for the delay and worked together to meet the deadline. In the end, they reached an agreement and managed to restore client confidence.

docs.title: Domande di comprensione
docs.text:
1. Which collocations describe improvement?
  2. Which collocations describe responsibility?
  3. Which collocation is connected to time pressure?

docs.title: 🗣️ Speaking
docs.text:
Rispondi oralmente usando almeno 5 collocations dalla lezione.

  1. Describe a difficult decision you had to make.
  2. Talk about a time when you made progress in something important.
  3. Describe a situation where someone took responsibility.
  4. Explain how people can gain confidence in English.

docs.title: Mini-model
docs.text: Last year I had to make a difficult decision about my job. At first, I was uncertain, but in the long run it was the right choice. It helped me gain experience and make progress much faster.

docs.title: Task 1
docs.text:
Completa le frasi con una collocation naturale.

  1. We need to __ action before the situation gets worse.
  2. She always __ responsibility for her mistakes.
  3. The team managed to __ the deadline.
  4. This report __ attention to an important issue.
  5. I want to __ more confidence when I speak English.

docs.title: Task 2
docs.text:
Scrivi un breve testo di 120–150 parole su uno di questi temi:

  • a decision that changed your life
  • a project where you made progress
  • a situation in which someone took responsibility

Usa almeno 8 collocations della lezione.

docs.title: ✅ Self-check
docs.text:
Controlla se sai:

  • distinguere una parola corretta da una combinazione naturale
  • riconoscere almeno 15 collocations utili
  • evitare 5 errori tipici da traduzione letterale
  • usare collocations in un mini-testo e in un breve speaking task

Se riesci a farlo con una certa naturalezza, l’unità è stata assimilata bene.

docs.title: 🎯 Lesson Goals
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • trattare le collocations come unità di significato e non come semplici parole accostate
  • distinguere tra inglese corretto e inglese idiomaticamente naturale
  • riconoscere e usare collocations ad alta frequenza in contesti professionali, accademici e argomentativi
  • evitare translation traps tipici degli italofoni, soprattutto nei pattern verb + noun, adjective + noun, adverb + adjective e verb + preposition
  • migliorare la qualità del tuo output orale e scritto attraverso lexical chunking e retrieval by pattern
  • costruire frasi più credibili, dense e fluide, coerenti con un vero livello C1

Questa unità non riguarda il “vocabolario avanzato” nel senso tradizionale. Riguarda il fatto che l’inglese naturale si costruisce per combinazioni preferenziali, non per parole isolate.

docs.title: 🧠 Beyond vocabulary: why collocations are a C1 marker
docs.text:
Molti studenti pensano che il salto verso C1 dipenda dall’imparare parole più difficili. In realtà, una parte decisiva del salto riguarda come le parole si combinano.

Confronta:

  • The company did progress after the new strategy.
  • The company made significant progress after the new strategy.

La prima frase è comprensibile. La seconda, però, mostra:

  • maggiore naturalezza
  • migliore controllo lessicale
  • maggiore probabilità di essere prodotta da un parlante competente

Le collocations sono quindi un indicatore forte di:

  1. fluency
  2. processing speed
  3. idiomaticity
  4. register awareness
  5. writer/speaker credibility

A livello C1 non basta “sapere una parola”. Devi sapere:

  • con quali verbi compare
  • con quali aggettivi compare
  • in quali registri è tipica
  • quali combinazioni suonano neutre, forti, formali o colloquiali

docs.title: 1. Verb + noun
docs.text: Questa è una delle famiglie più importanti perché influenza moltissimo la naturalezza percepita.

docs.title: High-frequency academic / professional patterns
docs.text:
• reach a conclusion
  • draw attention to
  • meet expectations
  • raise awareness
  • address an issue
  • pose a threat
  • play a role
  • gain insight
  • take into account
  • launch an initiative

docs.title: Compare
docs.text:
• We need to address the issue immediately.
  • The report draws attention to several structural weaknesses.
  • The results did not meet expectations.
  • The campaign helped raise awareness.

docs.title: 2. Adjective + noun
docs.text:
Qui la differenza tra “corretto” e “naturale” è spesso sottilissima, ma molto visibile.

  • strong argument
  • significant improvement
  • substantial evidence
  • key factor
  • major concern
  • serious risk
  • heavy workload
  • mutual understanding
  • widespread belief
  • growing pressure

docs.title: Compare
docs.text:
• a strong argument
  • substantial evidence
  • a major concern

Molti studenti direbbero big concern o important evidence. Non sempre è grammaticalmente impossibile, ma spesso non è la scelta più naturale.

docs.title: 3. Adverb + adjective / participle
docs.text:
Questi pattern sono fondamentali per il parlato e lo scritto maturi.

  • highly effective
  • widely accepted
  • deeply concerned
  • strongly opposed
  • closely linked
  • entirely reasonable
  • perfectly understandable
  • largely based on
  • deeply rooted
  • firmly established

docs.title: Compare
docs.text:
• This approach is highly effective.
  • The two issues are closely linked.
  • Their concerns are perfectly understandable.

docs.title: 4. Verb + preposition / particle patterns
docs.text:
Qui l’errore tipico non è solo scegliere il verbo sbagliato, ma rompere un pattern fissato dall’uso.

  • depend on
  • result in
  • lead to
  • focus on
  • cope with
  • contribute to
  • stem from
  • engage in
  • deal with
  • account for

docs.title: Compare
docs.text:
• Several factors contributed to the decline.
  • The delay resulted in further complications.
  • The problem appears to stem from poor planning.

docs.title: ⚠️ Translation traps for Italian speakers
docs.text:
Molti errori collocazionali nascono perché l’italiano usa accoppiamenti diversi.

Trap Natural English make a photo take a photo do a decision make a decision do progress make progress say an opinion express an opinion do experience gain experience make a question ask a question strong rain heavy rain high possibility strong likelihood / high probability big difference significant / major difference arrive to a conclusion reach / draw a conclusion

Il problema non è solo “ricordare la forma giusta”. Il problema è allenare il cervello a recuperare il chunk corretto invece di tradurre parola per parola.

docs.title: 🧠 Collocations and register
docs.text: Le collocations cambiano anche in base al contesto.

docs.title: More neutral / everyday
docs.text:
• make a choice
  • have a problem
  • give advice
  • take a break

docs.title: More formal / academic / professional
docs.text:
• exercise judgment
  • encounter difficulties
  • provide guidance
  • conduct an analysis

docs.title: Compare
docs.text:
• We had a problem with the schedule.
  • We encountered scheduling difficulties during implementation.

Entrambe sono corrette. Ma non appartengono allo stesso registro.

A livello C1 devi poter scegliere in modo consapevole.

docs.title: 🔬 Collocation depth: not just one correct answer
docs.text: Una stessa idea può essere espressa con collocations diverse a seconda di intensità, formalità e focus.

docs.title: Example: “there was criticism”
docs.text:
• There was some criticism.
  • The decision attracted heavy criticism.
  • The proposal came under considerable criticism.
  • The measure was met with widespread criticism.

docs.title: Example: “improve”
docs.text:
• make some progress
  • make steady progress
  • make significant progress
  • show marked improvement

Questo è un punto davvero C1: non esiste solo “la risposta corretta”, ma una gamma di opzioni con effetti diversi.

docs.title: 🛠️ Upgrade lab: from acceptable to convincing
1. Basic
docs.text: We did a big effort and arrived to a good result.

docs.title: Better
docs.text: We made a major effort and achieved a good result.

docs.title: Stronger C1 version
docs.text: We made a substantial effort and achieved a highly positive outcome.

docs.title: 2. Basic
docs.text: The meeting had many problems and created confusion.

docs.title: Better
docs.text: The meeting involved several problems and caused confusion.

docs.title: Stronger C1 version
docs.text: The meeting was marked by several issues and led to considerable confusion.

docs.title: 3. Basic
docs.text: This report says important things about the situation.

docs.title: Better
docs.text: This report highlights important aspects of the situation.

docs.title: Stronger C1 version
docs.text: This report draws attention to several key aspects of the situation.

docs.title: 📘 Reading analysis
docs.text:
Leggi il testo e individua le collocations.

The investigation drew attention to a number of structural weaknesses that had previously gone unnoticed. Although the organisation had made some progress, it had failed to address several key issues. As a result, growing pressure from stakeholders led to a strategic review. The final report raised serious concerns but also offered practical recommendations that could help restore public confidence.

docs.title: Individua nel testo:
docs.text:
1. una collocation con attention
  2. una collocation con key
  3. una collocation con pressure
  4. una collocation con raise
  5. una collocation con restore

docs.title: Reflection
docs.text:
Quali di queste useresti in:

  • una presentazione professionale?
  • un essay?
  • una riunione?

docs.title: Drill 1 – Personal experience
docs.text:
Parla per 90 secondi su una situazione in cui hai:

  • made progress
  • faced a challenge
  • took responsibility
  • reached a decision

docs.title: Drill 2 – Work / study discussion
docs.text:
Rispondi a queste domande usando collocations naturali:

  1. What factors contribute to successful teamwork?
  2. How can a company build public trust?
  3. What usually leads to poor communication in teams?
  4. Why do some projects fail to meet expectations?

docs.title: Drill 3 – Opinion and argument
docs.text:
Sostieni questa tesi per 2 minuti:

Strong communication skills play a key role in career development.

Usa almeno 6 collocations della lezione.

docs.title: Task 1 – Transformation
docs.text:
Riscrivi le frasi usando una collocation più naturale.

  1. The company did progress after the change.
  2. She said her opinion very clearly.
  3. The article gave attention to a serious issue.
  4. We arrived to a conclusion after the discussion.
  5. The reform created a big criticism.

docs.title: Task 2 – Controlled writing
docs.text:
Scrivi un testo di 170–220 parole su uno dei temi seguenti:

  • a difficult decision with long-term consequences
  • a project that failed to meet expectations
  • a problem that required immediate action
  • an issue that deserves greater public awareness

Vincoli:

  • usa almeno 10 collocations della lezione
  • includi almeno 3 adjective + noun patterns
  • includi almeno 2 verb + preposition patterns
  • evita formulazioni da traduzione letterale

docs.title: 🧪 Error clinic
docs.text:
Correggi gli errori.

  1. We did an important decision last month.
  2. The campaign made awareness about the issue.
  3. The plan received strong criticism because it didn’t meet the expectations.
  4. This problem depends from poor communication.
  5. The report says attention to several important points.
  6. She made a research before taking the decision.
  7. The pressure became always more strong.
  8. We need to do progress quickly.

docs.title: ✅ Self-assessment
docs.text:
Controlla se sai:

  • distinguere chiaramente tra parola corretta e combinazione naturale
  • riconoscere collocations tipiche di contesto professionale e accademico
  • evitare almeno 10 translation traps frequenti
  • migliorare una frase “accettabile” rendendola più idiomatica
  • usare collocations in modo spontaneo nel parlato e nello scritto
  • scegliere collocations coerenti con il registro

Se riesci a farlo con continuità, questa unità ti sta spostando davvero verso un inglese di livello C1.

docs.title: 🎯 Obiettivo
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità saprai:

  • riconoscere phrasal verbs avanzati molto frequenti in inglese naturale
  • distinguere tra significato letterale e significato figurato
  • evitare traduzioni parola per parola che portano a errori o frasi poco naturali
  • usare phrasal verbs utili in contesti di lavoro, studio, relazioni e problem solving
  • capire quando un phrasal verb suona più naturale di un verbo “singolo” più formale

A questo livello il problema non è più conoscere il verbo look, take, put o come. Il vero salto sta nel capire che, insieme a una particella, questi verbi possono cambiare completamente significato.

docs.title: 🧠 Il salto da B2 a C1
docs.text:
A livello B2 lo studente spesso conosce molti phrasal verbs di base:

  • get up
  • sit down
  • turn on
  • look for

A livello C1, invece, devi saper gestire phrasal verbs che:

  • hanno un significato meno trasparente
  • cambiano significato in base al contesto
  • compaiono spesso in inglese parlato e scritto naturale
  • rendono il tuo inglese più idiomatico e meno scolastico

Confronta:

  • We continued with the project.
  • We carried on with the project.

Entrambe sono corrette. La seconda, però, ha un tono più naturale in molti contesti.

docs.title: 🧩 Che cos’è davvero un phrasal verb?
docs.text:
Un phrasal verb è formato da:

  • un verbo
  • una particella (ad esempio up, out, off, on, through, over)

docs.title: Esempi
docs.text:
• give up → smettere / arrendersi
  • run into → incontrare per caso
  • turn down → rifiutare / abbassare
  • carry out → eseguire
  • come across → imbattersi in / dare un’impressione

Il punto chiave è questo: il significato non è sempre la somma delle singole parole.

docs.title: Più trasparente
docs.text:
• sit down → sedersi
  • stand up → alzarsi in piedi

docs.title: Meno trasparente
docs.text:
• bring up a topic → introdurre un argomento
  • figure out a problem → trovare una soluzione / capire
  • put up with noise → tollerare il rumore
  • carry on → continuare

docs.title: Confronto
docs.text:
• The plane took off at 7.00. → significato letterale collegato al volo
  • Her career took off after that interview. → significato figurato: ha iniziato ad avere molto successo

A livello C1 devi imparare a leggere il contesto, non solo la parola.

docs.title: Work / study
docs.text:
• carry out a task / a study / an investigation
  • come up with an idea / a solution
  • deal with a problem / a complaint / a challenge
  • go through a process / a document / a difficult period
  • take on responsibility / a role / a project
  • hand in an assignment / a report

docs.title: Relationships / people
docs.text:
• get along with someone
  • fall out with someone
  • look up to someone
  • let someone down
  • reach out to someone

docs.title: Change / progress
docs.text:
• move forward
  • step down
  • take over
  • work out
  • sort out
  • phase out

docs.title: ⚠️ Multiple meanings: il punto davvero importante
docs.text: Molti phrasal verbs non hanno un solo significato.

docs.title: Take off
docs.text:
• The plane took off. → decollare
  • The business took off. → decollare in senso figurato / avere grande successo
  • Take off your jacket. → togliersi qualcosa

docs.title: Come across
docs.text:
• I came across an interesting article. → imbattersi in qualcosa
  • He comes across as confident. → dare un’impressione di sé

docs.title: Work out
docs.text:
• We need to work out a solution. → trovare / elaborare una soluzione
  • Everything worked out in the end. → finire bene
  • She works out three times a week. → allenarsi

Per questo non conviene memorizzare un phrasal verb con una sola traduzione secca.

docs.title: Separable
docs.text:
Puoi separare verbo e particella:

  • turn off the light
  • turn the light off

Con il pronome, la separazione è obbligatoria:

  • turn it off
  • ❌ turn off it

docs.title: Inseparable
docs.text:
Non puoi separare il phrasal verb:

  • look after a child
  • ❌ look a child after

docs.title: Alcuni esempi utili
docs.text: Phrasal verb Tipo turn down an offer separable put off the meeting separable look after a client inseparable run into a friend inseparable get over a problem inseparable

docs.title: ❌ Errori tipici degli italiani
docs.text:
Errore Forma naturale tradurre parola per parola imparare il significato nel contesto usare sempre un verbo latino-formale alternare con phrasal verbs naturali turn down the volume it turn it down look the child after look after the child pensare che il phrasal verb sia “slang” in realtà molti sono neutri e molto comuni

Molti studenti evitano i phrasal verbs perché sembrano “troppo colloquiali”. In realtà molti sono perfettamente normali anche in contesti professionali:

  • carry out an analysis
  • deal with complaints
  • follow up on a request
  • set up a meeting

docs.title: Esempi
docs.text:
• continue → carry on
  • discover by chance → come across
  • tolerate → put up with
  • postpone → put off
  • reject → turn down
  • investigate / examine → look into

Non sempre il phrasal verb sostituisce il verbo singolo in tutti i contesti, ma spesso rende il tuo inglese più naturale.

docs.title: Confronto
docs.text:
• We postponed the meeting.

  • We put off the meeting.

  • She rejected the proposal.

  • She turned down the proposal.

docs.title: 📘 Reading
docs.text:
Leggi il testo e individua i phrasal verbs.

Last month our team came up with a new proposal, but management turned it down at first. Instead of giving up, we worked out a better plan and carried on with the project. In the end, the idea took off and the company decided to roll it out across all departments.

docs.title: Domande di comprensione
docs.text:
1. Which phrasal verb means “develop an idea”?
  2. Which phrasal verb means “reject”?
  3. Which phrasal verb means “become successful”?
  4. Which phrasal verb means “introduce / implement widely”?

docs.title: 🗣️ Speaking
docs.text:
Rispondi oralmente usando almeno 5 phrasal verbs dalla lezione.

  1. Talk about a time when you had to deal with a difficult situation.
  2. Describe a project or idea that eventually worked out.
  3. Have you ever turned down an opportunity? Why?
  4. Is there someone you really look up to?
  5. What helps you move forward after a setback?

docs.title: ✍️ Writing
docs.text:
Scrivi un testo di 140–170 parole su uno di questi temi:

  • a difficult situation you had to deal with
  • an idea you came up with
  • a time when something did not work out at first, but later improved

Usa almeno:

  • 6 phrasal verbs
  • 2 phrasal verbs figurati
  • 2 collegamenti logici (for example: however, as a result, in the end)

docs.title: A. Completa con la particella corretta
docs.text:
1. We need to carry ___ the plan carefully.
  2. She turned ___ the offer because the salary was too low.
  3. I ran ___ an old colleague at the station.
  4. They came ___ with a brilliant solution.
  5. Please look ___ the issue before tomorrow.

docs.title: B. Scegli il significato corretto
docs.text:
1. put up with
  2. a. remove
  3. b. tolerate

  4. c. discover

  5. step down

  6. a. resign from a position
  7. b. increase quickly

  8. c. meet unexpectedly

  9. take over

  10. a. continue
  11. b. assume control
  12. c. postpone

docs.title: C. Trasforma usando un phrasal verb
docs.text:
1. continue → __
  2. reject → __
  3. tolerate → __
  4. discover by chance → __
  5. investigate → __

docs.title: ✅ Review
docs.text:
Controlla se ora sai:

  • distinguere significato letterale e figurato
  • riconoscere alcuni phrasal verbs ad alta frequenza di livello avanzato
  • evitare la traduzione parola per parola
  • usare phrasal verbs in speaking e writing in modo più naturale
  • capire che un phrasal verb può avere più significati

docs.title: 🚀 Mini challenge
docs.text:
Parla per 4–5 minuti oppure scrivi un testo breve in cui usi almeno questi phrasal verbs:

  • come up with
  • carry out
  • turn down
  • work out
  • put up with
  • move forward

Obiettivo: non inserirli a forza, ma usarli in modo coerente e naturale.

docs.title: 🎯 Lesson Goals
docs.text:
Alla fine di questa unità sarai in grado di:

  • trattare i phrasal verbs come unità lessicali complesse e non come combinazioni casuali di verbo + particella
  • distinguere tra significato letterale, metaforico, esteso e pragmatico
  • riconoscere phrasal verbs ad alta frequenza in contesti professionali, accademici e conversazionali avanzati
  • evitare translation traps tipici degli italofoni e capire quando un phrasal verb suona più naturale di un verbo monolexico più formale
  • gestire polysemy, register, separability, pronoun placement e figurative extension
  • produrre un inglese più idiomatico, fluido e credibile, coerente con un vero livello C1

Questa unità non serve a memorizzare una lista lunga di espressioni. Serve a capire come funzionano i phrasal verbs nel sistema dell’inglese reale.

docs.title: 🧠 Why phrasal verbs matter at C1
docs.text:
Molti studenti associano i phrasal verbs a un inglese “casual” o “slang”. È una semplificazione fuorviante.

In realtà, i phrasal verbs:

  • sono centralissimi nell’inglese parlato naturale
  • compaiono spesso anche nello scritto giornalistico, professionale e semi-formale
  • permettono di esprimere azione, valutazione, gestione del processo e relazione interpersonale in modo rapido e idiomatico
  • segnalano processing fluency e buona esposizione alla lingua autentica

Confronta:

  • The company rejected the proposal.

  • The company turned down the proposal.

  • We continued despite the setback.

  • We carried on despite the setback.

  • They investigated the issue.

  • They looked into the issue.

Le versioni con verbo singolo non sono sbagliate. Ma le versioni con phrasal verb spesso suonano più naturali, più idiomatiche o più vicine alla comunicazione reale.

A livello C1 il punto non è “preferire sempre il phrasal verb”. Il punto è scegliere consapevolmente.

docs.title: 🧩 Phrasal verbs as meaning units
docs.text:
Il modo meno efficace di studiare un phrasal verb è questo:

  • bring up = menzionare
  • turn down = rifiutare
  • work out = risolvere

Il problema è che questa strategia:

  • ignora il contesto
  • ignora i pattern sintattici
  • ignora il registro
  • ignora i significati multipli

Meglio pensare così:

docs.title: bring up
docs.text:
• bring up a topic / issue / concern
  • funzione: introdurre un punto in una conversazione
  • contesto: meeting, discussion, feedback, disagreement

docs.title: turn down
docs.text:
• turn down an offer / proposal / request / invitation
  • funzione: rifiutare
  • tono: molto naturale, neutro, comune

docs.title: work out
docs.text:
• work out a solution / a compromise / a budget
  • things worked out in the end
  • work out regularly

Qui vedi subito che un solo phrasal verb può attivare frame di significato diversi.

docs.title: 🔬 Polysemy: one phrasal verb, multiple meanings
docs.text: La polisemia è uno dei motivi principali per cui i phrasal verbs sembrano difficili.

docs.title: Take off
docs.text:
1. The plane took off at 6.30. → decollare
  2. Sales took off after the campaign. → crescere rapidamente / decollare in senso figurato
  3. Take off your shoes. → togliere

docs.title: Come across
docs.text:
1. I came across a fascinating article. → imbattersi in qualcosa
  2. She comes across as highly competent. → dare un’impressione di sé

docs.title: Pick up
docs.text:
1. I’ll pick you up at the station. → passare a prendere
  2. Business is starting to pick up. → migliorare / riprendersi
  3. He picked up a few phrases while traveling. → acquisire incidentalmente

docs.title: Put down
docs.text:
1. Put the bag down. → appoggiare
  2. He was constantly put down at work. → sminuire / umiliare verbalmente

Per gestire davvero i phrasal verbs a livello C1 devi allenare tre domande:

  1. Qual è il significato in questo contesto?
  2. Che tipo di frase accompagna di solito questo uso?
  3. Il tono è neutro, informale, professionale, figurato?

docs.title: 🧠 Figurative extension and conceptual meaning
docs.text: Molte particelle ricorrono con valori semantici abbastanza coerenti, anche se non assoluti.

docs.title: up
docs.text:
Spesso suggerisce:

  • completamento
  • aumento
  • emergere
  • costruzione

Esempi:

  • build up confidence / pressure / tension
  • set up a system / a meeting / a company
  • bring up a topic
  • use up resources

docs.title: out
docs.text:
Può suggerire:

  • emersione
  • distribuzione verso l’esterno
  • eliminazione
  • completamento dell’elaborazione

Esempi:

  • carry out a task
  • figure out a problem
  • rule out an option
  • point out a flaw
  • phase out an old system

docs.title: off
docs.text:
Spesso richiama:

  • separazione
  • rimozione
  • interruzione
  • distanza

Esempi:

  • put off a meeting
  • turn off a device
  • cut off communication
  • take off a jacket
  • write off a loss

Questi pattern non vanno trattati come regole rigide, ma aiutano a costruire intuizione lessicale.

docs.title: 🔁 Register and stylistic choice
docs.text: Non tutti i phrasal verbs hanno lo stesso effetto stilistico.

docs.title: More neutral / conversational
docs.text:
• put off a meeting
  • sort out a problem
  • look into an issue
  • turn down an offer

docs.title: More formal / Latinate alternatives
docs.text:
• postpone a meeting
  • resolve a problem
  • investigate an issue
  • reject an offer

docs.title: Compare the effect
docs.text:
• We had to put off the meeting.
  • We had to postpone the meeting.

La seconda è più formale e più “institutional”. La prima è estremamente naturale e molto comune anche in ambienti professionali.

A livello C1 il controllo vero sta nel saper scegliere tra:

  • phrasal verb idiomatico
  • verbo monolexico più formale
  • perifrasi neutra

in base a audience, genre, tone, distance.

docs.title: ⚠️ Separability, pronouns and syntax traps
docs.text: Uno dei problemi classici per gli italofoni non è il significato, ma la struttura.

docs.title: Separable phrasal verbs
docs.text:
• turn down the offer
  • turn the offer down
  • turn it down

Con i pronomi, la separazione è obbligatoria:

  • look the number up
  • look it up
  • ❌ look up it

docs.title: Inseparable phrasal verbs
docs.text:
• look after the children
  • run into an old friend
  • get over the disappointment
  • ❌ run an old friend into

docs.title: Three-word phrasal verbs
docs.text:
• put up with noise / delays / incompetence
  • come up with a proposal / a workaround / an idea
  • look forward to the event / the launch / the result
