docs.text: Questa sezione è divisa in 3 grandi chunk, ciascuno con una funzione diversa.

docs.title: Chunk 1 — Core Everyday Vocabulary
docs.text:
Lessico quotidiano, concreto e ad alta frequenza. È il blocco fondamentale per costruire basi solide da A1 ad A2, con estensioni utili verso B1.

Qui troverai soprattutto: - parole essenziali - significati chiari - esempi semplici - combinazioni frequenti - errori tipici da evitare

docs.title: Chunk 2 — Social, Study, Work, Communication Vocabulary
docs.text:
Lessico per studio, lavoro, comunicazione, tecnologia, collaborazione, opinioni e vita contemporanea. Questo chunk accompagna soprattutto il passaggio da A2/B1 a B2.

Qui troverai soprattutto: - lessico operativo e funzionale - collocations ad alta utilità - patterns per speaking e writing - lessico per email, meeting, problemi, decisioni e discussione

docs.title: Chunk 3 — Advanced Nuance, Precision, Idiomaticity
docs.text:
Lessico avanzato per C1–C2, centrato su precisione, sfumature, registro, stance, diplomazia, argomentazione e naturalezza alta.

Qui troverai soprattutto: - definizioni più fini - differenze tra near-synonyms - controllo del tono e del registro - lessico per analisi, argomentazione e scrittura avanzata

docs.title: Il formato cambia in base al livello
docs.text: Per rendere il vocabulary più utile, il formato non è identico in tutti i chunk.

docs.title: Nei blocchi A1–A2
docs.text:
Il lessico è presentato in modo molto chiaro e accessibile.

Di solito troverai: - word - significato - esempio semplice

Obiettivo: capire e ricordare il lessico essenziale con rapidità.

docs.title: Nei blocchi B1–B2
docs.text:
Il lessico non viene solo tradotto, ma mostrato anche nel suo uso più naturale.

Di solito troverai: - word / expression - meaning - common combination / collocation - example

Obiettivo: imparare a usare parole naturali in combinazioni naturali.

docs.title: Nei blocchi C1–C2
docs.text:
Il focus non è più solo sul significato base, ma sulla scelta lessicale più precisa.

Di solito troverai: - word - meaning / functional meaning - nuance / register - contrast with similar words - example in context

Obiettivo: sviluppare controllo, precisione, diplomazia, naturalezza e consapevolezza del registro.

docs.title: Modalità Base e Professional
docs.text: Questo sito offre due modalità di studio.

docs.title: Base
docs.text: Nella modalità Base trovi: - lessico fondamentale - significati chiari - combinazioni frequenti - esempi semplici - errori comuni da evitare

docs.title: Professional
docs.text: Nella modalità Professional trovi in più: - collocations più ricche - lexical precision - register awareness - pragmatic fit - nuance tra parole simili - alternative più naturali per speaking e writing - maggiore attenzione a tone, stance e idiomaticity

docs.title: Cosa cambia davvero in modalità Professional
docs.text:
La modalità Professional non aggiunge semplicemente “più parole”.

Aggiunge soprattutto:

  • differenze tra parole apparentemente simili
  • collocazioni ad alta frequenza e ad alta naturalezza
  • contrasto tra inglese corretto e inglese idiomaticamente credibile
  • attenzione a formalità, distanza, diplomazia e precisione
  • passaggio da vocabolario “tradotto” a vocabolario “pensato in inglese”

In altre parole, il lavoro lessicale diventa non solo quantitativo, ma anche qualitativo.

docs.title: Come studiare davvero il vocabolario
docs.text:
Per ottenere risultati reali, usa questa sequenza:

  1. leggi il lessico del tema;
  2. individua le combinazioni utili;
  3. nota gli errori tipici;
  4. crea esempi personali;
  5. prova a usare lo stesso lessico in speaking;
  6. ricicla le parole in writing;
  7. torna sul tema dopo qualche giorno per consolidarlo.

docs.title: Cosa NON fare
docs.text:
Evita di studiare il vocabolario in modo puramente passivo.

Non basta: - leggere liste di parole - tradurre mentalmente parola per parola - memorizzare equivalenze isolate senza contesto

Il lessico va studiato soprattutto come: - word + meaning - word + example - word + collocation - word + register - word + context

docs.title: Chunk 1 — Core Everyday Vocabulary
docs.text:
Lessico quotidiano, concreto, ad alta frequenza, utile per:

  • identità personale
  • famiglia
  • casa
  • routine
  • cibo
  • shopping
  • città
  • viaggi
  • salute
  • hobby
  • interazione sociale quotidiana

Formato prevalente: parola + significato + esempio semplice.

docs.title: Chunk 2 — Social, Study, Work, Communication Vocabulary
docs.text:
Lessico per:

  • studio
  • lavoro
  • collaborazione
  • tecnologia
  • email
  • messaggi
  • opinioni
  • problemi
  • decisioni
  • società e comunicazione contemporanea

Formato prevalente: parola/espressione + significato + collocation + example.

docs.title: Chunk 3 — Advanced Nuance, Precision, Idiomaticity
docs.text:
Lessico avanzato per:

  • precisione espressiva
  • argomentazione
  • gestione del tono
  • idiomaticità
  • controllo del registro
  • stance
  • naturalezza alta C1–C2

Formato prevalente: parola + funzione/significato + nuance + contrasto + esempio.

docs.title: Come ripassare questa sezione senza perderti
docs.text: Dato che questa reference è ampia, puoi usarla anche per cicli di revisione.

docs.title: Revisione breve
docs.text: Scegli un solo tema e lavora su: - core vocabulary - useful combinations - due o tre errori tipici

docs.title: Revisione intermedia
docs.text: Scegli due temi e crea: - 10 frasi - 5 domande di speaking - 1 mini testo

docs.title: Revisione avanzata
docs.text: Scegli un tema da un chunk basso e uno da un chunk alto, poi prova a: - confrontare registro e precisione - sostituire parole generiche con parole più precise - trasformare un testo semplice in un testo più naturale

docs.title: Strategia Professional per usare davvero il vocabolario
docs.text: Per ottenere un salto qualitativo reale, prova a studiare ogni tema su 5 livelli:

docs.title: Level 1 — Recognition
docs.text: Capisci il significato delle parole e delle combinazioni.

docs.title: Level 2 — Controlled production
docs.text: Usi il lessico in frasi guidate.

docs.title: Level 3 — Contextual production
docs.text: Usi il lessico in mini-dialoghi o piccoli paragrafi.

docs.title: Level 4 — Flexible production
docs.text: Riformuli, sostituisci e adatti il lessico a contesti diversi.

docs.title: Level 5 — Pragmatic control
docs.text:
Scegli la parola o la formula più adatta in base a: - tono - distanza - precisione - contesto - intento comunicativo

Questo è il vero passaggio da vocabolario scolastico a vocabolario maturo.

docs.title: Self-check finale
docs.text:
Prova a rispondere a queste domande:

  1. Riesci a trovare rapidamente il lessico per un tema specifico?
  2. Sai distinguere tra parola corretta e parola naturale?
  3. Sai riconoscere una collocation forte?
  4. Sai evitare i false friends più comuni?
  5. Sai adattare il lessico al registro?
  6. Sai trasformare il vocabolario studiato in speaking e writing?

Se la risposta è “non sempre”, questa sezione non va solo letta: va usata, ripresa, annotata e riciclata.

docs.title: Conclusione
docs.text:
Questa sezione vocabulary è pensata come una grande mappa lessicale del corso.

Usala in parallelo con: - le unità dei livelli - la sezione grammar - la sezione pronunciation - la sezione exercises - la sezione tests

Il vocabolario non cresce davvero quando impari più parole isolate. Cresce davvero quando impari a usare meglio le parole insieme, nel contesto giusto, con il tono giusto e con il livello di precisione giusto.

docs.title: Chunk 1 — Core Everyday Vocabulary
docs.text:
Questo primo chunk raccoglie il lessico quotidiano fondamentale del corso, con una progressione che parte da A1–A2 e si estende gradualmente verso B1–B2. L'obiettivo non è solo imparare più parole, ma imparare a usare parole naturali in combinazioni naturali, con più controllo su significato, collocazioni, errori tipici e sfumature d’uso.

In questa versione rigenerata: - il core vocabulary è presentato in tabelle più leggibili; - il lessico base è accompagnato da significato ed esempio breve; - restano presenti useful combinations, common mistakes e i blocchi Professional; - la struttura è pensata per essere più utile in revisione, speaking e writing.

docs.title: 1. Personal Identity and Basic Personal Information
Core vocabulary
docs.text: Word / Expression Significato Example name nome My name is Anna. surname / last name cognome My surname is Rossi. first name nome di battesimo Laura is my first name. age età What's your age? date of birth data di nascita Please write your date of birth. nationality nazionalità His nationality is Italian. country paese Italy is my country. city città I live in a big city. address indirizzo What's your address? phone number numero di telefono Can I have your phone number? email address indirizzo email Please give me your email address. job lavoro / impiego My job is quite demanding. student studente / studentessa She is a university student. language lingua English is an international language. beginner principiante I'm a beginner in German. intermediate intermedio I'm at an intermediate level. advanced avanzato She has an advanced level of English.

docs.title: Useful combinations
docs.text:
• introduce yourself
  • spell your name
  • say your surname
  • give your email address
  • tell someone your age
  • mention your nationality
  • speak English
  • study languages
  • work as a teacher
  • live in Rome

docs.title: Typical sentence patterns
docs.text:
• My name is ...
  • I'm from ...
  • I live in ...
  • I'm ... years old.
  • I work as a ...
  • I study ...
  • I speak Italian and English.

docs.title: Common mistakes for Italian speakers
docs.text:
• I am 25 years → I am 25 years old
  • I live at Rome → I live in Rome
  • I work like engineer → I work as an engineer
  • I am married with → I am married to
  • I know to speak English → I can speak English / I know English

docs.title: Mini examples
docs.text:
• Hi, my name is Laura and I'm from Milan.
  • I work as a project manager and I speak English every day.
  • Could you spell your surname, please?

docs.title: Professional upgrade
docs.text: At higher levels, personal information becomes more nuanced: - background - profile - professional background - area of expertise - field - sector - role - position - responsibilities - qualifications

docs.title: Higher-value expressions
docs.text:
• I work in the education sector.
  • My role involves customer communication and project coordination.
  • I have a background in design and digital marketing.
  • My main area of expertise is language training.

docs.title: Nuance and register
docs.text:
• job = common, everyday
  • role = more professional
  • position = often formal / HR-like
  • career = long-term professional path
  • background = education + experience + profile

docs.title: Upgrade lab
docs.text: Basic: I am a teacher. More natural: I work as a teacher. More professional: I teach English to adult learners. More developed: I work as an English teacher, mainly with adult learners and professionals.

docs.title: Core vocabulary
docs.text: Word / Expression Significato Example family famiglia My family is very close. parents genitori My parents live nearby. mother / mum madre / mamma My mum works at home. father / dad padre / papà My dad loves cooking. brother fratello I have one brother. sister sorella My sister is younger than me. husband marito Her husband is a doctor. wife moglie His wife works in finance. partner partner / compagno-a My partner speaks French. boyfriend ragazzo / fidanzato She has a new boyfriend. girlfriend ragazza / fidanzata His girlfriend is from Spain. son figlio They have a teenage son. daughter figlia Their daughter is ten. child / children bambino/a; figlio/a; bambini/e They have two children. relative parente We visited some relatives. cousin cugino/a My cousin lives abroad. uncle zio My uncle lives in Naples. aunt zia My aunt is very kind. nephew nipote (maschio) My nephew is five. niece nipote (femmina) My niece is at school. single single She's single at the moment. married sposato/a I'm married. divorced divorziato/a He's divorced. engaged fidanzato/a ufficialmente They're engaged.

docs.title: Useful combinations
docs.text:
• have a brother / sister
  • get married
  • start a family
  • spend time with your family
  • be close to someone
  • get on well with someone
  • grow up in a small town
  • have children
  • raise children
  • support each other

docs.title: Typical sentence patterns
docs.text:
• I have two sisters.
  • I'm married and I have one child.
  • We get on very well.
  • I grew up in a small village.
  • My parents live near me.

docs.title: Common mistakes for Italian speakers
docs.text:
• I have 30 years → I am 30
  • I am married with Anna → I am married to Anna
  • I have a son of 10 years → I have a ten-year-old son / My son is ten
  • We are in four in family → There are four people in my family
  • I am an only child is correct, but do not say unique son for figlio unico

docs.title: Mini examples
docs.text:
• I have a younger brother and an older sister.
  • We don't live together, but we're very close.
  • She got married last year and now lives abroad.

docs.title: Professional upgrade
docs.text: Useful vocabulary for more mature descriptions: - sibling - household - upbringing - childhood - adolescence - adulthood - elderly relatives - caregiving - family responsibilities - work-life balance

docs.title: Higher-value expressions
docs.text:
• I come from a close-knit family.
  • My upbringing had a strong influence on my values.
  • Balancing work and family responsibilities can be challenging.
  • I help take care of an elderly relative.

docs.title: Nuance and lexical precision
docs.text:
• childhood = the period when you were a child
  • upbringing = the way you were raised
  • household = the people living in one home
  • relative = general family member
  • sibling = brother or sister

docs.title: Common translation traps
docs.text:
• education is not always educazione. In family contexts, educazione is often better translated as:
  • manners
  • the way someone was raised
  • upbringing

docs.title: 3. Home, Rooms, Furniture, and Everyday Objects
Core vocabulary
docs.text: Word / Expression Significato Example house casa We live in a small house. flat / apartment appartamento I rent a flat in the city centre. building edificio That building is very old. room stanza The room is quite bright. bedroom camera da letto My bedroom is upstairs. bathroom bagno The bathroom is next to the kitchen. kitchen cucina We usually eat in the kitchen. living room soggiorno The sofa is in the living room. dining room sala da pranzo We use the dining room on Sundays. hallway corridoio / ingresso Leave your shoes in the hallway. balcony balcone The flat has a small balcony. garden giardino They have a lovely garden. table tavolo The keys are on the table. chair sedia There are four chairs in the room. sofa divano I sat on the sofa. bed letto I need to make the bed. desk scrivania I work at my desk every day. wardrobe armadio My clothes are in the wardrobe. fridge frigorifero The milk is in the fridge. washing machine lavatrice The washing machine is in the bathroom. shower doccia I had a quick shower. keys chiavi I left my keys at home. charger caricatore My phone charger is missing.

docs.title: Useful combinations
docs.text:
• live in a flat
  • rent a house
  • move house
  • clean the kitchen
  • make the bed
  • do the washing
  • hang clothes
  • charge your phone
  • leave your keys at home
  • sit on the sofa
  • put something on the shelf
  • tidy your room

docs.title: Typical sentence patterns
docs.text:
• There is a desk in my bedroom.
  • There are two bathrooms in the house.
  • I left my keys on the table.
  • My flat is small but comfortable.
  • We moved house last summer.

docs.title: Common mistakes for Italian speakers
docs.text:
• In my house there is... → There is ... in my house often sounds better
  • I have forgotten the keys at home → I left my keys at home
  • I have the phone in charge → My phone is charging / I'm charging my phone
  • I live in a home → I live in a house / flat
  • I do the bed → I make the bed

docs.title: Mini examples
docs.text:
• My flat is on the third floor and it has a small balcony.
  • Could you put the bags in the kitchen?
  • I usually work at the desk in my room.

docs.title: Professional upgrade
docs.text: Vocabulary for more precise description: - spacious - cosy / cozy - bright - cluttered - tidy - messy - well-furnished - open-plan - storage space - neighbourhood / neighborhood - residential area - commute

docs.title: Useful higher-level combinations
docs.text:
• a bright open-plan kitchen
  • a quiet residential area
  • limited storage space
  • a long commute to work
  • a well-connected neighbourhood

docs.title: Nuance and natural use
docs.text:
• house = building / home type
  • home = emotional / personal concept
  • flat = British English
  • apartment = more common in American English
  • area is more neutral than zone in most everyday contexts

docs.title: Upgrade lab
docs.text: Basic: My house is big. Better: My flat is quite spacious. More natural: I live in a fairly spacious flat in a quiet area. More developed: I live in a fairly spacious flat in a quiet residential area, but the commute is quite long.

docs.title: Core vocabulary
docs.text: Word / Expression Significato Example morning mattina I study in the morning. afternoon pomeriggio We have class in the afternoon. evening sera I usually read in the evening. night notte I don't go out at night. weekday giorno feriale On weekdays I'm very busy. weekend fine settimana We relax at the weekend. today oggi I'm busy today. tomorrow domani I'll call you tomorrow. yesterday ieri I stayed at home yesterday. early presto I woke up early. late tardi Sorry, I'm late. always sempre I always have breakfast. usually di solito I usually go by bus. often spesso I often study at night. sometimes a volte Sometimes I cook dinner. rarely raramente I rarely watch TV. hardly ever quasi mai I hardly ever go out on Mondays. never mai I never drink coffee at night. before prima I study before dinner. after dopo I relax after work. during durante I answer emails during the day. while mentre I listen to music while I cook. wake up svegliarsi I wake up at seven. get up alzarsi I get up quickly. have breakfast fare colazione I have breakfast at home. go to bed andare a letto I go to bed early.

docs.title: Common mistakes for Italian speakers
docs.text:
• I make breakfast → for eating, use have breakfast
  • I go to the bed → I go to bed
  • I do sport → I do sport / I exercise
  • At the morning → In the morning
  • At night vs in the evening: they are not interchangeable

docs.title: 7. Town, Public Places, and Local Services
Core vocabulary
docs.text: Word / Expression Significato Example town città / paese I live in a small town. city città Rome is a busy city. village villaggio / paese piccolo He grew up in a village. street strada My office is on this street. road strada The road is very busy. square piazza Let's meet in the main square. area zona It's a quiet area. neighbourhood / neighborhood quartiere I like this neighbourhood. centre / center centro The café is in the city centre. station stazione The station is over there. bus stop fermata dell'autobus The bus stop is near the bank. post office ufficio postale I need to go to the post office. bank banca The bank closes at 4 p.m. hospital ospedale The hospital is quite far. pharmacy farmacia Is there a pharmacy near here? park parco We walk in the park on Sundays. museum museo The museum is free today. cinema cinema We went to the cinema last night. library biblioteca I borrowed a book from the library. police station stazione di polizia The police station is opposite the school.

docs.text: Word / Expression Significato Example body corpo The human body is complex. head testa My head hurts. eyes occhi My eyes are tired. ears orecchie My ears hurt on planes. nose naso My nose is blocked. mouth bocca Open your mouth, please. neck collo My neck is stiff. shoulder spalla I hurt my shoulder. arm braccio My arm is sore. hand mano Wash your hands. back schiena I have back pain. leg gamba My leg hurts when I run. knee ginocchio I injured my knee. foot / feet piede / piedi My feet are cold. stomach stomaco I have a stomach ache. health salute Health is important. illness malattia He recovered from his illness. pain dolore She is in a lot of pain. medicine medicina / farmaco Take your medicine after lunch. doctor medico I need to see a doctor. nurse infermiere/a The nurse was very helpful. patient paziente The patient is feeling better. appointment appuntamento I made a doctor's appointment. symptom sintomo What symptoms do you have? headache mal di testa I have a headache. cough tosse My cough is getting worse. cold raffreddore I think I've caught a cold. flu influenza He had the flu last week. sore throat mal di gola I've got a sore throat. back pain mal di schiena I often get back pain.

docs.text: Word / Expression Significato Example weather tempo meteorologico The weather is nice today. season stagione Spring is my favourite season. spring primavera Spring starts in March. summer estate It gets very hot in summer. autumn / fall autunno Autumn is often rainy here. winter inverno Winter can be very cold. sun sole The sun is out today. sunshine sole / luce del sole We had lots of sunshine. cloud nuvola There isn't a cloud in the sky. wind vento The wind is very strong. rain pioggia We had heavy rain yesterday. snow neve It snowed all night. storm tempesta A storm is coming. temperature temperatura The temperature is dropping. heat caldo intenso I can't stand the heat. cold freddo I don't like the cold. warm caldo / tiepido It's warm today. cool fresco The evening is cool. mild mite The weather is quite mild. humid umido / afoso It gets very humid in August. foggy nebbioso It's foggy this morning. icy ghiacciato / con ghiaccio The roads are icy.

docs.title: 13. Everyday Social Interaction and Small Talk
Core vocabulary
docs.text: Word / Expression Significato Example conversation conversazione We had a nice conversation. question domanda Can I ask you a question? answer risposta That's a good answer. opinion opinione What's your opinion? interest interesse We have similar interests. plan programma / piano Do you have plans for the weekend? invitation invito Thanks for the invitation. message messaggio I sent you a message. reply risposta I got your reply this morning. friend amico/a She's an old friend. neighbour / neighbor vicino/a di casa My neighbours are very friendly. colleague collega I met a colleague for coffee. classmate compagno/a di classe My classmates are nice. stranger sconosciuto I don't usually talk to strangers. guest ospite We had guests for dinner. host ospite / padrone di casa / organizzatore Our host was very welcoming. help aiuto Thanks for your help. favour / favor favore Can I ask you a favour? advice consiglio / consigli She gave me some useful advice. news notizie / novità I have some good news. update aggiornamento I'll send you an update later.

docs.title: Useful social verbs
docs.text:
• greet
  • introduce
  • invite
  • accept
  • refuse
  • suggest
  • offer
  • thank
  • apologise / apologize
  • ask for help
  • keep in touch
  • catch up
  • chat
  • mention
  • explain

docs.title: Useful combinations
docs.text:
• make small talk
  • ask a favour
  • give advice
  • keep in touch
  • catch up with a friend
  • thank someone for something
  • apologise for being late
  • invite someone over
  • make plans
  • have a quick chat
  • ask how someone is doing

docs.title: Common sentence patterns
docs.text:
• How have you been?
  • Do you fancy grabbing a coffee sometime?
  • Thanks for your help.
  • Sorry I'm late.
  • Let me know what you decide.
  • It was nice seeing you.

docs.title: Common mistakes for Italian speakers
docs.text:
• I propose you to go out → I suggest going out / I'd suggest going out
  • I ask you sorry → I'm sorry / I apologise
  • I give you an advice → I give you some advice / a piece of advice
  • I feel myself good → I feel good / I'm feeling good
  • I know him from years → I've known him for years

docs.title: Mini examples
docs.text:
• We had a quick chat after class.
  • Thanks again for your support yesterday.
  • Let's keep in touch after the course.

docs.title: Professional upgrade
docs.text: Social language with more nuance: - rapport - interaction - misunderstanding - clarification - follow-up - update someone on something - maintain contact - networking - informal exchange - smooth communication

docs.title: Useful combinations
docs.text:
• build rapport quickly
  • avoid misunderstandings
  • ask for clarification
  • follow up on a conversation
  • maintain regular contact
  • handle an interaction tactfully

docs.title: Nuance and register
docs.text:
• chat = informal conversation
  • conversation = neutral
  • discussion = more focused / substantial
  • exchange = often used in professional or analytical contexts
  • interaction = broader and more formal

docs.title: Core themes covered
docs.text:
• personal identity
  • family and relationships
  • home and objects
  • routine and time
  • food and eating out
  • shopping and money
  • town and local services
  • travel
  • health
  • leisure
  • clothes and appearance
  • weather
  • everyday social interaction

docs.title: What you should be able to do after this chunk
docs.text:
• introduce yourself naturally
  • describe your family and background
  • talk about your home and daily routine
  • order food, shop, and ask for basic services
  • ask for directions and describe places
  • talk about travel problems and plans
  • describe common health issues
  • speak about hobbies and free time
  • describe clothes, appearance, and weather
  • use more natural collocations instead of literal translations

docs.title: Quick self-check
docs.text: Try to speak or write for 1–2 minutes on each: 1. Introduce yourself and describe your background. 2. Describe your home and daily routine. 3. Talk about your eating habits and shopping habits. 4. Describe your area and how you usually get around. 5. Talk about a recent trip or a health problem you had. 6. Describe what you do in your free time and why you enjoy it. 7. Describe what you usually wear and what the weather is like where you live.

docs.title: Professional self-check
docs.text: At Professional level, you should also be able to: - upgrade basic vocabulary into more precise vocabulary - use collocations naturally - distinguish between neutral and more professional wording - avoid the most common literal translations from Italian - choose words based on tone, precision, and context

docs.title: Final upgrade challenge
docs.text:
Rewrite these basic sentences in a more natural and developed way:

  • I am a teacher and I live in a house near the city.
  • I am very busy every day and I eat out a lot.
  • I like travelling and I go often to other countries.
  • I had a cold and I stayed at home.
  • In my free time I like music and sport.

Try to improve: - lexical precision - collocations - sentence flow - register - naturalness

docs.title: Chunk 2 — Social, Study, Work, and Communication Vocabulary
docs.text:
Questo secondo chunk raccoglie il lessico funzionale e relazionale che diventa centrale da B1 a B2, ma che parte da bisogni molto concreti già presenti ad A2. Qui il focus non è solo “sapere più parole”, ma imparare a gestire situazioni reali: studiare, lavorare, collaborare, comunicare, spiegare problemi, prendere decisioni, discutere idee e muoversi in contesti contemporanei.

Il lessico è organizzato per aree d’uso, con una progressione interna: - core vocabulary per la base operativa - useful combinations per evitare traduzioni letterali - typical sentence patterns per lo speaking e il writing - common mistakes for Italian speakers - blocchi Professional per collocations, register, nuance, tone e lexical precision

In questa versione rigenerata, il Core vocabulary è stato reso più leggibile e più utile per lo studio attivo tramite tabelle con: - word / expression - significato in italiano - example

L’obiettivo non è solo capire il lessico, ma usarlo meglio in contesti reali.

docs.title: Core vocabulary
docs.text: Word / Expression Significato Example study studiare I study English every day. learn imparare Children learn quickly. lesson lezione Today's lesson was useful. class classe / lezione I have class at 6 p.m. course corso I'm taking an online course. unit unità We're revising Unit 3. level livello My level is B1. teacher insegnante The teacher explained it clearly. student studente Every student needs a notebook. classmate compagno di classe My classmate helped me. homework compiti I finished my homework. exercise esercizio This grammar exercise is difficult. task compito / consegna We have a listening task today. test test / verifica We had a vocabulary test yesterday. exam esame I'm studying for an exam. mark / grade voto She got a good grade. mistake errore I made a mistake in exercise 2. correction correzione The teacher gave us corrections. answer risposta Check your answers carefully. question domanda Can I ask a question? note appunto / nota I wrote a note in the margin. notebook quaderno I always bring a notebook. topic argomento Today's topic is travel. subject materia English is my favourite subject. skill abilità Listening is an important skill. progress progresso I'm making progress. method metodo I need a better study method. revision ripasso I usually do revision on Sundays. deadline scadenza The deadline is Friday. result risultato I'm happy with my result.

docs.title: Useful combinations
docs.text:
• attend a class
  • take a course
  • study for an exam
  • revise for a test
  • hand in homework
  • do an exercise
  • complete a task
  • make progress
  • improve your skills
  • take notes
  • check your answers
  • correct a mistake
  • miss a lesson
  • fall behind
  • catch up
  • pass an exam
  • fail a test
  • get a good mark
  • develop a study method

docs.title: Typical sentence patterns
docs.text:
• I'm studying for an exam next week.
  • I need to revise this unit again.
  • We had a vocabulary test yesterday.
  • I'm making good progress, but I still make mistakes with tenses.
  • Please hand in your homework by Friday.
  • I missed the lesson, so I need to catch up.

docs.title: Common mistakes for Italian speakers
docs.text:
• I give an exam → I take an exam / sit an exam
  • I do a mistake → I make a mistake
  • I lost the lesson → I missed the lesson
  • I read at university → I study at university
  • I have done an exam and I have taken 28 → I took an exam and got 28
  • I prepare an exam can work in some contexts, but usually study for an exam is more natural

docs.title: Mini examples
docs.text:
• I usually revise in the evening because I'm more focused then.
  • She passed the exam, but she wasn't happy with her grade.
  • I'm trying to improve my listening skills and expand my vocabulary.

docs.title: Professional upgrade
docs.text: At higher levels, study vocabulary becomes more precise: - assignment - assessment - submission - feedback - learning outcome - academic performance - self-study - study load - revision strategy - time management - concentration span - retention - academic writing - critical thinking - source - reference

docs.title: Higher-value expressions
docs.text:
• I'm working on improving my academic writing.
  • The feedback was useful, especially on structure and clarity.
  • I need a more effective revision strategy.
  • My main challenge is balancing study load and work.
  • The course focuses on both language accuracy and communicative effectiveness.

docs.title: Nuance and lexical precision
docs.text:
• study = general learning effort
  • learn = acquire knowledge or skill
  • revise = review what you studied before an exam
  • review = more general, often neutral or professional
  • mark = common in British English
  • grade = common in American English
  • feedback = comments on performance, not just correction

docs.title: Upgrade lab
docs.text: Basic: I study English. More natural: I'm studying English at an intermediate level. More developed: I'm focusing on speaking, vocabulary, and listening comprehension. More professional: I'm following a structured course and working on accuracy, fluency, and vocabulary range.

docs.title: Core vocabulary
docs.text: Word / Expression Significato Example work lavoro / lavorare I work in marketing. job lavoro / impiego She's looking for a new job. role ruolo My role involves customer support. career carriera He wants to build an international career. company azienda I work for a software company. office ufficio Our office is in the city centre. team team / squadra I work with an international team. colleague collega A colleague helped me with the report. boss capo My boss is supportive. manager manager / responsabile The manager approved the plan. employee dipendente Every employee received training. employer datore di lavoro My employer offers flexible hours. client cliente We work with international clients. customer cliente The customer asked for help. task compito I have three tasks to finish today. duty mansione / dovere One of my duties is checking orders. responsibility responsabilità I'm responsible for weekly reports. shift turno She works the evening shift. schedule programma / orario My schedule is very busy this week. meeting riunione We have a meeting every Monday. project progetto I'm working on a new project. report report / relazione I need to finish the report today. training formazione New staff receive training. contract contratto I signed a new contract. salary stipendio The salary is competitive. promotion promozione She got a promotion last year. experience esperienza I want to gain more experience. skill competenza / abilità Communication is an important skill. sector settore I work in the finance sector. industry industria / settore The tech industry changes quickly.

docs.title: Useful combinations
docs.text:
• work as a designer
  • work in marketing
  • work for a company
  • work with clients
  • have responsibility for something
  • manage a project
  • attend a meeting
  • write a report
  • meet a deadline
  • gain experience
  • change jobs
  • get promoted
  • apply for a position
  • do training
  • work full-time
  • work part-time
  • work remotely
  • solve a problem
  • support a colleague
  • deal with customers

docs.title: Typical sentence patterns
docs.text:
• I work in the finance sector.
  • My role involves customer support and project coordination.
  • We have a meeting every Monday morning.
  • I'm responsible for preparing weekly reports.
  • She's looking for a new job because she wants more flexibility.
  • I work with an international team, so I use English every day.

docs.title: Common mistakes for Italian speakers
docs.text:
• I make experience → I gain experience
  • I do smart working → I work remotely / I work from home
  • I have a work → I have a job
  • My work is stressful but I like my work → often better: My job is stressful, but I like what I do
  • I am responsible to manage → I am responsible for managing
  • I participate to a meeting → I participate in a meeting / attend a meeting

docs.title: Mini examples
docs.text:
• I work for a software company, mainly with international clients.
  • One of my main responsibilities is managing deadlines.
  • I'm thinking about changing jobs to find a better work-life balance.

docs.title: Professional upgrade
docs.text: Useful vocabulary for more developed work contexts: - workload - deliverable - stakeholder - alignment - objective - target - performance - output - workflow - coordination - supervision - accountability - leadership - initiative - onboarding - career path - role description - promotion cycle - strategic priority

docs.title: Higher-value expressions
docs.text:
• My workload has increased over the last few months.
  • We need better alignment across teams.
  • The project has clear objectives but limited resources.
  • One of my strengths is cross-functional coordination.
  • I'm looking for a role with more responsibility and long-term growth.

docs.title: Nuance and lexical precision
docs.text:
• job = specific employment
  • work = activity in general
  • role = function within an organisation
  • position = often formal or HR-related
  • career = long-term path
  • task = single piece of work
  • responsibility = area you are accountable for
  • duty = obligation, often formal or procedural

docs.title: Register notes
docs.text:
• boss is common but can sound informal.
  • manager is more neutral and professional.
  • staff is a collective noun, not normally pluralised as staffs in this meaning.
  • collaborator is often a false friend. In English, prefer:
  • colleague
  • coworker / co-worker
  • team member
  • collaborator only in specific academic/creative contexts

docs.title: Upgrade lab
docs.text: Basic: I work in an office. More natural: I work in an office and support the sales team. More developed: My role involves client communication and internal coordination. More professional: I work in operations, where I coordinate internal processes and support client-facing projects.

docs.title: 3. Office Life, Projects, Meetings, and Workflow
Core vocabulary
docs.text: Word / Expression Significato Example office ufficio The office is quiet today. desk scrivania My laptop is on my desk. laptop portatile I forgot my laptop at home. document documento Please review the document. file file I shared the file with the team. folder cartella Put it in the project folder. calendar calendario / agenda Check the calendar before booking. agenda ordine del giorno What's on the agenda today? meeting riunione We have a team meeting at 10. call chiamata / call Let's have a quick call later. video call videochiamata Join the video call at 3 p.m. presentation presentazione She's preparing the presentation. slide slide / diapositiva I've updated the last slide. update aggiornamento I'll send you an update later. task list lista di attività Check the task list first. deadline scadenza The deadline is very tight. priority priorità This is our top priority. progress avanzamento / progresso We're making steady progress. issue problema / questione We need to solve one issue. solution soluzione This could be a practical solution. delay ritardo There was a delay in approval. approval approvazione We need final approval. process processo The process is too slow. step passaggio / fase What's the next step? plan piano We need a clear plan. schedule pianificazione / tabella The project is on schedule. project progetto The project starts next week. team member membro del team Every team member received the update.

docs.title: Useful combinations
docs.text:
• schedule a meeting
  • join a call
  • share a document
  • send an update
  • discuss a project
  • set a deadline
  • change priorities
  • solve an issue
  • ask for approval
  • follow a process
  • keep track of progress
  • postpone a meeting
  • prepare a presentation
  • make a decision
  • review a document
  • assign a task
  • move something to next week
  • stay on schedule
  • fall behind schedule

docs.title: Typical sentence patterns
docs.text:
• Let's schedule a meeting for Thursday afternoon.
  • I'll send you an update by the end of the day.
  • We're slightly behind schedule, but we're still on track overall.
  • Could you share the latest version of the document?
  • We need approval before we move to the next step.
  • The main issue is that the deadline is too tight.

docs.title: Common mistakes for Italian speakers
docs.text:
• make a reunion → have a meeting
  • respect the deadline → meet the deadline
  • actual version → current version / latest version
  • control the progress → monitor the progress / keep track of progress
  • open the camera → turn on the camera
  • I close the call → I end the call / wrap up the call

docs.title: Mini examples
docs.text:
• Can we move the meeting to tomorrow morning?
  • I've updated the slide deck and shared it with the team.
  • We're waiting for final approval before launching the new page.

docs.title: Professional upgrade
docs.text: More developed project vocabulary: - milestone - blocker - dependency - follow-up - action point - decision-maker - scope - handover - implementation - rollout - ownership - escalation - bottleneck - timeline - resource allocation - status update

docs.title: Higher-value expressions
docs.text:
• We need to clarify ownership before moving forward.
  • The project is at risk because of a dependency on another team.
  • Let's capture the key action points before we wrap up.
  • The rollout has been delayed due to a technical issue.
  • We need to avoid unnecessary escalation and solve this directly.

docs.title: Nuance and lexical precision
docs.text:
• issue is usually more neutral and professional than problem
  • deadline = fixed final date
  • timeline = broader sequence of dates
  • delay = something happens later than planned
  • postpone = move to a later time intentionally
  • reschedule = change the time or date

docs.title: Meeting language upgrade
docs.text:
• Let's align on priorities.
  • Could you walk us through the latest update?
  • Just to clarify, who's responsible for the next step?
  • Before we close, let's confirm deadlines and ownership.

docs.title: 4. Technology, Digital Tools, and Online Communication
Core vocabulary
docs.text: Word / Expression Significato Example computer computer My computer is very slow today. laptop portatile I use my laptop for work. phone telefono I'll check it on my phone. tablet tablet She reads on her tablet. app app / applicazione This app is really useful. platform piattaforma We use a different platform now. account account I can't access my account. password password Reset your password first. login accesso / login My login isn't working. file file I uploaded the wrong file. attachment allegato Please open the attachment. link link / collegamento I'll send you the link. website sito web The website is down. browser browser Open it in another browser. screen schermo Share your screen, please. keyboard tastiera My keyboard isn't working. microphone microfono Turn on your microphone. camera videocamera My camera is off. connection connessione The connection is unstable. internet internet The internet is very slow. network rete The network is down. bug bug / errore software We found a bug in the app. update aggiornamento The system needs an update. setting impostazione Check your settings. notification notifica I got a notification. message messaggio I sent you a message. chat chat Write it in the chat. online meeting riunione online Join the online meeting now. screen sharing condivisione schermo Screen sharing isn't working. upload / download caricare / scaricare I'll upload the file now.

docs.title: Useful combinations
docs.text:
• log in to your account
  • reset your password
  • open a file
  • attach a document
  • share a link
  • update a page
  • check your settings
  • turn on your microphone
  • lose the connection
  • upload a file
  • download an attachment
  • send a message
  • reply in the chat
  • join an online meeting
  • share your screen
  • report a bug
  • fix an error
  • install an update

docs.title: Typical sentence patterns
docs.text:
• I can't log in to my account.
  • Please attach the file to the email.
  • My connection is unstable, so I may need to turn my camera off.
  • Could you share the link in the chat?
  • The app needs an update before it works properly.
  • I uploaded the wrong file by mistake.

docs.title: Common mistakes for Italian speakers
docs.text:
• I have problem of connection → I have a connection problem
  • Can you send me the invite? works, but often more natural: Can you send me the link / invitation?
  • I have blocked the microphone → My microphone isn't working / My microphone is muted
  • I cancel the meeting when you mean postpone → I postponed / rescheduled the meeting
  • I am in mute → I'm muted
  • Open the camera → Turn on the camera

docs.title: Mini examples
docs.text:
• I'll upload the revised file in a few minutes.
  • Sorry, I was muted and didn't hear the question.
  • The system crashed while I was sharing my screen.

docs.title: Professional upgrade
docs.text: More advanced digital communication vocabulary: - interface - access rights - permissions - integration - feature - functionality - outage - workaround - user experience - technical issue - troubleshooting - support ticket - sync - backup - data loss - deployment - dashboard

docs.title: Higher-value expressions
docs.text:
• We're experiencing a temporary outage.
  • As a workaround, you can use the browser version.
  • I don't seem to have the right permissions.
  • The new feature improves the user experience.
  • Please open a support ticket if the issue continues.

docs.title: Nuance and register
docs.text:
• bug = software defect
  • issue = general technical or process problem
  • error = can be human or technical
  • outage = service interruption
  • workaround = temporary practical solution
  • crash = system or app stops suddenly

docs.title: Core vocabulary
docs.text: Word / Expression Significato Example email email I sent you an email yesterday. message messaggio Did you read my message? subject line oggetto dell’email The subject line is too vague. attachment allegato The attachment is missing. reply risposta / rispondere Thanks for your reply. response risposta We need a quick response. request richiesta I received a formal request. reminder promemoria This is a quick reminder. confirmation conferma Please send confirmation by Friday. update aggiornamento I'll send you an update soon. invitation invito Thanks for the invitation. availability disponibilità Please confirm your availability. schedule agenda / calendario My schedule is full today. detail dettaglio Let me know the details. information informazioni I'm writing to ask for information. apology scusa / messaggio di scuse We sent an apology to the client. thanks ringraziamenti Many thanks for your help. greeting saluto iniziale Use a polite greeting. closing formula di chiusura The closing sounds too informal. signature firma Add your full signature. tone tono The tone is clear and polite. formal formale This email is quite formal. informal informale That's too informal for a client. polite educato / cortese Try to sound polite. urgent urgente This is an urgent request. follow-up sollecito / follow-up I'll send a follow-up tomorrow. clarification chiarimento We need some clarification.

docs.title: Useful combinations
docs.text:
• send an email
  • reply to a message
  • ask for information
  • confirm your availability
  • apologise for the delay
  • follow up on something
  • send a reminder
  • attach a file
  • clarify a point
  • thank someone for their help
  • ask for confirmation
  • check whether something is possible
  • get back to someone
  • keep someone updated
  • write a formal email
  • make a polite request

docs.title: Typical sentence patterns
docs.text:
• I'm writing to ask for some information.
  • Could you please confirm your availability for Friday?
  • Sorry for the late reply.
  • I'm following up on my previous message.
  • Please let me know if you need any further details.
  • Thank you in advance for your help.

docs.title: Common mistakes for Italian speakers
docs.text:
• I wait your reply → I look forward to your reply / I await your reply (formal)
  • I ask you kindly → better: Could you please...?
  • I write you for... → I'm writing to...
  • Thanks in advance for your answer → Thank you in advance for your reply
  • Dear Mr. Marco → use correct name format; often Dear Mr Rossi
  • Kind regards is fine, but avoid mixing very casual and very formal tone in the same email

docs.title: Mini examples
docs.text:
• I'm writing to follow up on our conversation last week.
  • Could you send me the updated document when you have a moment?
  • Thank you for your patience and sorry again for the inconvenience.

docs.title: Professional upgrade
docs.text: More advanced email and messaging vocabulary: - acknowledgment - availability window - rescheduling - clarification request - turnaround time - next steps - action required - escalation - courtesy copy - point of contact - misunderstanding - alignment - pending item - request for approval

docs.title: Higher-value expressions
docs.text:
• Just a quick follow-up on the email below.
  • Could you clarify what you mean by “final version”?
  • Please find attached the updated file.
  • Let me know how you'd like to proceed.
  • I'd appreciate it if you could confirm by tomorrow.
  • At this stage, no action is required from your side.

docs.title: Tone and register
docs.text:
• Can you send it today? = direct, neutral
  • Could you send it today, if possible? = softer
  • Would you mind sending it today? = polite, but context-sensitive
  • I'd appreciate it if you could send it today. = more formal / professional

docs.title: Upgrade lab
docs.text: Basic: Send me the file. Better: Could you send me the file, please? Professional: Could you please send me the latest version of the file when you have a moment? More developed: I'd appreciate it if you could send me the latest version today, as I need it for tomorrow's meeting.

docs.title: 6. Opinions, Agreement, Disagreement, and Discussion Language
Core vocabulary
docs.text: Word / Expression Significato Example opinion opinione In my opinion, it's a good idea. idea idea That's an interesting idea. point punto / osservazione That's a good point. argument argomento / tesi The argument is convincing. reason motivo / ragione What's the main reason? example esempio Let me give an example. discussion discussione / confronto We had a useful discussion. debate dibattito The debate was lively. agreement accordo We finally reached an agreement. disagreement disaccordo There was some disagreement. advantage vantaggio One advantage is the price. disadvantage svantaggio The main disadvantage is time. solution soluzione We need a better solution. perspective prospettiva From my perspective, it's risky. view punto di vista I understand your view. evidence prova / evidenza We need more evidence. fact fatto That's an important fact. claim affermazione / tesi The claim needs evidence. position posizione What's your position on this issue? topic argomento Let's change the topic. issue questione / problema This is a complex issue. point of view punto di vista From my point of view, it works.

docs.title: Useful combinations
docs.text:
• express an opinion
  • agree with someone
  • disagree with a point
  • support an argument
  • make a claim
  • give an example
  • raise an issue
  • discuss a topic
  • change your mind
  • see someone's point
  • make a distinction
  • compare two ideas
  • reach an agreement
  • solve a disagreement
  • challenge an assumption

docs.title: Typical sentence patterns
docs.text:
• In my opinion, this is the best option.
  • I agree with you up to a point.
  • I can see your point, but I have a different view.
  • One advantage is that it's cheaper.
  • The main issue is that we don't have enough time.
  • That's a good point, but I'm not fully convinced.

docs.title: Common mistakes for Italian speakers
docs.text:
• I am agree → I agree
  • I am not agree → I don't agree
  • According to me → In my opinion / From my point of view
  • I think differently to you → I think differently / I have a different opinion
  • I am contrary → I disagree / I'm against it
  • We discuss about this → We discuss this / talk about this

docs.title: Mini examples
docs.text:
• From my point of view, the plan is realistic.
  • I agree with the general idea, but not with the timing.
  • Let's look at both the advantages and the risks.

docs.title: Professional upgrade
docs.text: More advanced discussion vocabulary: - stance - assumption - implication - counterargument - rebuttal - trade-off - nuance - framework - perspective shift - strong claim - weak point - evidence-based argument - balanced view - line of reasoning

docs.title: Higher-value expressions
docs.text:
• I take your point, but I think the issue is more complex than that.
  • That argument sounds convincing at first, but it ignores a key factor.
  • There's a trade-off between speed and quality.
  • We need to distinguish between short-term results and long-term impact.
  • I'm not rejecting the idea outright, but I think it needs refinement.

docs.title: Nuance and register
docs.text:
• I disagree = clear, neutral
  • I'm not sure I agree = softer
  • I see where you're coming from, but... = natural, diplomatic
  • That may be true, however... = more formal / structured
  • I'm not convinced that... = analytical, controlled

docs.title: Core vocabulary
docs.text: Word / Expression Significato Example problem problema We have a small problem. issue questione / problema This issue needs attention. difficulty difficoltà We had some difficulties. challenge sfida / difficoltà Time is our main challenge. obstacle ostacolo Money is a major obstacle. delay ritardo There was a delay in delivery. error errore We found an error in the file. mistake errore I made a mistake. risk rischio This plan carries some risk. consequence conseguenza What are the consequences? option opzione We considered every option. choice scelta It's your choice. decision decisione We made a final decision. plan piano We need a clear plan. alternative alternativa Is there an alternative? solution soluzione We need a practical solution. result risultato The result was positive. improvement miglioramento We've seen some improvement. change cambiamento This change is necessary. progress progresso / avanzamento We're making progress. failure fallimento Failure can teach you a lot. success successo The launch was a success. pressure pressione I'm working under pressure. priority priorità Safety is the top priority. emergency emergenza It was an emergency. strategy strategia We need a better strategy.

docs.title: Useful combinations
docs.text:
• face a problem
  • solve an issue
  • make a decision
  • consider an option
  • find a solution
  • deal with pressure
  • reduce a risk
  • improve a process
  • make a change
  • change direction
  • avoid a mistake
  • learn from failure
  • take action
  • handle a situation
  • respond quickly
  • weigh the alternatives
  • make progress
  • cause a delay

docs.title: Typical sentence patterns
docs.text:
• We need to find a practical solution.
  • The main challenge is the lack of time.
  • We considered several options before making a decision.
  • This change should improve the overall process.
  • One small mistake can cause a big delay.
  • We had to act quickly because it was an emergency.

docs.title: Common mistakes for Italian speakers
docs.text:
• affront a problem → face a problem / deal with a problem
  • take a decision is possible in British English, but make a decision is safest and most frequent
  • actual problem → current problem / real problem
  • do a choice → make a choice
  • resolve the problem is correct, but solve the problem is often more natural in everyday English
  • change idea → change your mind

docs.title: Mini examples
docs.text:
• The problem isn't technical, it's organisational.
  • We need a short-term solution and a long-term strategy.
  • After discussing the alternatives, we made a final decision.

docs.title: Professional upgrade
docs.text: More advanced problem-solving vocabulary: - root cause - impact - constraint - mitigation - contingency plan - trade-off - corrective action - strategic shift - bottleneck - implementation risk - unintended consequence - priority conflict - decision-making process - resource limitation

docs.title: Higher-value expressions
docs.text:
• We need to identify the root cause, not just react to the symptoms.
  • The proposed solution may create new problems elsewhere.
  • There are budget constraints we need to take into account.
  • We should think in terms of impact, not just effort.
  • A contingency plan would reduce the overall risk.

docs.title: Nuance and lexical precision
docs.text:
• problem = neutral and broad
  • issue = often more professional or less emotionally loaded
  • challenge = can sound constructive or motivating
  • risk = possible negative future outcome
  • obstacle = something that blocks progress
  • constraint = limitation, often external or structural

docs.title: 8. Media, News, Culture, and Public Life
Core vocabulary
docs.text: Word / Expression Significato Example news notizie I read the news every morning. article articolo I read an interesting article. report rapporto / servizio This report is reliable. interview intervista I watched the interview online. headline titolo / titolo principale The headline was misleading. media media / mezzi di informazione Social media spread news quickly. newspaper giornale He still buys a newspaper. website sito web I found it on a news website. source fonte Check the source before sharing. information informazioni We need accurate information. fact fatto That's an important fact. opinion opinione That's just one opinion. rumour voce / diceria It was only a rumour. update aggiornamento We got an update this morning. event evento The event attracted a large audience. culture cultura The film reflects local culture. film film The film was very powerful. series serie I'm watching a new series. documentary documentario The documentary was informative. book libro The book became very popular. review recensione I read a review before buying it. audience pubblico The audience reacted strongly. trend tendenza This trend is growing fast. public pubblico The public response was mixed. society società The issue affects society deeply. campaign campagna The campaign raised awareness. influence influenzare / influenza Media can influence opinion. reaction reazione Her reaction surprised everyone.

docs.title: Useful combinations
docs.text:
• read an article
  • follow the news
  • watch a documentary
  • check a source
  • compare different reports
  • form an opinion
  • share information
  • influence public opinion
  • write a review
  • raise awareness
  • cover an event
  • report on something
  • attract attention
  • start a discussion
  • react to the news
  • shape a trend

docs.title: Typical sentence patterns
docs.text:
• I usually read the news online in the morning.
  • The article raises an interesting question about education.
  • It's important to check the source before sharing information.
  • The documentary had a strong impact on public opinion.
  • The series became popular because people related to the characters.
  • Media coverage can influence how people see an issue.

docs.title: Common mistakes for Italian speakers
docs.text:
• an information → information (uncountable)
  • a news → news (uncountable singular in grammar)
  • I saw a reportage → I watched a report / documentary
  • actuality for attualità is often wrong; use current affairs
  • argoment → topic / issue / subject (unless you mean a real argument)
  • I am informed on this → I'm informed about this / I know about this

docs.title: Mini examples
docs.text:
• This report is based on several reliable sources.
  • The film deals with social inequality in a very subtle way.
  • Public reaction was stronger than expected.

docs.title: Professional upgrade
docs.text: More advanced media and public discourse vocabulary: - coverage - narrative - framing - bias - public perception - misinformation - credibility - transparency - media landscape - audience engagement - discourse - representation - controversy - public debate - social impact

docs.title: Higher-value expressions
docs.text:
• The issue has received extensive media coverage.
  • The way the story was framed influenced public perception.
  • We need to distinguish between verified information and speculation.
  • The documentary contributes to a broader public debate.
  • The article adopts a critical but balanced tone.

docs.title: Nuance and lexical precision
docs.text:
• news = information about recent events
  • current affairs = more serious / public issues
  • coverage = how media report something
  • framing = how something is presented
  • bias = tendency toward a particular viewpoint
  • narrative = broader interpretive story or angle

docs.title: Core vocabulary
docs.text: Word / Expression Significato Example society società Society is changing quickly. community comunità The local community helped a lot. environment ambiente We need to protect the environment. pollution inquinamento Air pollution is getting worse. waste spreco / rifiuti We should reduce waste. climate clima Climate change is a global issue. energy energia The city is investing in clean energy. resource risorsa Water is a precious resource. habit abitudine Small habits can make a difference. behaviour comportamento We need to change behaviour. inequality disuguaglianza Inequality affects many communities. education istruzione Education should be accessible. healthcare sanità / assistenza sanitaria Healthcare is a major public issue. housing alloggi / casa Affordable housing is hard to find. public service servizio pubblico Public services need investment. technology tecnologia Technology changes the way we live. privacy privacy / riservatezza Online privacy matters. mobility mobilità Urban mobility is improving slowly. lifestyle stile di vita A healthy lifestyle matters. well-being benessere Work affects our well-being. responsibility responsabilità We all share responsibility. impact impatto This change has a positive impact. measure misura / provvedimento The measure was introduced last year. policy politica / linea d’azione The policy aims to reduce waste. support sostegno / supporto Families need more support. awareness consapevolezza The campaign raised awareness.

docs.title: Useful combinations
docs.text:
• protect the environment
  • reduce waste
  • save energy
  • raise awareness
  • improve public services
  • support local communities
  • change behaviour
  • have an impact on society
  • address inequality
  • invest in education
  • improve access to healthcare
  • develop a policy
  • encourage sustainable habits
  • respond to public needs
  • promote well-being

docs.title: Typical sentence patterns
docs.text:
• Small daily habits can have a positive impact on the environment.
  • Many cities are investing in public transport and cleaner energy.
  • This policy aims to improve access to education.
  • Technology has changed the way people work and communicate.
  • We need long-term solutions, not just short-term measures.
  • Raising awareness is important, but action matters too.

docs.title: Common mistakes for Italian speakers
docs.text:
• sensibilize people → raise awareness
  • respect the environment is understandable, but often better: protect the environment
  • the politics of a country when meaning government action → often policy / policies
  • society is changing quicklys → society is changing quickly
  • do differences → make a difference
  • environmental problematics → environmental issues / environmental challenges

docs.title: Mini examples
docs.text:
• More people are trying to reduce plastic waste in everyday life.
  • Access to affordable housing is a major issue in many cities.
  • Digital technology has created opportunities, but also new risks.

docs.title: Professional upgrade
docs.text: More advanced social vocabulary: - sustainability - inclusion - social cohesion - affordability - accessibility - digital divide - long-term impact - public trust - structural issue - policy response - behavioural change - collective responsibility - social pressure - unintended effects - civic engagement

docs.title: Higher-value expressions
docs.text:
• This is not just a personal issue but a structural one.
  • We need a policy response that addresses root causes.
  • Affordability remains a key concern for younger people.
  • The long-term impact may be more significant than the short-term gains.
  • There is growing pressure for more sustainable practices.

docs.title: Nuance and lexical precision
docs.text:
• issue is often better than problem in analytical contexts
  • policy = a plan or approach adopted by a government or organisation
  • politics = political activity, debate, power
  • sustainability = ability to continue without major harm or exhaustion
  • accessibility = how easy something is to reach or use
  • affordability = whether people can pay for it

docs.title: 10. Everyday Communication, Social Behaviour, and Small Talk
Core vocabulary
docs.text: Word / Expression Significato Example conversation conversazione We had a good conversation. chat chiacchierata / chat We had a quick chat after class. question domanda Ask a follow-up question. answer risposta That's a good answer. reply risposta Thanks for your reply. comment commento He made an interesting comment. greeting saluto Use a friendly greeting. introduction presentazione That was a polite introduction. invitation invito Thanks for the invitation. compliment complimento She gave me a nice compliment. apology scusa His apology sounded sincere. suggestion suggerimento That's a useful suggestion. request richiesta I have a small request. favour favore Can I ask you a favour? habit abitudine Small social habits matter. attitude atteggiamento She has a positive attitude. behaviour comportamento His behaviour was polite. politeness cortesia / educazione Politeness matters in English. confidence sicurezza Confidence helps in conversation. awkward silence silenzio imbarazzante Nobody likes awkward silence. misunderstanding malinteso It was just a misunderstanding. relationship relazione / rapporto Good communication builds relationships. impression impressione She made a great impression. interest interesse Show real interest in people. topic argomento Let's change the topic. joke battuta / scherzo He made a joke to relax the mood. opinion opinione What's your opinion on that?

docs.title: Useful combinations
docs.text:
• make small talk
  • start a conversation
  • keep a conversation going
  • ask a follow-up question
  • make a good impression
  • show interest
  • give a compliment
  • apologise for something
  • make a suggestion
  • ask for a favour
  • avoid awkward silence
  • handle a misunderstanding
  • change the subject
  • sound polite
  • keep in touch

docs.title: Typical sentence patterns
docs.text:
• How's your week going?
  • That's interesting — how did that happen?
  • Sorry, I didn't mean it like that.
  • Would you mind helping me with this?
  • It was nice talking to you.
  • We should keep in touch.

docs.title: Common mistakes for Italian speakers
docs.text:
• make a question → ask a question
  • I present you my friend → I'd like to introduce you to my friend
  • I excuse me → I apologise / Excuse me
  • How do you think about this? → What do you think about this?
  • I am boring when meaning “mi annoio” → I am bored
  • Sympathetic is not simpatico; often use nice, friendly, likeable

docs.title: Mini examples
docs.text:
• He made a very good impression because he was calm and friendly.
  • I wasn't sure what to say, so I asked a follow-up question.
  • There was a small misunderstanding, but we cleared it up quickly.

docs.title: Professional upgrade
docs.text: More advanced interaction vocabulary: - rapport - interpersonal skills - confidence without arrogance - conversational flow - turn-taking - tact - diplomacy - social awareness - active listening - follow-through - mixed signals - conversational repair - softening strategy - politeness marker - audience awareness

docs.title: Higher-value expressions
docs.text:
• Good communication depends on active listening as much as clear speaking.
  • She has strong interpersonal skills and builds rapport quickly.
  • A little tact can make difficult conversations much easier.
  • The key is to sound engaged without dominating the conversation.
  • When there's a misunderstanding, quick clarification helps restore trust.

docs.title: Nuance and register
docs.text:
• friendly = warm and positive
  • polite = respectful, maybe more distant
  • confident = self-assured
  • arrogant = too self-important
  • direct = clear, but can sound strong
  • tactful = careful not to offend

docs.title: Chunk 2 Review — From Functional Vocabulary to Social and Professional Use
docs.text: This chunk is designed to help learners move from general communication to functional, purposeful English. At this stage, vocabulary growth is not just about learning more words; it is about learning: - which words naturally go together - how different situations require different tones - how the same idea can sound casual, neutral, or professional - how to avoid literal translations from Italian

docs.title: Core learning goals
docs.text: By working through this chunk, the learner should become more confident in: - talking about study and work - describing roles and responsibilities - managing meetings, tasks, and digital tools - writing clearer emails and messages - expressing opinions and disagreeing diplomatically - discussing problems, decisions, and solutions - talking about media, society, and current issues - handling social interaction more naturally

docs.title: High-value vocabulary habits
docs.text: To make this chunk truly useful, the learner should: 1. study words in chunks, not in isolation 2. review collocations regularly 3. rewrite simple sentences in more natural English 4. note down recurring false friends 5. practise moving from basic to professional versions of the same message

docs.title: Professional upgrade checklist
docs.text: At the higher end of this chunk, learners should start noticing: - issue vs problem - role vs job - feedback vs correction - follow up vs simple ask again - current vs false-friend actual - raise awareness vs literal translations - meet a deadline vs respect a deadline - gain experience vs make experience

docs.title: Final upgrade challenge
docs.text:
Take one simple sentence from each area and upgrade it: - study - work - project communication - email writing - discussion - social behaviour

Example: Basic: I work in an office and send emails. Professional: I work in an office-based role and regularly communicate with clients by email. More advanced: My role is mainly office-based and involves frequent written communication with clients and internal teams.

docs.title: Chunk 3 — Advanced Nuance, Precision, and Idiomaticity
docs.text:
This chunk develops the higher-level vocabulary that helps learners move from strong intermediate or upper-intermediate English into genuinely advanced C1–C2 control. The focus is not simply on “more difficult words”, but on precision, register, stance, idiomaticity, discourse effect, framing, and lexical choice.

At this stage of learning, the main question is often no longer Do I know the word? but rather:

  • Which word is most accurate here?
  • Which choice sounds natural in this context?
  • Does this phrase sound neutral, formal, diplomatic, analytical, personal, or exaggerated?
  • Am I showing the right degree of certainty, caution, distance, or involvement?
  • Am I choosing vocabulary that fits the tone, relationship, purpose, and genre?

For that reason, this chunk is organised around themes that matter especially at advanced levels:

  • lexical precision
  • abstract and conceptual vocabulary
  • stance and certainty
  • register and tone
  • argumentation and critique
  • framing and reframing
  • nuance between near-synonyms
  • idiomaticity and polysemy
  • narrative voice and reflective language
  • the small discourse choices that make English sound native-like rather than translated

Use this chunk differently from a beginner word list. Do not try to memorise everything at once. Instead:

  1. choose one section
  2. learn the items in contrast pairs or functional groups
  3. reuse them in sentences, mini-paragraphs, discussion answers, or email fragments
  4. notice where a word sounds too strong, too vague, too formal, or too casual
  5. recycle the same items over time in speaking and writing

In Professional mode, the goal is not just range but control. That means learning to manage:

  • lexical nuance
  • pragmatic fit
  • degree of commitment
  • rhetorical effect
  • audience awareness
  • institutional and professional tone
  • discourse shaping through vocabulary choice

A very advanced learner is not the person who knows the most obscure words. It is the person who can choose the right word, in the right register, with the right force, in the right moment.

docs.title: Lexical Precision: Choosing the Right Word
Core vocabulary
docs.text: Word Significato Example issue questione / problema This raises an important issue. concern preoccupazione / aspetto delicato Cost is a major concern here. challenge sfida / difficoltà impegnativa The main challenge lies in timing. drawback svantaggio / limite negativo One drawback is the lack of flexibility. trade-off compromesso tra vantaggi e svantaggi There is a trade-off between speed and quality. approach approccio / metodo We need a more balanced approach. framework quadro concettuale / struttura This framework helps us analyse the topic. shift cambiamento / svolta There has been a clear shift in priorities. outcome esito / risultato finale One possible outcome is a delay. implication implicazione / conseguenza The decision has wider implications. claim affermazione da verificare That claim needs stronger evidence. assumption supposizione / presupposto The argument rests on a weak assumption.

docs.title: Useful combinations
docs.text:
• raise an issue
  • address a concern
  • pose a challenge
  • have a drawback
  • weigh the trade-offs
  • adopt an approach
  • work within a framework
  • mark a shift
  • anticipate an outcome
  • challenge a claim
  • support a claim
  • question an assumption

docs.title: Typical patterns
docs.text:
• This raises an important issue.
  • A more balanced approach would be to...
  • One possible outcome is that...
  • This has broader implications for...
  • The main challenge lies in...
  • It would be a mistake to assume that...

docs.title: Common mistakes for Italian speakers
docs.text:
• a strong problem → a serious issue / a major problem — in advanced English, nouns often change, not only adjectives
  • actual argument → current debate / actual argument depending on meaning — avoid confusion between actual and current
  • sensible in the sense of sensitive → sensitive — sensible means practical or reasonable
  • eventual meaning possible → possible / likely / eventual only if final — eventual does not mean eventuale

docs.title: Mini examples
docs.text:
• Calling everything a problem sounds flat; at higher levels, issue, concern, challenge, and drawback help you show precision.
  • In a professional discussion, What are the implications? sounds more analytical than What happens next?
  • Trade-off is useful when both options have value but neither is perfect.

docs.title: Professional upgrade
docs.text:
• Distinguish between near-synonyms by scope, seriousness, emotional load, and register.
  • Use issue when you want neutral professionalism; use problem when the difficulty is direct and concrete.
  • Use concern when the point involves risk, sensitivity, or caution.
  • Use challenge when the tone is constructive rather than negative.
  • Notice how lexical choice can make the same statement sound dramatic, neutral, diplomatic, or analytical.
  • At C1–C2 level, precision often matters more than complexity.

docs.title: Core vocabulary
docs.text: Word Significato Example awareness consapevolezza There is growing awareness that change is needed. insight intuizione / comprensione profonda The report offers useful insight into user behaviour. perception percezione Public perception has shifted. mindset mentalità / modo di pensare We need a more flexible mindset. accountability responsabilità effettiva Clear accountability is essential. credibility credibilità The speaker has a lot of credibility. coherence coerenza The essay lacks coherence in places. relevance rilevanza We should focus on relevance, not quantity. resilience resilienza / capacità di recupero The team showed resilience under pressure. constraint vincolo / limitazione Budget is our main constraint. capacity capacità We do not have the capacity to do that now. alignment allineamento / coerenza tra parti We need better alignment across teams.

docs.title: Useful combinations
docs.text:
• raise awareness
  • gain insight into
  • shape perception
  • adopt a mindset
  • ensure accountability
  • establish credibility
  • maintain coherence
  • demonstrate relevance
  • build resilience
  • operate under constraints
  • expand capacity
  • strengthen alignment

docs.title: Typical patterns
docs.text:
• There is growing awareness that...
  • This offers useful insight into...
  • Public perception of the issue has shifted.
  • The report lacks coherence in places.
  • We need greater alignment between strategy and execution.

docs.title: Common mistakes for Italian speakers
docs.text:
• the coherence is missing → the text lacks coherence — English often prefers verb-based phrasing
  • make awareness → raise awareness — the collocation is fixed
  • have more possibilities → have greater capacity / more options — choose the more exact noun
  • be under pression → be under pressure — false friend from Italian spelling

docs.title: Mini examples
docs.text:
• Credibility is not the same as authority: one is about being believed, the other is about status or legitimacy.
  • Constraint is often more precise than limit in analytical writing.
  • Alignment is common in business English, especially in meetings, planning, and project updates.

docs.title: Professional upgrade
docs.text:
• High-level English often depends on abstract nouns that let you discuss ideas rather than only actions.
  • Use these nouns carefully: abstract vocabulary is powerful, but overuse can make writing vague.
  • Strong C2 writing balances abstraction with concrete illustration.
  • Pair abstract nouns with verbs and examples: build credibility, maintain coherence, show relevance.

docs.title: Stance, Attitude, and Degree of Certainty
Core vocabulary
docs.text: Word Significato Example apparently apparentemente / a quanto pare Apparently, the plan has changed. arguably si può sostenere che / probabilmente in senso argomentabile It is arguably the best option. presumably presumibilmente They will presumably reply tomorrow. reportedly secondo quanto riportato The company reportedly changed direction. seemingly apparentemente Seemingly minor changes can matter a lot. largely in gran parte The situation is largely unchanged. broadly in termini generali / in senso ampio I broadly agree with that view. to some extent fino a un certo punto The criticism is justified to some extent. undeniably innegabilmente It is undeniably an important issue. conceivably concepibilmente / teoricamente possibile This could conceivably happen again. virtually praticamente / quasi The process is virtually complete.
