docs.title: Useful combinations
docs.text:
• seemingly simple
  • reportedly true
  • arguably the best
  • largely unchanged
  • broadly speaking
  • undeniably important
  • to some extent justified
  • virtually impossible

docs.title: Typical patterns
docs.text:
• It is arguably one of the most useful tools available.
  • The situation has remained largely unchanged.
  • The criticism is, to some extent, justified.
  • Seemingly small changes can have major effects.

docs.title: Common mistakes for Italian speakers
docs.text:
• surely as a formal hedge → arguably / presumably / apparently — surely often sounds rhetorical, not analytical
  • certainly maybe → possibly / conceivably — avoid contradictory degree markers
  • it is obvious for academic tone → it seems clear / the evidence suggests — C1–C2 writing usually avoids overclaiming
  • definitely in every serious claim → quite clearly / strongly / based on the evidence — absolute certainty often sounds weak if unsupported

docs.title: Mini examples
docs.text:
• Arguably is excellent when you want to sound strong but still intellectually open.
  • Reportedly protects you from fully endorsing a claim.
  • To some extent is useful when full agreement would be too strong.

docs.title: Professional upgrade
docs.text:
• Advanced English uses stance markers to manage commitment, caution, and intellectual honesty.
  • Being precise about certainty makes you sound more credible, not less confident.
  • At C2 level, control of epistemic stance is a major sign of maturity.
  • Use strong certainty only when evidence is strong; otherwise hedge with purpose, not fear.

docs.title: Core vocabulary
docs.text: Word Significato Register note request richiedere / richiesta more formal than ask assistance assistenza / aiuto more formal than help purchase acquistare / acquisto more formal than buy residence residenza / domicilio formal / institutional depart partire / andarsene more formal than leave obtain ottenere more formal than get require richiedere / necessitare more formal than need inform informare more formal than tell regarding riguardo a formal written English therefore pertanto formal logic / writing however tuttavia formal contrast marker furthermore inoltre formal addition marker

docs.title: Useful combinations
docs.text:
• submit a request
  • provide assistance
  • make a purchase
  • place of residence
  • depart from
  • obtain permission
  • require approval
  • inform someone of
  • regarding your question
  • therefore conclude that
  • however, this means...
  • furthermore, it suggests...

docs.title: Typical patterns
docs.text:
• I am writing to request further information.
  • Participants are required to register in advance.
  • Regarding your question, the answer depends on...
  • However, there are several limitations to this view.

docs.title: Common mistakes for Italian speakers
docs.text:
• assist to me → assist me / help me — assist is not followed by to
  • demanding tone in email → Could you... / Would it be possible to... — register is about relationship, not grammar only
  • inform me about with double preposition → inform me about / inform me of — choose one correct pattern
  • purchase in casual chat → buy — formal vocabulary can sound unnatural in everyday speech

docs.title: Mini examples
docs.text:
• Formal does not always mean better; sometimes it simply means more distant or less natural.
  • High-level learners need to move flexibly between registers, not stay permanently formal.
  • Many words are grammatically correct but pragmatically mismatched.

docs.title: Professional upgrade
docs.text:
• Practice rewriting the same idea in three registers: informal, neutral professional, and formal.
  • At C1–C2 level, appropriateness matters as much as lexical range.
  • Use formal lexis when the context justifies it; otherwise prefer precise, natural neutrality.

docs.title: Tone Management: Softening, Hedging, and Diplomacy
Core vocabulary
docs.text: Word Significato Example concern preoccupazione / riserva diplomatica I have some concerns about the timeline. reservation riserva / dubbio I have a few reservations about that plan. limitation limite This approach has some limitations. scope portata / ambito The scope of the project is unclear. feasible fattibile / realistico That may not be feasible right now. constructive costruttivo We need constructive feedback. sensitive delicato / sensibile This is a sensitive issue. premature prematuro It would be premature to decide now. misleading fuorviante That comparison is slightly misleading. overlook trascurare / non notare This argument overlooks a key point.

docs.title: Useful combinations
docs.text:
• express concern
  • raise a reservation
  • highlight a limitation
  • within the scope of
  • technically feasible
  • offer constructive feedback
  • handle a sensitive issue
  • premature conclusion
  • potentially misleading
  • easily overlooked

docs.title: Typical patterns
docs.text:
• I have a few reservations about the current plan.
  • This may be slightly misleading.
  • It would be premature to draw firm conclusions.
  • With respect, I think that overlooks a key issue.

docs.title: Common mistakes for Italian speakers
docs.text:
• You are wrong → I’m not sure that fully reflects the situation — softening is often more effective than blunt contradiction
  • This is impossible → This may not be feasible in its current form — diplomatic language keeps the conversation open
  • That makes no sense → I’m struggling to see how that would work in practice — tone matters in professional English
  • a sensible topic meaning delicate → a sensitive topic — false friend

docs.title: Mini examples
docs.text:
• May, might, perhaps, not entirely, and to some extent are essential for tactful disagreement.
  • Premature is powerful when you want to push back without sounding emotional.
  • Constructive helps frame criticism as useful rather than hostile.

docs.title: Professional upgrade
docs.text:
• Advanced English is often about managing relationships through language.
  • Softening is not weakness: it is a tool for precision, diplomacy, and strategic influence.
  • Compare: This is wrong vs This seems to rest on a questionable assumption.

docs.title: Core vocabulary
docs.text: Word Significato Example premise premessa The argument rests on a weak premise. reasoning ragionamento I’m not convinced by the reasoning. evidence prove / elementi a supporto There is little evidence to support that claim. counterargument controargomentazione A likely counterargument is that costs are too high. rebuttal confutazione / replica Her rebuttal was clear and persuasive. bias pregiudizio / distorsione The article shows clear bias. distinction distinzione We need to make a clear distinction here. interpretation interpretazione That is one possible interpretation. assessment valutazione ponderata The assessment seems fair. criterion criterio Cost is not the only criterion. justification giustificazione The decision lacks justification. consistency coerenza / costanza There is not enough consistency in the argument.

docs.title: Useful combinations
docs.text:
• rest on a premise
  • follow the reasoning
  • provide evidence
  • anticipate a counterargument
  • offer a rebuttal
  • show bias
  • draw a distinction
  • advance an interpretation
  • make an assessment
  • meet a criterion
  • require justification
  • maintain consistency

docs.title: Typical patterns
docs.text:
• The argument rests on a doubtful premise.
  • There is little evidence to support that claim.
  • We need to distinguish between correlation and causation.
  • A likely counterargument would be that...

docs.title: Common mistakes for Italian speakers
docs.text:
• motivation as reason in every context → reason / rationale / justification — motivation is about inner drive, not always logic
  • demonstration meaning protest → demonstration / protest depending context — be careful with double meaning
  • argumentation too vague → reasoning / line of argument / case — choose a more specific noun when possible
  • evidence singular and plural confusion → evidence (uncountable) — not evidences

docs.title: Mini examples
docs.text:
• Bias can be cognitive, ideological, institutional, or emotional.
  • Criterion becomes criteria in the plural.
  • Distinction is excellent in advanced essays: it shows analytical control.

docs.title: Professional upgrade
docs.text:
• High-level argumentation depends on vocabulary that lets you analyse thought, not just express opinion.
  • Use counterargument and rebuttal to build depth rather than one-sided certainty.
  • Strong C2 writing often moves from claim to evidence to limitation to refined conclusion.

docs.title: Core vocabulary
docs.text: Word Significato Example compelling convincente e coinvolgente The article makes a compelling case. insightful acuto / ricco di intuizioni It is an insightful analysis. superficial superficiale The overview feels superficial. coherent coerente The argument is coherent overall. flawed viziato da difetti importanti The conclusion is flawed. ambitious ambizioso It is an ambitious project. convincing persuasivo / convincente Her explanation was convincing. balanced equilibrato The review is balanced and fair. inconsistent incoerente / discontinuo The second part feels inconsistent. opaque opaco / poco chiaro Some sections are unnecessarily opaque. rigorous rigoroso The method is rigorous. overstated eccessivo / sovrastimato The final claim seems overstated.

docs.title: Useful combinations
docs.text:
• a compelling argument
  • an insightful overview
  • a superficial treatment
  • a coherent structure
  • a flawed assumption
  • an ambitious aim
  • a convincing explanation
  • a balanced assessment
  • an inconsistent tone
  • an opaque passage
  • a rigorous method
  • an overstated conclusion

docs.title: Typical patterns
docs.text:
• The article offers an insightful overview, though parts of it remain superficial.
  • The overall argument is coherent, but one assumption appears flawed.
  • The conclusion feels slightly overstated.

docs.title: Common mistakes for Italian speakers
docs.text:
• interesting in every review → insightful / compelling / ambitious / limited — be more precise than generic praise
  • it is objective when you mean neutral → balanced / neutral / impartial depending meaning — these words are not exact synonyms
  • The text is not clear → The text is opaque / underdeveloped / loosely structured — precision strengthens evaluation
  • critic instead of critique → critique / criticism — critic is a person

docs.title: Mini examples
docs.text:
• Compelling is stronger than interesting because it implies persuasive force.
  • Rigorous is excellent for research, methodology, or disciplined thinking.
  • Superficial is useful, but harsh; in diplomacy, underdeveloped may work better.

docs.title: Professional upgrade
docs.text:
• At high levels, good evaluation avoids both empty praise and vague negativity.
  • Advanced critique is specific: identify strength, limitation, effect, and implication.
  • Use balanced evaluation formulas like while X is persuasive, Y remains underexplored.

docs.title: Core vocabulary
docs.text: Word Significato Example frame inquadrare / presentare in un certo modo The issue is framed as a technical problem. reframe riformulare / re-inquadrare We need to reframe the question. narrative narrativa / cornice interpretativa That narrative is too simplistic. lens lente / prospettiva analitica We can examine this through a social lens. emphasis enfasi / accento The emphasis should be on long-term value. priority priorità Safety remains the top priority. context contesto Context matters here. dimension dimensione / aspetto The issue also has an ethical dimension. angle angolazione / prospettiva That is an interesting angle. perspective prospettiva / punto di vista From a policy perspective, the move makes sense.

docs.title: Useful combinations
docs.text:
• frame an issue
  • reframe a question
  • shape a narrative
  • through a lens
  • shift the emphasis
  • set priorities
  • in context
  • add a dimension
  • take an angle
  • from a perspective

docs.title: Typical patterns
docs.text:
• The issue is often framed as a technical problem, but it also has a human dimension.
  • If we reframe the question, a different solution becomes possible.
  • From a policy perspective, the measure is understandable.

docs.title: Common mistakes for Italian speakers
docs.text:
• vision for opinion → perspective / view / stance — choose the word that fits the analytical function
  • contexture → context — false form
  • change point of view as literal translation → shift perspective / take a different angle — use natural English phrasing
  • narration for public narrative → narrative — narration is more about the act of telling

docs.title: Mini examples
docs.text:
• Narrative is very useful in political, social, media, and cultural analysis.
  • Lens is common in academic and reflective English, but should not be overused.
  • Frame and reframe help you talk about meaning, not just facts.

docs.title: Professional upgrade
docs.text:
• C2 English often involves not only what you say, but how you position it.
  • Reframing is central in negotiation, writing, debate, and advanced speaking.
  • Compare: This is a cost issue vs This is a long-term investment decision.

docs.title: Core vocabulary
docs.text: Pair Difference Example accurate / precise accurate = correct; precise = exact in detail The summary is accurate but not very precise. efficient / effective efficient = little waste; effective = gets the result The system is efficient, but not always effective. sensible / sensitive sensible = practical; sensitive = delicate / easily affected This is a sensitive issue. historic / historical historic = important in history; historical = related to history It was a historic decision. economic / economical economic = related to the economy; economical = not wasteful This car is economical to run. credible / plausible credible = believable and trustworthy; plausible = seems possible The explanation sounds plausible, but not fully credible.

docs.title: Useful combinations
docs.text:
• factually accurate
  • historically important
  • credible claim
  • highly sensitive
  • an economical option
  • an economic issue
  • a precise definition
  • an effective solution
  • an efficient process
  • a plausible explanation
  • a sensible choice
  • a historical source

docs.title: Typical patterns
docs.text:
• The summary is accurate but not very precise.
  • The new system is efficient, but it may not be effective in every context.
  • This is a sensitive issue, so the wording matters.

docs.title: Common mistakes for Italian speakers
docs.text:
• sensible for sensitive → sensitive — major false friend for Italian speakers
  • economic for cheap to use → economical — economic relates to the economy, not thrift
  • historic for old → historical — historic means important, not simply past
  • plausible as true → credible / true / plausible depending evidence — plausible means believable, not necessarily correct

docs.title: Mini examples
docs.text:
• Accurate and precise often overlap, but not completely: something can be accurate in general but imprecise in detail.
  • Efficient and effective are a classic pair in professional English.
  • Credible often sounds stronger than plausible, because it implies more trustworthiness.

docs.title: Professional upgrade
docs.text:
• Near-synonym control is one of the clearest signs of lexical maturity.
  • Create personal contrast pairs and reuse them in sentences, not isolation.
  • At C2 level, nuance is often the difference between good English and excellent English.

docs.title: Core vocabulary
docs.text: Expression Significato Example carry weight avere peso / autorevolezza His opinion carries weight. a grey area zona grigia / caso poco definito This remains a grey area legally. a long shot possibilità remota It is a long shot, but worth trying. on the same page essere allineati We need to make sure we are on the same page. a turning point punto di svolta The merger was a turning point. a wake-up call campanello d’allarme The results were a wake-up call. the bigger picture il quadro generale We should look at the bigger picture. hold up reggere / restare valido The argument does not hold up under scrutiny. backfire ritorcersi contro That strategy could backfire. fall through saltare / non andare in porto The deal fell through at the last minute.

docs.title: Useful combinations
docs.text:
• carry real weight
  • remain a grey area
  • be a long shot
  • get on the same page
  • mark a turning point
  • serve as a wake-up call
  • look at the bigger picture
  • hold up under scrutiny
  • badly backfire
  • fall through unexpectedly

docs.title: Typical patterns
docs.text:
• His criticism carries weight because he knows the field well.
  • It is still a grey area legally.
  • The original plan fell through, so we had to improvise.

docs.title: Common mistakes for Italian speakers
docs.text:
• use idioms everywhere → use idioms selectively — idiomaticity means natural fit, not saturation
  • translate Italian idioms directly → replace them with natural English phrases — literal transfer is dangerous
  • overuse slang for sounding native → prefer high-utility idioms and chunks — C2 naturalness is controlled, not theatrical
  • same page literal confusion → on the same page — idioms are fixed forms

docs.title: Mini examples
docs.text:
• Not all idioms are equal: some are common and useful, others are old-fashioned or too informal.
  • High-level learners should focus on high-utility idiomatic chunks, not random idioms from lists.
  • Idiomatic English often works best when mixed with otherwise plain, clear language.

docs.title: Professional upgrade
docs.text:
• Idiomaticity is about sounding naturally placed in the discourse community.
  • Ask not only Is this idiom correct? but also Would a skilled speaker use it here?
  • Prefer idioms that serve a communicative purpose: summary, evaluation, contrast, reaction, or framing.

docs.title: Core vocabulary
docs.text: Phrasal verb Meanings Example bring up mention; raise a child She brought up an important issue. work out exercise; solve; develop well We need to work out a solution. set up arrange; establish; frame someone They set up a new system. take on accept; challenge; employ; acquire She took on more responsibility. come across find by chance; seem to others He comes across as confident. carry out perform; execute We carried out a detailed review. phase out remove gradually The old model will be phased out. roll out launch officially The company rolled out the update. break down stop functioning; analyse; collapse emotionally Let’s break the issue down step by step. hold back restrain; delay; not reveal Something is holding the project back.

docs.title: Useful combinations
docs.text:
• bring up a point
  • work out a solution
  • set up a process
  • take on responsibility
  • come across well
  • carry out research
  • phase out a product
  • roll out an update
  • break down a task
  • hold back information

docs.title: Typical patterns
docs.text:
• How did the idea come across to the audience?
  • They plan to phase out the older version next year.
  • We carried out a detailed review of the process.

docs.title: Common mistakes for Italian speakers
docs.text:
• translate every phrasal verb literally → learn meaning in chunks — polysemy makes word-for-word logic unreliable
  • set up it → set it up — pronoun placement matters in separable phrasal verbs
  • use phrasal verbs in ultra-formal text by default → choose between phrasal verb and Latinate alternative — register choice is part of mastery
  • take in charge → take charge / take on responsibility — avoid Italian-based calques

docs.title: Mini examples
docs.text:
• Many advanced phrasal verbs are common in professional English, not only in casual speech.
  • Come across is especially useful for talking about impression and tone.
  • Phase out and roll out are key verbs in change management and policy language.

docs.title: Professional upgrade
docs.text:
• At C1–C2, the challenge is not only knowing phrasal verbs, but choosing them for the right effect.
  • Compare investigate vs look into, establish vs set up, execute vs carry out.
  • Polysemy means the same phrasal verb may feel natural in one sentence and wrong in another.

docs.title: Core vocabulary
docs.text: Family Significato / function Example assume / assumption / assumed presupporre / supposizione That assumption seems weak. justify / justification / justifiable giustificare / giustificazione The decision is hard to justify. rely / reliable / reliability fare affidamento / affidabile / affidabilità We need reliable data. respond / response / responsive rispondere / risposta / reattivo The system is highly responsive. analyse / analysis / analytical analizzare / analisi / analitico The paper takes an analytical approach. persist / persistence / persistent persistere / persistenza / persistente The problem is persistent. adapt / adaptation / adaptable adattare / adattamento / adattabile Teams must remain adaptable. perceive / perception / perceptive percepire / percezione / perspicace Public perception matters. resist / resistance / resistant resistere / resistenza / resistente There was strong resistance to the change. sustain / sustainable / sustainability sostenere / sostenibile / sostenibilità Sustainability is now a strategic priority.

docs.title: Useful combinations
docs.text:
• justify a decision
  • rely on evidence
  • analytical approach
  • persistent problem
  • adapt to change
  • perception of risk
  • sustainable growth

docs.title: Typical patterns
docs.text:
• The decision is difficult to justify.
  • There are concerns about the reliability of the data.
  • The paper takes a highly analytical approach.

docs.title: Common mistakes for Italian speakers
docs.text:
• analytic for adjective always → analytical — analytic exists but is not always the default choice
  • resistence → resistance — spelling trap
  • sustainability as abstract decoration → use with specific meaning — high-level nouns should not be empty buzzwords
  • make an analyse → do an analysis / analyse — wrong derivational form

docs.title: Mini examples
docs.text:
• Control of word families helps you vary sentence structure and avoid repetition.
  • Knowing assume / assumption / assumed / presumably lets you move across registers and functions.
  • Derivation is useful, but over-nominalisation can make writing heavy.

docs.title: Professional upgrade
docs.text:
• C2 learners should build lexical networks, not isolated vocabulary items.
  • Train by rewriting the same sentence with verb, noun, adjective, and adverb forms where possible.
  • Derivational control is a major asset in writing, paraphrasing, and formal speaking.

docs.title: Core vocabulary
docs.text: Verb Significato Example evaluate valutare We need to evaluate the results carefully. outline delineare / riassumere i punti principali The report outlines three options. clarify chiarire Could you clarify that point? synthesise sintetizzare / integrare idee The article synthesises several perspectives. prioritise dare priorità a We need to prioritise the urgent tasks. allocate allocare / assegnare Resources should be allocated more carefully. facilitate facilitare This tool facilitates collaboration. implement implementare / mettere in pratica The plan was implemented in stages. monitor monitorare We need to monitor the impact over time. mitigate mitigare / ridurre We should mitigate the risks. justify giustificare How do you justify that choice? substantiate comprovare / supportare con prove The claim needs to be substantiated.

docs.title: Useful combinations
docs.text:
• evaluate performance
  • outline a proposal
  • clarify a point
  • synthesise ideas
  • prioritise tasks
  • allocate resources
  • facilitate discussion
  • implement a plan
  • monitor progress
  • mitigate risk
  • justify a choice
  • substantiate a claim

docs.title: Typical patterns
docs.text:
• Could you clarify what you mean by that?
  • The proposal outlines three possible approaches.
  • We need to monitor the impact over time.

docs.title: Common mistakes for Italian speakers
docs.text:
• realize a project → carry out / implement a project — avoid literal transfer from Italian realizzare
  • argumented → well-argued / substantiated — false friend
  • control a process meaning monitor → monitor / oversee — control often implies power, not observation
  • supports with evidences → supports with evidence — evidence is uncountable

docs.title: Mini examples
docs.text:
• These verbs are common across business, policy, education, and academic English.
  • Mitigate is especially valuable when discussing risk, impact, or downside.
  • Substantiate sounds stronger and more formal than support.

docs.title: Professional upgrade
docs.text:
• Professional English often depends on verb choice more than on complexity of grammar.
  • Strong high-level writing uses verbs that specify function: evaluate, clarify, synthesise, mitigate, allocate.
  • Learn not only meaning but also object type: we allocate resources, mitigate risk, substantiate claims.

docs.title: Core vocabulary
docs.text: Word Significato Example turning point punto di svolta That was a turning point for me. aftermath conseguenze / periodo successivo The aftermath was harder than expected. insight intuizione / comprensione profonda The experience gave me new insight. realisation presa di coscienza The realisation came much later. hesitation esitazione I noticed a moment of hesitation. tension tensione There was visible tension in the room. ambiguity ambiguità The ending leaves room for ambiguity. closure chiusura emotiva / conclusione The conversation brought some closure. setback battuta d’arresto It was a serious setback. breakthrough svolta positiva / progresso decisivo The meeting led to a breakthrough. lingering persistente / che rimane There was a lingering sense of doubt. underlying sottostante / latente We still need to address the underlying issue.

docs.title: Useful combinations
docs.text:
• a difficult aftermath
  • gain insight
  • come to a realisation
  • a moment of hesitation
  • build tension
  • leave ambiguity
  • seek closure
  • suffer a setback
  • achieve a breakthrough
  • a lingering feeling
  • an underlying cause
  • a turning point in

docs.title: Typical patterns
docs.text:
• In hindsight, it was a turning point.
  • What stayed with me was the realisation that...
  • The experience brought a kind of closure, though not in the way I had expected.

docs.title: Common mistakes for Italian speakers
docs.text:
• emotion words too basic only → expand with reflection vocabulary — advanced narrative requires interpretive language
  • history for story → story / account / narrative — choose the right discourse noun
  • closure as just ending → closure — this word is about emotional or narrative completion
  • under-the-surface literal phrasing → underlying — use the established adjective

docs.title: Mini examples
docs.text:
• Reflective English often moves between event, interpretation, and implication.
  • In hindsight, looking back, and what struck me most was... are high-value narrative frames.
  • Ambiguity is useful when a story does not end with simple certainty.

docs.title: Professional upgrade
docs.text:
• C2 narrative control is not only about sequencing events but about shaping voice, reflection, and meaning.
  • Use reflective vocabulary to move beyond simple storytelling into interpretation.
  • Strong personal writing often balances vivid detail with abstract insight.

docs.title: C2 Naturalness: From Correct to Native-Like
Core vocabulary
docs.text: Expression Significato Example slightly leggermente The tone feels slightly too strong. fairly abbastanza / piuttosto The task is fairly straightforward. pretty abbastanza / parecchio It is pretty common in spoken English. roughly all’incirca The process takes roughly an hour. technically tecnicamente / in senso stretto Technically, that is correct. basically in sostanza / praticamente Basically, we need more time. strictly speaking a rigor di termini Strictly speaking, that is not accurate. oddly enough stranamente / sorprendentemente Oddly enough, nobody objected. in practice in pratica / nei fatti In practice, the rule works differently. all things considered tutto sommato All things considered, it was a good result. that said detto questo / ciò detto That said, there are still some risks. if anything semmai / al contrario If anything, the problem is getting worse.

docs.title: Useful combinations
docs.text:
• slightly misleading
  • fairly common
  • pretty straightforward
  • roughly speaking
  • technically correct
  • basically the same
  • strictly speaking
  • oddly enough
  • in practice
  • all things considered
  • that said
  • if anything

docs.title: Typical patterns
docs.text:
• Technically, that is correct, but in practice it rarely works like that.
  • The process is fairly straightforward once you get used to it.
  • That said, there are still a few risks we should keep in mind.

docs.title: Common mistakes for Italian speakers
docs.text:
• avoid small discourse words as unimportant → learn them as fluency tools — naturalness depends heavily on such items
  • use only big vocabulary to sound advanced → mix precision with natural softeners — native-like control is not constant grandiosity
  • translate micro-markers literally from Italian → learn English discourse chunks — small markers are rarely one-to-one across languages
  • basically everywhere → use selectively — overused discourse markers weaken style

docs.title: Mini examples
docs.text:
• Very advanced English often sounds natural because of small control words, not spectacular vocabulary alone.
  • Micro-markers help with tone, speed, stance, and live interaction.
  • That said, strictly speaking, in practice, and all things considered are especially powerful.

docs.title: Professional upgrade
docs.text:
• The difference between correct and native-like often lies in tiny choices.
  • Build automaticity with discourse chunks, tonal softeners, and compact evaluative phrases.
  • At C2, elegance is often the result of selection, not ornament.

docs.title: Chunk 3 Review and Self-Check
docs.text:
Use these questions to review the whole chunk:

  • Can you explain the difference between issue, concern, challenge, drawback, and problem?
  • Can you speak or write with different levels of certainty?
  • Can you soften disagreement without becoming vague?
  • Can you move between informal, neutral professional, and formal language?
  • Can you evaluate an idea using words like compelling, flawed, coherent, rigorous, overstated?
  • Can you frame the same topic from different perspectives?
  • Can you distinguish between near-synonyms such as accurate / precise, efficient / effective, credible / plausible?
  • Can you use idiomatic language without overdoing it?
  • Can you control advanced phrasal verbs and choose between them and formal alternatives?
  • Can you use word families flexibly to paraphrase and vary sentence structure?
  • Can you sound more natural through small discourse markers like that said, strictly speaking, in practice, and all things considered?

docs.title: Suggested personal practice
docs.text:
• Rewrite five plain sentences with more precise vocabulary.
  • Rewrite three strong statements with more diplomatic tone.
  • Rewrite one email in informal, neutral professional, and formal versions.
  • Record yourself giving a short argument, then improve your stance markers and hedging.
  • Write a reflective paragraph using narrative vocabulary from this chunk.
  • Build your own personal list of ten near-synonym contrasts and ten high-utility discourse chunks.

docs.title: Professional extension
docs.text:
For maximum benefit, combine this chunk with:

  • advanced speaking tasks from B2–C2
  • writing tasks involving critique, reflection, and argumentation
  • pronunciation work on thought groups, nuclear stress, and stance through intonation
  • error tracking focused on false friends, overclaiming, and register mismatch

At this level, vocabulary work is not separate from communication. It is the engine behind:

  • stronger arguments
  • more credible tone
  • more natural interaction
  • better writing style
  • better listening comprehension
  • more flexible reformulation
  • more precise self-expression
